May 11

Decomposed character note across psychology, backstory, and narrative reveal layers
Decomposed character note across psychology, backstory, and narrative reveal layers
This is a good test case because it contains four distinct assertions that belong in four different places. Let me decompose it.

Track B (Layer 2 — Objective Backstory): "Applejack genuinely mastered the machine gun, built solidarity." This is a pure chronological fact. What she did, objectively, in the pre-story period. No psychology, no reader experience. Just the event.

Track A (Layer 3 — Psychological Architecture): "She feels like a liar because she drummed up hope against what felt like unwinnable odds." This is her opening wound — the psychological state she enters the story with. It belongs in Track A not as a change that happens during the story, but as the baseline architecture you need documented before Chapter 1. Specifically, the mechanism is important: her imposter syndrome is uniquely structured. She didn't lie. She performed genuine competence and genuine solidarity. The wound comes from the gap between what she controlled (her own performance, her leadership) and what she didn't control (the material asymmetry of the changeling military). That specific causal structure of the wound belongs here.

Also Track A — a separate note: "Her imposter syndrome isn't truly warranted." This is the epistemological gap between her self-perception and objective reality. Your framework already names this explicitly: "Applejack believes she is at the machine to contribute. The reader feels, through implication, that Applejack is at the machine gun to escape the burden of leadership." That gap is a Layer 3 asset — it must be documented as part of her psychological architecture so that every scene you design with her in Chapter 1 can be checked against it. The reader's perception diverges from hers, and Track A is where you hold the true picture the reader should be forming.

PlotPointSubjectLink Payload (Layer 4 — Reveal Arc): The fact that this backstory "isn't dramatized but revealed and apparent later" means the reveal sequence is a Layer 4 concern, not Layer 2 or 3. It doesn't go in Track B because Track B holds the objective fact independent of when the reader learns it. The unveiling — which plot points expose which layer of this backstory, and what gets recontextualized when they do — belongs on the payload track of the Applejack-to-PlotPoint links for the scenes where it surfaces. For example, Scene 4.5 (The Last Roundup callback) is clearly one of those reveal moments. The payload note there should say: this scene exposes the Track B fact about the machine gun training, and the reader now retroactively understands why she was at the gun in Chapter 1.

The asymmetric material reality clause ("because of the asymmetric material reality of the changelings' military strength") is a cross-reference, not a note. It points to a Codex entry or world physics note about changeling military capability. The causal arrow from that external reality into Applejack's psychology should appear as a brief link in Track A, not duplicated as a full note. Track A holds the psychological effect; the cause lives in its own entity.

"Faust's original Applejack was the star of the town (Applebuck Season, The Last Roundup, Leap of Faith where everypony trusts her endorsement). Later Applejack didn't have initiative and was a background pony. This manifests as Applejack's imposter syndrome at the start of the story and subsequent need to be at a machine gun to feel like she's contributing in chapter 1. Applejack was trust into a season 1-tier spotlight as a general, but she has internalized her late-season passivity and background-ness after feeling small and helpless at Ain Trotgourait behind Twilight's shield when her Honesty was worthless against warlords with no moral compass. (She actually played a part in breaking Fizzlepop's worldview, but doesn't realize it because it's worthless against all the other warlords who are mindless)"

Here is another dense note. Instead of me asking you to put into track buckets that were designed but not validated yet, I think it's more productive if I ask: from first principles, identify what kinds of claims/assertions are being made in this block of text?

The goal of separating notes into tracks is to make it so every track answers a particular kind of claim type about the project in only one cognitive mode, without messiness, because that is how the categorization will actually be effective.

May 11

Delineated seven distinct claim types underlying character psychology analysis
Delineated seven distinct claim types underlying character psychology analysis
Let me go through the note and name what each sentence is actually doing as a claim.

"Faust's original Applejack was the star of the town... Later Applejack didn't have initiative and was a background pony."

This is a source material observation. It makes a claim about the canonical FiM text — about patterns in how Hasbro wrote the character across seasons. It is not a claim about the story world at all. The author is treating the source material as an external dataset to be analyzed.

"This manifests as Applejack's imposter syndrome..."

This is a causal bridge claim — it connects the source material observation to an in-world psychological state. The mechanism being asserted is: the inconsistency in canon writing explains why AJ psychologically is where she is. This is a unique and somewhat unstable claim type because it has one foot in the metatextual register and one foot in the in-world psychology register. It is doing two jobs simultaneously and those jobs belong in different places.

"...subsequent need to be at a machine gun to feel like she's contributing in chapter 1"

This is a psychological state claim — what she believes, feels, and needs, stated in the omniscient voice. It is a Layer 3 assertion about who she is at story-open.

"Applejack was thrust into a season 1-tier spotlight as a general, but she has internalized her late-season passivity and background-ness after feeling small and helpless at Ain Trotgourait behind Twilight's shield when her Honesty was worthless against warlords with no moral compass."

This contains two distinct claim types fused together:

A backstory event claim — Ain Trotgourait happened, she was behind Twilight's shield, her Honesty failed against warlords. This is an objective fact about what occurred, stated as if by an omniscient historian.

A causal mechanism claim — the event produced the psychological state. "She has internalized X after Y." This is the arrow of causation connecting the event to the psychology. It is tempting to put this in the same bucket as either the event or the psychology, but it is actually a third thing: the explanation of how one became the other. It is an assertion about mechanism, not about the state or the event itself.

"She actually played a part in breaking Fizzlepop's worldview, but doesn't realize it because it's worthless against all the other warlords who are mindless."

This is an epistemological gap claim. It holds two things simultaneously: what is objectively true (she did contribute), and what she perceives (she didn't). The claim is specifically about the discrepancy between those two. This is not a psychological state claim and not a backstory event claim — it is a claim about the gap, which is reader-facing. The reader is meant to know something the character doesn't. This is the most distinctly different claim type in the entire note.

Summary of distinct claim types found:

Source material observation — a claim about the canonical text being used as input
Causal bridge (source → psychology) — explaining in-world psychology through canonical patterns; lives in two registers simultaneously
Psychological state — what the character believes, feels, or needs at a given point in time, stated omnisciently
Backstory event — what objectively happened, independent of anyone's perception
Causal mechanism — the arrow connecting an event to a psychological outcome
Epistemological gap — the discrepancy between what is objectively true and what the character perceives, which is inherently reader-facing
The practical implication for your track design is that types 3, 4, and 5 are frequently fused in practice but are genuinely separable, and type 6 is categorically different from all the others because it requires a second observer to exist — the reader — and therefore belongs in a different cognitive mode from the omniscient ones.

Maybe the task forward should not be trying to break up notes into sentences that go here or there, but rather to read my notes and build each track cleanly from scratch?

May 11

Validated synthesis approach over decomposition methodology
Validated synthesis approach over decomposition methodology
Yes, and the reason is precise: your existing notes were written in a mixed cognitive mode, so decomposing them sentence by sentence preserves that mixed-mode thinking in a different container. You'd be doing cleanup work that leaves the underlying problem intact.

Writing each track from scratch, using the existing notes as source material, is the correct direction because the track's cognitive mode acts as a constraint on the writing. When you sit down to write the Layer 3 Track A note for Applejack, the question "who is this creature and why, independent of story events" will force you to synthesize across all the raw material and produce a claim that is actually that kind of claim. The existing notes become inputs you mine, not outputs you reorganize.

There's a second benefit that only shows up with the from-scratch approach: gaps become visible. When you try to write the epistemological gap track for Applejack at story-open and find you can't articulate what the reader should perceive versus what she perceives, that difficulty is diagnostic. It tells you the Layer 4 thinking hasn't been done yet. Decomposing existing notes would have hidden that gap by filling the track with authorial omniscient assertions that look like reader perception tracking but aren't.

The practical implication is that your existing dense notes don't get deleted — they become a reference layer, roughly equivalent to raw research notes before a document is written. The track notes are the document.

What are the pros and cons of starting with Applejack who is the main character versus starting with a minor character?

May 11

Weighed tradeoffs between stress-testing rigor and iteration risk
Weighed tradeoffs between stress-testing rigor and iteration risk
Starting with Applejack

Pros:

She has the most raw material, so the synthesis exercise is richest
Her tracks define the standard everything else is measured against — if the track design handles her complexity, it can handle anyone
She is the perceptual lens for most of the story, so her Layer 3 and Layer 4 tracks are foundational to designing every other character's reveal arc
Cons:

She is the hardest case, so you're simultaneously stress-testing the track design and doing the most cognitively demanding synthesis work at the same time
Her psychology is deeply entangled with Twilight, Celestia, and Kemerskai — fully articulating her tracks requires partial clarity on those relationships first, which you don't yet have
Most raw material also means hardest to know when a track is complete
Starting with a minor character

Pros:

Simpler case validates whether the track structure actually works before you commit it to the most important entity
Lower stakes if you discover the track design needs revision mid-exercise
Faster to complete, giving early signal on whether the approach is working
Cons:

Too simple a case might give false confidence — the tracks might appear to work and then break on Applejack
Minor characters' Layer 4 tracks are partially defined by what Applejack perceives, so you'd be building dependent tracks before the anchor exists
The underlying problem with both

There's a circular dependency. Applejack's tracks require some clarity on how other characters function against her. Other characters' Layer 4 tracks require clarity on what Applejack perceives. You can't fully resolve either end first.

The practical solution is probably to start with a character who has a complete, bounded arc that doesn't require the main character's tracks to be finished first — someone whose Layer 3 and Layer 2 are self-contained even if their Layer 4 connects back to Applejack later. Trimmel is the strongest candidate for this in your material.

Look through all the conversations in this Claude Project "The Lioness of Tall Tale Story Design" and give an overview of the conclusions regarding the tracks.

May 11

Synthesized taxonomy frameworks and architectural systems for narrative track organization
Synthesized taxonomy frameworks and architectural systems for narrative track organization
Here is a consolidated reference of everything concluded across the project conversations.

The Goal
The problem being solved is cognitive mode mixing. When notes contain multiple assertion types in the same container, every time you open an entity to do design work you pay the overhead of mentally filtering which notes are relevant to what you are trying to do. A dense note forces you to be a psychologist, historian, director, and project manager simultaneously.

The purpose of track separation is: each track answers exactly one kind of question about the project, written in exactly one cognitive mode, so that opening a track puts you in the right thinking state without friction. A scan of Track A should never require you to shift cognitive gears mid-read. When the scan breaks, a category boundary has been violated.

A secondary purpose is diagnostic emptiness. A required track with nothing in it is a signal that design work hasn't been done, not that it doesn't need to be done. The completion profile of an entity is derived from note states, not guessed.

The Assertion Type Taxonomy
Every piece of text belongs to exactly one type. The type determines the track.

Story Content — claims about what is true in the world, written in an omniscient voice independent of any reader or character:

World Truth — invariant mechanics and structural rules of the world, true regardless of when any character discovers them or when the reader encounters them. Cognitive mode: physicist and systems engineer.
Historical Record — objective chronological events with causal chains. Carries WorldDate. What happened, stated as an omniscient historian would state it. Cognitive mode: historian.
Psychological Architecture — who a character is and why, including their epistemic limits (what they can and cannot perceive due to their worldview), stated as a standing truth at TLTT-start. Independent of story events. Cognitive mode: psychologist and biographer.
Thematic Proposition — what the story argues, through what dialectical structure, and what evidence must be deposited to convince the reader. Cognitive mode: philosopher and rhetorician.
Narrative Architecture — claims about how story content is experienced by the reader. Not what is true but when and how truth enters the reading experience:

Macro Architecture — information control decisions across multiple scenes or the whole story. What the reader knows when, what is withheld at chapter or act level, arc shape, planned GoalTrajectory beats. Cognitive mode: strategic architect.
Scene Architecture — how a specific scene produces a specific reader experience through focalization. POVBelief, ReaderPerception, GapType, FIDAnchors. Cognitive mode: director and reader psychologist.
Project Management — the author talking to themselves as a project manager, not making claims about the story world:

Obligation — work that must be done before a note can reach Confirmed state
Revision Directive — records a structural change and its downstream effects
Cross-Reference — a dependency not formally captured in the data model
Development Note — reflective record of project evolution and paradigm shifts
Bibliographic Note — real-world analogues, historical parallels, authorial research. Permanent. Does not contaminate world-bible content with extra-diegetic material.
Note State
Every note carries one of three states:

Unset — captured but not reviewed. Provisional. Not safe to use as an axiom for downstream design.
Flagged — reviewed and found to require further work. Carries a FlagNote field (separate from content) explaining why it is flagged. FlagNote is Project Management text attached directly to the claim.
Verified (Confirmed) — stable within current structural context. Safe for downstream design work to depend on, though still subject to revision if a better idea emerges.
Verified notes display first at full prominence. Unset notes display second at reduced prominence. Flagged notes display with the FlagNote visible. This visual hierarchy enforces cognitive mode without requiring the author to remember to switch.

Track Architecture Per Entity Type
Character:

Track A — Psychological Architecture (Layer 3, standing truth at TLTT-start, epistemic limits)
Track B — Historical Record (Layer 2, pre-TLTT objective backstory, carries WorldDate)
Track C — Bibliographic (real-world inspirations, historical analogues, authorial meta)
Project Management track
Codex Entry (all subtypes):

Track A — World Truth or Structural Description (how it works, Layer 1 or equivalent)
Track B — Historical Record (when it emerged and how it evolved, Layer 2)
Track C — Bibliographic
Project Management track
Theme:

Track A — Thematic Proposition (Layer 5, the argument and its dialectical structure)
Track B — Social Commentary (author's meta-level real-world relevance analysis)
Project Management track
Story Thread:

Narrative Arc track — Macro Architecture singleton. The question the reader is tracking, the arc shape, planned beats, authorial directives for what this arc must accomplish.
Project Management track
Chapter:

Reader Arc track — Macro Architecture singleton. What question the reader enters with and exits with.
Project Management track
PlotPoint Architecture
PlotPoint is the primary unit of Scene Architecture design. Its fields split into three stages:

Stage 1 — Narrative Justification (Story Content elevated to scene level):

Stakes — what narrative question does this scene exist to answer, raise, or complicate
ReaderState — master reader experience statement; what the scene should make the reader think and feel
Stage 2 — Scene Architecture panel (distinct, visually separated):

POVBelief — what the POV character sincerely believes is true about themselves and their situation right now. Scene-specific and transient, not the same as Track A psychological architecture.
POVReaderPerception — what the reader perceives about the POV character that the POV character does not
GapType enum — Ironic / Tragic / Closing / Aligned
FIDAnchors — non-negotiable concrete details that must appear in prose to make the gap legible to the reader
Stage 3 — Delivery:

Synopsis — ordered delivery sequence as experienced by POV character. Delivery blueprint only. No psychology, no reader-state analysis.
Outcome — what changed; verifies the scene serves a purpose
Discrete axis fields retained: CoreDriver, TensionPhase, ConflictType, Presentation, POVGapType, DraftStatus, WorldDate.

Connection Entity Tracks (PlotPointCharacter, PlotPointCodexEntry, etc.)
Connection entities sit between a plot point and a subject. Their tracks serve two sequential work phases:

Stage 1 — Objective Record: What is objectively happening with this entity in this scene. Written in zero focalization. Should be substantially complete before Stage 2 begins.

Stage 2 — Observed Experience: How the POV character perceives this entity in this scene, and how the reader perceives the gap between that and the objective record. ObservedBelief, ObservedReaderPerception, ObservedGapType. Written in director mode.

The UI should make the dependency visible: Stage 1 fields are primary when viewing from the subject side; Stage 2 fields are primary when viewing from the plot point side.

The Five Signals That a Track Boundary Has Been Violated
The Name Override — you rationalize why a note belongs in a track rather than recognizing it
The Scan Breaks — you mentally skip notes mid-scan because they require a different cognitive mode
The Same Question Gets Different Answers — the note answers different questions depending on how you read it; it contains two assertion types and should be split
Three Instances Make a Pattern — one misfit note is malformed; three with the same misfit quality signal a missing category
The Tooltip Would Mislead — following the track's display question would produce worse content than ignoring it
I later moved away from the hard categories defined in that conversation in favor of making the application configurable so that I could determine the tracks based on data. I'm at that stage now and want insight and analysis on how to move forward. What are the goals now? What information would be helpful?

May 11

Synthesized current architecture state and identified dual-layer goals ahead
Synthesized current architecture state and identified dual-layer goals ahead
Good framing for a re-entry point. Let me synthesize what the project files reveal about current state versus what the conversations established as the target, and then identify what information would help focus the next step.

What the Current Code Reveals
The model layer refactor is substantially complete. Looking at the project files directly:

The Note entity is in the target architecture — OwnerId, OwnerType, NoteTrackDefinitionId, NoteState enum (Unset/Flagged/Confirmed), FlagReason, SortOrder, WorldDate. This is the right shape.

NoteTrackDefinition exists with CognitiveMode, DisplayQuestion, Explanation, IsSingleton, SupportsWorldDate. The CognitiveMode enum has five values: ZeroFocalization, SceneArchitecture, Metatextual, Analogical, LinguisticExecution.

NarrativePropertyDefinition, NarrativePropertyValue, NarrativePropertyValueDefinition exist as the structured property system alongside notes.

INoteable is implemented on Chapter, PlotPoint, PlotPointSubjectLink, Subject — the composition contract is in place.

However, SubjectDefinition still carries navigation properties (List<NoteTrackDefinition>, List<NarrativePropertyDefinition>), and Subject still has a navigation property to SubjectDefinition rather than a bare SubjectDefinitionId. These were flagged for removal in the architectural conversations but appear not yet cleaned up.

The ViewModel layer is partially stubbed — OwnerViewModel with InitializeCollections, PlotPointViewModel and SubjectViewModel exist but are thin. The IViewModelRegistry still references PlotPointViewModelOld suggesting a mid-migration state.

The Two-Level Goal
The project is now at the intersection of two distinct goals that have different next actions:

Goal 1 — Application: The configurable track system needs to be usable. That means: seed data exists defining what tracks a Character has, what tracks a PlotPoint has, etc., and the UI can render those tracks and accept notes against them.

Goal 2 — Story design: Once the system accepts input, the actual work of writing clean from-scratch notes per track begins — starting with Trimmel or another bounded character as established earlier.

These are sequential. Goal 2 is blocked by Goal 1. But Goal 1 can be broken down further into what is blocked versus what is not.

What Would Help Focus the Next Step
Three questions whose answers determine the concrete next action:

1. Does the application currently render anything? Is there a UI that loads entities and shows their notes, even if the tracks are empty? Or is the executable a successful build with no working screens yet?

2. Does seed data exist? Have the NoteTrackDefinition records been inserted — the rows that say "a Character entity has a Psychological Architecture track with ZeroFocalization mode" and so on? Or is the track definition layer empty, meaning the UI would render no tracks even if it could render tracks?

3. Is the navigation property removal blocking anything? SubjectDefinition still has the lists, Subject still has the navigation property. Is this causing friction in the ViewModel layer, or is it currently harmless because the ViewModel is still using the navigation properties as a convenience while the refactor is in progress?

The answers tell us whether the next step is finishing the application layer, writing seed data, or beginning story design content — and which of those is actually blocked by what.

May 11

Weighing GitHub integration against direct file uploads
Weighing GitHub integration against direct file uploads
A GitHub MCP connector may be available if you opt in to connector discovery — that would let me read your repository directly in future conversations.

But let me give you the honest tradeoffs first so you can decide what's worth setting up.

GitHub MCP connector — if available and connected, I could read files from your repo on demand in any conversation. The upside is you never have to manually upload anything. The downside is it requires OAuth setup and the connector reads files at call time, meaning I'd be fetching individual files rather than having everything pre-loaded in project context.

Uploading .cs and .xaml files to the project — Claude projects do support multiple uploaded files and they all become part of the persistent project context, available in every conversation without re-uploading. There is no folder hierarchy — files sit flat — but the filenames are preserved so SubjectViewModel.cs stays identifiable. For a WPF project the practical limit is how many files you have and whether the total token count of all of them fits comfortably in context alongside conversation and story content.

The honest recommendation for your situation is a middle path: rather than uploading every .cs and .xaml file, create a single exported architecture document — a markdown file that contains the complete, current model and viewmodel class definitions concatenated together with filename headers. This gives me the full picture in one scannable file, avoids the flat-file clutter of 30+ individual uploads, and is easy to regenerate when the architecture changes significantly. The .xaml files matter less for architectural analysis and can be included only if a specific UI question comes up.

The questions I actually need answered from your code are: what the model classes look like, what the ViewModel base and key ViewModels look like, and what the service interface looks like. That's probably 10-15 classes that matter architecturally. Everything else is implementation detail I can reason about from description.

Would you want to try the concatenated markdown approach, or do you want to first check if the GitHub connector is available to you?

Go ahead.

# Story Planner Architecture


```csharp

```

[continue for each model class]

```csharp

```

[continue for key viewmodels]

```csharp
[paste full file]
```
The files that matter most for architectural analysis, in priority order:

Must include: Note.cs, NoteTrackDefinition.cs, NarrativePropertyDefinition.cs, NarrativePropertyValue.cs, Subject.cs, SubjectDefinition.cs, PlotPoint.cs, PlotPointSubjectLink.cs, Chapter.cs, INoteable.cs, OwnerType.cs, IStoryService.cs, OwnerViewModel.cs

Include if substantial: SubjectViewModel.cs, PlotPointViewModel.cs, any seed data or migration files

Skip: .xaml files unless a specific UI question comes up, App.cs, anything that's pure boilerplate

Once that file is in the project, every future conversation starts with the actual current architecture visible rather than reconstructing it from memory.

Mapped data organization workflow and identified configuration sequencing requirements
Mapped data organization workflow and identified configuration sequencing requirements
This clarifies the situation precisely. Let me restate what you actually have so the path forward is clear.

Current state: ~500 entities (subjects + plot points) exist in the database with names. ~300,000 words of notes exist attached to those entities via OwnerId, but sitting in the unassigned track because NoteTrackDefinitionId is null for all of them. SubjectDefinitions have not been configured, so subjects have no type assignment. NoteTrackDefinitions have not been created, so there are no tracks to assign notes to yet.

The dependency chain has a fixed order:

Create SubjectDefinitions (what types of entities exist — Character, Location, Organization, etc.)
Assign existing subjects to those types
Create NoteTrackDefinitions per type (what tracks a Character has, what tracks a Location has)
Go through notes and assign them to tracks
You cannot do step 3 before step 1. You cannot meaningfully do step 4 before step 3. Steps 1 and 2 are the current blocker for everything downstream.

The practical question before anything else: When you look at your 500+ subjects, how mixed are they really? Is it that you broadly know the types (Character, Location, Faction, Codex concept) but just haven't done the data entry, or are there subjects where you genuinely aren't sure what type they belong to?

That answer determines whether steps 1 and 2 are a fast configuration pass or a slow analytical task — and that changes what the application needs to support efficiently for you right now.

May 11

Analyzed data structure, identified claim-type confusion, recommended minimal viable track configuration
Analyzed data structure, identified claim-type confusion, recommended minimal viable track configuration
Now I have a clear picture of the actual data. Let me give you the analysis you need.

What the Data Actually Contains
Reading across both files and the old model structure, the notes fall into a smaller number of practically distinct claim types than the full taxonomy suggests. Here is what actually appears in the data:

Dated chronological events. The Chrysalis notes are the clearest example — Note 12, 16, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 30 are each a year-stamped event in an objective sequence. "981: Chrysalis arrives in Strawberry." "988: Her griffon parents die." These are pure Track B material. They carry WorldDate, they are omniscient, they have no reader perception component. There are many of these across the data and they are easy to identify. The Applejack backstory notes (0, 6) are the same thing without the explicit date stamps.

Psychological architecture. Notes about who a character fundamentally is, stated in the omniscient voice without a date. Applejack's imposter syndrome mechanism (Notes 1, 5). Chrysalis's narcissism and its specific structure — "she doesn't want a friend who accepts her; she wants subjects who worship her." These are Track A content. They are standing truths at story-open, not event records.

Historical analogies and source material observations. The Pétain note, the Washington note, the Lech Wałęsa question, the Faust-vs-Hasbro AJ note. These are Track C / Bibliographic. They are the author's analytical frame, not fabula truths. Crucially, they are also the most likely notes to have been partially decomposed into Track A claims — Pétain's role has already been partially synthesized into AJ's arc description. The raw analogy note and the synthesized psychological claim are two different things.

Arc trajectory / macro narrative architecture. Applejack Note 8 — "From the start to the town hall... from the town hall to Trimmel... post-Trimmel." This is not psychology and not backstory. It is the author's planned arc structure: where the character starts, the pivot points, where they end. This needs its own track because it is strategic, not descriptive.

World mechanics / structural physics. The Charitostatics note on "Establishing the Equestrian Republic" (Note 2) is the clearest example. This is not a thematic argument — it is a physical law that is true in the world independent of any character's knowledge. "Extracted love is thermodynamically inferior to donated love. Not because of ethics. Because extraction destroys the conditions for generating the input." This is Layer 1 content that was filed under a StoryThread or Theme entity because there was no better place for it.

Thematic propositions. "Power is a burden to be carried, not a prize to be seized." The republic theorem that Twilight's paper will prove. These are what the story argues, not what is physically true in the world. The distinction from world mechanics is subtle but important: the charitostatic effect is a fact; the republic being morally preferable because of it is an argument.

Authorial decisions and open obligations. "Don't have Chrysalis meddle in Aquileia at all." "Need to figure out the Rockefeller warning." "Lech Wałęsa as more inspiration?" These are project management notes masquerading as world-bible content. They belong in a separate track not because they're unimportant but because reading them during design work breaks the cognitive mode.

Scene delivery blueprints. The Synopsis content on plot points — Scene 28.3 is the most extreme example. This is a narrative delivery sequence, but it also contains backstory (the Chrysalis/Eagleclaw school history), psychological analysis, and dialogue fragments all fused together. The double-newline migration split them into separate notes but each note may still contain multiple claim types. This is the hardest category to organize because the content inside is genuinely mixed.

Scene-specific reader perception notes. The TwiJack thread updates — "Dramatic irony. Of course they'll be together." "It proves that Love requires Humility, which Chrysalis lacks completely." These are the author designing what the reader should perceive and feel at this moment. They are not delivery blueprints and not world truths. They are Layer 4 notes that were correctly placed on the connection entity but mixed with objective arc records.

Prose fragments. Dialogue that was written into synopsis notes. "I knew what you were, and I loved you anyway." "She chose to be the manticore they laughed at instead of be my sister." These are committed language, not design claims.

The Three Biggest Category Problems in This Specific Data
Problem 1: Codex Entries are not a coherent category. Looking at what's actually in there, you have at minimum: world physics (Charitostatics), organizational entities (The Equestrian Republic, VOPS, the Regency Council), geographic references, historical eras, and what should properly be StoryThread or Theme entities. "Establishing the Equestrian Republic" is a StoryThread, not a codex entry. These need to be re-sorted first before track assignment because the right tracks for a StoryThread entity are different from the right tracks for a World Principle entity.

Problem 2: Character appearance analysis conflates Layer 3 with Layer 4. The character appearance notes in plot points (the "Analysis:" fields in the markdown) contain a mix of standing psychological truth about the character and scene-specific observation. Chrysalis's narcissism architecture in Scene 28.3 is Track A content about Chrysalis that got written in the scene context. The scene-specific perception — what Eagleclaw observes about Chrysalis in this moment — is link payload content. These need to be split, with the standing psychological truth migrated to the Character entity's Track A.

Problem 3: Themes vs. World Mechanics vs. StoryThreads are blurred. The old Theme category contains: thematic propositions ("Power is a burden"), world physics explanations (Charitostatics), and what should be StoryThread arc notes (TwiJack dynamic analysis). Themes in your new model should hold only what the story argues. World physics belongs in Codex entries. Arc tracking belongs in StoryThreads.

Practical Minimum Track Configuration for the Organizing Task
You do not need the full taxonomy to start organizing. You need enough tracks that a note has exactly one obvious home. Based on what is actually in the data, this is the minimum viable set:

For Character subjects:

Psychology — standing truths about who this character is, their wound, their epistemic limits. No dates, omniscient voice.
Backstory — dated or sequenced events in their pre-story history. Carries WorldDate where applicable.
Research & Analogies — historical parallels, source material observations, real-world inspirations. Author voice, not fabula.
Notes / Open Questions — authorial obligations, questions to resolve, project management.
For Codex subjects (after re-sorting into proper types):

Mechanics — how this system, organization, or place works structurally. Layer 1.
History — when and how it developed, key events in its timeline. Layer 2.
Research — real-world parallels.
For Theme subjects:

Argument — what the story claims, through what evidence, against what counterargument.
For StoryThread subjects:

Arc — the reader's question, planned trajectory, significant GoalTrajectory beats.
For Plot Points:

Planning Notes — catch-all for now. The synopsis delivery content can stay as-is until you reach the Scene Architecture phase. Do not try to reorganize plot point notes during the entity organization pass.
For PlotPointSubjectLink connections:

Objective Record — what is objectively happening with this subject in this scene.
Arc & Perception — reader perception design, dramatic irony notes, arc impact.
Recommended Order of Operations
Given the state of the data, the natural sequence is:

First, re-sort the Codex Entries and StoryThreads into proper subject types. This is a classification task, not a note-writing task. You are deciding which entity is a World Principle, which is an Organization, which is a StoryThread, which is a Theme. The notes themselves don't move yet — just the entity type changes.

Second, create the SubjectDefinitions and NoteTrackDefinitions as described above. This is configuration data entry, not content work.

Third, begin the note assignment pass on Character entities — these are the highest-value entities and their notes are the most clearly classifiable. The Chrysalis dated events are obvious Track B. The AJ psychological architecture notes are obvious Track A. Starting here gives early signal on whether the track structure is working.

Fourth, address the Character appearance analysis problem as you go — when you encounter a note on a PlotPointCharacter link that is actually standing psychological truth about the character, move it to that character's Track A during the character pass.

The plot point synopsis notes and the link payload notes are the last and hardest pass. Do not start there.

After stepping away and reflecting and recalling, I think it's worth reflecting on the full casual chain of how I got to the position I am in today with regard to the story design project of TLTT and where my current 300k words of data comes from, how it is structured and what it contains and why I made it.

This project started in February 2025 as my classic setup of putting notes in a google doc for a story. In November 2025 I discovered that Gemini 2.5 Pro could reason about story logic (but was awful at prose). I had a week long thread rapidly expanding my story plans, and then there was a UI bug and I couldn't access it anymore, so I had to use Google Takeout to recover the raw data. The google doc would not be able to sustain the volume of insights made in those 120-ish prompts so I wanted to make a desktop application to organize it. I thought it would take a weekend but it ended up taking 3 weeks. By December 2025 I had a minimum viable story planner that could hold data. I was not planning on writing prose any time soon. I just needed a reasonable way to dump my data, then copy the full context, paste back into the gemini web app and ask my next question to get more exciting insights about my world building. Some notes are copy and paste from Gemini's output, the parts that made sense. Some notes/text fields are written by me, filtering out what I didn't like and typing out what I did. I put in the system instruction "no prose". Eventually the context grew too large to paste into the text window and I had to upload the document. This led to worse results due to RAG on the consumer app. Eventually I found Google AI Studio where I could be sure the full context was read and further specify more constraints like "no direct address" to make the text more copy and paste-able in some situations. I also used Notebook LM at some point but that had other problems with RAG and not full context. Eventually the AI Studio answers were degrading too.

Then I tried out Claude (you) and found better reasoning results and longer, complete answers, but Claude would not take the full context and truncate it; even if the consumer interface took everything, that would not be good for the usage limits. The Claude usage limits were more honest than Gemini's model of taking larger context but lowering the reasoning and output length to save compute and offer "unlimited" to the whole userbase. So I figured at 300k words this was no longer suitable for pasting the whole thing and I needed to redesign the story planner where the notes were organized for two main purposes: selective context to provide for further questions, and organizing in a way that would eventually help me write prose.

To be clear, prose is still not even on my immediate radar for the past 5 months of story design expansion. I still have around 65 outstanding questions and many more unintegrated notes. My plan before was to get all those questions answered with insights. My pattern of where to put text was as follows: If I knew what plot point in the story/chapter plan an insight sort of belong to, I would put it in the synopsis or the link between that plot point and the subject. If I didn't know yet or the insight was an overarching thing, I put it in notes of a subject. My thinking before was: if the data was in a plot point or link, it was usable to inform prose later. If it's in a note of a subject but not in a plot point, it had to make it into a plot point or link and get marked as "Incorporated". These raw capture of insights led to the mixed, anything goes nature of the notes today. There were a couple times where I had particular ideas for prose outlines, but these would get outdated or invalidated potentially so I stopped that and focused on outstanding worldbuilding questions.

Now the problems I encountered today with this data: it is all sitting in my new, squeaky clean note track separated story planner v2 after a migration I did yesterday. But now I am seeing that this data is "dirty" or so thoroughly mixed. The goal of getting cleaner context export for further analysis cannot accommodate the dirty data and I want total data to go down and duplications resolved instead of more expansion. I thought I could split up the old text into the tracks, but that doesn't seem to be the case because the data is genuinely tightly coupled, I think. The way I wrote those notes seems to be so tightly coupled, which was a product of its entry method and not necessarily wrong but served a different purpose of raw capture convenience not analysis and prose informing suitability. It seems I don't want to keep the "unclean" data in the new story planner at all. The new story planner v2 is basically a whole new architecture. The parallel was me making the story planner v2 code. I had immense friction dealing with stripping out the old v1 concepts and then getting compiliation errors so new stuff wasn't testable until I wiped out old v1 things. It seems in place manipulation isn't what I want. Is this a real problem others have with codebases?

I want to be able to see progress. I can't see progress in stuff moving from unset to the tracks if I can't delete from unset or cleanly move fragments over into separate tracks.

Therefore my new radical idea is to keep all the old data in the v1 story planner. Have both v1 and v2 open. Read the data in v1 and type what belongs in v2 in tracks. This way the v1 data is viewable in the form it lived in, which has association with how I entered it in, and the old text will not be a part of the v2 data to interfere with the new way. Use v1's isincorporated to track entry into v2. Please give an analysis of the whole causal chain and the pros and cons and nuances of this new idea for data migration.

May 11

Analyzed causal chain, validated strangler pattern migration strategy
Analyzed causal chain, validated strangler pattern migration strategy
The Causal Chain, Named Precisely
What you built in v1 was a raw capture instrument, not a reference document. The data entry pattern you described — "if I knew where it belonged, put it in the scene or link; if not, put it in subject notes" — was the correct pattern for that instrument's purpose. The instrument's job was to reduce friction between insight and persistence while Gemini was generating at high velocity. The mixed, tightly coupled nature of the notes is not a mistake. It is the correct output of a raw capture instrument operating as designed.

The problem is not that the data is dirty. The problem is that you are now trying to use a raw capture instrument's output as input to a reference document system. Those are different ontologies. You cannot mechanically transform between different ontologies — you need a semantic translation pass that a human performs. The double-newline migration preserved syntactic integrity but not semantic integrity. The data landed in the right containers by reference, but the claims inside the containers were written for a different purpose than the containers now require.

This is a named problem in information science. The v1 notes answer the question: "where does this thought go so I can export it and continue the conversation?" The v2 tracks answer the question: "what kind of claim is this and what cognitive mode does reading it require?" These questions produce different structures even when describing the same facts.

Whether This is a Real Problem in Codebases
Yes, this is one of the most well-documented problems in software engineering and it has a canonical solution. The pattern is called the strangler fig. The principle is: you never migrate a legacy system by modifying it in place. You build the new system alongside the old one. You incrementally re-implement functionality in the new system, verify it works, then stop routing to the old system. The old system stays alive as long as any of its functionality has not yet been replaced. Then you kill it.

The compilation error problem you described — where stripping out v1 concepts broke the build until you finished, meaning you couldn't test anything in between — is exactly why big-bang migration fails. It creates a period where nothing works and you can't verify progress. The strangler fig avoids this by keeping both systems operational simultaneously so you always have a working baseline.

Your proposal is the strangler fig applied to story data migration. This is the correct pattern.

The Dual-System Approach: Analysis
What is genuinely right about it:

The re-entry act is not copying. It is synthesis. When you read a v1 note and decide what to type into v2, you are making a claim-type decision at the point of entry. The Track A note you write about Applejack's imposter syndrome mechanism will be better than anything you could extract from the v1 notes because you are now thinking in Track A mode while writing it. The v1 note was written in capture mode. You are now writing in assertion mode. These produce different sentences even when describing the same psychological truth.

The v1 context is valuable as a reference precisely because it preserves the original associations. Knowing that a note lived in the Chrysalis character entity versus in a PlotPointCharacter link tells you something about how you were thinking about the claim when you wrote it. That context helps you correctly classify it in v2.

The isIncorporated flag solves the progress visibility problem completely. You have a denominator (all v1 entities) and a numerator (all incorporated entities). Progress is countable. This matters more than it might seem — the lack of visible progress is one of the primary reasons people abandon large organization projects.

The non-obvious risks:

The volume is not a weekend project. 300,000 words of source material, read and re-synthesized into clean track notes, is several months of disciplined work at whatever pace is sustainable alongside the day job. The risk is not that the approach is wrong — it is that you underestimate the duration and lose momentum mid-migration.

Not everything should go in. Some v1 content is speculative hypothesis that was never validated. Some is Gemini output that you accepted provisionally. Some is authorial obligations that are now outdated. The re-entry pass is a filter, not a copy. Everything that goes into v2 should be something you are willing to treat as an axiom for downstream design. If you are not sure whether a claim is solid, it goes in as Unset with a FlagNote, not as Confirmed. The re-entry pass and the verification pass are the same pass.

The expansion temptation is real. Re-reading v1 notes will surface the 65 outstanding questions constantly. There needs to be a clear rule: migration pass is migration only. New questions, new expansions, new insights triggered by re-reading go into a queue and do not enter v2 until the migration pass is finished. Otherwise the work expands faster than you complete it and progress stalls.

You still need the track configuration to exist before migration can begin. The prerequisite is: define your SubjectDefinitions, define your NoteTrackDefinitions per type. This is a half-day of configuration work that unblocks everything. Without it, you have nowhere to put the re-entered notes.

The deepest nuance:

What you are calling a data migration is not actually a data migration in the technical sense. It is a world bible compilation pass. Every author who works through an extended research and ideation phase eventually has to do this. The research phase produces raw notes. The compilation pass produces the reference document. The v1 is your research notes. The v2 is the reference document you are compiling from them. Re-framing it this way matters because it sets the correct expectation for what the work feels like and how long it takes. It is editorial work, not technical work. It is slow, deliberate, and the output quality depends entirely on your judgment at each decision point — which is why it cannot be automated.

The v1 does not become obsolete when v2 is complete. It becomes your research archive. If you ever need to verify why you made a particular claim in v2, the v1 note it was derived from is the citation.

In addition to the 65 v1 notes that need further research, there are also hidden questions/obligations in plot point synopses and link payloads which didn't have the boolean flag to make it apparent. I also have more notes in Google Keep which I captured and didn't put into v1 yet, and also probably 10+ conversations with Claude or Gemini on topics about the story design with new insights which I had while writing the code for v2 in parallel. I don't have delusions that project scope will stop growing. I don't have any financial deadlines or obligations regarding the project. The utility value is in the process.

I believe v2 can still have new questions added, and that is what the Flagged note state and Flag Reason are for. There will be more things added as I compile the research data into the world bible compilation. Story Planner v1 and v2 are both a world bible (or attempted to be one) and a project management tool; tracks are labeled by their purpose. Does this make sense?

Show more
May 11

Validated dual-purpose design and identified structural safeguards
Validated dual-purpose design and identified structural safeguards
Yes, this makes sense, and the reason it works is structural rather than just conceptual.

The dual purpose doesn't create a mixing problem because the two purposes live in different fields. The note content is world bible material — a psychological claim, a backstory event, a thematic argument. The note state and FlagReason are project management metadata attached to that content. They describe the confidence level and the open obligation without contaminating the claim itself. A Track A note about Applejack's imposter syndrome can simultaneously be a clean psychological assertion and a flagged item because she needs to be reconciled with the Ain Trotgourait timeline. Those two things don't interfere with each other because they're in different structural positions.

The problem in v1 was that obligations, claims, and questions were all inside the same text blob. "Need to figure out the Rockefeller warning" lived in the same Note.Content field as world-state assertions about Chrysalis's financial network. In v2, the equivalent would be: the note content holds only the world-state assertion, and if the Rockefeller question is still open, a separate note with that question as content gets created in a project management track with Flagged state and a FlagReason explaining what needs resolution.

The one boundary condition worth naming: FlagReason should stay narrow. It should describe what specific research or decision would allow the note to reach Confirmed state, not become a container for extended analysis. The moment FlagReason starts growing into paragraphs it has become another text blob, and the project management and world bible purposes start mixing again.

The accumulation picture you're describing — v1 obligations, hidden obligations in synopses, Google Keep backlog, AI conversation insights, new questions that emerge during compilation — this is not a problem with the project. It is the correct shape of a project where the utility is in the process. The system needs to be designed for a stable frontier of open questions, not for a finish line. The Flagged state is exactly the right mechanism for that. The frontier never reaches zero and that is the intended behavior.


All Applejack data.md
md

Here is data from v1 of Applejack notes and all plot points that link to her. Give a renewed analysis of what claim types are present in the v1 data which should become tracks in v2, why, with examples, and the display question and reasoning included. Give tracks for Character, for PlotPoint, and for PlotPointSubjectLink when the subject type is character. Not only layers 1-5 but also the authorial and meta textual and anything else

May 11

Categorized character, plot point, and link claim types into distinct cognitive tracks
Categorized character, plot point, and link claim types into distinct cognitive tracks
This is a rich enough dataset to do a thorough analysis. Let me go through all three entity types and name every distinct claim type I find with examples from the actual text.

Character Entity
Psychological Architecture

The clearest Layer 3 content in the file. These are standing truths about who Applejack is at TLTT-start, stated omnisciently and without a date or event trigger. Note 1 is the cleanest example: "Her imposter syndrome isn't truly warranted, but she feels it because of the asymmetric material reality of the changelings' military strength." Note 5 contains the epistemic limit: she has internalized late-season passivity and cannot see that she is hiding rather than contributing when she chooses the machine gun. The "Desc" field is partially this: "stepping up to leadership to not be a poseur" is psychology. Her belief that industry kills the soul (Note 4, the Flim and Flam wound) is also Track A material. Her epistemic blindspot about the machine gun scene — she believes she has found her proper place when the reader knows she has found her hiding place — belongs here as a named gap.

Display question: Who is this character, why are they this way, and what can they not see about themselves at the start of TLTT? CognitiveMode: ZeroFocalization

Objective Backstory

Chronologically sequenced pre-TLTT events stated as omniscient record. Note 0 and Note 6 together form this sequence: Mount Aris → feeling useless behind Twilight's shield → drift from Twilight → Luna's first offer refused → training camp → mastering the machine gun → solidarity built → Luna's second offer accepted. These carry implicit WorldDate and are Layer 2 history. The distinction from Track A is that these are what happened, not who she became as a result. The result belongs in Track A; the events belong here.

Display question: What happened to this character, in what order, before TLTT begins? CognitiveMode: ZeroFocalization

Arc Trajectory

Note 8 is the clearest pure example of this type and it has no equivalent in the other tracks. "From the start to the town hall, Applejack feels like an Imposter... From the town hall to Trimmel, she is the Honest Worker... Post Trimmel, she accepts the Hard Truth." This is not psychological architecture (it describes change, not standing truth) and not backstory (it is about the main story, not pre-story). It is the author's macro design plan for the arc: the planned pivot points, the planned emotional states at each phase, the intended trajectory. This type appears frequently in the Analysis fields too — "the fact that she returns to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3, but this time with resolve instead of guilt, serves as a visual indicator of character growth" is an arc design observation, not a scene observation. It belongs on the character entity as arc planning, not on the link entity as scene analysis.

Display question: How does this character change across the story, and what are the planned pivot points? CognitiveMode: SceneArchitecture (author as architect of the journey)

Real-World Analogies and Source Material

Two subtypes that can share a track or be separated depending on how many notes accumulate. The Washington and Pétain notes are analogical: real historical figures whose arc patterns inform the character design. The Faust vs. late-season-AJ note (Note 5, the source material part) and the Applejack's Day Off note (Note 9) are metatextual: claims about the FiM canon being used as raw material. Both types share the feature that they are written in the author's voice talking about external material, not in the omniscient in-world voice. They contain no fabula truth. The Lech Wałęsa note (Note 7) is a flagged version: an open question rather than a completed analogy, which should go in this track with Unset state. The Pétain note also contains an Arc Design observation fused in: "this story features the Anti-Vichy arc." That phrase belongs in Arc Trajectory; the Pétain analogy itself belongs here.

Display question: What real historical figures, events, or source material inform this character's design, and why? CognitiveMode: Analogical / Metatextual

Thematic Function

A smaller but distinct type. "The theme is that the truth hurts" (Note 4) and "the moral foundation of the Republic: Power is a burden to be carried, not a prize to be seized" (Note 2) are claims about what this character's arc argues, not who she is or what happened to her. These are the author naming what thematic proposition this character is designed to prove or embody. They are distinct from Arc Trajectory (which describes the journey shape) and from Track A psychology (which describes who she is). Whether this merits its own track or is combined with Arc Trajectory depends on volume. The AJ file has three or four of these, which is borderline. For a character as central as Applejack, a dedicated track is probably warranted.

Display question: What thematic proposition does this character's arc exist to prove or demonstrate? CognitiveMode: Metatextual (author speaking about the project's purpose)

PlotPoint Entity
Looking at the plot point entries in the file, the structured fields (Stakes, Synopsis, Outcome) are doing most of the work, but they have contamination problems. The Synopsis field in particular has three separate claim types fused inside it.

Delivery Sequence is what Synopsis is supposed to hold: what happens in what order as experienced by the POV character. "Applejack, Mali, Tally Stock, and a terrified volunteer run the machine gun. The four of them operating the one machine gun killed hundreds of changelings. At the end of the assault, Applejack goes to sleep in the bunker." That is pure delivery blueprint.

But many Synopsis entries also contain embedded dialogue that has been pre-written to a prose level ("I am not ordering you to stay. I am telling you that I am staying. They shall not pass.") and embedded authorial decisions ("Not sure if I want this," "Should Applejack mention poseurs in the speech?" "Maybe this goes at the end?"). The dialogue fragments are committed prose that belongs in a Prose / LinguisticExecution note track or simply accepted as part of the delivery blueprint. The authorial decisions are project management contamination.

Scene Design Notes is a track that does not exist in v1 but should exist in v2. Looking across all the plot points, there is no place in v1 for: what should the reader feel during this scene, what does the reader know that the POV character doesn't, what concrete detail must appear for the irony to be legible. These are Layer 4 scene architecture claims that belong on the PlotPoint entity itself (not on any link entity), because they describe the whole scene's reader experience design.

Display question: What should the reader feel, know, and perceive during this scene? CognitiveMode: SceneArchitecture

Authorial Directives is the project management track for the plot point. Every embedded "not sure if I want this," "need to figure out," or "maybe" in a Synopsis becomes a note in this track with Unset or Flagged state. The track exists so the delivery blueprint can be kept clean.

Display question: What design decisions or open questions need to be resolved before this scene can be written? CognitiveMode: Metatextual

PlotPointSubjectLink Entity (Character Appearance)
This entity type has the most mixed content in the entire file. Every "Analysis:" field is a blend of multiple claim types. The splitting here is the most important work.

Objective Scene Observation

What is materially and psychologically true about this character in this scene, stated in the omniscient voice. Behavioral facts: "She lost her general's uniform on the run." "She sloppily slaps a star to her standard infantry uniform." Psychological facts: "She is only a motivated volunteer, not a general." "Applejack's role as 'General' is a performative shell. She delegates the actual strategy to Star Energy and the Aquileians because she is self-aware enough to know she lacks the expertise." These are zero focalization observations. They are not arc analysis (they do not claim what the scene does to the arc). They are not reader design (they do not claim what the reader perceives differently from the character). They are factual assertions about this character's state in this moment.

Display question: What is objectively true about this character's state, situation, and psychology in this scene? CognitiveMode: ZeroFocalization

Reader Perception Design

This is the type that is most consistently absent in v1 and most needed for v2. When it does appear in v1, it is usually embedded inside an Analysis that also contains objective observations, making it invisible. The clearest example of this type in the file: "The more they cheer for 'General Applejack,' the more she feels like a liar. She feels she is wearing a costume Luna gave her." This is designed as a reader perception statement — the author is describing the gap between what the character experiences (wearing a costume) and what the reader should perceive (a genuine general who cannot see her own legitimacy). The Analysis note from the Luna confrontation scene also has this: "Applejack appears flippant of the morals, singularly focused on saving as many pony hostages as possible." This is the author designing what the reader perceives that the character cannot step outside of to see.

The FID anchors — the concrete details that make the gap legible — are embedded in Synopsis text. "She sloppily slaps a star to her standard infantry uniform" is both a delivery blueprint detail and a reader perception anchor. Ideally both live somewhere accessible, but the reader perception track is where you note why that detail must be there.

Display question: What does the reader perceive about this character in this scene that the character cannot see about themselves? What specific details must appear to make that gap legible? CognitiveMode: SceneArchitecture

Arc Function Observation

What this scene does to the character's arc, stated as the author observing from outside the scene. "The fact that she returns to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3, but this time with resolve instead of guilt, serves as a visual indicator of her character growth. She isn't hiding in the trench anymore; she is holding it." "When Trimmel says 'You played Synovial like a fiddle' it relieves Applejack of some of the imposter syndrome." These claims require knowing the overall arc trajectory to make, which is why they are a different cognitive mode from the objective observation. They are the author stepping out of the scene and placing the scene on the timeline of the character's journey. This is distinct from Arc Trajectory on the character entity: Arc Trajectory is the overall planned design; Arc Function is the scene-by-scene accounting of how individual scenes execute against that plan.

Display question: What does this scene do to this character's arc, and where does it place them on their planned trajectory? CognitiveMode: SceneArchitecture

Authorial Directives and Open Questions

Embedded in Analysis fields: "She is Eisenhower here." "In the hard speech, she must be the general because the madmare didn't work in chapter 1." "Is this a reference I want to use?" These are project management claims that have no place in any of the other tracks. Their presence inside Analysis fields is the primary source of cognitive contamination in the v1 link data. In v2 they need a dedicated track so they can be extracted from the observation and perception notes without losing them.

Display question: What authorial decisions, open questions, or obligations exist for this character's role in this scene? CognitiveMode: Metatextual

Summary Table
Entity	Track	Layer	CognitiveMode	Display Question
Character	Psychological Architecture	Layer 3	ZeroFocalization	Who is this character and why, including what they cannot see about themselves?
Character	Objective Backstory	Layer 2	ZeroFocalization	What happened to this character, in order, before TLTT begins?
Character	Arc Trajectory	Layer 4 macro	SceneArchitecture	How does this character change across the story, and what are the pivot points?
Character	Thematic Function	Layer 5	Metatextual	What proposition does this character's arc exist to prove?
Character	Analogies & Source Material	Analogical/Metatextual	Analogical	What real figures or source material inform this character's design?
PlotPoint	Scene Design	Layer 4	SceneArchitecture	What should the reader feel, know, and perceive during this scene?
PlotPoint	Authorial Directives	Project Mgmt	Metatextual	What design questions or obligations need to be resolved for this scene?
Link (Character)	Objective Observation	Layer 2/3	ZeroFocalization	What is objectively true about this character's state in this scene?
Link (Character)	Reader Perception Design	Layer 4	SceneArchitecture	What does the reader perceive that the character cannot? What details make that visible?
Link (Character)	Arc Function	Layer 4	SceneArchitecture	What does this scene do to this character's arc?
Link (Character)	Authorial Directives	Project Mgmt	Metatextual	What decisions or questions about this character's role in this scene need resolution?
I'm thinking the thematic function needs to go into the theme subject entity that embodies that proposition, not in Applejack's subject entity
When you say scene design doesn't exist in v1 do you mean the field was not part of the architecture but the content is there, scattered across other fields? Or is the content missing and I don't have any because I didn't do it yet?
I don't think authorial directives (project management directives, obligations) should be a track. They should be put into FlagReason on a note in a track, and that note goes to the Flagged state which is separate from Unset and Confirmed.
I noticed "ArcTrajectory" is about "how does this character change", but there's another planning layer which is about notes that plan out how the objective backstory and psychology are revealed. It seems like how it's revealed and how they change are orthogonal tracks, distinct enough they deserve separate tracks. For example, Applejack's core wound about her parents leaving the farm (built on top of canon, so there is stuff to Applejack I made for TLTT that FiM fans will discover) and also her trauma from Mount Aris are reveals, not changes/development. Flim & Flam validated it all. It's too easy to flip a switch and make bad product.

She learns that the world is cruel, but more importantly, she learns that her innate Earth Pony virtues are insufficient to stop that cruelty. After the Aquileian and Republican volunteers decimate the horde and Twilight rescues Fizzlepop, Applejack returns to Sweet Apple Acres. She seeks grounding in the dirt, attempting to cope with her feelings of powerlessness through hard, physical labor. She does not accompany Twilight to Canterlot to present Fizzlepop to Celestia.
Twilight goes to Canterlot and is brutally berated by Celestia. Celestia weaponizes Twilight’s trauma, framing Twilight’s frontline intervention not as heroism, but as a reckless flirtation with the exact supremacist violence that created Nightmare Moon.
Because Applejack is not in Canterlot, she does not see Celestia crush Twilight’s spirit. Applejack only sees the results: Twilight abruptly shuts down the School of Friendship, relocates to Ain Trotgourait with Pinkie to perform top-down charity, and eventually locks herself in an underground lab to build magical supply organization/teleportation networks.

The "old nerd" who loved grassroots friendship is dead, replaced by a "new nerd" who treats the world like a supply chain. They drift apart because Applejack views Twilight’s new methodology as soulless, while Twilight is too consumed by her "Reactive Atlas Complex" to explain that she is actually hiding from Celestia's geopolitical terror.
Because Applejack felt useless behind Twilight's shield in 1006, she is highly motivated to never feel useless again. When Luna eventually offers her the General's star, Applejack refuses to be a mere figurehead. She volunteers for basic training and masters the machine gun because she believes that tactical competence will prevent a repeat of Mount Aris. Applejack enters 1011 ALB believing she understands the cruelty of the world. She thinks, "I saw the warlords. I know it gets ugly. But this time, I have a gun, and I have the solidarity of my troops." The Imposter Syndrome triggers because Chrysalis’s Heer is not the Storm King’s horde. The Storm King was a disorganized, performative bully. Trimmel’s Panzer divisions are a mechanized, industrialized superpower executing Auftragstaktik. Applejack’s breakdown in Chapter 1 occurs because she realizes her preparation was fundamentally asymmetrical to the threat. She trained for a gritty infantry defense; the Changelings arrived with a macroeconomic war machine. She feels like a "Fake General" because she brought a machine gun to a logistics war. Her hard-earned, grassroots solidarity is instantly vaporized by Blitz-Essenz and artillery."

I'm not sure if the arc trajectory and backstory/psychology reveal plan are zero focalization or scene architecture.

public enum CognitiveMode
{
    ZeroFocalization,      // User as in-universe historian/physicist
    SceneArchitecture,     // User as director
    Metatextual,           // User to future self, treating the project as a project rather than in-universe
    Analogical,            // User connecting the project world to the real world
    LinguisticExecution,   // User as writer
}
Here are my existing cognitive modes. I think SceneArchitecture is too narrow. It should be about the text not being story content (layers 1, 2, 3, 5) but it's specifically layer 4/syuzhet and it's notes about designing the reader experience. I need a better term for this second enum value.
