May 11

Synthesized narrative architecture layers, disambiguated reveal planning from character arcs, and reconciled bidirectional analogical thinking
Synthesized narrative architecture layers, disambiguated reveal planning from character arcs, and reconciled bidirectional analogical thinking
Working through each point in turn.

Thematic Function belongs on the Theme entity
You are correct. "What proposition does this character's arc prove?" is a claim about the Theme, not about the Character. What lives on the Character entity instead is the mechanism: the specific psychological wound, belief change, and arc shape that constitute the character's role in testing that proposition. That is already captured by Psychological Architecture and Arc Trajectory. The thematic function is the connection between the two entities, which the PlotPoint-Theme link expresses structurally. No separate Thematic Function track is needed on Character.

Scene Design in v1: scattered and partially absent
Both things are true simultaneously for different subtypes.

The objective observation content — what is happening with this character in this scene — is present throughout the Analysis fields. It is scattered and mixed with other types, but the raw material exists.

The arc function content — what this scene does to the arc — is also present but embedded: "the fact that she returns to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3, but this time with resolve instead of guilt, serves as a visual indicator of character growth" is arc function observation. It exists but is tangled into objective observation text.

The reader perception design — what the reader perceives that the character cannot — is almost entirely absent as an explicit, standalone assertion. What you have are fragments that imply it without naming it. "The more they cheer for General Applejack, the more she feels like a liar" is the closest thing in the file: it gestures at the gap without articulating it as a designed reader experience. The FID anchors — the specific concrete details that must appear to make the gap legible — are almost completely absent.

The onion-layer reveal plan — what the reader learns about this character's backstory at what point, and what earlier scenes get recontextualized by each reveal — is genuinely absent in the AJ-specific data. The backstory content lives in other subject entities (Mount Aris, Applejack's Parents), as you found. But the planning layer — "the reader learns X at scene Y, which makes scene Z mean something different retroactively" — does not appear in any of those entries either. It has not been designed yet. This is outstanding work, not a scatter problem.

Authorial directives as FlagReason
This is the correct resolution and it is cleaner than a dedicated track. A note in any track can carry "this claim is uncertain and needs X resolved before it can be confirmed." The obligation lives in the FlagReason field on the specific uncertain note, not as a separate pile of meta-content alongside it. This preserves single-track cognitive mode while keeping the obligation attached to the claim it concerns.

Arc Trajectory and Reveal Plan are orthogonal, and their CognitiveModes differ
You have identified a real distinction. Let me be precise about what each track asserts.

Arc Trajectory asserts what is true about the character at successive points in the story. "At story-open she is in imposter mode. After the town hall she crosses into Honest Worker mode. Post-Trimmel she accepts the Hard Truth." These are factual claims about the character's psychological state at intervals. They are written from an omniscient position looking at the whole arc. The cognitive mode is ZeroFocalization: you are the in-universe historian who knows the whole timeline, not just the pre-story portion.

Reveal Plan asserts how and when the reader learns about the character's backstory and psychology. "The reader's understanding of the machine gun scene in Chapter 1 gets recontextualized when the Mount Aris backstory surfaces in Chapter X. Until then the reader sees competence and resolve; after the reveal they see that the same behavior was originally motivated by shame." This is a claim about the reading experience, not about what is true in the world. The backstory facts themselves are ZeroFocalization content on the Backstory track. The plan for when and how those facts enter the reader's understanding is a different assertion entirely. Its cognitive mode is the Layer 4 design mode — the author controlling the reader's epistemological journey.

They are orthogonal because Arc Trajectory describes character change (what the character learns and becomes) while Reveal Plan describes reader knowledge change (what the reader learns about the character). A character can change without any reveal plan attached to it. A piece of backstory can have a complex reveal plan without any character change involved. Applejack's parents leaving the farm is a reveal, not a change — she does not change because the reader learns about it. The reader's understanding of her changes. These belong in different tracks.

Renaming SceneArchitecture
Your diagnosis is correct. The mode is not specifically about scenes; it is about any assertion whose subject is the reader's experience rather than the world's facts. It covers macro-level information control (reveal sequencing, the onion-layer plan), scene-level gap design (POVBelief vs ReaderPerception), and story thread arc tracking (what question the reader is carrying). The common thread is that the author is standing outside the story world and designing how that world is experienced over time.

A name that captures this: NarrativeDesign. It is broad enough to cover both macro and scene-level work without implying it is limited to individual scenes. The author in NarrativeDesign mode is a dramaturge or narratologist — asking not "what is true" but "how does truth enter the reader's experience, and when, and in what order."

The contrast with ZeroFocalization becomes clean: ZeroFocalization asserts what is true in the world independent of any reader. NarrativeDesign asserts how what is true should reach the reader across the story's timeline.

Analogical as bidirectional and social commentary placement
The cognitive mode is the same in both directions because the thinking operation is identical: you are standing at the boundary between the story world and the real world and making a connection across it. Whether you are drawing from Pétain to inform Applejack's design, or drawing from TLTT's Stagnation of Harmony to comment on present-day political conservatism, you are doing analogical work.

What differs is the entity that owns the note, because the assertion types are different. A historical inspiration for a character's design is a claim about the character and belongs on the Character entity. A claim about what the story argues regarding the real world is a claim about a Theme and belongs on the Theme entity. The same CognitiveMode.Analogical is valid for both; the track and entity differ because the content serves different purposes.

Your conclusion is right: social commentary belongs on Theme subjects, not on Character or other subjects. The Theme entity holds both the internal proposition (what the story argues within its world) and the external relevance (what that argument means about the real world). Both tracks on a Theme entity use Analogical mode, but they face in opposite directions.

For characters, Now I'm thinking there are 3 axes: psychology vs events, pre-story vs in-story, and story-wide scope (goes in character) vs scene-level scope (goes in link). There are 8 tracks that span the character and link. Then the analogy track exists outside these 8. Is this too simple or is it precise? Is nuance lost?

I think Reader Perception Design goes only on plot point, not on character. I would be reading the NarrativeDesign note tracks from all the subjects that connect to the plot point, then put in notes for the reader perception design (Note that I am nowhere near this stage of the project yet)

May 11

Validated framework precision while identifying cognitive mode gaps
Validated framework precision while identifying cognitive mode gaps
The framework is precise where it works and has two specific places where nuance is lost. Let me go through it systematically.

Where the framework is clean
Tracks 1, 2, 7, and 8 are unambiguously correct and non-overlapping.

Track 1 (Psychology + Pre-story + Character) is Psychological Architecture. Who she is at story-open and why, formed by pre-story experience. Standing truth, no date needed, omniscient voice.

Track 2 (Events + Pre-story + Character) is Backstory Record. What happened before the story, in sequence. Carries WorldDate. Omniscient historian voice.

Track 7 (Psychology + In-story + Link) is POVBelief. What this character believes, feels, and wants specifically in this scene. Scene-local, transient, not the same as the standing Track 1 truth even when it expresses it.

Track 8 (Events + In-story + Link) is Objective Observation. What is materially happening with this character in this scene. Behavioral facts, situational facts, stated omnisciently.

These four are where your framework is doing precise work.

Track 3 is real but sits between two cognitive modes
Track 3 (Psychology + In-story + Character) is Arc Trajectory — the planned stages of psychological change across the whole story. Note 8 is its canonical example. This is a valid, necessary track.

The cognitive mode nuance: Arc Trajectory is mostly ZeroFocalization because you are asserting what is true about the character's psychological states at successive points in the story — the in-universe historian can describe a complete timeline, not just the pre-story portion. But Arc Trajectory also has a planning dimension that pure ZeroFocalization lacks. "From the start to the town hall, she is in imposter mode" is simultaneously a designed intention and an in-world fact you are committing to. The notes on this track will read slightly differently from pure Backstory notes because they are prospective rather than retrospective. This is a nuance worth naming in the track's display question — "What are the psychological stages of this character's arc, and what are the pivot points between them?" signals that the author is both the historian and the architect.

Track 4 is the problematic cell
Track 4 (Events + In-story + Character) is where the framework loses precision. In-story events naturally distribute across PlotPoint records. The Character entity is not a natural home for a sequential record of what happens to the character during the story — that would duplicate the plot. So Track 4 cannot be "in-story events record."

But the cell is not empty. What belongs there is the Reveal Map: the macro-level plan for how pre-story content (Tracks 1 and 2) enters the reader's knowledge across the story's timeline. "AJ's parents backstory is seeded through three scenes in chapters 1-3 via the Comet Shine connection, and fully disclosed at the earth pony magic revelation in chapter 5, which recontextualizes the chapter 2 speech." This is story-wide scope, it concerns events (revelations of backstory), and it is in-story (it plans scenes). So it fits the cell.

The nuance the framework misses here is CognitiveMode. Track 4 is NarrativeDesign mode, not ZeroFocalization. You are not describing what is true in the world — you are planning when and how what is true enters the reader's understanding. The content of Tracks 1 and 2 is ZeroFocalization; the Reveal Map that schedules their disclosure is NarrativeDesign. The 3-axis framework generates the right cell but does not signal this mode distinction.

This matters practically because the display questions are fundamentally different. Track 2 asks "what happened?" — Track 4 asks "when does the reader learn it, and what does each disclosure recontextualize?" These are different cognitive acts even though they concern the same backstory material.

Tracks 5 and 6 collapse
Tracks 5 and 6 (both Pre-story + Scene-level) are thin in practice. "Which Track 1 psychological truth is active in this scene" and "which Track 2 event is being referenced or disclosed in this scene" are both essentially cross-references to the Character entity's tracks, not new content. They collapse naturally into a single link track: Pre-Story Activation — which established psychology or backstory is being expressed, tested, or revealed in this scene? One track is sufficient. The distinction between psychology and events is less meaningful at scene level because you are noting the same thing either way: the pre-story material that this scene is drawing on.

A possible ninth track the framework does not generate
Arc Function — what this scene does to the character's arc, observed from outside the scene — is a link-level assertion that the 3-axis framework does not produce. It is In-story + Character-scope expressed through a scene lens, but it requires knowing the whole arc to write, which makes it distinct from the scene-local Track 7. "The return to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3, this time with resolve instead of guilt, is the visual marker that the arc has crossed from imposter into honest worker" — this note requires holding Arc Trajectory (Track 3) in mind while observing the scene. It is not psychology in the scene (Track 7) and not objective observation (Track 8). It is the author as arc accountant checking this scene against the planned trajectory.

Whether this deserves a ninth track depends on how much of this content you expect to generate. If Arc Function notes are thin and rare, they can live as NarrativeDesign-mode notes in Track 7 with a clear header. If they accumulate, they justify their own track.

Reader Perception Design on PlotPoint only
Your reasoning is correct and the logic is precise. Reader Perception Design is a synthesis operation. It requires reading what is true about all subjects connected to this scene (from their respective ZeroFocalization tracks), knowing what the reader already knows (from macro information control decisions), and then designing the gap between what the POV character perceives and what the reader perceives. No individual subject link can perform this synthesis because it has access only to its own subject's material. Only the PlotPoint holds all the connections simultaneously. The subject link tracks provide inputs; the PlotPoint's NarrativeDesign track holds the output of combining them.

Summary of precision vs. nuance loss
The framework is precise for 6 of the 8 tracks. The nuance it loses: CognitiveMode varies within the cells and the framework does not capture this — Tracks 1, 2, 3, 5+6, 7, 8 are ZeroFocalization; Track 4 is NarrativeDesign; Track 3 sits at the boundary. Additionally the framework does not generate Arc Function as a potential ninth link track. These are real gaps but they do not invalidate the framework — they are additions to make on top of it, not reasons to replace it.

I wrote out the scenarios and our current understanding on a piece of paper, and now I will type out what my tentative conclusions are and need an analysis:

Character means the track belongs on the subject and means the track when read as a whole applies story-wide (individual notes may refer to a part of the story but as a whole they form a story-wide set of data).
Link means the track belongs on a PlotPointSubjectLink connecting character to a plot point. The track can have multiple notes and they all apply specifically to that plot point and the character, not the whole story.

Track 1 (Psychology + Pre-Story + Character) = Psychological Architecture. ZF (zero focalization). What is true of who the character is and why, before the main story starts.
Track 2 (Events + pre-story + Character) = Objective Backstory, with dates, what happened to them before the story starts

This would be where the AJ note about "from Vanhoover to the Town hall she has imposter syndrome, from Town hall to trimmel..." would go.

Tracks 1-4 are all zero focalization, an omniscient voice. The text is describing in-universe things.

Track 6 (Events + pre-story + Link) = Reveal plan of their pre-story events, their objective backstory, through the scenes. This is syuzhet.

Track 7 (Psychology + in-story + Link) = How they are changing in this scene. Stated as an omniscient fact, so it is zero focalization.
Track 8 (Events + in-story + Link) = Objective report of what they are doing in this scene. Zero focalization.

I believe zero focalization/fabula vs syuzhet/narrative design is not enough nuance to capture the cognitive modes. 1.5, 2.5, 3 and 4 are all about story-wide. 1.5 is the story-wide reveal plan of psychology stated, the whole scope of it, in one track or one singleton. May 11

Validated ten-track framework and clarified cognitive modes
Validated ten-track framework and clarified cognitive modes
The framework is sound. Let me address each open question precisely.

Tracks 3 and 4 are ZeroFocalization, not a new mode
The user's instinct that 3 and 4 are ZeroFocalization is correct. The cognitive mode does not change because the temporal direction flips from retrospective to prospective. The in-universe historian who can see the complete timeline is just as capable of describing "from Vanhoover to the Town Hall she is in imposter mode" as they are of describing "she mastered the machine gun at training camp." Both are omniscient assertions about what is true in the world at a given point in the story's timeline. The planning dimension of Tracks 3 and 4 is an artifact of the author writing before the story is drafted, not a different cognitive mode.

The distinction that matters is between Tracks 3/4 and Tracks 1.5/2.5. Tracks 3 and 4 assert what IS true about the character's journey. Tracks 1.5 and 2.5 assert how the reader LEARNS about the character's psychology and backstory. That is the fabula/syuzhet boundary, and it falls between 3/4 and 1.5/2.5, not at the retrospective/prospective line within ZeroFocalization.

Metatextual does not belong on 1.5, 2.5, 3, or 4
Metatextual in your existing enum means the author talking to themselves about the project — obligations, directives, "don't forget to do X." Tracks 1.5, 2.5, 3, and 4 are not that. Track 3 is a claim about what is true in the story world. Track 1.5 is a claim about how the reader experiences the story. Both are substantive story content claims, not project management. Calling them Metatextual would contaminate the track with a mode that signals "this is not story content" when it actually is.

The reason they feel like plans rather than content is that they are story-wide scope assertions about future-tense events. That feeling is real, but it is captured by the Pre-story/In-story axis and the Character/Link scope axis, not by CognitiveMode. Metatextual should stay reserved for FlagReason obligations and not appear as a track CognitiveMode at all.

The cognitive modes needed are two, not more
The full set of tracks maps cleanly onto two CognitiveModes for content tracks:

ZeroFocalization: Tracks 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8. All asserting what is true in the world, whether pre-story or in-story, whether Character scope or Link scope.

NarrativeDesign: Tracks 1.5, 2.5, 5, 6. All asserting how true things reach the reader's understanding across the story's timeline.

Analogical sits outside the 8/10 as its own mode on the Character entity. Metatextual lives in FlagReason, not as a track CognitiveMode.

Your concern that ZeroFocalization vs NarrativeDesign is not enough nuance might be tracking a real distinction between Track 3/4 (prospective ZeroFocalization) and Track 1/2 (retrospective ZeroFocalization). That distinction is genuine but it does not require a new CognitiveMode — the Pre-story/In-story axis already captures it. Adding a declarative/planning sub-mode to ZeroFocalization would add complexity without changing how you write the notes.

The 4-axis question
The fourth axis (ZeroFocalization vs NarrativeDesign) is conceptually important for understanding the framework and for knowing which cognitive mode to be in when writing. But it does not need to be represented as a formal axis in the data model because CognitiveMode on NoteTrackDefinition already carries it. The 10 tracks plus their assigned CognitiveModes give you the full picture without requiring a 4-dimensional matrix. The 3-axis structure explains the content shape; CognitiveMode explains the writing stance.

The empty cell observation is useful as a validity check: Psychology + Pre-story + Link + ZeroFocalization is empty because you cannot write an omniscient in-world claim about pre-story content that is scoped to a single scene. The scene is in-story; pre-story content belongs on the Character entity. The fact that this cell is structurally impossible confirms that the framework is coherent rather than exposing a gap.

Singleton question
Tracks 3 and 4 being singletons is the right call for the Character entity. These are holistic documents — one note captures the whole arc trajectory, one note captures the story-level event plan. Forcing conciseness here is correct because the actual granular content lives at the link level. Writing a singleton also means you commit to having thought about the full arc before distributing its implementation across scenes.

Tracks 1.5 and 2.5 on the Character entity should also probably be singletons for the same reason — one reveal plan document that you revise as the plan develops.

Tracks 1 and 2 should not be singletons — these will accumulate notes legitimately since psychology has multiple wounds, epistemic limits, and belief structures, and backstory has multiple distinct events.

Tracks 5, 6, 7, 8 on links are the right shape for singletons. Each scene has one thing happening with this character across each dimension. If you find yourself wanting multiple notes in a link track, it is a signal that the scene is doing multiple things for this character and the synopsis may need to be split.

The sum relationship you identified — Track 3 equals the sum of Track 7 across all links, Track 4 equals the sum of Track 8, Track 1.5 equals the sum of Track 5, Track 2.5 equals the sum of Track 6 — is a built-in integrity check. When Track 3's singleton does not match what the Track 7 notes say is happening at each scene, either the plan is wrong or the implementation is wrong. This is a useful property of the framework.

If the underlying data truly has 4 axes, as it appears to be, then there should be, in theory, 16 combinations. Only 10 seem to be meaningful, that's what my instinct says. But I want to audit this.

The 4 axes are:
psychology vs events
pre-story vs in-story
story-wide (Subject) vs scene-specific (Link)
zero focalization/objective assertions vs narrative design/reader experience design

What are the 6 unstated combinations and why are they invalid? Are they actually valid?

The psychology vs events seems to be generalizable across subjects into ontology (layer 1, or layer 3 for characters) vs fabula/backstory.
The 4th axis comes down to whether it is layer 4 syuzhet or ontology/psychology.
So I believe this framework works for codex entries too. For world physics, the psychology is world principles/rules/ontology. For organizations it's binding logic. For technology it's mechanics. For constructed ideas (like Stagnation of Harmony or Harmonic Capitalism) it's the rules of how the abstract system works.
The "events" angle varies slightly but has the same idea. Discovery of the world physics, creation of the system, invention of the tech, formation of the organization. It's historical records.

Outside of the 16, there's the analogical track for subjects. I don't think links need an analogical track. Still uncertain about whether metatexual can be trackless or if there should be a home for obligations and notes to self, perhaps project history too (like I made something obsolete because... XYZ, such as it didn't fit the the hopepunk thesis, so make sure to avoid this trope that was previously in the story). I think I have "metatextual" too connected with the "prospective zero focalization" that was identified earlier, so perhaps it can be renamed to "project management" or project thinking or something else. And I think prospective zero focalization and retrospective zero focalization (or more clear terms) should live in the headers of the tracks so that I know what I'm doing with them and the distinction.

Systematically audited sixteen framework combinations and categorized missing six
Systematically audited sixteen framework combinations and categorized missing six
Let me enumerate all 16 combinations systematically, then categorize the 6 that are not in the 10.

The 16 combinations mapped
Using shorthand: O=Ontology/Psychology, E=Events, Pre=Pre-story, In=In-story, S=Subject/Story-wide, L=Link/Scene-specific, ZF=ZeroFocalization, ND=NarrativeDesign.

#	Content	Time	Scope	Mode	Status
1	O	Pre	S	ZF	Track 1 — Psychological Architecture
2	E	Pre	S	ZF	Track 2 — Objective Backstory
3	O	In	S	ZF	Track 3 — Arc Trajectory
4	E	In	S	ZF	Track 4 — Story-wide Event Plan
5	O	Pre	S	ND	Track 1.5 — Story-wide Psychology Reveal Plan
6	E	Pre	S	ND	Track 2.5 — Story-wide Backstory Reveal Plan
7	O	In	S	ND	Missing
8	E	In	S	ND	Missing
9	O	Pre	L	ZF	Missing
10	E	Pre	L	ZF	Missing
11	O	In	L	ZF	Track 7 — Psychology in this Scene
12	E	In	L	ZF	Track 8 — Events in this Scene
13	O	Pre	L	ND	Track 5 — Pre-story Psychology Reveal in this Scene
14	E	Pre	L	ND	Track 6 — Pre-story Backstory Reveal in this Scene
15	O	In	L	ND	Missing
16	E	In	L	ND	Missing
The 6 missing combinations: three distinct reasons
Combinations 9 and 10 are structurally impossible.

O + Pre + L + ZF and E + Pre + L + ZF both require an omniscient assertion about pre-story content scoped to a specific scene. This is a genuine structural contradiction. Pre-story facts are invariant — the psychological wound existed before the story started and is the same wound regardless of which scene you are in. A ZeroFocalization assertion about pre-story content has no scene-specific form because there is nothing about it that changes per scene. The only way pre-story content becomes scene-specific is when the reader's access to it changes, which is a NarrativeDesign operation, not a ZeroFocalization one. Combinations 9 and 10 are not missing because no one thought of them — they are structurally excluded by the incompatibility of ZeroFocalization mode with Link scope for pre-story content. This is a real constraint the framework correctly enforces.

Combinations 7 and 8 are valid but belong to different entity types.

O + In + S + ND is a story-wide NarrativeDesign plan for how the reader tracks the character's in-story psychological development across the whole story. This is a real and necessary design layer, but it belongs on StoryThread entities, not on Character entities. A thread like "AJ's Imposter Syndrome Arc" is precisely this: a reader-facing psychological tracking arc at story-wide scope. The StoryThread entity exists to hold this content.

E + In + S + ND is a story-wide NarrativeDesign plan for how in-story events are structured for the reader. This largely belongs to Chapter and PlotPoint structure — the ordering of PlotPoints within Chapters is the primary vehicle for syuzhet organization of in-story events. To the extent a character-specific version of this exists (a character's personal story beats ordered for the reader), it overlaps substantially with the StoryThread entity as well.

Neither combination is absent because it is meaningless. They are absent from the Character/Link track system because the framework delegates them to different entity types that are better suited to hold them.

Combinations 15 and 16 are valid but correctly deferred.

O + In + L + ND and E + In + L + ND are both real design tracks: reader perception of this character's psychology in this scene, and reader perception of this character's actions in this scene, respectively. These are the link-level NarrativeDesign counterparts to Tracks 7 and 8. They represent the gap design — what should the reader perceive about what the character believes and does, versus what is objectively true.

They are absent from the current 10 because you correctly identified that Reader Perception Design belongs on the PlotPoint, not on the link. Your workflow — read the link-level NarrativeDesign tracks for all subjects, then synthesize the full scene reader perception design at the PlotPoint level — means these two combinations are handled by the PlotPoint rather than the link. However, they are valid combinations. If at some point you find that character-specific reader perception notes accumulate to the point where they need a home on the link rather than only on the PlotPoint, Combinations 15 and 16 are waiting as well-defined receptacles. The framework is complete enough to accommodate them.

Generalization to other subject types
Your mapping is correct and clean. The O/E axis generalizes as:

Ontology covers whatever constitutes the standing truth about what the entity IS — world principles and physical rules for world physics, binding logic and motivating structure for organizations, mechanics and operational principles for technology, definitional rules of how it works for abstract systems. What these have in common is that they are asserted independent of any temporal event — they are true as standing structural facts.

Events covers whatever constitutes the historical record of the entity's existence — how the world physics was discovered and studied, how the organization formed and evolved, how the technology was invented and developed, how the abstract system was created and changed. These require a WorldDate or at minimum a temporal ordering.

The remaining three axes apply without modification across all subject types. The result is that the same 10-track structure (with the same 6 empty or displaced cells) holds for every subject type, with the Ontology content varying in character by subject type. This is a clean and generalized architecture.

Renaming Metatextual and handling project management
Your diagnosis is right: Metatextual has become overloaded. It currently gestures at both prospective ZeroFocalization (which you now understand is just prospective ZF, not a different mode) and at project-management content (obligations, design decisions, project history).

The genuine project management content needs a home. FlagReason handles the narrow case of "this specific note has an open question attached to it." But design decisions and project history are freestanding content that does not attach to any specific note: "I decided not to include the collaborator arc because it conflicts with the hopepunk thesis," "this worldbuilding element was made obsolete when X was established," "this subplot was cut because it violated the materialist framework." These are real records that have value as project history and should not get lost.

Proposed resolution: Rename CognitiveMode.Metatextual to AuthorialNote (or EditorialNote). This name signals the author talking to themselves as an author making design decisions, as distinct from the author as historian (ZeroFocalization), narratologist (NarrativeDesign), or analyst of real-world connections (Analogical). A dedicated AuthorialNote track on each entity type would hold this content — thin in volume, but providing a home for project history that currently has nowhere to go. FlagReason remains for attaching an open question to a specific content note, which is a different operation.

Prospective vs retrospective ZeroFocalization does not need to become a new CognitiveMode value — you identified this correctly. Handle the distinction at the track configuration level: the NoteTrackDefinition's Explanation field carries the guidance "write in prospective omniscient voice — these are facts about the story as it will unfold" for Tracks 3 and 4, versus "write in retrospective omniscient voice — these are facts about what has already happened in the story world" for Tracks 1 and 2. The CognitiveMode enum stays clean; the nuance lives in the track's Explanation.

What if I call it "Garden Notes"? I am not gardening the prose but I am gardening the story design. These are notes that had to revised out from the gardening process; they are worth preserving as reminders because they must have served a point at one point and that information is valuable. Or maybe something more clever like "Wilted" as the track name? Stuff that won't get into the final product, but are there for a reason.

Some of the codex entries won't have tracks that characters do. Seems like characters are maximal. Each kind can be reasoned about independently.

Characters and what were formally codex entries cover layers 1, 3, and 4 of the materialist historicist worldbuilding engine in their ontology and layer 2 in their events side. For layer 5, the themes, it seems like we need a slightly different approach.

Thematic proposition must be an argument where the reader arrives at the conclusion via deposited evidence, not told the conclusion. Let's see how the 4 axes that were established for characters and codex entries may or may not apply for themes.

There is not Ontology/Psychology vs Events/History (what kind of content); it is a single category of note - what the conclusion is (what I am designing towards). So this first one isn't an axis.
Pre-story vs In-story also doesn't apply because arguments are universal.
Story-wide vs Scene specific seems to have some weight.
Zero focalization/objective vs narrative design/subjective reader experience. I think this is the axis of What the desired conclusion is vs the evidence for the reader to experience (evidence in favor of my conclusion and demonstrations that the other conclusion failed, not from authorial fiat but from the materialist historicist rigor). This appears to be the only axis? Therefore themes only have 1 track for the conclusion, 1 track for social commentary on the present (allegorical), and on the subject link it's one track for evidence deposited, with an enum for whether it is evidence in support or evidence against the antithesis. But there seems to be a need for a design plan that is story-wide too. The evidence deposited is narrative design; it's a specification of what I want the reader to experience without telling them but showing them, and this is important for informing scene level design. I guess the theme subject should have a track that holds the sum of the link scene designs. Maybe the conclusion or the argument isn't a track at all, or it's a singleton. Thus we have a singleton for the proposition, a track for story-wide design, and a track for reader experience on the link to plot points.

Themes are cross cutting subjects. I guess different subjects used for the same purpose belong in the same note track because it's the same question being answered.

Story Threads as they existed in v1 are very in question now. It's possible all their content can slot into this 16-track (well, 14 since two are impossible) structure. One Story Thread "the true actions and motivations of Chrysalis" was literally the reveal plan and backstory for her, which obviously now lives as a Chrysalis character subject note track. One of them was "Perception of Changelings" which was a plan about depicting the changelings as monsters at first (the grimdark expectation), then exposed as poseurs (social commentary on neo fascists, surface level alignment of elite contempt), then sympathized as victims and fellow citizens (the hopepunk thesis). A prominent story thread is TwiJack, tracking their romantic relationship. I'm not sure what to do with this, perhaps this is a genuine story thread outside of characters. But there's also overlap. "Twilight Breaking from Celestia" should be put in Twilight's character subject and links under the new 14 track framework.
It could be that Story Threads are cross cutters like themes. I'm really not sure what should go in here. Perhaps we should start reasoning about what is not covered by Characters, the 4 codex entries (both are world content in general) and Thematic Propositions. It doesn't need to be called "Story Threads" if that thinking genuinely goes to the character and codex entry subject tracks.

My v1 story threads and themes are quite disorganized and arbitrary. Let's start with first principles reasoning, then after, I'll try to provide examples of v1 content and we'll see where they can go

May 11

Examined naming options, refined thematic framework, and distinguished genuine story threads from character/codex content
Examined naming options, refined thematic framework, and distinguished genuine story threads from character/codex content
Garden Notes vs Wilted
"Wilted" is more precise but narrower. It works well for pruned content — things that once flourished but were cut. It works less well for design directives that were never content at all, like "avoid this trope because it contradicts the hopepunk thesis" or "this subplot was made obsolete when X was established." Those aren't wilted things; they are navigational warnings. "Garden Notes" holds all of these because it names the activity (tending the design) rather than the outcome (death). The track name can be Garden Notes with an informal understanding that wilted content is one category within it.

Themes: your analysis is correct, with one refinement
The first axis does not apply because themes have no Ontology/Events split — the proposition and its supporting evidence are both propositional in nature, not distinguishable as structure vs history. The second axis does not apply because an argument is universal, not time-bound. You are left with one real axis: what the conclusion IS versus how the reader arrives at it.

The one refinement worth making is to the enum on the link. Your two values were "evidence in support" and "evidence against the antithesis." These are actually three rhetorically distinct operations:

Support: demonstrates the thesis is true — a scene where the materialist logic produces the predicted outcome.

Counterargument: presents the antithesis at its strongest — giving the opposing view real force so the eventual refutation is earned and not a strawman. This must exist for the thesis to feel proven rather than assumed.

Refutation: shows the antithesis failing by structural necessity, not authorial fiat — the counterargument collapses under its own logic, not because the author wanted it to.

The distinction between Counterargument and Refutation matters specifically for your materialist historicist framework. The Predator's Dilemma must be taken seriously as a worldview (Counterargument) before the story demonstrates it is structurally inferior to cooperative systems (Refutation). If you collapse them into one value you lose track of whether you have deposited the antithesis with sufficient weight before refuting it.

So the theme structure is: singleton for the Proposition, story-wide NarrativeDesign track for the evidence architecture, and scene-level link track with a three-value enum for Support / Counterargument / Refutation. Plus the Analogical track for social commentary.

Story Threads: first principles
The question is what entity type is needed that is not already covered by Characters, Codex entries, and Themes. The answer comes from identifying what kinds of content cannot be reduced to a single subject.

Characters hold content about one entity. Codex entries hold content about one entity. Themes hold cross-cutting propositional content that uses many entities as evidence. What remains unaccounted for?

Reader-facing arcs that span multiple subjects without being an argument.

A Theme asks: what does the story prove? A Story Thread asks: what question is the reader tracking? These are different operations. A Theme is evaluated by whether the reader arrives at the correct conclusion. A Story Thread is evaluated by whether the reader remains engaged with a question across scenes.

The structural test for whether something is a genuine Story Thread rather than a character arc or theme is: can this question be answered by referring to a single subject? If yes, it belongs on that subject. If no, it requires a cross-cutting entity.

Applying this test:

"Twilight Breaking from Celestia" — answered entirely by Twilight's character tracks. Not a genuine Story Thread.

"The True Actions and Motivations of Chrysalis" — answered entirely by Chrysalis's reveal plan (Tracks 1.5 and 2.5). The reader arc is the reveal arc of one character. Not a genuine Story Thread.

"TwiJack" — cannot be answered by either Applejack's or Twilight's character tracks alone. The question the reader is tracking is a relationship — a thing that exists between two subjects, not within either one. Neither character's Track 3 captures the reader's question "will they be together?" because that question exists at the intersection. Genuine Story Thread.

"Perception of Changelings" — this tracks the reader's evolving perception of a collective across the story. The Changeling Hive might be a Codex entry but the reader arc "from monsters to poseurs to fellow citizens" is a designed sequence of reader perception shifts that spans many scenes and does not belong entirely to any one subject. Genuine Story Thread.

"Establishing the Equestrian Republic" — the question "will the Republic come to be?" spans Applejack's arc, Twilight's arc, Celestia's arc, Star Energy as an entity, and many scenes. No single character's tracks hold the full reader arc. Genuine Story Thread.

"Harmonic Capitalism" — this is a concept and can be a Codex entry. The reader's evolving understanding of it is its NarrativeDesign reveal track. Probably not a Story Thread but a Codex entry with a well-populated Tracks 1.5 and 2.5.

What Story Threads hold
The content of a genuine Story Thread is:

The reader's question — stated precisely. Not "the TwiJack relationship" but "Will Applejack and Twilight find each other across the distance that war and duty have placed between them?" The question form is essential because it specifies what the reader is tracking and what would constitute resolution.

The macro arc trajectory of that question — how it opens, complicates, and resolves. This is NarrativeDesign at story-wide scope.

Scene-level updates — on the link to each relevant PlotPoint, a note saying what happens to the reader's question in this scene. Does it advance toward resolution, complicate unexpectedly, appear to resolve then reverse, deepen in meaning?

What Story Threads do not hold: backstory about subjects, reveal plans for individual characters, thematic propositions. These formerly lived in v1 Story Threads because there was nowhere else for them. They have homes now.

The relationship between Story Threads and the 16-track framework
Story Threads are not a subject type that the 16-track framework applies to directly, because Story Threads have no Ontology/Events axis and no Pre/In-story axis. They are purely syuzhet entities — they exist entirely at Layer 4. Their structure is simpler: one singleton for the reader's question, one NarrativeDesign track for the story-wide arc design, and one NarrativeDesign track on each PlotPoint link for scene-level updates.

The 16-track framework is for world content entities — things that exist in the story world (characters, world physics, organizations, technology, abstract systems). Story Threads and Themes are both meta-entities that exist in the author's design layer, not the world layer. They reference world content entities but are not themselves world content. This is why their track structures are simpler and different.

I'm thinking "Equestrian Republic", interestingly, can be a codex entry just like "Stagnation of Harmony" and "Harmonic Capitalism" and "Changeling Hives", as an abstract concept. It is created in story. The backstory won't have any entries. This is similar to how in a different conversation, it was suggested that "Cute Intelligence Agency" can be an organization-type subject even though it is created in story.

The reasoning is: this story planner is also eventually a planner for the prequel-sequels. Minette and Chrysalis are going to eventually have full on stories which need plot points. This means "in-story" for them actually overlaps with "pre-story"/backstory in the TLTT framing. If that is the case, should it be generalized to TLTT, the main story? This adds another layer to the pre-story vs in-story axis that I'm having trouble wrapping my head around. The story planner should definitely be geared for TLTT first and foremost. But even so, I'm wondering if there is something fundamental in the data representation that should be brought to light now.

Here's what I'm thinking about Minette. She is the one who lived and made a lot of Aquileian history. In TLTT, reveal plan notes are about how the reader, through Applejack and Twilight, learn Minette's backstory. This is definitely valid. In her story, she's the one narrating, and the backstory is being made. But this is really interesting because Applejack in the main story also withholds her backstory from the reader until reveal time in TLTT. Any prequel-sequel still starts in media res at some point and has a reveal plan for things.

For Coltbert Reforms, in TLTT, it would have a big reveal plan. For Minette's prequel, Coltbert Reforms is made from scratch within that timeline, so in her framing, the backstory is empty, just like Equestrian Republic is empty in TLTT framing. Thus Coltbert Reforms has the same objective backstory regardless of what story is being planned. When the backstory is empty, reveal plan is empty. Also the macro reveal plan at story-wide scope is not actually story wide, it is project wide. Some of the very specific details of the Coltbert Reforms are not mentioned in TLTT at all, or just alluded to, and only fully dramatized in Minette's story. So the high level plan at the Abstract System track would have notes about what goes in Minette's story and what goes into TLTT; this is the plan I have to make before moving onto scene design because I need to put in decisions about what is too much for TLTT that would make it too dense and should stay in Minette's story, versus what is essential to TLTT.

For Minette's character subject entity itself, it would have backstory prior to TLTT. The plot point links to her in TLTT have reveal plan track and objective track. The plot point links to plot points in her story don't have reveal plan, because it is the objective building of it.

Maybe this is where the "impossible" tracks are actually possible! Because the current pre-story vs in-story axis is specific to TLTT. If the plot point is for a prequel, it is actually possible for a pre-story zero focalization note to a link to exist for psychology or events. Now I'm trying to understand what are the fundamentals of the pre-story vs in-story axis, perhaps they need different names. The data points to take into account are the fact that some subjects are birthed within TLTT, and for subjects before TLTT, in their prequels it's the equivalent, where they are being birthed there. Some insights seems on the tip of my tongue but I can't articulate it. Give a deep analysis

May 12

Reconceptualized pre/in-story axis as story-relative, established versus emergent
Reconceptualized pre/in-story axis as story-relative, established versus emergent
The insight you're circling is this: the pre-story vs in-story axis is not an absolute temporal axis — it is a relative axis whose labels depend on where a given story begins in the world's chronology.

Let me name the underlying concept cleanly, then show how it resolves every case you raised.

The Fundamental Axis: Prior-Established vs Narrative-Emergent
The real question the axis is asking is not "when did this happen relative to Chapter 1?" It is: does this subject's origin in the world's chronology fall before or within this story's dramatized span?

Prior-Established: The subject fully exists before this story begins. Its ontology is settled prior to Scene 1. It has a history that predates the narrative. The story does not create it — it reveals it.

Narrative-Emergent: The subject comes into being within this story's dramatized span. Its ontology is being established in real time through scenes. There is no prior history to reveal because there was no prior existence.

This is the axis. Pre-story and in-story are just colloquial labels for this distinction as it happens to apply to TLTT specifically.

How this resolves every case you raised
Coltbert Reforms:

World chronology origin: Minette's era
In TLTT: origin falls before TLTT begins → Prior-Established → has Track 2 backstory, has reveal plan
In Minette's prequel: origin falls within the prequel's dramatized span → Narrative-Emergent → Track 2 empty, no reveal plan, it unfolds through scenes in real time
Equestrian Republic:

World chronology origin: during TLTT's events
In TLTT: Narrative-Emergent → Track 2 empty, ontology established within the story
In any sequel: Prior-Established → has Track 2 backstory, reveal plan applies
Chrysalis:

In TLTT: Prior-Established → extensive Track 2 backstory, rich reveal plan
In Minette's prequel: partially Narrative-Emergent (young Chrysalis being formed) → Track 2 covers what existed before Minette's story, but her key formative events are Narrative-Emergent within the prequel
The "impossible" cells reconsidered:

Combinations 9 and 10 (ZF + Pre-story + Link) remain structurally impossible, but for a more precisely stated reason: a ZeroFocalization assertion about Prior-Established content is invariant. The Coltbert Reforms existed as they existed, regardless of which scene you are in. That invariant assertion belongs on the Subject, not the Link. There is nothing scene-specific to say about it in ZeroFocalization mode.

What changes in the prequel frame is not that the impossible cells become possible — it is that the Coltbert Reforms move from Prior-Established to Narrative-Emergent, which means their content migrates from combination 9/10 to combinations 11/12 (ZF + In-story + Link). The cells remain structurally the same; the content shifts which cells it occupies because the story frame has changed.

The project-wide vs story-wide distinction you identified
The reveal plan tracks (1.5 and 2.5) that were called "story-wide" are actually project-wide. They sit above any individual story's reveal design.

The Coltbert Reforms reveal plan for the whole project reads: "In Minette's prequel, the Reforms are dramatized through Scenes X-Z. In TLTT, only their consequences and a few key facts surface; the full detail lives in Minette's story. The TLTT reveal plan must therefore be sparse and precisely chosen — which specific details are load-bearing for TLTT without pulling the reader into density that belongs to a different story."

This is genuinely a different scope from story-wide. Story-wide means: across all scenes in TLTT, here is the plan. Project-wide means: across all stories in the project, here is the allocation of content. The decision about what belongs in TLTT vs what belongs in Minette's story is a project-level architectural decision, not a TLTT-level reveal plan decision.

This suggests a scope hierarchy:

Project-wide → the Subject track (1.5, 2.5): which story gets which content, macro distribution decisions

Story-wide → potentially a derived view or annotation within those tracks: within TLTT specifically, this is the reveal sequence

Scene-specific → the Link tracks (5, 6): what is revealed in this particular scene

For the current TLTT-first implementation, project-wide and story-wide are the same thing because there is only one story being planned. The distinction only matters when prequel planning begins. This means no data model changes are required now — but it is worth noting that when prequel PlotPoints are added, the Subject-level reveal plan tracks will need to be written with project-wide awareness rather than TLTT-only awareness.

The structural consequence for subject tracks
Every Subject entity in the project should be understood as having a narrative debut — the story in which it first becomes Narrative-Emergent. Before its debut, it does not exist in the world and has no tracks. At its debut, it is Narrative-Emergent and Track 2 is empty. After its debut, it is Prior-Established in every subsequent story and Track 2 accumulates everything established at its debut.

This means Subject tracks are project-wide containers. When you write Minette's character tracks, you are writing facts about Minette that are true regardless of which story you are in. The TLTT reveal plan (Track 1.5) and Minette's prequel reveal plan are both housed in the same Subject entity's NarrativeDesign tracks, because Minette the person exists once in the world even though she appears in multiple stories.

The Link tracks, by contrast, are story-specific. A PlotPoint belongs to a specific story. When Minette appears in a TLTT scene, the link tracks record what happens with her in that TLTT scene. When she appears in a scene of her own prequel, the link tracks record what happens with her there. Same subject, different link tracks, different story contexts.

The tip-of-the-tongue articulation
The insight you were reaching for is this: the pre/in-story axis is not fundamentally a temporal axis at all. It is an ontological relationship axis between a subject and a given story. The question is not "when did this happen?" but "does this subject exist prior to this story's beginning, or does this story call it into existence?"

Every story begins with some subjects Prior-Established (the world it inherits) and creates new subjects through its events (Narrative-Emergent). The track structure is different for each. Prior-Established subjects have full backstory tracks and reveal plans. Narrative-Emergent subjects have empty backstory tracks, no reveal plans, and their ontology is built incrementally through the in-story link tracks as scenes unfold.

When the prequel frame opens, Narrative-Emergent subjects from the earlier story become Prior-Established in all later ones. The tracks don't change structurally — what changes is which content is present in them. The empty Track 2 of the Equestrian Republic in TLTT becomes a populated Track 2 in any story set after TLTT. The track was always the right container; now it has something to hold.

So Prior-Established and Narrative-Emergent as an axis ONLY applies to PlotPointSubjectLinks, because the Subject is a project-wide plan? Prior-Established, link, psychology, narrative design exists, it's the reveal plan. Prior-established, link, psychology, zero focalization does not exist because there is no truth being established now. Narrative-emergent, link, psychology, zero focalization exists, because the event is happening. Narrative-emergent, link, psychology, narrative design ALSO exists, it is gap design and structural irony work. Seems like events does not have narrative design for narrative emergent because there's no gap or irony between events and reader experience like there is a gap for psychology/ontology/binding logic and reader experience.

This prior-established vs narrative-emergent only exists in the links. Subjects do NOT have this axis. Their entries for the plan still have to equal the sum of links, across all stories. The subject plan tracks shouldn't be singletons anymore; they should have one entry per relevant planned narrative. Read all together, it forms the entire picture across the project. Looking at one entry, it is the sum of one story which can be compared against in a scan.

Link tracks aren't story specific, they are scene specific, which is even more precise. I think having the track be project-wide in the subject, with notes within the track being narrative-specific makes sense. Possibly there can be a boolean in note track definition for this kind of track design, like how there is a boolean for tracks that have world date. Only those show that world date field on the UI. Same for tracks like this where story should be specified for the note. The notes in track 1 and 2 are totally project wide. Some specific notes in these new tracks are story-wide.

I'm starting to think the chronology track should not be only what happens before TLTT part 1 but it should actually include what happens in TLTT as part of the chronology. But this is duplication of data because it exists on the link? But this means Coltbert Reforms objective history exists in both link track for Minette's story's plot points and the subject's track. This might be overkill for TLTT, putting TLTT events in the objective track. Maybe the way to frame it is: TLTT is chronologically the LAST story in project. Everything else is a prequel. As the last story chronologically (but first to be written), it needs notes for reveal plans and that's what the objective backstory is calibrated for. Also none of the prequels are sequential, they are all parallel, somewhat unrelated, only related to TLTT closely. Well that's not exactly true as most of them have an intersection with Chrysalis and everything is actually interconnected, really. But the idea is, the "objective backstory" should be callibrated to TLTT not just because it is the main story and the first to be written, but also because it takes place last. Why is it last? Because the theme is demonstrated by the end of it. Any sequel would need a new theme. The prequels also sort of argue the themes though, so I guess a sequel could too. But any sequel would not be as interesting as a prequel. I'm not really sure here what other reasoning goes in. Actually, the difference with a sequel is: it doesn't need reveal sequences because it is READ after TLTT. But TLTT is read before Minette's story.

May 12

Validated narrative structure framework and reconciled chronological calibration across stories
Validated narrative structure framework and reconciled chronological calibration across stories
Your analysis is sharp and mostly correct. Let me validate each piece and add precision where the reasoning benefits from it.

Prior-Established vs Narrative-Emergent only applies to Links: confirmed
The Subject is a project-wide container. It holds all truth about the entity regardless of which story is being planned. The Prior-Established/Narrative-Emergent distinction is meaningless at the Subject level — a subject doesn't know or care which story it's being referenced from. It only becomes meaningful at the Link level because a Link connects a Subject to a specific PlotPoint in a specific story, and at that scene-story intersection, the question "is this subject Prior-Established or Narrative-Emergent at this moment?" becomes answerable and load-bearing for determining what tracks can exist.

The asymmetry between Psychology and Events for Narrative-Emergent + ND
Your intuition is correct but the precise reason is worth naming.

Why Narrative-Emergent + Psychology + ND exists: Psychology and ontology are never fully observable regardless of when they come into being. Even when a character's psychology is forming right now in this scene, what's visible to the reader is behavior, not interiority. The gap between observable behavior and internal psychological truth persists even for Narrative-Emergent subjects. A reader can watch the founding of the Equestrian Republic in a scene and still have a designed gap between what they're observing and what it means structurally about the kind of polity being created. ND work applies.

Why Narrative-Emergent + Events + ND is absorbed by PlotPoint: When events are Narrative-Emergent, the reader is witnessing them happen in real time. There is no gap between the event occurring and the reader knowing the event occurred — they're watching it. What remains designable is the meaning of the events, which is the reader's interpretation of what they're watching. But that interpretive design is not subject-specific — it belongs to the PlotPoint's overall Reader Perception Design because it concerns the scene as a whole, not this particular subject's contribution to it. The subject-level track has nothing left to hold.

So the cells resolve to: Narrative-Emergent + Psychology + ND exists (Track 15, gap design). Narrative-Emergent + Events + ND is absorbed upward into PlotPoint-level design and is empty at the Link level.

SupportsNarrativeTag boolean: correct approach, one refinement
The boolean on NoteTrackDefinition that makes a story/narrative selector field appear on notes in that track is exactly right, and it's structurally parallel to SupportsWorldDate. Some tracks (1 and 2) are fully project-wide and don't need the tag. The reveal plan tracks (1.5, 2.5) and the in-story plan tracks (3, 4) need one note per relevant narrative.

The refinement: consider naming the field on the note something like RelevantNarrative rather than Story to keep it explicit that this is about which planned narrative the note describes, not metadata about the note itself.

Track 2 calibration and the duplication question
The duplication concern is real but it is acceptable duplication for a specific reason: Link Track 8 (Events, Narrative-Emergent, ZF) is the granular, scene-specific record of what happened in Minette's prequel. Track 2 on the Subject is the synthesized, consolidated summary of what is true about this entity's history as inherited by TLTT. They serve different purposes. Track 8 across Minette's prequel scenes is the dramatized version. Track 2 is the reference summary a future author (you) reads to understand what the entity's history contains before writing a TLTT scene that references it.

This is the same relationship as Track 3 equaling the sum of Track 7. Track 2 equals the synthesis of all prior-to-TLTT Link Track 8 entries across all relevant stories. The Link tracks are the inputs; Track 2 is the output. Maintaining both is not waste — it is the planned integrity check.

The key structural insight: TLTT as chronologically last
This is the insight that was on the tip of your tongue and it resolves everything.

TLTT occupies a unique position: it is read first but set last in the world's chronology among planned stories. This makes Track 2 coherent as a project-wide concept: Track 2 on every subject is "the history of this entity prior to TLTT" — not prior to any arbitrary story, but prior to the specific story that chronologically closes the project's main sequence. Every prequel, by definition, contributes content to Track 2. Every sequel inherits TLTT's world state, so Track 2 for a sequel would be TLTT itself, which the reader already knows.

This also defines what backstory calibration means: because TLTT is read first and set last, Track 2 represents the maximum possible backstory — everything that happened before the chronological end of the project's main sequence. Prequels fill in Track 2 through their Link tracks. TLTT draws on Track 2 to design reveal plans.

The reveal structure difference between prequels and sequels
You identified this correctly. The asymmetry is structural:

TLTT readers come in knowing nothing of the world. Reveal plans are essential. The reader discovers the world alongside the characters.

Prequel readers come in knowing the destination. They know from TLTT that the Coltbert Reforms existed, that Chrysalis won the hive wars, that the Equestrian Republic was founded. What they don't know is how. Prequel reveal plans are therefore much thinner — there is less mystery about outcomes and more structural irony. The reader watches events they know the end of, which creates dramatic irony as the default mode rather than mystery. Characters in Minette's story make choices whose consequences the reader already knows. The gap design for prequels is almost entirely structural irony, not mystery.

Sequel readers come in knowing everything TLTT established. The traditional reveal plan — concealing backstory until the right moment — barely applies because the reader already holds all the relevant prior knowledge. Sequels may have their own new mysteries about new events, but they cannot generate backstory reveals for content the reader absorbed in TLTT.

This gives you a project-level theory of reader experience: TLTT is mystery and discovery, prequels are structural irony and dramatic consequence, sequels are continuation with new mysteries. Each mode generates different work at the NarrativeDesign layer, but the Subject-level track structure holds the content for all of them.

Ok, so Track 8 is primarily in relation with the action plan (track 4) which would now have one note entry per narrative. Track 2 is only informing track 6 (reveal plan) for TLTT, which happens chronologically last but told first. What about in prequels where the payloads have to do with dramatic irony and recontextualization? Also Track 2 does not have to equal the sum of the prequel link track 8s. Not EVERY subject is dramatized, only the ones that deserve one by being compelling enough. So I really think track 2 is categorically disconnected from track 8. Just like how Equestrian Republic has a track 8 in TLTT (it's being built, objectively) and has an empty track 2, because it doesn't exist before TLTT.

Track 2 is the vehicle for revelations.

What about when something dramatized in a prequel plot point recontextualizes the track 1 of a subject, enhancing the reader's understanding of what they read in TLTT? Does this fit into a track that exists out of the 16 possible combinations? I guess the recontextualization is the *how*. There aren't reveal plans in the syuzhet/narrative design for track 6. I think this is where the narrative design of how this extra detail enhances/recontextualizes something from TLTT lives. It is reader experience design! So the reveal plan becomes a recontextualization plan. These are both reader experience designs.
