May 12

Delineated mutual transformation principle distinguishing qualifying bonds from one-directional influence
Delineated mutual transformation principle distinguishing qualifying bonds from one-directional influence
The defining criterion is not romance. It is mutual transformation through a bond that carries its own thematic arc independent of either party's individual arc.

Three conditions all need to hold:

Both parties are substantively changed by the connection — not one character being acted upon by another, but both entering the relationship as one version of themselves and exiting as a different version specifically because this bond existed.

The bond itself has a trajectory that cannot be captured on either character's individual subject tracks. If everything meaningful about the connection can be fully described through Applejack's psychology and Twilight's psychology separately, there is no independent arc. If removing either character's individual arc still leaves something that belongs to the connection itself — the binding logic of why these two, what each provides the other that no one else can — then a Bond subject is warranted.

The bond carries thematic load. It is not decorative but demonstrative. It shows rather than tells something the story's central propositions require to be shown. TwiJack demonstrates what authentic Pink Love looks like under the pressures of war and duty — this cannot be demonstrated by either character alone.

Working through your examples
TwiJack — yes, cleanly. Mutual transformation, independent arc, tightly coupled to the charitostatic argument. The bond is the proof that genuine conscience-based connection generates its own fuel in a way that extracted love cannot.

Minette and Réni — yes, by parallel structure. The prequel's thematic argument presumably works through their bond in the same structural way TwiJack works through TLTT's argument.

Henri and Fleur — yes, and this is the clearest evidence that romance is not the criterion. Healthy friends-with-benefits is still a mutually transformative bond with its own arc. Henri's martial vitality and Fleur's intellectual elegance produce something through their dynamic that neither sustains alone. If their bond has a trajectory in the story — if the relationship itself changes, not just each of them individually — it qualifies. The lack of romantic commitment is irrelevant to the structural question.

Applejack's Parents — yes, conditionally. The farm feud being in conflict with their love is precisely the structure: their bond produced a tension whose resolution (choosing industry over the farm) shaped the conditions that made Applejack who she is. The bond itself has a history and a thematic arc. Whether it warrants a full Bond subject depends on how much of the story's argument passes through their relationship specifically versus through each of them individually as characters. If their story is told mainly through AJ's psychology and backstory tracks, no Bond subject is needed. If their love-vs-ambition tension carries independent thematic weight — if it is itself a demonstration of something the project's propositions require — then yes.

Trimmel and Chrysalis — no. Your test is correct and the reason is precise: the transformation is one-directional. Chrysalis shaped Trimmel; Trimmel did not shape Chrysalis. What is meaningful here lives entirely on Trimmel's Track 1 (his psychology was formed partly by his relationship to Chrysalis as a figure) and on Chrysalis's Track 8 link notes (what she did with Trimmel in particular scenes). There is no independent arc belonging to the connection.

Fleur and Coltbert — uncertain, and the right question to ask is your own test: was Coltbert changed by Fleur? If Fleur's application of his framework — her specific synthesis of his economics with earth pony magic and Aquileian vitalism — produced something that recontextualized his own understanding of what he had built, then there is a bilateral transformation and the bond qualifies. If the relationship was pedagogical in one direction only — Coltbert transmitted, Fleur received — then it belongs on Fleur's Track 2 (he is a backstory event in her formation) and possibly on a Coltbert Reforms or Coltbert character subject. The mentor-student relationship only becomes a Bond subject when the student's reception transforms the mentor.

POV character involvement is correlational
The reason your bond subjects tend to involve POV characters is that POV characters are the ones whose arcs must carry the thematic proof. A bond that is thematically load-bearing for the story's central argument will almost always involve a POV character because the argument is proved through what the POV character experiences and becomes. But the principle is the mutual transformation and independent thematic arc, not POV status as such. Applejack's Parents might qualify even without being POV characters if their bond is directly load-bearing for the thesis rather than merely explanatory of Applejack's psychology.

The subject type
Call it Bond or Relationship — either works as a SubjectType string. The tracks would include an ontology equivalent (what the bond IS, its binding logic, what each party provides the other), Prior-Established and Narrative-Emergent event and psychology tracks parallel to Character but scoped to the connection rather than either party, and the full NarrativeDesign apparatus. It is structurally similar to Organization at the scale of two people — which you identified correctly. The Generic Subject Type will capture anything that almost-but-not-quite fits. If you find a third or fourth instance of the mentor-student pattern where both parties are transformed, that pattern has enough instances to warrant its own SubjectType with focused questions distinct from Bond.

What are possible ways to visualize 4 binary axes in 2d? Punnett squares? Game theory? Something from dld?

This is reasoning about and classifying the 16 possible track types from the 4 binary axes

May 12

Evaluated K-maps, Punnett squares, and hypercube projections for visualization
Evaluated K-maps, Punnett squares, and hypercube projections for visualization
Four binary axes in 2D has several well-established solutions with different tradeoffs.

Karnaugh Map — the DLD answer

A K-map for 4 variables is a 4×4 grid where rows represent two variables in Gray code order (00, 01, 11, 10) and columns represent the other two in Gray code order. The defining property is that adjacent cells — horizontally, vertically, and wrapping around — differ by exactly one variable. This makes structural relationships visible: cells that are logically similar cluster together. For your framework, the two impossible cells (PE+L+ZF for both O and E) would appear as adjacent cells in the same region, making the structural reason for their impossibility visible — they occupy the same "zone" of the map. The K-map was designed for Boolean minimization, so it emphasizes logical adjacency over semantic grouping.

Nested Punnett Square

A 2×2 outer grid where each cell contains a 2×2 inner grid, giving 16 cells total. You choose which two axes form the outer grid and which two form the inner. The outer grid axes should be the most semantically meaningful split — for your framework that is clearly Subject vs Link (outer column) and Ontology vs Events (outer row), with ZF vs ND and PE vs NE as the inner axes. This layout keeps all Subject tracks on the left half and all Link tracks on the right half, matching how you actually think about and use the tracks. The impossible cells appear in a natural position within the Link+ZF column, immediately visible as a pattern.

Boolean Hypercube Projection

A 4D hypercube (tesseract) has exactly 16 vertices, one per combination. It projects to 2D as an inner cube connected to an outer cube. Edges connect vertices differing by one bit. This is mathematically elegant and shows which combinations are "one step away" from each other, but it is hard to read for non-specialists and adds visual complexity without practical benefit for your purpose.

Truth table

The degenerate case — 16 rows, 4 binary columns, one row per combination. Fully explicit, no ambiguity, but shows no structure or relationships between combinations. Useful as a reference but not as a reasoning tool.

For your specific purpose, the nested Punnett square wins.

The K-map's Gray code ordering is optimized for Boolean minimization, which is not what you need. What you need is a visualization that shows: which combinations exist, which are impossible and why, which are displaced or absorbed, and how the Subject and Link halves relate. The nested Punnett square with color coding — green for exists, yellow for deferred, red for impossible, gray for absorbed/displaced — communicates all of this at a glance and keeps the Subject/Link split as the primary visual division.

Want me to generate that visual now?

So it seems like the K map is for abstract Boolean expression simplification, not for preserving semantic meaning? Thus the nested punnett square is better? 

The left and right outer split definitely is subject vs link, but I'm not sure whether the second outer axis should be ontology vs events or if it should be fabula/zero focalization vs syuzhet/narrative design. What are the implications of either choice? It seems like the outer axes make it harder to do direct comparison from adjacency but good for drawing parallels across the two worlds. It's hard to wrap my head around the pros and cons of each approach clearly 

It seems like whether the split happens on the outer vs the inner has different implications for data interpretation of the differences of that axis. Like whether it's more important to see difference within that axis (put axis in inner square) or if it's better for the axis to be fixed because the other axis variation is more meaningful side by side (put axis in outer square)

Show more
May 12

Weighed outer versus inner axis placement implications for visualization design
Weighed outer versus inner axis placement implications for visualization design
Your intuition about inner vs outer placement is exactly right. Let me make it precise.

The principle:

Outer axis = you are fixing a context and comparing what happens inside each context. The quadrant is a world defined by that axis value. You compare worlds against each other.

Inner axis = you are showing variation of that axis within a fixed context. You're asking "how does this axis behave when everything else is held constant?"

So the question becomes: which axis is more useful as a fixed context, and which is more useful as a shown variation?

The implication of each choice for your second outer axis:

If Ontology/Events goes outer, the four quadrants become:

Subject + Ontology
Subject + Events
Link + Ontology
Link + Events
Each quadrant is "what happens when we're working with this content type at this scope." You compare ontology work against events work across the left-right split. The ZF/ND variation lives inside each quadrant, so you see "how does the cognitive mode vary within psychology content at link scope" etc.

If ZF/ND goes outer, the four quadrants become:

Subject + ZeroFocalization
Subject + NarrativeDesign
Link + ZeroFocalization
Link + NarrativeDesign
Each quadrant is "what happens when we're working in this cognitive mode at this scope." The Ontology/Events variation lives inside each quadrant. This means the entire ZeroFocalization world is the left half and the entire NarrativeDesign world is the right half.

The ZF/ND outer choice has a decisive advantage for your framework:

The Link + ZeroFocalization quadrant would contain exactly: Impossible (O+PE), Impossible (E+PE), T7 (O+NE), T8 (E+NE). The entire Prior-Established row is impossible. The structural reason is immediately readable as a quadrant-level pattern: ZeroFocalization at Link scope can only handle Narrative-Emergent content. Prior-Established content in ZF mode is invariant and therefore belongs on the Subject, not the Link. This constraint is invisible if ZF/ND goes inner — the two impossible cells appear scattered across two different quadrants rather than concentrated in one.

The Subject + ZeroFocalization quadrant would be completely clean — all four cells exist. This reinforces the structural insight: Subject-level ZeroFocalization is fully expressive because a subject can make omniscient assertions about anything, PE or NE. Link-level ZeroFocalization is constrained to NE only.

Recommendation:

Outer axes: Subject/Link (columns) and ZF/ND (rows).
Inner axes: Ontology/Events (columns within quadrant) and PE/NE (rows within quadrant).

This gives four quadrants whose character you can summarize in one phrase each:

Subject + ZF: the world bible — all four cells exist cleanly
Subject + ND: the project-wide reveal architecture — NE cells are displaced/absorbed because in-story design belongs at PlotPoint level
Link + ZF: the objective scene record — PE cells impossible, only NE exists
Link + ND: the reader encounter design — the richest and most nuanced quadrant
The framework's deepest insight — that ZeroFocalization and NarrativeDesign are fundamentally different cognitive modes that require different structural homes — becomes the primary visual axis of the diagram. Want me to generate this?

Let's not assume subject and link are outer. I'm curious about all possible setups. Actually I'd wager they are all helpful in different context, maybe.

How many ways are there to arrange the 4 axes in a nested punnett square (this seems to be both a combination and permutation question?) and are all the configurations helpful in some way or are some just inferior?

May 12

Enumerated 24 arrangements, evaluated each configuration's pedagogical utility
Enumerated 24 arrangements, evaluated each configuration's pedagogical utility
The combinatorics answer
There are 4! = 24 total arrangements if you treat all four positional slots (outer row, outer column, inner row, inner column) as distinct. There are C(4,2) = 6 essentially distinct arrangements if you consider row/column swaps within a pair as equivalent (just transpositions of the same diagram). The 6 corresponds to the number of ways to choose which 2 axes pair together as the outer context versus which 2 pair together as the inner variation.

The 24 comes from permuting 4 items into 4 positions. The 6 comes from the fact that swapping outer-row ↔ outer-column (rotating the whole diagram 90°) and swapping inner-row ↔ inner-column (rotating all inner grids simultaneously) each produce equivalent information, reducing 24 by a factor of 4.

The 6 essentially distinct configurations
The four axes abbreviated: S/L (Subject/Link), ZF/ND (ZeroFocalization/NarrativeDesign), O/E (Ontology/Events), PE/NE (Prior-Established/Narrative-Emergent).

Config 1 — Outer: S/L + ZF/ND, Inner: O/E + PE/NE

The four quadrants are Subject+ZF, Subject+ND, Link+ZF, Link+ND. The impossible cells both land in Link+ZF, making that quadrant visually distinctive — the entire PE row within it is empty. Subject+ZF is the cleanest quadrant: all four inner cells exist. Best for: showing the structural constraint and the difference between what ZeroFocalization can express at Subject scope versus Link scope.

Config 2 — Outer: S/L + O/E, Inner: ZF/ND + PE/NE

The four quadrants are Subject+Ontology, Subject+Events, Link+Ontology, Link+Events. The parallel structure between the ontology tracks and the events tracks becomes visible — the top half and bottom half mirror each other in interesting ways. The impossible cells appear in the same inner cell position (PE+ZF) in both Link quadrants. Best for: showing the symmetry and asymmetry between psychology/ontology content and events/history content across all scope combinations.

Config 3 — Outer: S/L + PE/NE, Inner: O/E + ZF/ND

The four quadrants are Subject+PE, Subject+NE, Link+PE, Link+NE. The Link+PE quadrant is partially populated: the ZF cells are impossible but the ND cells exist (Track 5 and 6). This makes the asymmetry vivid — Prior-Established content at Link scope can only exist in NarrativeDesign mode, never in ZeroFocalization mode. Best for: showing what is and is not available for prior-established versus narrative-emergent content at each scope level.

Config 4 — Outer: ZF/ND + O/E, Inner: S/L + PE/NE

The four quadrants are ZF+Ontology, ZF+Events, ND+Ontology, ND+Events. Each quadrant represents a fundamental thinking mode: "omniscient psychology work," "omniscient history work," "reader-experience psychology design," "reader-experience history design." The inner grid then shows how scope and temporal position vary within each mode. Unusual framing — this is the author's cognitive mode as the primary axis, with everything else subordinate. Best for: understanding which thinking mode you are in and what options that mode affords.

Config 5 — Outer: ZF/ND + PE/NE, Inner: S/L + O/E

The four quadrants are ZF+PE, ZF+NE, ND+PE, ND+NE. This is the most structurally revealing configuration. The ZF+PE quadrant contains: Subject+Ontology=T1, Subject+Events=T2, Link+Ontology=IMPOSSIBLE, Link+Events=IMPOSSIBLE. The rule becomes a visual axiom: ZeroFocalization combined with Prior-Established content never works at Link scope. The entire Link column of that quadrant is empty. No other configuration makes this constraint as immediately obvious. Best for: demonstrating why the impossible cells are impossible, structural exposition of the framework's internal logic.

Config 6 — Outer: O/E + PE/NE, Inner: S/L + ZF/ND

The four quadrants are O+PE, O+NE, E+PE, E+NE. This suspends the scope and mode axes and makes content type combined with temporal position the primary framing. Each quadrant asks: "for this specific kind of content (psychology of prior-established things, psychology of emerging things, history of prior-established things, history of emerging things) — what do scope and mode options look like?" Best for: world-bible thinking, reasoning about content from the perspective of the subject matter itself rather than where it lives or what mode you're in.

Are any configurations inferior?
None are strictly inferior — they each make different things most visible. However they have different audiences:

For explaining the framework to someone new: Config 1 or Config 5, because the structural constraint (impossible cells) is most immediately visible and the explanation feels logical rather than arbitrary.

For a story designer asking "what tracks do I write for this subject?": Config 2 or Config 6, because the content type (ontology vs events) is the first decision you make when writing notes, and these configurations organize around that first.

For a theorist asking "why is the framework shaped this way?": Config 5, because it exposes the structural rule most clearly.

For a writer in the middle of a session asking "what mode am I in and what are my options?": Config 4, because cognitive mode is the primary framing.

The practical implication is that you might want more than one. A reference card showing Config 5 explains the structure. A working reference during note-writing might use Config 2 or 6. A presentation to a collaborator might use Config 1. The same 16 cells, different lenses.

Now the million dollar question about this framework is whether this setup of 4 axes each with two values is "suitably exhaustive" or if there are more possible tracks to consider within the combinatorics. Allegorical sits completely separate and so does Garden Notes so things that can be separate are not what I'm looking for. I'm wondering if any of the current axes have more than two meaningful values or not, and whether there are any other meaningful axes that could warrant thought.

May 12

Interrogated four axes for exhaustiveness; identified scope as potentially ternary
Interrogated four axes for exhaustiveness; identified scope as potentially ternary
This is the right question to stress-test the framework. Let me examine each axis for hidden additional values, then ask whether any axis is missing entirely.

Axis 1: Ontology vs Events — appears genuinely binary
The boundary between "what the entity is" and "what happened to the entity" has fuzzy edges in practice — a character's relationship to another character can be framed as either psychology (who she is includes how she relates to others) or history (the relationship developed through specific events). But fuzziness at the boundary does not mean a third category exists. The two values are exhaustive for the kinds of claims you make about story content.

The one candidate for a third value is causal mechanism — the explanatory arrow connecting an event to its psychological effect. "AJ's imposter syndrome developed because the changeling military proved asymmetrically stronger than her training." This is neither pure ontology (it's not just stating who she is) nor pure events (it's not just stating what happened). But in practice, causal mechanism notes are always written as a variant of either ontology notes ("who she is and why") or events notes ("what happened and what it produced"). They don't require their own separate track — they're a mode of writing within existing tracks.

Verdict: binary, no missing third value.

Axis 2: Prior-Established vs Narrative-Emergent — appears genuinely binary
The tempting third value would be something like In-Transition — an entity that existed before the story but is substantially transformed within it. Chrysalis is PE but her military capabilities, her infiltration network, her relationship with Ditrysium develop further during the war. Does she need a third temporal status?

No — because the entity can simultaneously have populated PE tracks (what she was before TLTT, what her history contains) and populated NE tracks (her arc trajectory, her actions in TLTT scenes). An entity having content in both halves is not a third value on the axis; it's just an entity that straddles the division. The binary is about the origin point of the content, not the entity as a whole.

Verdict: binary, no missing third value.

Axis 3: Subject vs Link — potentially ternary
This is the axis most likely to be hiding a third value. The current binary captures the two extremes of scope: project-wide (Subject) and scene-specific (Link). But there is a real intermediate scope that the framework does not currently address cleanly:

Chapter scope. Some content is broader than one scene but narrower than the whole story. A character's emotional throughline within a chapter. The chapter-level arc of a theme. How the reader's understanding of a subject shifts across a chapter as a unit. These are genuinely intermediate between "true across the whole project" and "true in this specific scene."

In the current model, chapter-level subject content has no natural home. It either gets pushed up into Subject tracks (too broad — it conflates a chapter-specific observation with a project-wide truth) or pushed down into Link tracks (too narrow — you'd need to repeat the same observation in every scene of the chapter). The Chapter entity has its own notes, but those notes describe the chapter itself, not a specific subject's relationship to the chapter.

A Chapter-Subject link with its own track set would complete the scope axis as a ternary:

Subject / Project-wide
Chapter / Chapter-wide
Link / Scene-specific
This would expand the combinatorial space from 16 to 24 (3 × 2 × 2 × 2), and some of the same structural constraints would apply. Chapter + ZF + PE would be impossible for the same reason Link + ZF + PE is impossible: omniscient assertions about prior-established content don't change per chapter any more than they change per scene.

Whether this is worth implementing depends on whether chapter-scope subject content actually accumulates in your notes. For TLTT-focused work at current development phase it may not be necessary. But it's the most structurally honest gap in the framework.

Verdict: potentially ternary. Chapter scope is a real candidate.

Axis 4: ZeroFocalization vs NarrativeDesign — appears genuinely binary
The temptation here is to split ZeroFocalization into retrospective ZF (recording established facts) and prospective ZF (planning what will be true). Tracks 1 and 2 are retrospective; Tracks 3 and 4 are prospective. We addressed this earlier: the distinction is real but it's already captured by the PE/NE axis (PE content is retrospective because it predates the story; NE content is prospective because it's being designed). The cognitive mode itself — asserting omniscient world facts — is the same operation regardless of temporal direction. The axis stays binary.

The other candidate is a Structural Analysis mode — notes where the author is analyzing why something works, articulating design rationale, thinking as a critic of their own work. "The return to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3 functions as a visual marker that the arc has crossed from imposter to honest worker." This is not ZF (not asserting a world fact) and at first glance seems distinct from ND (not designing reader experience in the sense of specifying what the reader should perceive). But look more closely: the structural analysis note IS a reader experience design note. It says "the reader will perceive growth here because of this specific callback." Articulating why a design choice works is the same cognitive mode as making the design choice. ND covers it.

Verdict: binary, no missing third value.

Missing axes entirely
Confidence/certainty — is this content settled or speculative? Already captured by NoteState (Unset/Flagged/Confirmed). Not a structural axis.

Granularity — is this a high-level summary or a detailed account? Not a structural concern, more a note quality issue.

Direction of causality — does this note explain why (cause → effect) or what (description)? Collapses into the Ontology/Events axis in practice.

POV / epistemic access — who knows this? What a character knows vs what is objectively true is already the ZF/ND divide plus the gap design in Tracks 5, 6, and 15.

The one axis worth examining seriously for potential missing value is narrative function — whether a note specifies what something IS, or what it DOES (its function in the design). For codex entries especially, "what the charitostatic effect is" versus "what role the charitostatic effect plays in proving the thematic argument" feel different. But this again resolves into existing tracks: the former is Track 1, the latter is handled by the Theme entity's evidence tracks and the PlotPoint-Subject link tracks. No new axis needed.

Verdict on exhaustiveness
The framework is suitably exhaustive with one caveat. The four binary axes cover all the meaningful track types for scene-specific and project-wide content. The structural constraints (two impossible cells, displaced and absorbed content) all follow from the logic of the axes rather than being arbitrary exceptions, which is a sign the axes are doing real work.

The one genuine gap is Chapter scope as a potential third value on the Scope axis. If chapter-level subject arcs accumulate in your notes in volume — if you find yourself consistently wanting to make chapter-scope assertions about subjects that are too broad for scene-level Links and too specific for project-wide Subject tracks — that is the signal to expand Axis 3 to ternary. The Generic Subject Type and FlagReason can absorb this content until the pattern is clear enough to formalize.

Story Planner Fundamentals, Made Explicit
Drive

How do the potential 16 track types from the 4 axes compare to this original document outlining the different modes and functions of the story planner? Are they incompatible or is there a synthesis that is possible and there is overlap, that they are different angles of the same thing?

May 12

Synthesized spreadsheet framework with 4-axis model, identifying complementary angles
Synthesized spreadsheet framework with 4-axis model, identifying complementary angles
The document has three tables. Let me lay them out, then map them against the 4-axis framework.

What the document contains
Six story planner uses: World bible, Narrative architecture document, Scene design tool, Scene blueprint tool, Project management tool, Continuity Tracker.

Ten work blocks (ordered workflow phases): Raw Capture → Classification → World Mechanics Construction → Causal History Construction → Character Psychology Construction → Thematic Argumentation Design → Macro Narrative Architecture → Scene-Level Experience Design → Scene Blueprint → Prose Drafting.

Eight assertion types: Canonical claim, Architectural decision, Thematic proposition, Design specification, Obligation, Generative hypothesis, Bibliographic note, Craft observation.

The mapping
The assertion types are the most direct comparison point. Every one of them maps onto the 4-axis framework, but the mapping reveals both overlap and genuine gaps.

Canonical claim (asserts world truth) → ZeroFocalization mode. Depending on content type and temporal position, this lands in Track 1, 2, 3, or 4. The spreadsheet treats this as one category; the 4-axis framework splits it into four distinct tracks because PE psychology and NE events require different writing stances even though both assert world truth.

Architectural decision (asserts information structure across the whole story) → NarrativeDesign mode at Subject/project scope. This is Tracks 1.5 and 2.5 — the story-wide reveal and recontextualization plans. The spreadsheet correctly identifies this as distinct from canonical claims, and the 4-axis framework correctly identifies it as the Subject-scope NarrativeDesign cells.

Thematic proposition (what the story argues + evidence plan) → Theme entity tracks. The proposition is a singleton; the evidence plan is the NarrativeDesign track on the Theme entity. The spreadsheet bundles both into one assertion type; the 4-axis framework separates them because one is ZeroFocalization (what the argument IS) and the other is NarrativeDesign (how evidence reaches the reader).

Design specification (how a specific scene produces reader experience) → NarrativeDesign mode at Link/scene scope. This is Tracks 5, 6, 15, and the PlotPoint-level Reader Perception Design. The spreadsheet correctly identifies this as distinct from architectural decisions; the 4-axis framework distinguishes it by scope (Link vs Subject).

Obligation (work that needs to be done) → FlagReason + NoteState.Flagged in v2. Not a track — a metadata field on specific notes. Garden Notes handles the broader project history.

Generative hypothesis (world truth not yet decided) → NoteState.Unset in v2. The 4-axis framework does not treat this as a separate assertion type — it's a canonical claim in draft state. The spreadsheet implicitly names something the 4-axis framework handles through note state metadata rather than through a separate structural category.

Bibliographic note (real-world parallel or inspiration) → The Analogical track, explicitly outside the 16-combination framework. Both frameworks agree this is structurally separate from world content.

Craft observation (how prose should function, not an experience design) → This is the only assertion type that does not have a clean home in the 4-axis framework. It corresponds to CognitiveMode.LinguisticExecution in the original v1 enum, but LinguisticExecution was never incorporated into the binary Axis 4 (ZF vs ND). The 4-axis framework handles world truth (ZF), reader experience design (ND), real-world parallels (Analogical), and project management (Garden Notes). But "how the prose itself should work at the craft level" is none of these. A craft observation is neither asserting world facts nor designing reader experience — it's the author as writer thinking about language. This sits outside the 16 cells, alongside Analogical and Garden Notes.

What the work blocks reveal
The work blocks describe a workflow sequence rather than assertion types, so they don't directly correspond to tracks. But they map onto tracks as outputs:

World Mechanics Construction produces Track 1 content for codex entries. Causal History Construction produces Track 2 content and Track 8 link content. Character Psychology Construction produces Track 1 content for characters. Thematic Argumentation Design produces Theme entity tracks. Macro Narrative Architecture produces Tracks 1.5 and 2.5. Scene-Level Experience Design produces Tracks 5, 6, 15 and the PlotPoint Reader Perception Design. Scene Blueprint produces the Synopsis field on PlotPoint — not a note track but a structured field, which the spreadsheet correctly identifies as categorically distinct from design specifications.

The spreadsheet's workflow sequence is actually the correct order for working through the 4-axis tracks: ZF tracks before ND tracks (you must know what is true before you design how the reader encounters it), Subject-scope before Link-scope (you must have the project-wide plan before designing individual scenes), earlier layers before later layers (world mechanics before character psychology before narrative architecture).

The synthesis
They are genuinely complementary views of the same system, not competitors.

The spreadsheet describes the system by purpose and workflow — what is each mode of use for, what work block produces what, what cognitive mindset does each require. The 4-axis framework describes the system by structural classification — what logical position does each track occupy in the combinatorial space, why do certain combinations not exist.

The spreadsheet's three-category split (Canonical claim / Architectural decision / Design specification) is exactly the ZeroFocalization / NarrativeDesign-at-Subject-scope / NarrativeDesign-at-Link-scope mapping from Axes 3 and 4. It arrived at the same fundamental distinction from a different starting point.

What the spreadsheet has that the 4-axis framework adds specificity to: the PE/NE distinction (prior-established vs narrative-emergent), the Subject/Link scope distinction, and the multi-story project scope.

What the spreadsheet has that the 4-axis framework doesn't address: the workflow sequence (which phase comes before which), and the explicit naming of Craft observation as a separate assertion type.

What the 4-axis framework has that the spreadsheet doesn't: the structural explanation for why certain combinations are impossible, the PE/NE axis that distinguishes backstory from in-story content, and the project-scope reveal planning that spans multiple stories.

The one genuine gap the comparison surfaces: CognitiveMode.LinguisticExecution (Craft observation) needs a track outside the 16 combinations, alongside Analogical and Garden Notes. The original v1 CognitiveMode enum included it for a reason. It belongs on Subject entities (character voice notes, prose register guidance for a specific entity) and possibly on PlotPoints (scene-level prose craft notes). It sits outside the combinatorial space for the same reason Analogical does — it's not asserting world content and not designing reader experience, it's designing language execution.

I'm still not completely concrete on what each of the potential 16 tracks mean and whether they lead to a meaningful question for a particular subject type. It seems like some subject types won't have some tracks. Give an analysis of this plan to move forward and potential pitfalls:

I am going to put in all 16 tracks titled by their axis values and then try to sort/rearticulate all my notes about Applejack. If by the end of that, one of the tracks is empty it probably means that track doesn't apply and there is probably a reason for it. Hopefully my Applejack notes have enough material that whatever type of track that can exist already does in the notes and hopefully the 4 axes are suitably exhaustive (which the analysis above greenlighted). This is because for some of the combinations I am not able to articulate the exact question or explanation/purpose.

Every axis should have a single question to force focus, plus an explanation that speaks to what the contents of the track will be used for in the story planning process, right?

The excel sheet mentioned single claims or assertion types, is this still applicable?

May 12

Analyzed framework validation plan, identified sorting pitfalls, recommended establishing questions first
Analyzed framework validation plan, identified sorting pitfalls, recommended establishing questions first
Analysis of the plan
The empirical approach is sound in principle — use real data to validate theoretical framework, let empty tracks speak for themselves. But there are specific pitfalls worth naming before you start.

Pitfall 1: The compilation problem

Applejack's v1 notes are distributed across multiple subject entries — her own entry, Applejack's Parents, Battle of Mount Aris, Rebuilding Ain Trotgourait, various plot point links. If you only sort the notes in her character entry, several tracks will appear empty not because the track type doesn't apply to characters, but because the relevant content sits in a different subject. Track 2 (backstory) in particular will look thin unless you also pull from Mount Aris and the Parents entry. The validation only works if you treat the exercise as a compilation pass across all subjects that touch her, not just her own entry.

Pitfall 2: Sorting v1 notes is not the same as writing v2 notes

We established earlier that v1 notes are too tightly coupled to cleanly split. If you sort them as-is, you will end up with fragments of mixed-mode content in each track — a Track 1 note that also contains backstory, a Track 7 note that also contains arc function analysis. This defeats the purpose of the track separation. The better version of this exercise: use v1 notes as reference material visible in one window, write clean from-scratch notes for each track in the other. If a track ends up empty because you can't write a clean note for it drawing on what you know about Applejack, that's more reliable evidence than a track being empty because you couldn't extract a fragment from a mixed note.

Pitfall 3: Three kinds of empty tracks

When a track ends up empty, it could mean three different things and they matter differently:

The track type genuinely doesn't apply to characters — structural evidence, the track can be excluded from the Character SubjectDefinition
The notes haven't been written yet — common for Tracks 3, 4 (in-story arc planning), Track 15 (gap design, explicitly deferred)
The content correctly lives on a different entity — Track 1.5/2.5 content about TwiJack belongs partly on the TwiJack Bond entity, not on Applejack alone
Mark which reason applies to each empty track. Don't just note emptiness — note the reason.

Pitfall 4: Display questions must come first

You noted that some track combinations don't yet have a clearly articulable question. If you start sorting before establishing what each track's question is, you won't know what a note for that track should look like, and you won't know whether a given note belongs there or not. The sorting exercise depends on having the question, not the other way around.

On display questions and explanations
Yes — each track needs exactly two things before it's usable:

Display question: The single focused question every note in this track should answer. Written in second person, present tense, as a prompt: "Who is this character and why, and what can they not see about themselves?" This is what the author reads when opening the track and what they hold in mind while writing notes.

Explanation/purpose: What this track's content is used for downstream. Not a restatement of the display question but an answer to "why does this track need to exist?" — what design work does it enable, what would be harder or impossible without it? This connects the track back to the spreadsheet's "use" framework: which of the story planner's functions does this track serve?

Having both is important because the display question tells you what to write, and the explanation tells you whether what you've written matters. A note might answer the display question but still be misplaced if it's actually serving the purpose of a different track.

On assertion types from the spreadsheet
Still applicable, but as a quality check within each track rather than as a classification mechanism between tracks. In a well-formed track, every note should be a single atomic assertion of the appropriate type. ZeroFocalization tracks (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8) should contain only canonical claims — one claim per note. NarrativeDesign tracks (1.5, 2.5, 5, 6, 15) should contain architectural decisions or design specifications — one per note.

The test: if you can't state a note as a single sentence of one assertion type, it contains more than one note and should be split. If a note answers the track's display question but uses a different assertion type than expected for that track, it's misplaced regardless of its content. A canonical claim in a NarrativeDesign track means you're asserting world truth where you should be designing reader experience — different cognitive act, wrong track.

Recommended sequence
Before sorting or writing anything: write the display question and explanation for all 11 existing character tracks. For the 5 non-existent tracks, write why each one doesn't exist — this cements the structural logic and gives you something to verify against the data.

Then, using v1 notes as reference in one window, write clean from-scratch notes for each existing track. At the end, any track that stays empty despite having access to all v1 content about Applejack across all subjects is a candidate for exclusion from the Character SubjectDefinition, with the reason noted.

Want me to draft the display questions and explanations for all 11 tracks now as a starting point for the exercise?

The problem is we thought some tracks were deferred to other areas but that doesn't seem to be the case after realigning or axing "story thread". I now believe all 16 tracks can possibly hold meaning, but sometimes two tracks can collapse into one if the axis that makes them differ doesn't apply to the subject and the context of the other three axes. But it's hard to know without having data. I'm not sure if static analysis of first principles can do it; please give an analysis of this

May 12

Reconciled track displacement theory with empirical validation necessity
Reconciled track displacement theory with empirical validation necessity
This is a genuinely important revision and it changes the analysis significantly. Let me work through what static analysis can and cannot determine, and why.

What changed with the narrow StoryThread definition
When Story Threads were broad, S4 (O+NE+S+ND) and S8 (E+NE+S+ND) could be "displaced" there. Now that Story Threads only cover genuinely cross-cutting arcs like TwiJack, those two tracks have nowhere else to go. The content that would have gone in them — the story-wide reader experience design for a character's psychological arc, the story-wide design of how the reader tracks a character's events across the narrative — is now either a character track or it has no home at all. If you can write a coherent S4 note about Applejack that isn't TwiJack and isn't a StoryThread, then S4 is a character track. Similarly for L8: previously absorbed into PlotPoint-level design, but there are genuinely character-specific reader experience design notes (how the reader should feel watching AJ choose the machine gun specifically, not the whole scene generally) that PlotPoint synthesis might not fully capture.

The two truly impossible tracks (L1 and L5) are different in kind from the others. Their impossibility is structural and provable without data: an omniscient assertion about prior-established content is invariant by definition, and invariant content cannot have a scene-specific form. This is a logical contradiction, not an empirical observation. These two will always be empty regardless of subject type.

What static analysis can and cannot determine
Can determine:

The structural impossibility of L1 and L5. The argument is a proof, not a prediction. Prior-established content in ZeroFocalization mode does not change per scene. Scene-specific scope requires content that varies per scene. The combination is self-contradictory independent of any data.

Subject types where one value of an axis is logically inapplicable. A fully Narrative-Emergent subject (like the Equestrian Republic in TLTT) cannot have populated PE tracks because the entity did not exist before the story. You don't need data to conclude that Track 1 (O+PE+S+ZF) for the Republic is empty — empty by logic, not by oversight.

Cannot determine:

Whether a track that could theoretically hold content actually holds meaningfully distinct content from an adjacent track. This depends on what you find yourself writing. If your S4 notes and your Track 3 notes are repeatedly saying the same thing in different framing, the tracks are collapsing not because the axis is logically inapplicable but because for this specific subject type the distinction between "what the arc is" (ZF) and "how the reader experiences the arc" (ND) doesn't generate meaningfully different assertions in practice.

Whether "displaced" or "absorbed" judgments survive the narrow StoryThread revision. These judgments were made against a theoretical framework that has since changed. Static analysis that produced those conclusions is now operating against outdated premises.

The collapse concept is empirically discovered, not statically predicted
Your insight about collapse is correct and important. Two tracks collapse into one when the axis distinguishing them doesn't generate a meaningful difference in what you would actually write. This is subtle because:

The axis might be theoretically applicable but practically irrelevant for a specific subject. Consider a simple codex entry — a specific piece of technology with no meaningful history. Track 2 (E+PE+S+ZF) theoretically applies to all Prior-Established subjects. But for this technology, "what happened to bring it into existence" is a single sentence and then it's done. It doesn't collapse with Track 1 in the logical sense, but it's so thin it might as well be a footnote within Track 1 rather than a separate track.

Collapse by identity happens when writing Track A and Track B for the same subject produces the same assertions. If every Track 7 note (psychology in this scene, ZF) you write for Applejack is functionally identical to what you would put in Track 15 (gap design for psychology, ND) — because for her the objective psychological state IS the designed reader perception — then those two tracks have collapsed in practice for this subject even though they're theoretically distinct.

You cannot predict this without writing into both tracks and observing the result.

What the Applejack exercise can determine
Can establish: Which tracks produce meaningfully different content when you actually write notes into them. Which tracks stay empty after a serious attempt to populate them drawing on all available Applejack-related material across all subjects. Whether S4, S8, and L8 have genuine content or whether their content collapses into adjacent tracks.

Cannot establish: Whether tracks that appeared empty simply weren't written yet versus genuinely don't apply. Track 15 (gap design) was explicitly deferred — its emptiness is phase-specific, not structural. Track 3 and Track 4 might be thin simply because in-story planning for Applejack hasn't been completed. You need to distinguish between empty-because-not-written and empty-because-not-applicable, and this distinction requires intentional effort.

The collapse test specifically: When you populate two tracks and find the notes are saying the same thing or one track's notes feel redundant given the other, that's the empirical signal for collapse. The axis isn't generating a meaningful distinction for this subject in this context. The track doesn't need to be deleted from the model — it just doesn't need to be included in the Character SubjectDefinition's default track set.

The concrete implication
Static analysis correctly identifies a small number of logically impossible combinations and a small number of logically necessary ones. Everything in between — the borderline tracks, the collapse questions, the revised placement decisions post-StoryThread-narrowing — requires data. The Applejack exercise is the right way to get that data, but the signal you're looking for in each track is not just "populated or empty" but "does writing into this track produce assertions I couldn't have put in any other track?" That's the question that reveals both whether the track is necessary and whether it should be in the Character SubjectDefinition.

I drew out a nested punnett square where the two outer axes are ontology vs events and prior established vs narrative emergent. The inner axes are zero focalization vs narrative design and project wide vs scene specific. This is because the inner ones can inform each other and build on each other, so they should be close. The outer ones, their content is more independent. Psychology and events are different data points, even though events drive psychology and ontology drives events. The Prior Established content and Narrative Emergent content are different content.

Give an analysis of whether this nested punnett square configuration is the best one for determining track questions and explanations/uses, or not

May 12

Validated configuration's workflow logic and identified comparative strengths
Validated configuration's workflow logic and identified comparative strengths
Your reasoning is sound and the configuration is well-suited to the stated purpose. Let me work through the analysis precisely.

Why the outer axes are correctly chosen as "independent"
The outer split on O/E and PE/NE creates four content domains that are genuinely different in kind:

O + PE: what the subject IS, as already established before the story
O + NE: how the subject CHANGES through the story
E + PE: what HAPPENED to the subject before the story
E + NE: what HAPPENS to the subject during the story
These four domains require different writing stances. You think as a psychologist/physicist for O+PE, as an arc designer for O+NE, as a historian for E+PE, and as a chronicler of unfolding events for E+NE. The outer split means that before you even look at an inner cell, you know roughly what kind of thinking is required. This is exactly what you want for a reference that helps you derive track questions.

Your argument that these axes are "more independent" is also structurally accurate. Events and psychology are causally related but the notes themselves are categorically distinct — one asserts what happened, one asserts who someone is because of what happened. Similarly, PE and NE content is about genuinely different material — one exists before the story, one comes into being within it. An entity can populate both, but the notes don't overlap.

Why the inner axes are correctly chosen as "informing each other"
Within each outer quadrant, the inner 2×2 — ZF/ND × S/L — forms a natural workflow sequence. Consider the O+PE quadrant:

ZF + S = establish what the psychology IS, project-wide (Track 1)
ND + S = plan how the reader learns about it, project-wide (Track 1.5)
ZF + L = IMPOSSIBLE (prior-established psychology doesn't vary per scene in ZF mode)
ND + L = design the reader's encounter with it in this scene (Track 5)
The sequence ZF→ND is the authorial workflow: know what is true before designing how to reveal it. The sequence S→L is the scale workflow: plan at project scope before designing individual scenes. These are not independent decisions — you refer to the ZF+S note when writing the ND+S note, and you refer to the ND+S note when writing the ND+L note. The inner grid is a dependency chain within each content domain.

This holds across all four outer quadrants. In O+NE:

ZF + S = the arc trajectory (Track 3)
ZF + L = the psychology in this specific scene (Track 7)
ND + S = how the reader tracks the arc project-wide (S4, the previously displaced track)
ND + L = what the reader perceives about the psychology in this scene (Track 15)
The ZF notes are the content that the ND notes design around. The S notes are the plans that the L notes execute.

The structural constraint is visible but less prominent
The impossible cells (ZF + L within PE quadrants) appear in the same inner cell position across both PE outer quadrants — top-right within O+PE and top-right within E+PE. This creates a visual pattern: whenever you're in a PE outer quadrant, the ZF+L cell is empty. The constraint is detectable by comparison across quadrants rather than immediately obvious within a single quadrant.

This is a weakness relative to Config 5 (outer: ZF/ND + PE/NE), where an entire outer quadrant (ZF+PE) has its L column empty, making the structural rule more visually dominant. But Config 5's strength is explaining the framework, not using it. For your stated purpose — deriving questions and explanations — visibility of the constraint is secondary to the clarity of each cell's question. Config 6 wins on that criterion.

The previously displaced and absorbed tracks land in revealing positions
In your configuration, S4 (ND + S within O+NE) and S8 (ND + S within E+NE) appear as natural cells in the NE outer quadrants. Rather than looking like orphaned content from a displaced category, they appear as the project-wide narrative design counterpart to the scene-specific ND+L cells in the same quadrant. This is actually useful: seeing S4 adjacent to Track 15 makes it clear they're doing the same kind of work at different scopes — one plans how the reader tracks the psychological arc across the project, the other designs the reader's perception of psychology in one scene.

L8 (ND + L within E+NE) appears as the fourth cell of the E+NE quadrant alongside Track 8. Seeing them together makes the question derivable by contrast: Track 8 (ZF+L within E+NE) asserts what the character objectively does in this scene; L8 (ND+L within E+NE) designs how the reader should experience what the character does. Previously absorbed, now visible as a genuine question for characters.

The one refinement worth considering
Your reasoning for inner vs outer is correct, but the placement of ZF/ND and S/L within the inner grid matters too. The workflow sequence suggests ZF/ND should be rows (top-to-bottom reads as "establish truth then design experience") and S/L should be columns (left-to-right reads as "project-wide then scene-specific"). This reading direction reinforces the dependency chain: you read across from ZF to ND for the mode shift, and down from S to L for the scope shift. The impossible cell always appears in the same position (top-right: ZF+L) within PE outer quadrants, which is easier to remember as a consistent rule.

Verdict
For the specific purpose of determining track questions and explanations, your configuration is the best among the six essentially distinct options. The outer split creates clear content domains with distinct writing stances. The inner split creates a workflow sequence within each domain. The previously displaced and absorbed tracks land in positions where their question is derivable rather than requiring special justification. The impossible cells form a consistent visual pattern across PE quadrants. No other configuration achieves all four of these properties simultaneously.

From now on, no more referring to tracks by numbers or letter plus number, because it's too hard to parse and figure out what track that is. All tracks need to be referred to by their 4 axes values in the following order with plus signs:
O or E
PE or NE
S or L
ZF or ND

Now let me walk through the first analysis for characters, the O+PE quadrant.
O+PE+S+ZF is the classic, most obvious track. This is the character's established psychology, who they are as a character, at the start of the main story.
Moving one cell over to O+PE+S+ND, I believe it is something like: What is the plan for how the reader should learn the character's truth and why? It would start out with what a reader would assume based on tropes and first impressions and then outline a plan of the design for how that character is revealed.
The "impossible" combination of O+PE+L+ZF needs to be rethought. I believe the question here is: What truth is being revealed about who they are in this scene? This is objective, and the sum of all these links across the story should accumulate into the full psychology. We were previously putting this "what is revealed" in O+PE+L+ND, I think because the reveal informed narrative design and reader experience. But that's where the distinction comes in. "What truth is being revealed" is objective. "How should the reader feel about the reveal? How does their understanding change after learning the new truth? What gets recontextualized from before; what is the reader thinking about?" This IS what we are designing for in the reader's feelings. In O+PE+L, we are talking about what is being revealed about their established self in a specific scene. What is being revealed is the ZF portion; how the reader should feel about it is the ND portion.

I feel like there is this really complex interplay where in certain combos of the first two axes, the second two axes have swapped orders of what informs what in terms of intuitiveness.

For tracks on L, I believe that "prior established" and "narrative emergent" suddenly flip when the link is to a plot point of a prequel-sequel which is read after the main story. In this case, the narrative emergent stuff is the stuff happening chronlogically earlier. The "prior established" is what the reader already knows happens latter. Thus the L+ND cell acts as dramatic irony for the prequel (notes here talk about what the reader would recall as a truth they hold which the character doesn't) while L+ND in the main story is about revelation (notes here talk about what the reader's new truth makes them feel differently and reevaluate their current opinions)

May 12

Validated framework reconceptualization and identified epistemological shifts
Validated framework reconceptualization and identified epistemological shifts
Your reconceptualization of O+PE+L+ZF is correct and the original impossibility argument was wrong in a specific way. Let me articulate what the error was and why your version is right, then address the interplay observation and the prequel flip.

The error in the impossibility argument
The original argument was: prior-established psychology is invariant — the same truth regardless of which scene you're in — therefore it cannot be scene-specific in ZF mode. This confused the truth itself with the disclosure of the truth. The psychological truth is invariant. Which portion of that truth becomes accessible to the reader in this scene is not invariant — it is scene-specific. That disclosure is an objective fact and therefore a ZF assertion.

O+PE+L+ZF answers: what portion of who this character is gets disclosed in this scene?

O+PE+L+ND answers: how should the reader feel about that disclosure, what prior understanding does it destabilize, what do they now see differently?

The sum of all O+PE+L+ZF notes across all scenes in a narrative should equal the full O+PE+S+ZF content — or at least the portion of it that this narrative chooses to disclose. That relationship now makes the four cells of the O+PE quadrant a complete and coherent system:

O+PE+S+ZF: the full psychological truth
O+PE+S+ND: the project-wide plan for progressively disclosing it
O+PE+L+ZF: what is disclosed in this specific scene (objective)
O+PE+L+ND: how the reader should experience that disclosure (designed)
And the same correction applies to E+PE+L+ZF: which specific backstory event is disclosed in this scene, stated objectively. Also not impossible.

The interplay observation: dependency direction flips between PE and NE
Your intuition is pointing at something real. In the PE quadrants, the dependency flows top-down: the full truth exists before any scene begins, so S-scope content informs L-scope content, and ZF content informs ND content. You know the whole psychology before you design any specific scene's disclosure.

In the NE quadrants, the dependency inverts. The truth does not fully exist until the scenes create it. O+NE+L+ZF notes (the psychology in each scene) aggregate into O+NE+S+ZF (the arc trajectory). The L-scope content informs the S-scope content rather than the other way around. The ZF arc plan is a synthesis of what the scenes establish rather than a blueprint they execute against. Similarly, O+NE+S+ND (how the reader tracks the arc project-wide) can only be designed after you know what each O+NE+L+ND scene contributes to the reader's experience.

So within the inner grid:

PE quadrants: S informs L, ZF informs ND — top-down and left-to-right
NE quadrants: L informs S, ZF and ND develop more in parallel — bottom-up and circular
This is not a flaw in the configuration. It reflects a genuine difference in how prior-established and narrative-emergent content works. PE content has a complete truth that gets progressively disclosed. NE content has a truth that gets progressively built. These are different processes and they have different dependency structures.

The PE/NE epistemological flip for prequel links
This is the sharpest insight in the message. You are identifying that the PE/NE axis has two different referents that align in TLTT but diverge in prequels:

Chronological PE/NE: what exists before versus during this story's narrative span in world chronology. This is what the axes were defined against.

Epistemological PE/NE: what the reader already knows versus what they are learning for the first time. For a first-time TLTT reader, these two align — what is chronologically PE is also unknown to the reader, and what is chronologically NE is being witnessed in real time.

For a prequel reader who has already read TLTT, they diverge. The reader carries TLTT's world state as their epistemological PE — their established knowledge base. The prequel's chronologically NE content (events unfolding in the prequel) is being witnessed for the first time in terms of narrative experience, but the reader already knows where those events lead.

The consequence for ND tracks in prequel links:

O+PE+L+ND in TLTT: designs the reader's experience of learning something they did not know — revelation, the shift from assumption to truth.

O+PE+L+ND in a prequel: the prior-established content is known to the reader from TLTT. The ND note designs something different — how the prequel scene causes the reader to see that already-known truth with new depth or in a new light. The function is recontextualization, not revelation.

O+NE+L+ND in a prequel: the character is in a moment before they have become what the reader knows them to be in TLTT. The reader watches the formation of a psychology whose outcome they already hold. The ND note designs dramatic irony — the gap between what the character understands about themselves right now and what the reader knows they will become.

So the same track has a different ND question depending on narrative position:

In TLTT: O+PE+L+ND asks how the reader should feel about learning something new
In a prequel: O+PE+L+ND asks how the reader should experience something they already know gaining new dimension
In a prequel: O+NE+L+ND asks how the reader should feel watching formation knowing the outcome
This is precisely why SupportsNarrativeTag on ND tracks is necessary — not just to label which story a note applies to, but because the function of the ND cell changes based on the narrative's position in the project timeline relative to what the reader brings to it.

The insight that PE+ZF and NE+ZF refers to chronological framing while  PE+ND and NE+ND refer to epistemological framing is fascinating. I also noticed that S+ZF and E+ZF tends to mean objective truth while S+ND and E+ND is about a plan. I'm now wondering if it would be effective to actually go through all 16 two value possibilities and state what the resulting 4 quadrants have in common, because that framing changes based on certain axis value combinations. Does this mean the axes are NOT orthogonal, but their meaning is codependent/tightly coupled? That seems obvious, actually. It seems I've been trying to treat them as independent like vector algebra when really we are not talking numbers but semantic meaning, and semantics are tightly coupled (well I guess LLM training is about vectorizing semantics in such a high dimensional space that they become independent, but that doesn't apply for the scale of human narrative design).

It may also be possible that PE and NE are not accurate, and maybe this axis is about the gap between reader state and truth instead. The reader could know less than the truth (revelation), the reader could be learning the truth simultaneously with the character (this is narrative emergent?) or the reader could know more than the character (dramatic irony, from main story knowledge when reading prequel, or from perceiving something about the character that they can't see in themselves yet, which is the classic AJ at the machine gun in a hiding place example). But that also seems to be the ZF vs ND axis instead.

May 12

Reconciled semantic codependence with structural orthogonality in narrative framework
Reconciled semantic codependence with structural orthogonality in narrative framework
You've identified something real and important about how semantic dimensions work differently from mathematical ones. Let me work through it precisely.

Structurally orthogonal, semantically coupled
The axes ARE orthogonal in one sense: they generate genuinely distinct cells. Knowing a value on one axis does not logically determine the value on another. You can be at O+PE+S+ZF or O+PE+S+ND or O+PE+L+ZF — the axes generate independent choices.

But you are right that they are NOT orthogonal in semantic meaning. The meaning of each axis value shifts depending on what it is combined with. This is not a flaw in the framework — it is a property of semantic dimensions generally. Mathematical vectors are orthogonal when their dot product is zero, meaning they truly do not influence each other's meaning. Semantic dimensions modify each other's interpretation when combined. "Warm" means something different combined with "bright" than combined with "dark," even though brightness and temperature are orthogonal perceptual dimensions.

The practical consequence is that track questions cannot be derived by assembling axis value definitions mechanically. Each combination must be read holistically, as a gestalt.

The codependencies you identified, named precisely
PE/NE changes meaning with ZF vs ND:

PE+ZF = chronological marker: this content exists before the story begins in world time
NE+ZF = chronological marker: this content is being established during the story in world time
PE+ND = epistemological marker: this content is unknown to the reader and will be revealed
NE+ND = epistemological marker: the reader tracks the emergence of truth as it is created

With ZF, PE/NE marks position in the world's timeline. With ND, PE/NE marks position in the reader's knowledge acquisition journey. The same axis values mean categorically different things depending on which cognitive mode they are combined with.

O/E changes meaning with ZF vs ND:

O+ZF = asserting what is objectively true about structure or psychology
E+ZF = asserting what is objectively true about events
O+ND = designing the reader's encounter with psychological truth
E+ND = designing the reader's encounter with event truth

With ZF, O/E distinguishes the type of truth being asserted. With ND, O/E distinguishes the subject matter of the reader experience being designed. The axis is relatively stable here — it always marks "what kind of content" — but the activity changes from assertion to design.

PE/NE changes meaning with S vs L:

PE+S = the complete established truth across the whole project
PE+L = this scene's specific engagement with that established truth
NE+S = the complete arc of emerging truth across the project
NE+L = this scene's specific contribution to the emerging truth

At Subject scope, PE/NE marks temporal origin of a complete body of content. At Link scope, PE/NE marks which direction the scene is engaging with — drawing on established truth or building emerging truth.

ZF/ND changes meaning with S vs L:

ZF+S = project-wide omniscient assertion
ZF+L = scene-specific omniscient assertion
ND+S = project-wide reader experience design
ND+L = scene-specific reader experience design

This is the most semantically stable pairing — the combination means roughly what you'd expect from assembling the parts. The notable exception is that ZF+PE+L was previously called impossible precisely because the semantic combination seemed to exclude scene-specificity. Your reconceptualization (disclosure is scene-specific even when truth is not) resolved this.

PE/NE is the right axis; "reader gap" is a derived property
Your proposed alternative — reframing PE/NE as the gap between reader state and truth (reader knows less / simultaneously / more) — is valid as a description of functional consequences, not as a primary axis definition.

The gap between reader state and truth is not one thing. It is a result of multiple tracks interacting:

The reader knows less than truth (revelation) when PE+ND content has not yet been disclosed via the corresponding PE+L+ZF notes in prior scenes.

The reader learns simultaneously with the character (your NE case) when the scene is NE+L+ZF — truth being established right now for both.

The reader knows more than the character (dramatic irony) when the full O+PE+S+ZF truth is accessible to the reader but the character's O+NE+L+ZF state reflects incomplete self-knowledge. This is the AJ machine gun case: the reader holds the full Track O+PE+S+ZF truth about her psychology, while her Track O+NE+L+ZF note reveals a self-perception that diverges from it. The gap is the product of these two tracks together, not a single axis value.

For prequels, the reader knows more than the character in a different way: the reader's epistemological PE comes from having read TLTT, which is chronologically NE relative to the prequel's world. This case emerges from the divergence between chronological PE/NE and epistemological PE/ND, which is the codependency you identified earlier.

Replacing PE/NE with "reader gap" would lose the chronological grounding that makes ZF tracks coherent (ZF tracks assert world truths, and whether those truths existed before or during the story is a chronological fact). You would collapse the chronological and epistemological functions into one axis and lose the ability to distinguish them.

The "go through all 16 two-value combinations" exercise
This would be worth doing as a table, since you proposed it. There are C(4,2) = 6 axis pairs, each with 4 value combinations, giving 24 semantic descriptions. Doing this would make all the codependencies explicit and give you a reference for interpreting each cell holistically rather than mechanically. It would also reveal which pairings are semantically stable (O/E × ZF/ND is relatively stable; PE/NE × ZF/ND is the most unstable) and which generate the most surprising combined meanings.

The result would be a map of semantic drift across axis combinations — which combinations mean roughly what their parts suggest, and which require holistic reinterpretation. That map would be the actual reference for writing track questions, since track questions must be derived holistically.


Now I'm starting to to think PE and NE are incorrect labels which are only part of what they were supposed to capture. I'll still use PE and NE for this prompt but by the end of this response I want a few suggestions for replacement terms. The original terms were "pre-story" and "in-story" but I thi
