I pasted in a new hypothesis. Please analyze fully and answer all questions contained within.

May 13

Synthesized narrative framework, reconciled track merges, and evaluated character data comprehensively
Synthesized narrative framework, reconciled track merges, and evaluated character data comprehensively
On the track count: 13, not 14
Three merges happen, not two. You correctly identified two but the third was implicit in your own text.

Merge 1: O+PE+S+ND + E+PE+S+ND → PE+S+ND. You stated this explicitly.

Merge 2: O+PE+L+ND + E+PE+L+ND → PE+L+ND. You stated this implicitly when you wrote "(O and E)+PE+L+ND is specifically about the difference between what I anticipate the reader's opinion to be..." — you were treating it as already merged.

Merge 3: O+NE+L+ND + E+NE+L+ND → NE+L+ND. You stated this explicitly.

Three merges eliminate three cells from sixteen, giving 13 distinct tracks. O+NE+S+ND and E+NE+S+ND remain separate because at project-wide scope, designing how the reader tracks psychological transformation is a structurally different problem from designing the reader's experience of the event sequence — which events get dramatized versus referenced is an architectural decision that belongs to E+NE+S+ND alone.

What the full framework now is
13 distinct tracks, organized:

Track	Name
O+PE+S+ZF	Psychological Foundation — the complete established truth
E+PE+S+ZF	Historical Record — the complete objective backstory
PE+S+ND	Reader Opinion Design — unified plan for how psychology + history reshape the reader's understanding
O+PE+L+ZF	Psychological Disclosure in Scene — what portion of the foundation is revealed here
E+PE+L+ZF	Historical Disclosure in Scene — what backstory event surfaces here
PE+L+ND	Scene Reader Opinion Shift — how reader opinion should change from these disclosures
O+NE+S+ZF	Arc Trajectory — the full psychological transformation from birth to end
E+NE+S+ZF	Event Architecture — what events shall be dramatized across the project
O+NE+S+ND	Reader Arc Tracking — how the reader should track psychological development project-wide
E+NE+S+ND	Reader Event Tracking — how the reader experiences the narrative event sequence
O+NE+L+ZF	Character Development in Scene — what psychological change occurs here
E+NE+L+ZF	Events in Scene — what objectively happens here
NE+L+ND	Scene Reader Experience — what the reader should feel watching the development and events, and what device achieves it
What was missing in all previous formulations
The O+PE+L+ZF and E+PE+L+ZF impossibility was wrong. The previous argument confused the invariance of truth with the invariance of disclosure. The truth doesn't change per scene; which portion of it becomes accessible to the reader does. The rehabilitation of these two cells is structurally significant: it means the sum of L+ZF disclosures equals the S+ZF total, creating an integrity relationship across the whole framework.

The ND merges were unrecognized. Previous formulations treated O and E as always separate dimensions even within ND tracks. The insight that reader opinion and reader experience are unified (they cannot separate "how the reader feels about the psychology" from "how the reader feels about the history" because the reader has one integrated impression) was not articulated. This simplifies the model and reflects how storytelling actually works.

The epistemological role of PE+S+ZF was not stated. Previous formulations described it as "the full psychological truth." Your formulation clarifies why it exists: not for publication but as a consistency engine, so that scene-level disclosures don't contradict each other and so the ND plan has reliable material to work from. ZF tracks are the author's private canonical record. ND tracks are the reader experience design built on that record.

The trope/source material layer of PE+S+ND was not articulated. This is specific to your project's position as fanfiction building on FiM and EaW. The reader brings prior beliefs — cartoon villain tropes for Chrysalis, innocent honest pony framing for Applejack, foreign savior framing for Minette — and PE+S+ND begins there, not at a neutral starting point. This was completely absent from earlier formulations. It is genuinely distinctive to your project's relationship with its source material.

The causal chain from ZF to ND to prose was not stated. Every ZF fact that matters narratively needs an ND delivery somewhere across the project. The ND delivery might be in TLTT (revelation for a first-time reader) or in a prequel (dramatic irony or recontextualization for a post-TLTT reader). Prequels exist precisely because the ZF world is richer than a single story's ND capacity. The ZF facts that TLTT only alludes to must still reach the reader fully through prequel ND. This is a project-wide planning principle that was completely absent before.

The relationship between L+ND and the PlotPoint prose design phase was not stated. L+ND specifies what the reader should feel and what narrative device achieves it — not how to execute it in prose. The PlotPoint level takes L+ND inputs from all linked subjects and designs FID calibration, scene blueprint, and delivery. L+ND is the specification; the PlotPoint is the implementation. This chain was implicit at best.

The architect/gardener duality for ZF S↔L direction was not named. Going S→L is architectural (plan the full arc, then implement per scene). Going L→S is gardening (observe what the scenes establish, then synthesize the arc). Your observation that O has been more architectural while E has been more gardening in your actual process is honest and explains why E+NE+S+ZF content feels implicit rather than explicit in your v1 data.

Replacement terms for the PE/NE axis
The core of what PE actually means: completed truth that exists independently of the story's dramatized events. The core of what NE actually means: truth that comes into being through the story's dramatized events.

Option 1: Established / Emergent Clean and direct. Established means complete before dramatization begins. Emergent means being constructed through dramatization. Works well with ZF (established facts vs. emerging facts) and with ND (revealing what's established vs. tracking what's emerging). Loses the temporal language without losing the meaning.

Option 2: Foundation / Becoming More evocative for character work specifically. Foundation is who and what the subject is before the story starts — the ground they stand on. Becoming is the active transformation and events that define their story. Works better for characters than for codex entries. "Foundation truth" and "Becoming truth" map naturally to "established psychology" and "arc trajectory."

Option 3: Settled / Unfolding Captures the state of the truth rather than its temporal position. Settled truth exists completely and is not being constructed. Unfolding truth is being created through narrative events. The epistemic flavor is right: settled content is what you use for consistency checking; unfolding content is what you are actively designing.

Option 4: Given / Enacted Most precise for the project-wide model. Given content is what the subject brings to any story — their established nature and history. Enacted content is what happens to and through the subject within a story's dramatized events. Works cleanly across subject types: a world physics entry's given content is its rules; its enacted content is events where those rules are exercised or tested.

My recommendation: Established / Emergent for general use across all subject types, Foundation / Becoming as an alias specifically for character work.

Applejack examples from v1 data

Notes 1, 5, 6 in her character entry contain the core: imposter syndrome mechanism, industry-equals-soullessness belief, the formation of the wound through her parents' choices. Further content in Applejack's Parents and Battle of Mount Aris subjects. Needs compilation from multiple subjects to be complete; none of it was written explicitly for this track but the raw material is there.

The Battle of Mount Aris subject holds more. The WorldDate-stamped sequence is incomplete and missing key links (the Ain Trotgourait period, the full timeline from cutie mark to war). Needs to be written.

O+NE+S+ZF — Arc Trajectory Exists. Note 8 is precisely this: "From the start to the town hall, Applejack feels like an Imposter and losing her soul... From the town hall to Trimmel, she is the Honest Worker... Post Trimmel, she accepts the Hard Truth." This is the most complete track in her v1 data and can transfer to v2 with refinement.

E+NE+S+ZF — Event Architecture Exists implicitly. The 27 linked scenes collectively constitute this. But no explicit high-level statement of "these are the events that will be dramatized for Applejack across all stories" exists as a standalone note. The data lives in the plot point structure, not in a synthesized character-level event plan. Can be constructed from the plot structure but hasn't been stated independently.

O+NE+S+ND — Reader Arc Tracking (Psychological) Does not yet exist as a clean track. Fragments exist in Thread Updates — the TwiJack update noting that "Twilight didn't come to save the general, she came to save the farmpony. That is what AJ clings to" is partially this. The Stagnation's Redemption thread updates track this arc in pieces. But no unified statement of "here is how the reader should track Applejack's psychological transformation across the project" exists. Needs to be written.

E+NE+S+ND — Reader Event Tracking Does not exist. The macro design of how the reader should experience the sequence of Applejack's story events — what the escalating military operations should feel like as a sequence, how the rhythm of action and consequence should build — has not been articulated. This may be the emptiest track in her data.

O+NE+L+ZF — Character Development in Scene Exists in the more analytical Analysis notes. "Henri says the changelings are poseurs. Applejack wants to just be a farmer or machine gunner in the trench, but she realizes she carries the title of General whether she likes it or not" — that is O+NE+L+ZF for the Henri arrives scene. "The fact that she returns to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3, but this time with resolve instead of guilt" — that is O+NE+L+ZF for that scene. Exists in mixed-mode form throughout Analysis fields.

E+NE+L+ZF — Events in Scene Exists. The Synopsis fields capture this cleanly for most scenes. "Applejack, Mali, Tally Stock, and a terrified volunteer run the machine gun. The four of them operating the one machine gun killed hundreds of changelings." This is the most thoroughly populated track in v1 across all 27 scenes.

NE+L+ND — Scene Reader Experience Exists in fragments, primarily in Thread Updates. The TwiJack update for the Relaxing scene: "Twilight didn't come to save the general, she came to save the farmpony — that is what AJ clings to, a promise that she can go back to being the farmpony one day" is NE+L+ND. The Laughter and Resilience theme commentary on the 1st Battle scene — the explanation of why the trench scene works emotionally — is NE+L+ND. These are scattered across Thread Updates and Theme Commentary fields in v1, not captured as a unified reader experience design note. Can be extracted but will require restatement.

The framing of PE vs NE is still not correct. Some of the PE content IS dramatized directly in a prequel. What makes it important is that it exists as truth chronologically before the reader encounters it?
Where are for NE, the truth is formed simultaneously with the reader encountering it for the first time.

"exists independently of the story's dramatized events" is the ZF world. The dramatized events plan for the reader is the ND world.

Both PE and NE exist independently of the dramatized prose that the reader encounters. In PE it happened before the reader's first encounter with TLTT, their entry point in the chronological timeline. It can STILL be dramatized later with an ND spec in a prequel. Or if it's not important enough it is merely stated or explained in TLTT (or a prequel). It seems like a piece of content can be BOTH PE and NE. Chrysalis's psychology is PE that gets revealed, crashing against the reader's cartoon villain or evil Nazi dictator expectations, and also NE that is dramatized in her prequel. I think since the objetlaw of how it works can't change there is no O+NE+ZF or O+NE+ND. There is O+PE+ND which is how it the understanding of the law evolves, to characters and readers. So, for something whose truth can't change, there is no O+NE. Regarding which tracks are merged:
The possible merges have to do with PE+ND and NE+ND. For PE+S+ND it is the plan for how the reader's understanding of the subject changes, so O and E are combined in the question. For NE+L+ND it is what the reader should feel about the character's development/change in internal truth (O) It's intertwined with what is happening in the scene (E). May 13

Reconstructed PE/NE framework and reconciled ZF/ND conceptual distinctions
Reconstructed PE/NE framework and reconciled ZF/ND conceptual distinctions
What PE and NE are fundamentally about
Your correction is precise. The previous framing was contaminated by conflating temporal position with dramatization. Here is the corrected understanding:

PE (Prior-Formed): The truth already existed as an objective fact before the reader's first encounter with the project at TLTT's entry point. When the reader encounters PE content, they are discovering something that was already the case. The reader arrives after the fact. Whether or not it gets dramatized in a prequel later is irrelevant to this classification.

NE (Concurrent-Forming): The truth comes into being simultaneously with the reader's encounter. The reader witnesses the formation of the truth in real time. This happens in any dramatized narrative — the main story, a prequel, a sequel — wherever the reader is present for the moment a truth is created through events.

The critical correction your message makes explicit: the same content can be both PE and NE depending on narrative frame. Chrysalis's psychology is PE in TLTT (it existed before TLTT begins; the reader discovers it) and NE in her prequel (it is being formed through the events the reader witnesses). The classification is not a property of the content itself but of the relationship between the content's chronological existence and the reader's encounter with it in a specific narrative.

This also means: a subject with no existence before TLTT (the Equestrian Republic) has no PE content at all. A world law that cannot change (the Charitostatic Effect) has no NE ontology — its truth exists completely before any story begins and never forms through events. Its O content is entirely PE. Its E content (what it does in scenes, how it is studied and applied) is NE in each story where it is enacted.

The Charitostatic Effect therefore resolves cleanly:

O+PE+World-Truth+Subject: how the law objectively works
O+PE+Design+Subject: how the reader's understanding of the law should evolve
No O+NE: the law's truth cannot change through events
E+NE+World-Truth: what the law does in specific scenes and stories
What ZF and ND are fundamentally about
Your question cuts to something the "Zero Focalization" label obscures. Both World-Truth notes and Design notes are written by the author in a fully omniscient position. The distinction is not about who is seeing or knowing. It is about what the claim is asserting.

World-Truth (ZF): The claim asserts something about the objective reality of the story world. "This is true in this world." The author is acting as in-world historian, physicist, or psychologist. The test: could an omniscient being within the story world verify this as fact?

Experience-Design (ND): The claim asserts something about the reader's psychological experience. "This is what the reader should feel, understand, or conclude." The author is acting as director and audience psychologist. The test: does this claim make sense only from outside the story world, looking at a reader?

The confusion arises because ND notes are written using the same omniscient knowledge the author uses for World-Truth notes. You know what's true about Chrysalis when you write both "Chrysalis manipulates Trimmel through loyalty not love" (World-Truth) and "at this point the reader should be revising their cartoon villain assumption toward 'rational cutthroat CEO'" (Design). The omniscience is the same. The target of the assertion differs.

Better labels: World-Truth and Experience-Design, or simply World and Design.

The NE+S+ND merge: yes, 12 tracks
Your argument is correct. Events without an ontological conclusion are telling. The reader's experience of watching the character's arc is unified: witnessing events creates changes in understanding about ontology. You cannot separate "how the reader experiences the event sequence" from "what those events should make the reader conclude about the subject's nature and transformation." These serve the same purpose and produce the same kind of note. NE+S+ND is one track.

The result is 12 distinct tracks. All four ND tracks merge O and E, for the same reason at each scope: the reader's experience and conclusions are unified across psychology and events simultaneously.

The 12 tracks with corrected labels and display questions
Axis labels:

First: O (Ontology/Psychology) vs E (Events/History)
Second: PE (Prior-Formed) vs NE (Concurrent-Forming)
Third: S (Subject/Project-wide) vs L (Link/Scene-specific)
Fourth: W (World-Truth) vs D (Experience-Design)
PE group — Subject scope
O+PE+S+W — Psychological Foundation What is objectively true about this subject's psychology, architecture, and epistemic limits at the reader's entry point? Purpose: The author's consistency engine. Prevents contradictions in scene-level disclosures. Provides the complete psychological record that PE+S+D and O+PE+L+W draw from.

E+PE+S+W — Historical Record What objectively happened to this subject, in what sequence, before the reader's entry point? Purpose: The author's chronological record. Carries WorldDate. Material for PE+S+D planning and E+PE+L+W disclosures. Not for the reader to see directly — a consistency and reference layer.

PE+S+D — Reader Opinion Design (merged O and E) Starting from what the reader assumes based on tropes, source material, and genre conventions, how should their understanding of this subject change across the whole project as the ZF truths are progressively revealed? Purpose: The master plan for the reader's evolving opinion. Begins with the trope-baseline (innocent honest farmpony, cartoon villain, foreign savior) and designs the arc of revelation and recontextualization that ends with a nuanced understanding. This is the plan that PE+L+D notes execute scene by scene.

PE group — Link scope
O+PE+L+W — Psychological Disclosure in Scene What portion of the subject's established psychological truth is objectively disclosed to the reader in this scene? Purpose: Records what specifically becomes accessible to the reader here. The sum of all O+PE+L+W across a narrative should equal the relevant portion of O+PE+S+W. Informs PE+L+D for this scene.

E+PE+L+W — Historical Disclosure in Scene What specific prior event in this subject's history is disclosed to the reader in this scene? Purpose: Records which backstory event surfaces here. Sum equals the E+PE+S+W portion revealed in this narrative. Informs PE+L+D.

PE+L+D — Scene Reader Opinion Shift (merged O and E) Given what is disclosed about this subject's psychology and history in this scene, what should change in the reader's understanding? What conclusion should they arrive at, and what prior assumption does it destabilize or deepen? Purpose: The scene-level execution of PE+S+D. Includes what narrative device achieves the shift (revelation, recontextualization, irony). In TLTT: reader discovers something new. In prequel: reader recontextualizes or experiences dramatic irony from their TLTT knowledge.

NE group — Subject scope
O+NE+S+W — Arc Trajectory How does this subject's ontology change from the beginning to the end of its dramatized story — what are the stages and pivot points of transformation? Purpose: The complete psychological arc plan, project-wide. For prequels, this covers the forming of the subject's psychology. For TLTT, the transformation of it. Sum of O+NE+L+W across all scenes.

E+NE+S+W — Event Architecture What events involving this subject are to be dramatized across the project, as opposed to merely referenced or disclosed through PE tracks? Purpose: The architectural decision about what gets shown versus told. Choosing which events become scenes (E+NE+L+W entries exist) versus which are disclosed as prior facts (E+PE+L+W entries). The sum of E+NE+L+W across all scenes.

NE+S+D — Reader Arc Design (merged O and E) How should the reader's understanding and emotional relationship to this subject develop across the whole project as they witness the events and transformations? What should they conclude from the arc of what they witnessed? Purpose: The project-wide design of how the reader tracks the subject's emergence and change. The events are the vehicle; the reader's conclusion about what those events reveal about the subject is the goal. Showing not telling: this track specifies what the reader should independently conclude, not what to tell them.

NE group — Link scope
O+NE+L+W — Character Development in Scene What change in this subject's ontology, psychology, or internal truth is objectively occurring in this scene? Purpose: Objective record of the development happening right now. Not reader-facing. Informs NE+L+D.

E+NE+L+W — Events in Scene What is this subject objectively doing and what objectively happens to them in this scene? Purpose: The objective chronicle. Most granular level of the story record. Feeds NE+S+W when synthesized.

NE+L+D — Scene Reader Experience (merged O and E) Given what the subject is doing and how they are changing in this scene, what should the reader feel and what should they conclude? What narrative device — dramatic irony, earned revelation, structural irony — achieves it? Purpose: The scene-level specification for the experience designer and later the prose writer. Not FID calibration (that is the PlotPoint level) but the specification of what the FID must achieve. Every linked subject's NE+L+D note gets synthesized at the PlotPoint level when designing the scene.

Applejack examples from v1 data
O+PE+S+W — Psychological Foundation Data exists, distributed. Notes 1, 5, 6 on her character entry contain the core mechanism. The imposter syndrome causal chain (Faust's late-season passivity → internalized → activated by Mount Aris → triggered by Vanhoover asymmetry), the industry-equals-soullessness wound, the belief that the farm represents authentic self while the general represents corruption. More in Applejack's Parents and Battle of Mount Aris subjects. Requires compilation across subjects; no single note is the clean track entry but the raw material is there.

E+PE+S+W — Historical Record Data exists, sparse. Note 0 (training camp → solidarity built → generalship accepted), Note 6 (Mount Aris → drift from Twilight → Luna's second offer). The WorldDate-stamped sequence is incomplete — the cutie mark story, the Ain Trotgourait period, the full Vanhoover timeline are missing or in other subjects. The Battle of Mount Aris subject holds the most complete account of the pivot event but it is written in a mixed mode that will require extraction.

PE+S+D — Reader Opinion Design Does not exist as a track. This is the most important absent track in her data. The starting trope (innocent honest farmpony from a children's cartoon, or the TPatK collaborator if the reader came from EaW fanfiction) and the plan for how that trope is progressively corrected by TLTT's revelations and then deepened by the Battle of Mount Aris prequel — none of this is assembled. Note 3 (Pétain as the anti-collaborator arc) and Note 5 (Faust's late-season passivity as the trope) are raw materials but they are bibliographic notes, not a reader opinion design plan. Must be written from scratch.

O+PE+L+W — Psychological Disclosure in Scene Data exists in v1 Analysis fields, not explicitly labeled. "She is only a motivated volunteer, not a general" (1st Battle Analysis). "Her guilt during the Turkey Shoot isn't just about killing; it's about Usurpation" (Relaxing scene Analysis). "Applejack's role as General is a performative shell. She delegates the actual strategy because she is self-aware enough to know she lacks the expertise" (Relaxing scene Analysis). These exist embedded in mixed-mode Analysis notes alongside Experience-Design content and need clean extraction.

E+PE+L+W — Historical Disclosure in Scene Data exists as incidental notes. "Comet Shine says he knew Applejack's parents" triggers a historical disclosure in the Organization scene. "SAA rations in the bunker" is an Applejack's Parents codex note on the 1st Battle link. These are scattered through Synopsis and Codex link fields, not systematically captured as disclosure records.

PE+L+D — Scene Reader Opinion Shift Minimal data. The TwiJack thread update for the Relaxing scene: "Twilight didn't come to save the general, she came to save the farmpony — that is what AJ clings to" approaches this. The Laughter and Resilience theme commentary on the 1st Battle approaches it. But these are not systematically written as "here is what the reader's opinion of Applejack should be after this scene and here is the device that achieves it." The most absent link-level track.

O+NE+S+W — Arc Trajectory Exists. Note 8 is precisely this: "From the start to the town hall, Applejack feels like an Imposter... From the town hall to Trimmel, she is the Honest Worker... Post Trimmel, she accepts the Hard Truth." This is the most complete subject-level track in her v1 data and can be transferred and refined.

E+NE+S+W — Event Architecture Exists implicitly. The 27 linked scenes collectively constitute this. No standalone synthesis note exists. Can be constructed from the plot structure but has not been stated as an explicit subject-level claim.

NE+S+D — Reader Arc Design Does not exist. The project-wide design of what the reader should conclude from watching Applejack's transformation — what the arc of "imposter who becomes the Lioness" should make a reader think about the nature of reluctant leadership — has not been written. Thread Updates contain fragments: the Stagnation's Redemption thread tracks related beats. But no unified specification exists.

O+NE+L+W — Character Development in Scene Exists in Analysis fields. "Henri says the changelings are poseurs. Applejack wants to just be a farmer or machine gunner in the trench, but she realizes she carries the title of General, whether she likes it or not" (Henri arrives, Analysis). "By accepting Rarity's new uniform, Applejack is fully embracing her role after Trimmel's talk" (Accepting uniform, Analysis). Present throughout, embedded in mixed-mode notes.

E+NE+L+W — Events in Scene Exists. The Synopsis fields for all 27 scenes capture this cleanly. The most thoroughly populated track in her v1 data.

NE+L+D — Scene Reader Experience Exists in fragments. Thread Updates for TwiJack contain the clearest examples. The "Applejack comforts Twilight over the burden of destruction" update for the We Are Monsters scene: "The line 'We are all monsters' is not an admission of defeat, but a marriage vow of shared burden." The Loyalty theme commentary on the 2nd Battle scene: "Friendship is Combined Arms." These are Experience-Design assertions about what the reader should understand from the scene. Not systematically captured as a track but the material is present across Thread Updates and Theme Commentary fields.

I think the key insight is that PE and NE, whatever they truely are, are additive, not mutually exclusive. They operate in respect to each other as items in a checkbox list, not radio buttons. S vs L are radio buttons - a note is either project wide or for a specific scene. ZF vs ND SHOULD be radio buttons - a note SHOULD either Be truth or reader experience, it should not be both (that's what was unhelpful in the old system). Mixing the two claims is discouraged in v2 but was convenient in v1 for data capture. O vs E is radio buttons in ZF, but they appear to be one and the same in ND, completely coupled.

This is what I'm imagining Chrysalis's PE+S+ND track will contain:
1. Readers are expected to believe at the start that Chrysalis is a cartoon villain from FiM and an evil dictator from EaW and the trope of being a leader of a fascist state leads to the perception of being a "bugnazi".
2. Throughout TLTT, Chrysalis's actual actions and narcissism that I have designed are revealed in onion layers, first through changelings POWs, then through Trimmel, then through Eagleclaw, then through Synovial. By the end of TLTT, the reader should feel they Chrysalis is clearly not a cartoon villain or an irrational dictatorial bugnazi; she is a rational tyranical reformer like Peter the Great or Emperor Meiji.
3. In Chrysalis's prequel, the reader experiences firsthand, through chrysalis's 3rd person limited POV, how and why she became the tyrant that she is in TLTT. She wanted to solve resource scarcity in the hives and was bullied by Herzlander elites while learning how to modernize. By the end of her prequel, readers should feel that individual villains are not what cause injustice; the system they were a part of is the root cause.

Analogy and social commentary leaked into the reader experience design. Is this okay or not? Should they be exorcized into separate tracks? The purpose of the S+ND track is to inform the L+ND track which then informs the plot point. Not sure of if the analogy and social commentary help or hurt that goal or workflow.

Chrysalis's O+NE+S+ZF has to track how her ontology (her psychology) changes. It goes into how her world view changes. E+NE+S+ZF is a summary outline of what is dramatized, which is mostly in service of delivering the changes to the reader. NE+S+NE (both O and E) would then be a plan for what a reader should feel from the changes. Hmm, maybe this is the same as the PE version, as in, the stuff that's dramatized and what it means is already covered by what I outlined? If this is the case, then that means S+ND, the plan for reader experience across the project, covers ALL content possibilities: O+PE (truth reference), E+PE (backstory), O+NE (what truth changes/character development), E+NE (what is planned to be dramatized). In this case, "truth reference" at Project scale is honestly a snapshot. The snapshot I chose is TLTT/main story start because that's meaningful? But in the case of world physics, the truth is truly static and only the discovery (E - events detailing the physics law's study) varies.

PE must be an ontological/psychological snapshot of some sorts. It is neither the start (the start is nothing) or the end (because characters change). Why is a snapshot of main story start helpful and deserving of a track compared to any other snapshot? Because of the ND intending to be revelation?

I need a full analysis of whether ND is unified regarding PE/NE and O/E with the only variable axis being S or L. Or if this causes a loss in nuance and clarity. And I still need to decide on terms for PE or NE

I think World W and Experience Design D are okay labels, though I'm wondering if R for Reader is better or worse.
And maybe in-universe is better for world, thus U? I can't use "Canon" because that confuses with FiM canon or EaW canon.

May 13

Synthesized unified narrative design framework collapsing ten distinct tracks
Synthesized unified narrative design framework collapsing ten distinct tracks
PE and NE as checkboxes: the implication this has for the whole framework
Your checkbox/radio button framing is the key insight that resolves the remaining structural questions. Let me work through the implications completely.

S vs L: radio buttons. A note belongs to one scope.

U vs R: should be radio buttons. A note either asserts world truth or designs reader experience. Mixing was the v1 problem.

O vs E in U tracks: radio buttons. Psychology and events are categorically distinct kinds of world-truth claims and require different cognitive modes to write.

O vs E in R tracks: merged. Reader experience is holistic — you cannot separate "how the reader feels about the character's psychology" from "how the reader feels about the character's events" because the reader has one integrated response.

PE vs NE: checkboxes, not radio buttons. A subject accumulates both PE and NE content. A note is one or the other, but a subject holds both. This means PE and NE are not a toggle — they are two independent dimensions that can both be populated.

The crucial consequence: in R tracks, PE and NE merge for the same reason O and E merge. The reader's experience design across a project is a single unified arc that covers both discovery of what was already true AND witnessing what is coming into being. You cannot cleanly separate "the reader's revelation journey" from "the reader's witnessing journey" because they are one continuous experience.

The result: 10 tracks
U tracks: 8, fully differentiated

O/E and PE/NE remain distinct because asserting world truth requires different thinking modes for each:

Writing O+PE+S+U: you are a psychologist establishing reference
Writing E+PE+S+U: you are a historian recording chronology
Writing O+NE+S+U: you are a biographer tracing transformation
Writing E+NE+S+U: you are a dramatist choosing which events to stage
The same differentiation holds at L scope. All 8 U track combinations are genuinely distinct.

R tracks: 2, fully collapsed

O and E are merged. PE and NE are merged. Only S vs L remains. Result: S+R and L+R.

U (Universe/In-Universe)	R (Reader-Experience)
O+PE+S	Psychological Foundation	—
E+PE+S	Historical Record	—
O+NE+S	Arc Trajectory	—
E+NE+S	Event Architecture	—
S	—	S+R: Project-wide Reader Design
O+PE+L	Psychological Disclosure in Scene	—
E+PE+L	Historical Disclosure in Scene	—
O+NE+L	Character Development in Scene	—
E+NE+L	Events in Scene	—
L	—	L+R: Scene Reader Experience
What is lost vs. gained by collapsing to 10
What is lost: the ability to cleanly say "this R note is specifically about the PE revelation plan" vs "this R note is about the NE witnessing arc." In v1 this distinction was often meaningless anyway because the reader experience flows continuously through both — the Chrysalis example proves this: her S+R track naturally writes across PE revelation (TLTT onion layers) and NE witnessing (prequel dramatization) as one unified arc.

What is gained: S+R becomes the natural home for the whole reader journey narrative, and L+R becomes the natural home for "what should the reader feel in this scene regardless of whether the scene is revealing PE content or witnessing NE content." The cognitive mode when writing both is consistent: you are the audience psychologist designing what conclusions the reader reaches.

The potential nuance loss is recoverable within notes, not through separate tracks. When writing L+R for a scene that discloses PE content, you write "the reader discovers X and their picture of the character shifts from Y to Z." When writing L+R for a scene that stages NE events, you write "the reader witnesses X and should feel Y about it." Both are valid L+R notes. The PE/NE distinction shows up in the note's content, not in its track placement.

The snapshot question: why is PE the right reference?
PE is not precisely a temporal snapshot. It is the settled reference reality — the complete established truth the author uses as a consistency engine AND the material from which all revelation is drawn. For a world law, this is simply "what is permanently true." For a character, it is "the complete psychological and historical reality at the reader's entry point" — not because TLTT start is an arbitrary snapshot, but because that is the point from which the reader's discovery journey begins.

The reason this snapshot is THE meaningful one: every PE+L+U disclosure in every TLTT scene is revealing a portion of the total PE reference. The PE reference is the denominator that all disclosures must eventually cover. A different snapshot (after the Town Hall, after Trimmel) would be NE content — it captures a state that was produced by the story, not a settled reference the story reveals.

For the Charitostatic Effect, PE is just "the law as it works." There is no NE ontology because laws do not form through events. Its E+NE content is purely "what the law does in scenes" — its usage, not its constitution.

The analogy and social commentary question
Looking at Chrysalis's S+R example, note 3 contains: "readers should feel that individual villains are not what cause injustice; the system they were a part of is the root cause." This is thematic/social commentary content that has entered the reader experience design.

Is this okay? The purpose of S+R is to inform L+R which informs the PlotPoint. The test is: does this content help write L+R notes and PlotPoint designs?

The answer is: the analogical references (Peter the Great, Emperor Meiji) are productive in S+R because they precisely specify the target reader state. They give you a concrete picture of "rational tyrant" that you can use to calibrate specific scenes. These help downstream work.

The systemic-injustice conclusion in note 3 is more ambiguous. It is the thematic argument the prequel is designed to demonstrate. It does help you write L+R notes for the prequel — you are designing scenes that should make the reader reach this conclusion about Chrysalis specifically. In that sense it belongs in S+R.

However, the precise version of this content that belongs on Chrysalis's character track is: what the reader should conclude about Chrysalis. The general principle ("systems produce injustice, not individuals") belongs on the Theme entity. The character-specific version ("Chrysalis is a product of the hive's structural resource scarcity, not a cartoonish source of evil in herself") belongs on Chrysalis's S+R.

The practical rule: if removing the statement would make S+R less useful for writing Chrysalis scenes, it stays. If the statement is about general societal truth rather than about how the reader should perceive Chrysalis, it belongs on the Theme entity. In this case, the prequel note should probably read: "the reader should conclude that Chrysalis's tyranny was the product of specific material conditions she was responding to, not intrinsic evil" — this keeps the focus on Chrysalis and implicitly demonstrates the systemic theme without that theme being written as an abstract proposition.

Social commentary may appear in S+R as long as it is attached to the specific subject's reader arc. The Theme entity holds the universal argument; the character track holds the character-specific demonstration.

Label suggestions for PE/NE
With the corrected understanding: PE = the settled truth that already exists and serves as the substrate for revelation; NE = truth being formed through dramatized events that the reader witnesses.

Option 1: Substrate / Emergent Substrate captures that PE is the foundational layer underpinning all revelation — it exists beneath the story, not within it. Emergent captures the quality of NE content coming into being through events. These are clearly not mutually exclusive: a subject can have substrate content AND emergent content.

Option 2: Established / Emergent Familiar and accurate with the corrected understanding. Most direct. "Established" means settled and complete as a reference reality. "Emergent" means forming through events the reader witnesses.

Option 3: Latent / Enacted Latent = exists but not yet encountered by the reader, waiting to be revealed. Enacted = being enacted in real time through story events. This captures the reader-relationship dimension most precisely. PE content is latent until disclosed; NE content is enacted as it is experienced.

Option 4: Prior / Forming Simple and operational. Prior = came before the reader's entry point. Forming = coming into being during the reader's encounter. Loses some nuance but gains clarity.

My recommendation: Established / Emergent for the primary labels. They are familiar enough to be intuitive, accurate with the corrected understanding, and clearly non-mutually-exclusive. Latent / Enacted as an alternative if you want language that foregrounds the reader's relationship to the content rather than the content's temporal position.

Label suggestions for U/R axis
Your candidates: W (World), U (In-Universe), R (Reader), D (Design).

Against W (World): ambiguous — "world" could mean the real world or the story world.

Against Canon: correctly identified as a problem — confuses with FiM/EaW source canon.

For U (In-Universe): precise and unambiguous. Clearly marks "assertions about the fictional universe's objective reality." U is clean as a single letter.

For R (Reader): simple and precise. "Designed for the reader" is exactly what R tracks contain. Better than D because D could be confused with "diegetic" or "design" as a process term. R immediately signals the audience.

Recommendation: U and R. U+tracks assert in-universe truth. R+tracks design reader experience. The pair U/R is clean, memorable, and unambiguous within your framework. When writing a note you ask: "Am I asserting what is true in this universe (U) or what the reader should experience/conclude (R)?"

The 10-track framework with final labels
Using: O/E (Ontology/Events), Est/Em (Established/Emergent), S/L (Subject/Link), U/R (Universe/Reader)

Track	Name	Display Question
O+Est+S+U	Psychological Foundation	What is objectively true about this subject's psychology, structure, and epistemic limits as the settled reference reality?
E+Est+S+U	Historical Record	What objectively happened to this subject before the reader's entry point, and in what chronological sequence?
O+Em+S+U	Arc Trajectory	How does this subject's psychology/ontology change through the dramatized narrative from formation to end state?
E+Em+S+U	Event Architecture	What events involving this subject are to be staged directly across all planned narratives, as opposed to referenced?
O+Est+L+U	Psychological Disclosure in Scene	What specific portion of the settled psychological truth becomes accessible to the reader through this scene?
E+Est+L+U	Historical Disclosure in Scene	What specific prior event in this subject's history is disclosed to the reader through this scene?
O+Em+L+U	Character Development in Scene	What change in this subject's psychology or ontology is objectively occurring in this scene?
E+Em+L+U	Events in Scene	What is this subject objectively doing and what objectively happens to them in this scene?
S+R	Project-wide Reader Design	Starting from the reader's trope assumptions and prior-source knowledge, how should their understanding of and feelings toward this subject evolve across the whole project?
L+R	Scene Reader Experience	Given everything happening in this scene with this subject (disclosures, events, development), what should the reader feel, understand, or conclude, and what device achieves it?

I may have led you to oversimplify the framework by providing only Chrysalis as an example, because Chrysalis is not directly in the main story. Applejack never meets her face to face; she escapes as a coward in the end which is a thematic resonance point in and of itself. I think my implicit plans

May 13

Examined narrative framework tensions and character-type variations systematically
Examined narrative framework tensions and character-type variations systematically
On whether NE+S+R collapses into Themes
Evidence that they are the same thing:

The reader's experience of a character's arc IS the thematic argument at work. "By the end of Applejack's arc, the reader should feel that reluctant leadership produces more legitimate authority than sought power" is both an NE+S+R specification AND an articulation of the thematic proposition. You cannot cleanly separate them. Writing NE+S+R forces you to articulate what the arc means — which is exactly what a thematic proposition does. If you have both, you are writing the same claim in two places under different labels.

In v1, Themes were collecting evidence that should have lived on subject tracks. If you correct that, Theme entities may have little left to hold.

Evidence that they are different things:

A thematic proposition is universal. "Power is a burden to be carried, not a prize to be seized" is a claim about power in general, not about Applejack specifically. AJ's NE+S+R note describes the reader's experience of one character's arc. Celestia's arc demonstrates the same theme from the antagonist's side. Trimmel demonstrates it from the cautionary side. The Theme entity is the place where you track whether all three demonstrations exist, whether the antithesis has been given sufficient force, and whether the argument has been made rather than merely asserted.

Without Theme entities, you have no architectural home for: "the antithesis of this proposition is X, embodied by Y character, and it must be defeated through the materialist logic of Z rather than by authorial fiat." That design work is trans-character — it lives above any individual subject's tracks.

The tension's core:

NE+S+R is character-specific reader experience design. Theme entities hold the dialectical architecture of an argument that multiple characters collectively prove. These overlap at the level of individual notes ("this character's arc should make the reader feel X") but diverge at the architectural level ("across all characters, the argument structure requires A, B, and C"). The question is whether that architectural level justifies a separate entity type.

On whether Themes should be entities or views
Evidence for Themes as views (tags on notes, no entity notes):

The WorldDate parallel is exact. WorldDate is a field on notes, and a timeline view cross-cuts by that field. A ThemeId could be a field on L+R notes, and a theme view could cross-cut by that field. This eliminates double-writing entirely. Evidence lives once, on subject tracks, and the theme view aggregates it. The data model is simpler, no Cartesian explosion, no question of "what kind of notes does a Theme entity hold?"

The v1 problem of themes collecting content they shouldn't hold disappears because Theme entities hold nothing — they are just labels.

Evidence for Themes as entities with planning content:

The architectural design work — "what is the antithesis, which characters embody it, how must it fail on its own terms" — has no home in a pure-tags model. This work is not a property of any individual note. It requires a dedicated planning document. If Themes are just tags, the dialectical design becomes either homeless or shoved into a character's S+R notes where it doesn't belong.

Additionally: without theme entities, you cannot easily check whether you have deposited ENOUGH evidence. "Have I shown the antithesis failing?" requires looking at the argument structure from above the evidence, which requires some place to hold that structure.

Middle position:

Theme entities exist but hold only architectural content: the proposition, the antithesis, and a single planning note specifying what evidence must be deposited and who must demonstrate which side. No evidence notes on Theme entities. Evidence lives entirely on subject L+R notes tagged with ThemeId. Theme view shows that evidence as a read-only cross-cut. Theme entity's planning note is design specification, not evidence repository.

This maintains the subject-centric data principle while preserving the dialectical design function.

On whether the framework is over-engineered
Evidence that it is:

You have been designing the classification system for multiple conversation sessions without yet classifying a single note. A framework you understand completely but use slowly beats a framework you are still designing. The 300,000 words of v1 content is waiting. Every hour spent on framework design is an hour not spent on the world bible compilation that was the stated goal.

The 10-track framework is already more complex than most working writers use for any story. Most writers manage without ANY formal framework and produce great work. There is no evidence that you need 10 distinct track types to write TLTT well. You might need 4 or 5.

Some of the tracks may empirically collapse when you try to use them. O+NE+S+U and E+NE+S+U might collapse in practice because the event architecture is inseparable from the arc trajectory for many characters. L+R might naturally contain everything the separate PE+L+R and NE+L+R distinctions were trying to separate.

Evidence that the complexity is warranted:

You are attempting something specific: a multi-story fanfiction project built on materialist historicism with an explicitly designed reveal structure, prequel recontextualization, and thematic argumentation. This is not a standard writing project. The framework is not solving abstract classification problems — it is trying to manage 300,000 words of raw notes into a form that will support selective AI-assisted context queries AND eventually inform prose design across multiple narratives.

The distinctions between U and R tracks were identified empirically from your v1 data, not invented theoretically. The v1 problem (notes that mixed canonical truth with reader experience design) caused real downstream problems. The framework exists to solve that problem.

The project also has explicit prequel-recontextualization ambitions that most frameworks do not address because most stories are single-narrative. The PE/Established vs NE/Emergent distinction exists specifically because you need to design the reveal structure in TLTT AND the dramatic irony structure in prequels simultaneously, and these require different planning modes.

What the evidence suggests:

The framework is probably not over-engineered for what you are trying to do, but it may be over-theorized before being empirically tested. The difference matters. Over-engineered means the complexity is real but unnecessary. Over-theorized means you have built a theoretically complete system without verifying it holds under practical use.

The Applejack exercise was proposed precisely to answer this. The reason it keeps being deferred is that each session surfaces new conceptual questions that feel urgent to resolve before starting. That pattern is itself a signal. At some point the framework must be used to validate itself, and no amount of further theoretical refinement substitutes for that.

On the character type matrix revealing framework limits
The types you enumerated are genuinely distinct and stress-test different parts of the framework:

Chrysalis (PE-rich, no direct TLTT presence, prequel NE): Tests whether PE reveal design works without the character being a scene participant in the main story. Her PE+S+R plan must operate entirely through secondary evidence — POVs of other characters, references, consequences — not through direct scenes with her as subject.

Celestia (PE and NE in TLTT, no prequel): Tests whether the framework handles a character whose complete arc lives in one narrative. All tracks are TLTT-scoped. No narrative tag complexity. The simplest case.

AJ's Parents (all content in prequel, face-to-face in TLTT but unchanged there): Tests whether a character can appear in TLTT as essentially a PE-only subject — present but not developing. Their NE tracks would be empty for TLTT, which is informative rather than a gap.

Equestrian Republic (NE only, no PE): Tests whether an entity can exist in the framework with the entire Established half empty. This is the canonical case for a created concept that has no existence prior to the narrative that creates it.

Minette (everything): The maximal case. PE revealed in TLTT, NE changes in TLTT, rich prequel where PE-for-TLTT becomes NE-for-prequel. If the framework handles Minette cleanly, it likely handles all other cases. If it struggles on Minette, the complexity is real.

What this matrix suggests: the framework cannot be validated by any single example. Chrysalis validated one pattern. Applejack would validate another. Minette would validate the maximal case. The Equestrian Republic would validate the NE-only case. Testing on only one or two of these cannot confirm the framework is complete.

On the second problem: fragmentation vs. subject-centrism
Your diagnosis is accurate. v1 had two problems: mixing claim types within notes (addressed by the U/R distinction) and fragmenting content across entity types (Story Threads holding reveal plans, Themes holding evidence). The framework progressively moved content to subject tracks. But the question of what non-subject entities should hold is not fully resolved.

The evidence points toward: subject tracks hold all content; non-subject entities hold only structural/architectural specifications.

Story Threads hold the cross-character question the reader tracks (TwiJack, Perception of Changelings). They do not hold evidence or reveal plans — those live on character L+R notes.

Theme entities hold the dialectical architecture. They do not hold evidence — that lives on subject L+R notes with ThemeId tags. The theme view is read-only cross-cut.

Timeline view is read-only cross-cut by WorldDate on U track notes.

Under this model, the data model is: Subject (with its tracks), PlotPoint (with its tracks), PlotPointSubjectLink (with its tracks), and a small set of structural/architectural entities (Theme, StoryThread) that hold only design specifications, not content.

Whether this fully resolves the fragmentation problem or introduces new ones is something that can only be determined by trying to classify real notes under this model.

A correction on Minette: in TLTT chronologically she changes. She has character development after 1011. That's a U fact. But, the change is observed by AJ. When she has changes in her prequel, she is the POV character. But, it cannot be just pov character vs non pov character, as clean as that sounds. That is because AJ is the pov character but her pre 1011 truth about her cutie mark being a rejection of her parents and feeling small at Mount Aris is deliberately hidden from the reader despite AJ being the pov character (through paralipsis) so that the reveal comes at the most important point. Do maybe PE vs NE for R (reader experience) is about whether the change is delivered through FID or through external delivery/being told. For PE on Chrysalis backstory and psychology truth in TLTT, it is all told about Chrysalis; she doesn't appear face to face. For NE on Chrysalis in her prequel, it is shown through FID with the reader inhabiting her. For NE on AJ's changes the reader inhabits her in TLTT. For PE on revealing AJ's backstory and core wound, although AJ is the POV character, during these moments the reader does NOT inhabit her. AJ speaks the past wound to others in dialogue which is how the reader learns it as if it's an external look; they do NOT learn that she felt small at Mount Aris by experiencing her internal thoughts about it through FID. Same for the ration can: the reader doesn't read her thought that it's her parents' can being more effective than traditional farming for war. They see her scrunching at the can. Then she says in dialogue later how her parents are industrialists (actually we are shown her parents alive through Rarity's POV in a chapter that doesn't have AJ in it).

Minette's PE+E backstory is her telling AJ about it after 1011. Minette's NE+E history is the reader feeling it as her in her prequel before 1011 (1011 is the start of TLTT, if that wasn't clear).

But fid is meaningless for non characters, so this can't be the only answer. It's probably still only a subset of the broader first principles.

I think secondary evidence vs primary evidence is getting closer to the heart of PE (secondary) vs NE (primary).

Or, PE and NE are strictly defined by what reader experience they yield? PE yielded evolving reader understanding. NE yielded feelings about the events and ontology changes that are being dramatized. I'm not sure if these are the same or not.

Maybe there's another thing to consider about showing or telling. Reveal sequences are "Telling". Truth is being told to characters and therefore readers. Or the character is discovering a new truth about a world law (like Twilight theorizing a more complete explanation of the charitostatic effect). This is okay to be told because the content isn't the takeaway. But for a character changing their worldview, this shouldn't be told, but rather shown, so the reader doesn't feel like they are being told to change their world view and instead come to the conclusion themselves?

Chrysalis's prequel isn't going to tell the reader to that systems are the root cause. It shows the reader that the economic system is the root cause, not an evil dictator, because the story shows exactly how Chrysalis became an evil dictator because of the system she was fighting. 

AJ's character development arc isn't going to tell the reader "you should be strong and merciful". It is going to show how she went from a "little pony" to a "Lioness" and then to a president who rehabilates opponents who are willing (and not sadistic in bad faith). It shows how being merciful is more powerful than being extractive.

V1 was good enough until it wasn't. I need a framework which won't require another massive refactor of the data before I double the 300k words into 600k or something like that.

May 13

Synthesized primary/secondary evidence framework with stability concerns for scaling
Synthesized primary/secondary evidence framework with stability concerns for scaling
The primary/secondary evidence insight: how far it goes
Your observation is right and it generalizes past FID. The broader principle is epistemic distance between the reader and the truth:

PE content reaches the reader through mediation — a character describes it, dialogue reveals it, paralipsis signals it through absence, behavior is observed from outside. The reader's job is cognitive: updating their model of what is true. The content is filtered through some narrative layer between the truth and the reader's direct experience.

NE content reaches the reader through direct witness — they are present as the truth forms. FID is one mechanism for this, but third-person dramatic scenes where the reader watches events unfold with no mediating narrator also achieve it. The reader's job is experiential and inferential: they arrive at conclusions rather than receive information.

For the Charitostatic Effect: when it is described and theorized about (characters explaining how it works, Twilight's paper), that is PE delivery — secondary evidence. When the reader witnesses the trench scene holding under artillery because the bond is real, that is NE delivery — the law is demonstrated directly through events. No FID needed.

The FID point is a subset: FID is the primary evidence mechanism for interior psychological truth specifically. Dramatic scene events are the primary evidence mechanism for external truth. Both are NE delivery. Both contrast with the mediated telling that characterizes PE delivery.

Why these are not the same as U-track PE/NE but are derived from them
In U tracks, PE means the content already existed before the reader's entry point. NE means the content is being formed during the reader's encounter. These are claims about the story world's temporal structure.

In R tracks, the PE/NE distinction is a consequence of the U-track distinction: because PE content already existed, the author must find ways to deliver it retrospectively — and retrospective delivery is inherently mediated (secondary evidence). Because NE content is forming right now, the author can deliver it through direct witness (primary evidence).

But the correlation is not perfect, and this is exactly what the AJ paralipsis example shows. AJ's core wound is U-track PE — it existed before TLTT begins. But the AUTHOR CHOOSES to withhold the FID access that would deliver it directly, using paralipsis to make it secondary evidence even though she is the POV character. This is a deliberate R-track design decision: the PE content will be revealed through dialogue, through behavior observed from outside, through another character's POV — not through AJ thinking about it.

The implication: the R-track design for PE content is the question "by what secondary mechanism will this truth be delivered, and at what moment?" The answer for AJ is: not through her internal voice. The reader learns she felt small at Mount Aris when she says it to someone, or when Rarity observes it, not when AJ thinks it.

This means PE+S+R for AJ would specify: "the reader will not have direct FID access to the core wound; it will be delivered through dialogue and external observation, at the point in the narrative where it recontextualizes what came before most powerfully."

And NE+S+R for AJ: "the reader inhabits her contentment at the machine gun through FID, which is how they perceive the hiding without her perceiving it; the imposter syndrome scenes use her internal voice to show what she believes while the reader concludes something different."

These are genuinely different design tasks. The R track has to specify both what mechanism delivers PE truth AND what mechanism delivers NE truth, and for a character like AJ these mechanisms differ deliberately.

On showing vs. telling and why this has to be the principle
Your formulation is precise: PE content is told (even when told artfully — through paralipsis, dialogue, external observation rather than authorial statement), and NE content must be shown, because if you TELL the reader a character's worldview has changed, or TELL them what conclusion to reach from that change, you are preaching. The reader can resist a told conclusion. They cannot resist a conclusion they arrived at themselves from evidence.

This is why the thematic argument cannot live in NE+S+R as an explicit statement. "Power is a burden, not a prize" cannot appear in a character's reader experience design note as something to convey. It can only appear as: "the reader should, from witnessing these specific scenes in this specific sequence, arrive independently at the conclusion that reluctant leaders are more trustworthy." The experience design specifies the conditions for independent arrival, not the conclusion to be transmitted.

The PE/NE distinction in R tracks is therefore also a tell/show constraint:

PE+R notes describe how truths are delivered through secondary evidence (how they are told, even if artfully)
NE+R notes describe the conditions under which the reader arrives at conclusions themselves (how they are shown, through primary evidence)
Writing them separately keeps these two cognitive modes from bleeding into each other. When you are writing PE+R design, you are thinking: "at what moment, through what character's voice or external observation, does this truth surface?" When you are writing NE+R design, you are thinking: "what does the reader witness, and what can they not help but conclude from it?"

The stability concern: what this implies
You have identified the real constraint: the framework must be stable enough that 300k words becoming 600k does not require another structural refactor. This is an engineering question, not a philosophical one.

The risk is not that the framework is theoretically wrong. The risk is that the distinctions are too fine to maintain consistently under volume. When you are writing a scene at 3am and need to create a link note, will you reliably know whether what you're writing is PE+L+U or NE+L+R? If the answer is sometimes no, the framework will drift back toward v1 mixing under pressure.

The distinction that matters most for stability is U vs R, because it is the one that v1 violated most damagingly. That distinction is clean: am I asserting something true in the story world, or am I designing what the reader should feel? This question is answerable in under five seconds for any note.

The O vs E distinction in U tracks is also clean: am I asserting psychology/structure or am I asserting events/chronology?

The PE vs NE distinction is where it gets harder. Under the corrected understanding (PE = content that already existed, delivered through secondary evidence; NE = content being formed, delivered through primary evidence), the question for each note is: "am I writing about how existing truth reaches the reader, or am I writing about the reader witnessing truth being created?" This is answerable but requires more thought.

For stability, the framework probably needs the distinctions to hold at the note level without requiring extended deliberation. The AJ paralipsis example suggests the PE/NE distinction in R tracks is genuinely useful and meaningful — the decision to withhold FID access from her core wound is a different design task from the decision to USE FID for her imposter syndrome scenes. These are different notes that produce different prose instructions. If they were in the same track they would blur.

The stability risk is specifically: S+R becoming a new everything-dump. The single biggest threat to the framework is the Project-wide Reader Design track absorbing thematic architecture, social commentary, analogical references, and character-specific reader experience design all in one pile. The display question for S+R must be tight enough to exclude content that belongs elsewhere.

What remains genuinely open
Whether the U-track PE/NE distinction generates enough different content at the R-track level to warrant separate PE+R and NE+R tracks, or whether one unified L+R and one unified S+R track can hold both through note-level tagging, is something that cannot be answered theoretically. The AJ example suggests yes, they generate different content. But whether you would actually maintain the separation consistently under 600k words is an empirical question only the classification exercise will answer.

The showing/telling insight gives you a practical test for your own writing: if a note you're writing for an R track contains instructions about how to tell the reader something, it's PE delivery. If it contains conditions under which the reader will arrive at a conclusion themselves, it's NE delivery. Whether those two types of notes need separate tracks or can coexist in one track with different orientations is what you are trying to determine.

I didn't mean to say the universal thematic proposition lives in L+D. I said the evidence for the proposition lives in L+D notes. Which proposition it is evidence for should be tagged. 

I guess I am still not clear on what should go in a S+R track, an L+R track, or a plot point track. But there should be clearly defined boundaries. 

The PE and NE versions seem to have contradictory answers. PE+S+W asserts established truth. PE+S+R looks at that truth and decides reveals. But does it talk about how the reader feels? Hold on, maybe it's PE+L+W that decides what to reveal, not PE+S+R. Actually they kind of are doing the same thing? Or not? Please disambiguate. PE+L+R is definitely about what I am designing for the reader's opinion to change. This seems to me solidly a "showing" mode. I am not telling the reader how to change their opinion; like I am not telling the reader that Chrysalis is not a cartoon villain. I am giving them evidence that she is not a cartoon villain by telling about her past actions. I am not telling them that Applejack thinks industry kills the soul. I am showing it by telling her real cutie mark story.

In the NE+L+R track, I am not telling the reader that being merciful is more effective for economic output than being extractive. I am showing them evidence that being merciful/cooperative produced a stronger war machine than the extractive one. I am not telling the reader that the system produced Chrysalis the tyrant. I am showing how the system led to her choosing to be a tyrant (her Prequel starts with her with a noble goal of ending the hive tribal wars, trying to be nice and harmonic but finding it insulting; it shows how the noble goal became tyranny, not tyranny from cruelty).

Seems like both the L+R tracks should be detailing what I want the reader to conclude without telling them. This then informs the plot point on what needs to be delivered through showing instead of being told, but it didn't speak to how.
