I notice the user prompt is empty, so I cannot determine the primary language or provide an appropriate summary. Please provide the user prompt so I can create an accurate summary in the correct language
Yes, inferring psychology from stated events is exactly the challenge
That is the central craft problem. The framework specifies what should happen and gives the author a design specification for the plot point. The actual execution — finding the specific gesture, choice, or absence that encodes the psychological truth without stating it — is where writing skill lives. The framework can tell you that O+En+L+W holds "AJ is accepting the burden of leadership" and that this cannot go on the page. It cannot tell you that the right encoding is how long her hand rests on the uniform before her expression settles. That discovery requires knowing AJ intimately.

O is not "ontology" — it is "cannot be stated without preaching"
You have identified the actual principle. The O/E axis was correct but the reasoning behind it was incomplete. O doesn't mean Layer 3 specifically. It means: the content is thematically loaded enough that stating it directly would constitute delivering the conclusion to the reader instead of letting them arrive at it.

Layer 3 (character psychology) is always O because the core wound and transformation arc ARE the thematic proposition about human nature. Stating "AJ's imposter syndrome has resolved into honest acceptance of leadership" is stating the hopepunk argument about reluctant authority — you cannot state it without preaching.

Layer 1 (world physics mechanics) is different. When Twilight explains that earth pony magic scales with both pride and care, she is stating a mechanism. The mechanism itself is not the theme — it is the physical substrate that makes the theme demonstrable. The reader still has to do the second inference: what does this mechanism mean? "Friendship is literally magic in this world, not metaphorically" is the meaning they extract, and that meaning is never stated. So:

World law mechanics, once characters discover them: E (can be stated, goes on page)
What the world law mechanics mean for the themes: O (in reader's head, cannot be stated)
This holds for technology allegory too. Luna Nova Rifle mechanics could in principle be stated technically (it drains magic via the same mechanism as the love harvesters). The allegory — that AI can be used for liberation or predation depending on user psychology — cannot be stated. The reader must infer it. So technology allegory = O, technology mechanics = potentially E.

The working rule: if stating the content would be delivering the thematic conclusion directly, it is O. If it is factual substrate that the reader draws conclusions FROM, it is E.

The in-universe psychologist: a spectrum from full statement to paralipsis
You have identified a genuine narrative technique. Another character can perform the first inference step — they observe behavior and state their psychological reading — which puts that reading on the page as E content. The reader then does only one inference step to meaning.

This creates a spectrum of options for any given scene:

Option A — POV character states correct inference in FID "Rarity looked at the still white form and suddenly understood: Celestia wasn't numb. She was holding herself rigid so the whole country didn't shatter with her." This goes on the page. Reader does 1 inference to meaning. High efficiency. Rewards less attentive readers. Reduces impact for attentive ones.

Option B — POV character states wrong inference in FID (misdirection) "Rarity left the suite with a hollow feeling — Celestia was hopelessly out of touch, clinging to a dead era while her ponies bled." This goes on the page. Reader accepts the wrong inference as true. Later La disclosure corrects it (double correction: Rarity was wrong AND the truth is much more interesting). Maximum impact when the correction comes.

Option C — Partial hint with paralipsis (the intermediate technique) Rarity's FID describes the surface observations. She stops pressing, but her FID does NOT articulate why she stopped. The reader sees the behavior (she stopped) without getting the inference (she sensed something she couldn't name). The reader is left with an unresolved question that plants a seed.

Option D — Pure paralipsis Rarity's FID records only her observations. No inference. Maximum trust in reader. Maximum impact. Hardest to calibrate.

Applied to Scene 7.3
The v1 notes already made the design decision: Option B. Rarity leaves believing Celestia is "hopelessly out-of-touch and stubbornly clinging to a dead era." The materialist truth is completely withheld. The O+La+L+W note for Celestia in this scene is: "The Statue persona is Celestia using every ounce of willpower to project calm because she believes any visible crack in her composure will trigger national panic. She isn't indifferent — she is terrified and managing that terror through enforced stillness."

Rarity's wrong inference goes on the page as E+La+L+W. The La+L+R note is: prior belief (via Rarity's read) = aloof exhausted monarch bound by red tape; revised conclusion (when truth is revealed later) = a leader who understood her most important job was to be the eye of the hurricane precisely when she felt least capable of it.

The existing note "Celestia should exhibit similar symptoms to Pinkie... but Rarity can figure it out since she has an eye for detail, so she stops pressing Celestia" is actually the Option C partial hint embedded within the Option B misdirection. Rarity senses something is wrong but does not name what. Her FID carries the surface read (out-of-touch monarch) but her behavior (stopping, not pushing further) is the behavioral evidence that something in her read didn't fully satisfy. The plot point's O+En Translation Notes for this scene should specify: "Rarity stops pressing — her FID must NOT articulate why. The stopping is the hint. The reader should sense the gap between her stated conclusion and her behavior without Rarity closing that gap."

For Luna in this scene, the analysis note already has the O+La content: "Luna is very aware of her own folly. It wasn't random external corruption — it was a conscious choice to pull ambitions out of dreams that led to the corruption and nova event." This should NOT appear in Rarity's FID at this point. What Rarity observes is the fitful sleep, the non-ethereal mane. The pity Rarity feels is correct as far as it goes but incomplete. Later scenes (the white peace confrontation) will provide the fuller O+La+L+W for Luna that recontextualizes Rarity's surface pity into something more specific. The La+L+R for Luna at this scene: prior belief (pitiable, broken by war) → eventual revised understanding (suffering as the specific residue of perfect self-knowledge and full accountability).

The design question you raise at the end — should Rarity's inferences be held back or put in FID, and are they misdirections? — is already answered by the existing v1 design. Rarity's incorrect inference IS on the page in FID (Option B). The correct deeper truth is held in the O+La+L+W note for Celestia and disclosed in a later scene. What the track structure now gives you is the ability to explicitly mark which layer is which — so that when writing the prose, future-you knows that "Rarity leaves believing Celestia is hopelessly out-of-touch" goes in the FID (E+La+L+W via POV character proxy), while "the Statue persona is terror management" stays in the O+La+L+W note and off the page until the designated revelation.

Reader is supposed to infer that from observing Celestia through Rarity, where rarity stops pressing. In the next chapter, Rarity and Applejack talk over the radio. In this radio call Rarity says "she is like a statue". So in the first scene with rarity pov it is two inference. You are capable of following the constraints if i give them but the takes up context and compute, and by the time all constraints are in, one might as well have written it themselves. So it's not about "can or can't replicate" but about comparative advantage, whether the tool is more efficient than the human brain and lived experience. That isn't really talked about with full nuance in political discourse or even technical discourse.

May 15

Validated design choices and reconciled naming conventions for thematic argumentation tracks
Validated design choices and reconciled naming conventions for thematic argumentation tracks
The Rarity scene correction validates the v2 design
Exactly right. Scene 7.3 was always intended as 2-inference: reader observes Rarity stopping the pressure and draws the "out of touch" conclusion themselves — it was never supposed to be stated in her FID. The radio call next chapter ("she is like a statue") is the E+P+La+L note going on the page explicitly, reinforcing the seeded misdirection before the white peace correction arrives. V1 had no way to mark that distinction, so the analysis defaulted to assuming stated FID. The whole value of the track separation is exactly this: marking off "this conclusion belongs in the reader's head, do not state it in prose" as a design artifact rather than leaving it to memory and instinct.

On hooves and the comparative advantage point
The hooves error is a clean illustration. The constraint isn't "Claude doesn't know Applejack has hooves" — it could know that with proper context. The problem is the accumulation: species, physical form, voice calibration, psychological state at this specific scene, what's been established in prior chapters, what should not yet be stated, what inferential work the sentence is supposed to do. By the time all constraints are active, the authorial load required to specify them consumes more than the prose itself would have. The comparative advantage lies in auditing design against stated R targets, flagging coherence gaps, and generating candidate framings — not in producing the final prose. That observation deserves more honest treatment in discourse about AI and creative work.

What the story planner is actually for
The crystallization is correct with one small precision. The H/P/La/Em track system is for making readers infer rather than be told — which in this project is identical to thematic argumentation because everything worth showing is thematically load-bearing. Character psychology must be inferred because it IS the argument about human nature. Organization binding logic must be inferred because it IS the argument about what holds communities together. Political systems must be inferred because they ARE the political conclusions you want readers to reach. The exceptions — world law mechanics that can go on the page once stated — are the things that are mechanism rather than argument.

So: H+W tracks and M/R tracks = thematic argumentation machinery. P+W tracks = page content that serves both thematic inference and operational plot. Metatextual/garden notes = craft notes about pacing, emotional timing, thrilling moments, reminders — the things that live below the threshold of the track system and emerge from writing.

The plot point synthesizes all of this. The prose executes the plot point plus everything the track system correctly declined to specify: voice, warmth, humor, rhythm, the actual choice of which hoof rests where and for how long.

Renaming: H and P are better than O and E
H (in their Head) and P (Page) are better axis labels because they describe what the author does with each track rather than the type of content. The former names — Ontology/Events — were at best a useful approximation that became increasingly misleading as the framework extended beyond character subjects. H and P are instruction labels for the plot point designer: "H content never goes in the prose; P content does."

Track notation: H+La+S+W, P+La+S+W, H+En+S+W, P+En+S+W at subject scope. H+La+L+W, P+La+L+W, H+En+L+W, P+En+L+W at link scope.

The R axis: what to rename it
"Reader Experience" was too broad — that described the whole link, not specifically the R tracks. What R tracks contain is: the designed inference target — what the author wants the reader to infer, which they must arrive at themselves rather than be handed.

M for Meaning is cleaner than T for Theme Evidence because not every inference is strictly thematic — some are character understanding, some are world understanding, some are relationship reading. They're all about meaning being derived, which is why M fits.

The one nuance: La+M tracks need to specify not just the inference target but also the prior belief that will clash with the disclosure. That's not additional content requiring a different name — it's context that the track's display question should require. The La+M display question asks two things: "what does the reader currently hold as true about this subject, and what should they understand after this disclosure?" The En+M display question only asks the second. Same track, richer specification for La.

Track notation: La+S+M, En+S+M, La+L+M, En+L+M.

The full revised axis summary
Axis	Old Label	New Label	What it means
Page layer	E (Events)	P (Page)	Content stated in prose
Head layer	O (Ontology)	H (Head)	Content formed through reader inference, never stated
Revelation	La (Latent)	La	Prior truth disclosed; reader catches up
Witnessing	En (Emergent)	En	Present truth forming; reader watches
World truth	W	W	Author's canonical reference
Inference target	R (Reader)	M (Meaning)	What the reader should arrive at without being told
On the Latent vs Emergent/Enacted side, Latent required prior knowledge to clash. But actually Emergent content can form the prior knowledge too through misdirection. So they are intertwined. Are they still separate tracks on the L scope or not? I guess they are because it's different mechanisms for inference. Prior knowledge vs new knowledge (either in the page or inferred) equals new inference on theme is the La. For En it is sensory details (and the inference from it) and events equal new inference on theme. Are these truely different or not?

I believe R should be T for Theme specifically because what R was in the old classification was NOT all inference targets. It was only the inference target on themes. Inference target for character psychology (which is a world truth) was in what we were calling O+L+W (either La or En) which we are now calling H+L+W because it's in their head. World truth inferences are not the same as thematic proposition inferences. The world truth inference is the first inference step. The thematic argumentation is the second inference step of this two step process (thematic argumentation is the one inference when events are stated on the page. The distinction is it is always the last step, the end goal). Is this description accurate and most helpful, trumping all past design attempts?

If so, then what was previously the 4 values from orthogonal O vs E and R vs W become the trinary axis of P (page), H (head, intermediate inference of world truth), and T (theme, also in their head). This is for Links with are about "reader experience" informing the plot point. On the Subject level there will needs to be an O vs E separation about history versus what the subject is? (Psychology, binding logic, technology, political rules, world laws)

And also, I am now questioning if allegorical is strictly separate from this all. This whole system for thematic argumentation seems to need allegory? Is it really a different cognitive mode or not? Need to decide based on pros and cons. Remember that character psychology is a reflection of real world human nature. The political systems I've constructed are allegories. The technology in universe are allegories. The World Laws that I made up serve the theme, they kind of are allegories too, although perhaps this is more about themes being universal rather than the magical physics I invented being allegories; they are allegories by true of serving a universal theme?

May 15

Validated trinary P/H/T framework and reconciled allegorical elements systematically
Validated trinary P/H/T framework and reconciled allegorical elements systematically
La and En at L scope: still separate, despite the chaining
The observation that En can seed the prior belief that La later clashes with is accurate and important. Scene 7.3 (En: Rarity observes and the reader infers "out of touch") seeds the belief. The white peace scene (La: Celestia discloses the Statue persona truth) clashes with it. They chain.

But at any individual scene, the mechanism is different:

En mechanism: Page content accumulates → reader builds inference from pattern and sensory detail. No prior belief required. The reader is constructing understanding from scratch.

La mechanism: Disclosed fact on page + reader's existing belief (from wherever it came — tropes, seeded En, or prior La) → clash → revised understanding. The reader is revising an existing position.

The plot point instructions for each remain distinct. La needs: deliver the fact clearly, ensure the prior belief is active enough to clash (do you need to restimulate it before the disclosure lands?). En needs: design the staging and behavioral proxy so the reader can build the inference from what's on the page. These are different authorial tasks even when the two mechanisms chain together across chapters.

They stay separate at L scope.

The P/H/T trinary is the sharpest formulation yet
This is more precise than the binary and corrects an error the framework carried for a long time.

What we were calling R (now renamed) was never all inference targets. It was specifically the final thematic inference — the proposition the reader should arrive at. What we were calling H+L+W (formerly O+L+W) was the intermediate world truth inference — what the reader must first conclude about the subject itself before they can derive the theme.

The chain in every scene:

P (page) → H (intermediate: world truth inferred) → T (final: thematic proposition derived)

For character development: "AJ takes the uniform" (P) → "she has accepted the burden of leadership" (H, world truth about AJ's psychology) → "reluctant leaders produce legitimate authority" (T, the thematic conclusion)

For organization: "EEEE! votes to route their ammo repairs through the Republic rather than profit" (P) → "this organization's binding logic is humbled ambition channeled productively" (H, world truth about the organization) → "collective civic purpose outperforms extractive individual incentive" (T)

For technology: "traumatized ponies misuse the Nova Rifles to drain their neighbors" (P) → "the draining mechanism, originally volunteer-based, is being used coercively by people shaped by coercion" (H, world truth about the technology's behavior in this context) → "technology is an accelerant for the user's psychology, neither good nor evil in itself" (T)

The reason T could never be stated is not just that it's "the reader's experience" but specifically that it is the thematic conclusion the author is arguing for. Stating it is preaching. H could not be stated either, but for a slightly different reason: stating the intermediate world truth inference (AJ accepted leadership, the binding logic is X, the technology is being used coercively) still constitutes telling rather than showing — just at the world truth level rather than the thematic level.

Both H and T are in the reader's head. H is intermediate (world-truth level). T is final (meaning level). Neither goes on the page.

Does the S level still need the nature/history separation?
Yes, but the H/P language applies less cleanly at S scope. At S scope, all tracks are the author's canonical reference — nothing in them directly goes to the reader. What matters at S level is the content type:

Nature (what the subject IS): psychology, binding logic, political mechanics, world law mechanics, technology behavior. This is the reference the author draws from when writing H+L+W notes at each scene. "Chrysalis's full psychological architecture" — the canonical truth that all scene-level H inferences must be consistent with.
History (what happened to the subject): the chronological record. This is the reference the author draws from when writing P+L+W disclosure notes across scenes.
For the Emergent side:

Arc/Trajectory (how the nature changes through the story)
Staged Events (what events are dramatized)
The four S+W tracks remain. The H/P renaming is most useful at L scope where the distinction "does this go on the page or form in the reader's head" directly instructs the plot point designer. At S scope the question is "what type of content is this" — nature vs history — which is the original O/E distinction. Renaming those S-level labels from O/E to H/P would be misleading because at S level neither track is literally "on the page" — they're both the author's private reference.

The allegorical track: mostly absorbed, with a small residue
Your observation is correct: the entire P/H/T system IS thematic argumentation, and thematic argumentation in this project IS allegory. Character psychology is human-nature allegory. Organization binding logic is civic-virtue allegory. Political systems are political allegory. Technology behavior is technology allegory. The world laws serve the themes, making them instruments of universal truth rather than physics for its own sake. The thematic argumentation tracks and the allegorical function are the same thing.

This creates two distinct uses of "allegorical" content:

Use 1 — In-universe allegorical truth: "The Luna Nova Rifle drains the user's own magic when fired" is an in-universe fact. That it maps to AI weapons requiring human expertise and intent is the allegory. The in-universe fact goes in P+L+W. What the reader should infer about the technology's nature goes in H+L+W. The thematic conclusion about AI and human psychology goes in T+L. All fully handled by P/H/T. No separate track needed.

Use 2 — Author's meta-awareness of the real-world connection: "This maps to Pétain." "This is the social capital literature." "The Coltbert Reforms are the New Deal seen through a griffon economy." This is the author's external frame — it exists outside the story's internal logic. It belongs in metatextual notes. It helps the author design the T level content more precisely (knowing the allegory helps calibrate what readers should conclude) but it is not itself story content.

The Allegorical track as a standalone system can be collapsed. Use 1 is fully absorbed into P/H/T. Use 2 becomes a tagged metatextual note — a field that says "this subject is an allegory for X" as design context, not a separate track structure.

One value in keeping a lightweight allegorical tag: it flags when T-level content risks being too on-the-nose. If the real-world parallel is obvious enough that a reader could identify the allegory directly before completing the T inference themselves, the design has tipped from showing to telling at the meta level. The tag is a warning signal, not a track structure.

H in their head:
intermediate world truth inference — what the reader must first conclude about the subject itself before they can derive the theme.
Maybe it should not be H but instead WI: world inference. H is WI+T, both are in their head. Any other terms that could work?
Does every single Plot Point Subject Link need a note in T? My v1 notes sometimes didn't have it, but I'm not sure if it's because it's meant to have it and was inferred or incomplete or if there can be links without a T. On one hand I said this whole track system is about theme delivery enforcement. On the other hand it's not exhaustive. So is empty T a directive to think harder about it or cut? I suppose if every link must have T, the T doesn't have to be max states existential pondering every time, it can be simple or subtle or just small supporting evidence? If little things are allowed or even common then perhaps that clearly makes an empty T a signal to cut or rethink because it means the plot point out the subject of the thing on the page is padding without weight? I guess I also have to remember that the content on the links is not exhaustive of the prose either; the stuff like humor and warmth sit side by side. Those don't belong on a link with a subject because the subject is about tracking theme arguments across the project.
Now I'm starting to think allegory should not go in the link notes for T because that could cause leakage into the plot point? Which would be telling the allegory? If I make the allegory too obvious because the allegory is there? Should the L+T note be pure universal thematic proposition, not including the intended allegory? Or does the allegory help? Or does this depend on how I would actually write? Or would that induce a writing style?

May 15

Examined labeling conventions, thematic link requirements, and allegory placement in narrative tracking
Examined labeling conventions, thematic link requirements, and allegory placement in narrative tracking
On the label: WI is cleaner than H
You identified the problem exactly. H implies "anything in their head," which includes both the intermediate world truth inference AND the final thematic conclusion. WI (World Inference) is more precise: it specifically marks the intermediate step where the reader infers a world truth from page content, before they derive the theme from that world truth.

The chain becomes: P → WI → T

Other candidates worth considering:

Wi (lowercase) — avoids the acronym-heaviness but might look odd in notation
In for Inference — but this is ambiguous; T is also inference
W1 / W2 — world truth first inference, world truth to theme second inference. Somewhat mechanical but clear
Impl for Implied — what is implied by the page content but not stated. Somewhat evocative
WI is probably the best balance: it names what it IS (a world-truth-level inference) and it distinguishes itself from T (thematic inference) without requiring explanation. Track notation reads WI+La+L+W, WI+En+L+W, etc. Not as short as H but not ambiguous.

Does every link need T
Yes, but T can be minimal. The reasoning:

You established that the track system exists specifically for thematic argumentation. A plot point subject link asserts that this subject is doing thematic work in this scene. If a subject appears without doing thematic work, it shouldn't be a formal link — it's set dressing that can be handled in the Delivery Blueprint without requiring a full link entity with tracks.

This makes empty T a clear diagnostic signal: either the thematic contribution hasn't been articulated yet (think harder) or the link shouldn't exist (cut it). Neither is "the subject is doing thematic work but doesn't need a T."

T doesn't need to be a major existential statement. A minimal T for the friendship shield scene might be: "reader accrues small additional evidence that collective care provides literal protection, consistent with the charitostatic thesis." That's one sentence of subtle supporting evidence. It's valid T.

The observation about humor and warmth clarifies the boundary: those live in prose and metatextual notes precisely BECAUSE they're not tracked through subject links. Subject links are only created when a subject is doing thematic work. If a subject appears in a scene but the only reason you'd link it is that it happens to be present — empty T confirms it doesn't belong in the formal link.

T notes should be pure universal propositions, not allegory
The concern about leakage is real and the intuition is correct.

If the T note says "AI is an accelerant for user psychology" instead of "technology is an accelerant for user psychology," the plot point designer and prose writer reads AI into the scene. This creates gravitational pull toward writing that evokes the specific real-world referent rather than the in-universe story. The language starts echoing tech discourse. The behavior of the technology becomes recognizably AI-ish in ways that signal "I'm an allegory." The reader is handed the allegorical connection rather than making it.

The allegory works precisely because the author grounds it in the story's internal logic, writes it as in-universe truth, and the reader's own experience connects it to the real world without being directed to. "The technology amplifies the wielder's psychology" is universally true — in-universe it describes the Luna Nova Rifle, and for readers who recognize the real-world parallel, the connection forms in their head (WI → T) without being stated.

So: T note = universal in-universe proposition. Metatextual note = "the allegory I'm drawing on is AI — this informs how I calibrated the T note." The metatextual note is the author's design rationale; the T note is what the reader should arrive at. They're separate.

This also means the allegorical track proposed earlier is now fully described: it belongs in metatextual notes as calibration context for the T notes, not as a track in the link system. Including it in the link system would be inviting the leakage problem you identified.

"Subject links are only created when a subject is doing thematic work" seems like an ironclad design principle that has been discovered, or it was there but finally articulated. This seems to complement something I was already doing in V1: character plot point link was not done for every scene the character is in, only when something about them was delivering something meaningful. When Characters and Themes were both subject entities that had to link to plot points, I would end up copying text or restating it between the two links. Now theme evidence is a track on the character link.

I forgot what the deal was with metatextual. Did this term become obsolete and separated into allegorical and garden notes (project notes tracking the gardening process of putting together the story design)? Are these only S-scope or also L-scope? Why or why not?

May 15

Validated design principle and disentangled metatextual into scope-specific notes
Validated design principle and disentangled metatextual into scope-specific notes
The "subject links for thematic work only" principle
This IS a foundational design principle now articulated. It was already in your v1 practice but never stated as a rule, which is why the v1 notes sometimes had the thematic content appearing in both the character link AND the theme link. The duplication happened because both links needed to say something about what the scene was arguing. Now: the T track on the character link is the theme evidence. The theme entity is only the proposition architecture and a cross-cut view of tagged T notes. One link, both purposes, no copying.

What happened to metatextual
"Metatextual" as a CognitiveMode label in the C2 framework covered author-as-project-manager content. It didn't become obsolete — it clarified into two distinct things:

Garden notes — project-level design process content: why you made a decision, what you pruned and why, design history, anti-pattern warnings ("don't let this become a lecture"), craft reminders about emotional beats or thrilling moments that don't belong in the formal tracks. These are notes to future-you about the design itself, not notes about story content.

Allegorical context — the real-world connection the author is drawing on. As established, this stays metatextual and does NOT appear in the T track because it would leak into the plot point and produce over-explicit allegory.

LinguisticExecution as a CognitiveMode was probably a mistake in C2 to include — it's the output mode (writing the prose), not a planning mode. Nothing in the story planner's note tracks is LinguisticExecution content; that's what happens when you close the planner and open a text editor.

S-scope or L-scope for garden/metatextual notes — and why
S-scope: yes. Most garden content is about the subject as a whole — design decisions that shaped who Chrysalis is, why the Coltbert Reforms took the form they did, what was pruned from EEEE!'s binding logic and why. This is project history and lives naturally on the subject entity. The real-world allegorical connection ("this maps to rent-seeking and data harvesting") also belongs here — it's the author's calibration tool for designing T notes, not the T notes themselves.

Plot point scope: yes. Scene-level craft notes — "this scene needs to breathe before the revelation," "the thrilling element is the time pressure from the artillery" — belong on the plot point's own metatextual track. These don't belong in the P/WI/T link tracks because they're craft direction, not thematic specification.

L-scope (link): no, as a full track. The link is specifically a thematic work unit. Its three content levels (P, WI, T) are clean specifications. Adding a full metatextual track on every link would treat the link as a general-purpose notes container, which is exactly what the framework is organized to prevent.

The one genuine need at L-scope is the constraint note: "hold back the WI inference from Rarity's FID in this scene." But this is handled by the absence from the P+La+L track (if Rarity's inference doesn't appear there, it doesn't go on the page) combined with the plot point's delivery blueprint and any translation notes. The "don't state X" directive flows from the track architecture itself rather than needing a separate note to say it explicitly. If the WI track says Rarity should not express her inference in FID, the plot point designer reads that as a constraint without a dedicated note explaining it.

I think I'm out of holes to poke, which is good. Please synthesize all the latest insights, point out all red herrings that felt good at the time but were supplanted and why, and crystallize a first pass suggestion on the system.

I think what would be good is a table where the rows are the tracks (across subject, link, and plot point) and the columns are the display questions (for me when I am populating the track) and the usage directives (for me when I am reading the track as input to the next thing).

Also specify the articulated, precise purpose of each component of the v2 story planner as was deliberated and concluded.

Synthesized framework, catalogued superseded approaches, architected comprehensive track system

The La+S+Reveal and En+S+Arc are in the table as the former R tracks, renamed to be more descriptive.

A few things to flag about this table:

Design Notes at subject scope is the only track with a unified display question across all subject types. Every other track has six variants. How do I get started with interpreting the v1 notes to begin populating v2? Give some methodology recommendations. The task ahead feels arduous. How would I determine a path forward?

May 16

Architected phased migration strategy prioritizing characters over comprehensive conversion
Architected phased migration strategy prioritizing characters over comprehensive conversion
The task ahead is genuinely large, but it doesn't need to be done before you can write. The key reframe: v2 notes are not a destination to migrate to — they are a tool you write when you need them for writing the story. The v1 archive doesn't get "completed" into v2; it gets referenced and superseded scene by scene, character by character.

The core methodology: just-in-time, not all-at-once
Don't attempt a full migration. Instead: populate v2 tracks for what you need to design or write next, using v1 notes as reference material rather than source text to reorganize. The v1 notes were written in the wrong cognitive modes for v2 tracks — you cannot sort them into the new structure. You write fresh v2 notes while consulting v1 as a research archive.

This is the strangler fig pattern from software: new structure grows around the old, the old becomes irrelevant gradually, and you never attempt a flag-day cutover.

The order of operations
Step 1 — Configure NoteTrackDefinitions first. This is the one-time setup task before any note-writing. Take the Grand Track Table and create the NoteTrackDefinition records in the v2 app: track name, track identifier, display question, usage directive, cognitive mode. Do this for character subjects first. This gives you the containers before you fill them.

Step 2 — Write the Foundation tracks for your three most critical characters before anything else. Applejack, Chrysalis, Twilight. Foundation+La and Historical Record for each. These are the consistency references that every link-scope note will check against. Without them, link notes have no anchor.

Write these fresh from v1. Open every v1 note for AJ and read through it all. Then close v1 and write the Foundation note in the correct cognitive mode — omniscient author, complete psychological architecture, no mixing of history and inference. The act of writing forces synthesis. Copy-pasting would just move the problem.

For Foundation, ask: who is this person at their deepest level? What is the wound, the desire, the fear, the worldview through which they interpret everything? For Historical Record, ask: what happened, in what order, that produced this person?

Step 3 — Write the Revelation Plan and Witness Arc for those same three characters. These are the project-wide reader design plans. La+S+Reveal: what does the reader believe at the start, and what is the disclosure sequence? En+S+Witness: what should the reader have understood about each transformation by the end? These are short documents — a few paragraphs each — but they are the constraint against which every future link note is written.

Step 4 — Fully populate one complete chapter. Chapter 1, scene by scene. For each plot point:

Identify which subjects are doing thematic work (not just present — thematically working)
Write Scene Events for each linked subject (P tracks, what goes on the page)
Write Emergent Inference or Latent Inference (WI tracks, what should be inferred)
Write Scene Meaning (T tracks, what the reader should arrive at)
Write the plot point's Scene Justification, Behavioral Translation, Delivery Blueprint, and Outcome
This first chapter is your validation pass. The display questions will reveal where the cognitive modes are still fuzzy. You may find a track that feels redundant in practice, or a cognitive mode shift that requires more effort than expected. Adjust the track definitions based on what you learn before proceeding to chapter 2.

Using AI to accelerate the S-scope work
The v1 notes for AJ alone are likely tens of thousands of words. Reading and synthesizing them to write a Foundation note is tractable but time-consuming. This is one place where a reasoning model is genuinely useful: feed it the v1 Unassigned notes for a character and ask it to draft the Foundation+La note in the correct cognitive mode — omniscient author, psychological architecture, no mixing with history or inference. You then review, correct, and refine. The model cannot generate the note at the quality you'd accept as final, but it can produce a draft that accelerates the review-and-refine loop significantly.

This is not prose generation — it's note synthesis from research material. The model is doing what it is genuinely good at: reading a large disorganized body of material and producing a structured synthesis. You validate the synthesis against your authorial knowledge. Where the draft is right, you keep it. Where it misses nuance, you correct it.

The practical decision tree when you sit down to write
Before any prose session, ask: do I have the v2 tracks populated for the scenes I'm about to write? If not, populate them before writing prose. The tracks take 30-60 minutes per plot point when you're fluent with the cognitive modes. The payoff is that when you open the prose document, you know exactly what must go on the page, what must form in the reader's head, and what must never be stated.

Before any v2 note-writing session, ask: which subject's S-scope tracks do I need as reference? If those aren't written yet, write them first.

The recommended first month
Week 1: Configure NoteTrackDefinitions for character subjects. Write AJ's Foundation+La, Historical Record, Arc+En, Events+En, La+S+Reveal, and En+S+Witness from v1 reference.

Week 2: Same for Chrysalis and Twilight. These are the three subjects with the most Latent content and the longest Revelation Plans — getting them right first prevents contradictions when writing later scenes.

Week 3: Fully populate all plot points in Chapter 1 using the link tracks. Write the plot point tracks for each. This is the first end-to-end test.

Week 4: Review what the Chapter 1 pass revealed about the track structure. Refine display questions where needed. Then continue to Chapter 2 with a validated system.

After this, the practice is sustainable: populate the next chapter's tracks, then write the prose. The v1 archive gradually becomes historical reference that you consult less and less.

I'm not doing any writing at all though. I'm still expanding the material.

May 16

Recalibrated methodology for active story expansion phase
Recalibrated methodology for active story expansion phase
That changes the methodology significantly. The previous answer assumed you were preparing to write prose. If you're still expanding material, the tracks serve a different purpose: they prevent the v1 accumulation problem from recurring as new material comes in, and they make your AI collaboration sessions progressively more efficient as subjects stabilize.

The expansion phase workflow
The classification habit is the core practice. After any world-building session — whether with Gemini, on your own, or from external research — spend 15-30 minutes routing new notes to the right track. Don't try to write polished track content; just direct-assign new notes to the correct track type. "This is world truth about Chrysalis's psychology → Foundation+La. This is a historical event → Historical Record. This is a note about how the reader should understand this → Revelation Plan."

The goal is to never let new material pile up in Unassigned for longer than one session. The v1 problem was years of unclassified accumulation. The v2 practice is classification as a regular habit, not a migration project.

The "stable enough to anchor" threshold
You don't need to write polished S-scope tracks for every subject immediately. The signal that a subject needs its Foundation note written is: you find yourself re-explaining it to Gemini at the start of every session because the context keeps being lost or re-derived. When that happens, the subject has reached enough development stability that writing a clean Foundation note will pay off — you paste it as context rather than reconstructing it from scratch each time.

This makes the track-writing work feel purposeful rather than administrative. You're writing the Foundation note for AJ when AJ's psychology is stable enough that re-deriving it every session is wasteful. You're not writing it because a migration checklist demands it.

Starting point: configure the tracks, then use them for new material only
The practical first step is configuring the NoteTrackDefinitions in the v2 app from the Grand Track Table. This takes a few hours and unlocks the ability to route notes correctly going forward.

After that: stop worrying about the v1 backlog. Start using the correct tracks for everything new. The old Unassigned notes can be consulted as a research archive when you decide to write a subject's S-scope tracks. They don't need to be migrated in advance of that moment.

The path forward looks like:

New material coming in → classify it into tracks immediately
When a subject feels "done enough to anchor," write its S-scope Foundation and Historical Record from the accumulated notes
When that subject appears in a scene you want to design, write the link-scope tracks for that scene
How the tracks serve expansion differently than writing
During expansion, the tracks are primarily consistency enforcement and context management. When you've been developing the Coltbert Reforms for three months across many Gemini sessions, the Foundation+La note for that abstract system is the one place where the true mechanics live, checked for contradiction against new ideas before they're accepted. Without it, contradictions accumulate silently.

The Revelation Plan and Witness Arc tracks during expansion are design commitments — when you write them, you're deciding what the reader should eventually understand. This disciplines the expansion: does this new lore element I just developed fit the Revelation Plan, or does it require revising the plan? Having the plan written makes that question answerable.

The expansion phase never needs to be "complete" before populating tracks is useful. Partial coverage is already useful coverage.

The 300k words of content in v1 needs to be sorted and inserted into v2 appropriately so that v2 can format and feed in everything as context. If I'm adding to v2 but using v1 for context my new stuff won't be reasoned on.

May 16

Architected AI-assisted migration strategy prioritizing subjects by narrative importance
Architected AI-assisted migration strategy prioritizing subjects by narrative importance
You're right, and that's the correct diagnosis. A bifurcated source of truth — organized v2 plus unorganized v1 — means every AI session has to work around the gap. The migration isn't optional if you want the context export to be the complete picture.

The key reframe that makes it tractable: you don't write the migration, you review it.

The AI-assisted synthesis workflow
For each subject entity, the process is:

Export all v1 Unassigned notes for that subject
Feed them to Gemini (full context, 490k tokens handles large subjects easily) with the relevant rows from the Grand Track Table
Prompt: "Using only this source material, draft each of these tracks in the cognitive mode described by the display question. Do not invent content not present in the source material. If a track has insufficient material, say so."
Review the drafts — accept what's right, correct what's wrong, flag what's missing
Commit approved content to v2
This converts 300k words of migration from a writing task to a review task. The AI's job is synthesis from the source material you already have; your job is validation against your authorial knowledge. Where the draft is right, you accept it in minutes. Where it misses something, you correct it. The gaps the AI flags ("insufficient material for this track") tell you where the v1 coverage has holes that need new expansion work.

Prioritization order
Not all subjects are equally urgent or equally tractable. Migrate in this order:

First — S-scope tracks for primary characters. AJ, Twilight, Chrysalis. Their Foundation and Historical Record notes are referenced by the most downstream link notes. Getting these right first stabilizes everything else. These also have the richest v1 coverage, making the drafts most reliable.

Second — World mechanics and political systems. Charitostatic Effect, Stagnation of Harmony, Equestrian Republic, Coltbert Reforms. These are referenced constantly in expansion sessions. Having their S-scope tracks clean means Gemini sessions can use a precise extract rather than the full 490k dump.

Third — Organizations and secondary characters. EEEE!, Celestia, Luna, Rarity, Trimmel, Minette. In rough order of how much they interact with the primary characters in the early chapters.

Fourth — Link-scope notes. These require the S-scope tracks to exist first as reference. Don't start link notes until the relevant S-scope notes are stable.

The practical batch size
Subject by subject is the right grain. A single subject's v1 notes — even for a major character — is probably 10,000-30,000 words. That fits in one Gemini session with the track definitions as the prompt specification. One session produces a draft; one review session validates it. You could realistically migrate one major character per day if you work at it.

Your Drive content
You had Google Docs linked in this conversation — the design notes and personal context documents. If your v1 story content is also in Drive, I can read files directly and help process batches of migration work in this conversation rather than you manually exporting and re-pasting. Do you have the v1 subject content in Drive, or is it primarily in the v2 SQLite database?

May 16

Recalibrated methodology: author writes all notes in personal voice, Gemini serves inspiration only
Recalibrated methodology: author writes all notes in personal voice, Gemini serves inspiration only
Both corrections matter and they change the picture significantly.

If the notes must be in your voice — directions from you to your future self, personal and opinionated — then AI drafting isn't actually a shortcut. You'd spend more time correcting the register than writing it yourself. The v2 notes aren't documentation; they're the author's private thinking made explicit.

And if Gemini is for inspiration rather than answers, the workflow is:

Gemini sparks ideas → you capture as Unassigned → you write v2 tracks from your own synthesis of all accumulated material → the v2 export feeds the next Gemini session as structured context

Which means the migration is genuinely a writing task. The honest framing: it is months of ongoing work, not a project with an end date. It happens in parallel with expansion, not before it.

What makes it tractable
The display questions are your writing prompts. You don't face a blank page. You sit down with a subject's v1 Unassigned notes open for reference, and you answer: "Who is this character at their deepest level?" You write until you've answered it. Close v1. Read back through to check completeness. Done with that track.

One cognitive mode per session. Don't try to write all tracks for one subject in one sitting — you'll be switching between psychologist, historian, and arc designer constantly. Instead: one session writes Foundation notes for three characters. Next session writes Historical Records for those same three. The cognitive mode stays consistent and the work goes faster.

Write from synthesis, audit from v1. Write the track from your own understanding of the subject first. Then read back through v1 to check you didn't miss something important. V1 is the audit, not the draft.

Partial notes are immediately useful. A Foundation note that covers the core wound and worldview but leaves epistemological limits blank is already useful as Gemini context. You're not aiming for completeness before usefulness — write what you know solidly and leave space for what's still developing.

The "stable enough" threshold is the real trigger. A subject in active flux shouldn't have its Foundation written yet — you'd just be rewriting it. Write the S-scope tracks when a subject has settled enough that you'd explain it the same way twice. That's when writing the track is a record of something real rather than premature commitment.

The practical first sessions
Session 1: Write AJ's Foundation note. This is the most developed, most stable, most referenced subject in the whole project. If any subject is ready to anchor, it's her.

Session 2: Write Chrysalis's Foundation note. This is the subject with the most Latent design work — the whole rational-CEO revelation arc starts here.

Session 3: Write both of their Historical Records.

After those four notes exist, the most important S-scope reference material is in v2, and Gemini sessions involving either character can use clean structured context rather than the full dump.

Everything else follows that same pattern: one track type, a few subjects, written in your own voice, audited against v1.

Okay, so there are two radically different approaches? One is to read individual v1 mixed notes and classify them out break up into their constituent tracks. The other is to read the v1 notes and then synthesize the tracks independently using one cognitive mode for writing? Please give a thorough holistic comparative overview of the pros and cons of each and if there are other approaches and nuances

May 16

Weighed sorting versus synthesis migration strategies comprehensively
Weighed sorting versus synthesis migration strategies comprehensively
The two approaches defined precisely
Sorting/classifying: take each v1 note as-is, decide which track it belongs to, and move or copy it there. The output is the same content reorganized into new containers.

Synthesizing: read v1 notes as research, then write each track fresh from your own understanding in the correct cognitive mode. The output is new writing informed by v1 but not copied from it.

These are genuinely different operations with different costs, different outputs, and different failure modes.

Sorting: the full case
Pros

It scales to small time increments. You can sort for twenty minutes and stop at any note. There's no warmup required — you just pick up the next note and decide where it goes. This makes it compatible with fragmented work sessions.

It has a lower risk of information loss. You're explicitly handling every note, so nothing gets dropped by accident. Every detail from v1 gets at minimum a classification decision.

It's fast per item for notes that are already single-claim. A v1 note that says "AJ ran from the factory in 983 ALB" is pure historical record. One decision, done.

It works well for raw factual content — events with dates, world law mechanics that were stated plainly, organizational history. These were often written in v1 as relatively clean facts, and sorting them is appropriate.

Cons

The fundamental problem: v1 notes were not written in distinct cognitive modes. A single paragraph can contain psychology, history, reader design, and authorial commentary interleaved. Sorting that paragraph means either accepting it in the wrong track, splitting it into fragments, or rewriting it — which is synthesis.

Sorting produces fragments, not notes. If you sort fifteen v1 paragraphs about AJ's psychology into Foundation+La, you end up with fifteen fragments that each address a different aspect in a different register, with no coherent architecture. The Foundation note doesn't exist yet. The fragments still need to be synthesized before the track is usable as AI context.

Sorting preserves noise. V1 contains dead-end ideas, superseded thinking, design conversations that went nowhere, options that were considered and rejected. Sorting moves everything, including the noise. A subsequent cleanup pass is required, which means you haven't saved work — you've deferred it.

Sorting doesn't reveal gaps. If AJ's historical record in v1 has a gap — something you know but never wrote down, or something that was assumed but never articulated — sorting won't surface it. You'll have a complete-looking sorted collection that's actually missing something important.

Sorting doesn't force commitment. Two contradictory v1 notes about the same aspect of Chrysalis's psychology will sit in the same track after sorting, each with an implicit question mark. Synthesis forces you to decide which is right.

The classification decisions are hard for ambiguous notes. "Celestia is terrified of causing a panic cascade" — is this Foundation+La (who she is) or WI+En+L (what the reader should infer in scene 7.3) or both? Sorting requires answering this for every ambiguous note without the synthesis context that would make the answer clear.

Synthesis: the full case
Pros

The output is immediately usable as AI context. A synthesized Foundation note is a coherent document written in one voice, answering one question, in one cognitive mode. You can paste it into a Gemini session and it works. A collection of sorted fragments is not there yet.

Synthesis filters noise automatically. When you write from your current understanding, you only include what you currently believe to be true. Superseded ideas and dead-end options simply don't appear — you don't think of them because they're not your actual model of the subject.

Synthesis surfaces gaps. When you can't write the section of the Foundation note that covers epistemic limits, you've found a real gap in your thinking that v1 doesn't cover. This is information. Sorting would never reveal it.

Synthesis forces commitment. You can't write "her core wound is the moment she had to choose between family approval and authentic selfhood" without committing to that as the current truth. Commitment is what makes notes reliable references rather than open questions.

Natural volume reduction happens. Three hundred thousand words of mixed v1 notes might synthesize to thirty thousand words of clean v2 tracks. The reduction is evidence that synthesis is doing its job — integrating, prioritizing, discarding.

The result is in your voice. Because you wrote it, not sorted it, the register is yours. The note reads as direction from you to your future self, not as reorganized material from a previous session.

Cons

It requires subject stability. You cannot synthesize a Foundation note for a character whose nature you're still questioning. If you're still discovering who Chrysalis fundamentally is, you don't yet have a stable synthesis to write. Sorting has no such prerequisite — you can sort notes about a subject that's still in flux.

Higher cognitive load per session. You need to hold the entire track question in mind — who is this character at their deepest level — and write to it. This requires a focused block of time with mental warmup. You can't do it effectively in fragments.

Risk of omission without auditing. If you synthesize from memory and don't read back through v1 to check, you may write a Foundation note that's coherent but missing a specific detail that v1 contains. The audit step — read v1 after writing to check completeness — is mandatory for this reason.

Slower per note. Each synthesized track is a genuine writing task with a real time cost.

Other approaches and their role
Hybrid: sort as access layer, synthesize as finish layer

The most practical path for most subjects.

First pass is lightweight sorting — not moving content into tracks, just tagging each v1 note with a track label while reading through it quickly. This is fast, low-effort, and gives you grouped views of the material without committing to reorganization.

Second pass is synthesis — for each track type, read all the tagged notes together in one concentrated view, then close v1 and write the synthesized track from that position.

The sort provides organized raw material. The synthesis produces the actual track note. This approach works especially well for complex subjects where the v1 material is large and the tagged groupings help you see the landscape before writing.

Write first, audit second

A variant of synthesis that forces the strongest commitment. Write the Foundation note entirely from your own current knowledge without consulting v1 at all. Only open v1 after you've written to audit what you may have forgotten.

This reveals what you actually know versus what you have written down but never fully internalized. If you write a character's Foundation note from memory and find you can do it without gaps, the subject is genuinely stable in your thinking. If you stall, the subject needs more development before the track can be written.

Canopy notes for unstable subjects

For subjects still in active development, neither sorting nor synthesis is appropriate yet. A third option: write a brief canopy note — three to five sentences of current best understanding — as a placeholder. This gives AI sessions something to work with without requiring the subject to be fully resolved. Mark it explicitly as provisional.

Atomic sorting only for clean notes

Some v1 notes were written as single claims — a specific date, a single event, one crisp psychological observation. These can be sorted without synthesis. The practical rule: if a v1 note can go into exactly one track without splitting or rewriting, sort it. If it requires splitting or rewriting, that's synthesis work and should be treated as synthesis.

The decisive question
Sorting and synthesis produce different things:

Sorting produces organized raw material. The same thoughts, rearranged into containers. Still requires synthesis before the tracks are usable.

Synthesis produces committed author knowledge. The author's current true understanding, written in the right cognitive mode, with noise removed and gaps surfaced.

For the stated purpose — clean, reliable context for AI collaboration and future design work — synthesis is the appropriate output. But synthesis has an entry requirement: the subject must be stable enough to synthesize. Subjects still in flux cannot be synthesized yet.

The practical resolution: sorting is a development tool, synthesis is a completion tool. A subject's migration path is: sort as you develop → develop to stability → synthesize once stable.

The migration is never "done" in the project-completion sense. It's a practice that runs alongside the expansion work. When a subject reaches stability, you synthesize it. When new expansion work destabilizes a subject, you mark the synthesis as provisional until it stabilizes again. The v2 tracks are a living document, not a completed archive.

The thing I am most worried about is losing insights

May 16

Categorized insight types and architected protective safeguards
Categorized insight types and architected protective safeguards
That fear is worth taking seriously because it's pointing at something real. Let me name the specific types of insights that are actually at risk, because the risk is different for each.

What kinds of insights can be lost, and how
Precise phrasings that captured something exactly right. Sometimes a v1 note has a sentence that articulates a psychological truth or thematic connection in a way that took effort to arrive at. Synthesis from memory might produce something approximately right but less precise. The audit step — reading v1 after writing to check for what you missed — is the protection here. If the precise phrasing is in v1, you'll encounter it in the audit and can incorporate it.

Cross-subject connections. This is the highest-risk category. A v1 note might say "AJ's reaction to the ration can connects to the Charitostatic Effect because..." — when you synthesize AJ's tracks, you're in AJ-mode and may not think of the charitostatic effect. When you synthesize the world law's tracks, you're in physics-mode and may not think of AJ. The connection falls between subjects and neither synthesis captures it.

The practical protection: before synthesizing any subject, do a specific connection-hunting pass through its v1 notes. Look specifically for mentions of other subjects, other systems, other characters. Make a brief list of connections that appeared. Then ensure those connections end up somewhere — either in a T note (as thematic evidence linking two subjects), in the Design Notes for both subjects, or in a separate cross-subject notes document.

Hard-won conclusions that now feel obvious. You worked something out over a week of thinking, and now it just seems self-evident, so when you synthesize you don't bother noting it explicitly. This is the quietest failure mode. The audit step catches it: when you read the v1 note that contains the work, you recognize "I did work to get here."

Insights embedded in discarded design. V1 probably contains reasoning that went in a wrong direction and was abandoned — but the reasoning process itself might contain an observation that was correct even if the conclusion was wrong. Sorting preserves this by default. Synthesis discards the dead ends but might discard a valid observation that was sitting inside a dead end.

The actual protection: v1 stays intact
The most important thing: you never delete v1. The migration produces v2 content from v1 material, but v1 remains as the permanent archive. If something important was in v1 and didn't make it into v2, it's still there, still searchable, still available when you need it.

This means the worst case is not "insights are gone" — it's "insights are in v1 and haven't been migrated yet." That's recoverable. A truly lost insight would require both v1 and v2 to not contain it, which can't happen if v1 is preserved.

The audit step, done properly
For synthesis, the audit isn't just skimming v1 after writing. It's adversarial reading — specifically looking for what your synthesis might have missed. The right questions to ask during audit:

Is there anything in here that isn't captured in what I just wrote?
Is this an omission (add it) or a deliberate discard (noise)?
Is there a connection mentioned here to another subject that I haven't accounted for?
Is there a precise phrasing here that's better than what I wrote?
The audit takes as long as the initial v1 read. Budget for it.

For the cross-connection problem specifically
Before synthesizing a subject, run one focused pass through its v1 notes looking only for mentions of other subjects. Collect every instance where the notes reference another character, world law, organization, or system. Write these down as a connection list.

Then synthesize. Then audit.

Then, separately, decide where each connection lives: in the T note of the relevant link, in the Design Notes for both subjects, or in a dedicated cross-subject connections document if it doesn't belong to any single link.

This targeted pass is faster than fully reading v1 for the synthesis and prevents the highest-risk loss.

The reassurance
The insights are not at risk of being destroyed — they're at risk of not yet being in the right place. V1 is the permanent record of everything you've figured out. The migration is the process of moving things to where they're most useful. As long as v1 exists, nothing is gone.

One thing I'm noticing is how the mixed notes in v1 are good enough for AI which can reason about the mixed assertions with brute force reasoning. By compressing the insights into packed mixed statements with subtext, it saves tokens. But they don't help me as a writer, only me as a brute force planner expanding ideas. Give an analysis of this insight and how it would inform the the task ahead. Also note that there will still be more expansion to come.

May 17

Reconciled dense notes for expansion with clean tracks for writing preparation
Reconciled dense notes for expansion with clean tracks for writing preparation
The observation stated precisely
V1 notes are dense, mixed-claim documents optimized for one specific use: feeding AI reasoning during expansion. Their density is load-bearing for that purpose. When you paste a v1 note like "Chrysalis is a rational CEO who sponsored Equestrian industry for 30 years and the reader should feel sympathy through this revelation while the character believes she is purely pragmatic" into a Gemini session, Gemini parses all four claim types simultaneously, reasons across them, and produces useful expansion. The subtext and mixed assertions are features, not bugs — they compress a lot of context into few tokens, and AI brute-force reasoning handles the parsing.

But when you sit down to design scene 7.3, that same density is actively hostile. You need to know specifically: what goes on the page right now, and what must form in the reader's head? The mixed note doesn't answer that question — it requires you to parse it first, extract the relevant claim, then apply it. You're doing the disambiguation work that Gemini was doing automatically. As a writer you don't have brute-force reasoning — you have limited working memory and need to switch cleanly between cognitive modes.

What this reveals about the two formats
The mixed v1 format and the clean v2 track format are not competing versions of the same thing. They're tools for two distinct cognitive modes that you genuinely operate in:

Expansion mode: generative, exploratory, flowing. You're developing the world. AI is your collaborator. Dense mixed notes serve this mode perfectly. Gemini can hold a paragraph that's simultaneously psychology, history, reader design, and authorial note, and reason productively across all of it.

Writing-preparation mode: deliberate, mode-specific, show-don't-tell conscious. You're preparing to write prose. The questions are operational: what goes on the page, what must be inferred, what is the thematic conclusion. Clean track notes serve this mode. Mixed notes obstruct it.

Both modes are real and necessary. The mistake would be to convert everything to v2 tracks and lose the format that serves expansion — or to stay entirely in v1 and never build the format that serves writing preparation.

What this means for the task ahead
The migration is not a format conversion project. It's a tool addition project. You are adding v2 tracks as a new instrument for writing-preparation mode while keeping the dense mixed format for expansion mode. Both coexist. Neither replaces the other.

Unassigned notes in v2 are already the right format for expansion. The v1 notes currently sitting in v2 as Unassigned are serving their purpose — they can feed Gemini sessions as mixed context. You don't need to migrate them to tracks before they're useful for expansion. They're already useful.

Track synthesis happens when a subject graduates from expansion to writing-readiness. The trigger is not "this subject has been migrated" but "this subject is stable enough that I could write prose involving it, and I need the tracks to prepare." That moment is when you synthesize the clean v2 tracks from the accumulated Unassigned material. Until then, the Unassigned notes are the correct state.

New expansion material continues to flow in as Unassigned. Since more expansion is coming, new Gemini sessions will produce new insights. These go in as Unassigned notes, immediately serving future expansion sessions. They don't need to be synthesized into tracks until the subject stabilizes.

The context export system needs to distinguish these modes. For a Gemini expansion session, you want the dense Unassigned notes — full context, mixed claims, high density. For a Claude writing-design session, you want the clean synthesized tracks for the specific subjects relevant to the scene being designed. The export format matters for the task at hand, and these are different exports.

The corollary insight about token efficiency
The v1 format's efficiency for AI reasoning is a genuine property worth preserving. A synthesized Foundation note for AJ might be 500 words, carefully written in omniscient-author mode. The v1 Unassigned notes for AJ might be 8,000 words of mixed claims. For a Gemini expansion session working on AJ, those 8,000 words of rich mixed context are more useful than 500 words of clean tracks — Gemini benefits from the density and can reason across the subtext.

Clean tracks become more valuable than mixed notes only when the output of the context session is plot point design or prose writing rather than further world expansion.

Practical implication for right now
You don't need to touch the v1 migration at all during expansion phases. When you're in Gemini sessions developing new material, capture the results as Unassigned notes. When a subject reaches writing stability, synthesize its S-scope tracks in a focused session. Write the L-scope tracks as you approach actual plot point design for scenes involving that subject.

The migration pressure you felt was real but misplaced — it was pointing at a genuine need, but the need is "have clean tracks available when preparing to write," not "have all content in tracks before doing anything else." Since you're not writing yet, that need hasn't arrived for most subjects. When it does arrive, it arrives subject by subject, scene by scene, not all at once.

I'm now thinking instead of one display question and one usage directive and then another field for a general "explanation, why does this track exist" I need to establish that there are distinct modes of using the story planner v2, and the tracks need to have different Display Text according to the mode, and also configurable write allowance or readonly.

The modes I'm envisioning are:
Expansion Mode - Links are not shown, only the subject. Intended to expand the history or ontology and plans. Display questions for what to go into what track
Application Mode - apply the existing subject lore to plot point links. The lore and plans are read only and their display text switches to usage, how to use them for the link. The link tracks have display questions that state what should go into the track.
Audit Mode - Most tracks are read only, except for the subject tracks that are meant to be built retroactively from the links like the witness arc. The display text for all the tracks become what needs to be audited - whether the sum of the links adds up to the subject wide track contents or not, for example
Scene Designer Mode - links and the plot point are visible. Links are readonly, plot point tracks editable. Display Text for link tracks is usage, display text for plot point tracks is display questions

Give an analysis, suggest if the modes should be merged, if any modes are missing, and suggest nomenclature for the modes if there are alternatives
