May 17

Validated four distinct modes, identified missing audit variants, preserved separation
Validated four distinct modes, identified missing audit variants, preserved separation
What the mode proposal gets right
The core insight is sound: the same note means different things depending on what the author is doing at that moment. A subject's Foundation note is a destination when you're developing the subject, a reference when you're writing link notes, an audit target when checking coherence, and background context when designing a scene. The display text and permissions should reflect which function it's serving. Having one static display question that's half instruction and half reference is a compromise that serves neither use well.

The write/read permission model is also correct in principle: cognitive mode contamination is real. If you can edit the subject's Foundation note while writing link notes, you'll accidentally revise canonical truth in Application mode when you should be constrained to drawing from it.

Analysis of each mode
Expansion Mode is well-defined. Its job is clear: develop subject knowledge. One question worth asking is whether the Revelation Plan and Witness Arc tracks belong here. These are subject-scope plans, not W-content tracks — they're design documents about the reader journey. You might want to write them here alongside the W tracks rather than only in a separate planning context. If Expansion Mode is "everything subject-scope that I own and write," they belong here.

Application Mode is well-defined but reveals a sequencing question: do you write all link tracks for a subject before moving to the next subject, or do you write all P tracks for all subjects first, then all WI tracks? Application Mode should probably support both patterns. The key design principle is correct: subject tracks are the reference, link tracks are the workspace.

Audit Mode contains a useful distinction you identified — some subject-scope tracks are populated retroactively from the bottom up. Specifically, the Staged Events track (which scenes got dramatized) and the Witness Arc (what the accumulation of witnessed T notes argues) might not be written top-down at all. They might be written here, after the links exist. This is the right place for that work. The display text in Audit Mode should be audit questions rather than display questions or usage directives — "does the sum of your disclosed content notes across all links add up to what the Historical Record says should be disclosed?" rather than any of the productive prompts.

Scene Designer Mode is well-defined. The cognitive mode here is clearly the synthetic director mode — reading all link inputs as specifications and designing what goes on the page.

Missing modes
Theme Review Mode. Audit Mode is subject-centric: does this subject's arc cohere? Theme Review is thematically-centric: does the accumulated evidence across all subjects and scenes make the thematic argument? You need to see all T notes tagged to a specific theme in chapter order to audit whether the evidence is sufficient, well-sequenced, and not front-loaded or redundant. This is a cross-subject read-only aggregation. It can't be handled by Audit Mode because Audit Mode is necessarily anchored to one subject at a time. Without this mode, you can audit each subject's arc but never audit the thematic case across the whole project.

Narrative Review Mode. A purely read-only sequential mode for reading a subject's arc in chapter order — seeing all the link notes as a flowing narrative rather than as individual design units. This is different from Audit Mode (which is checking coherence) and Scene Designer Mode (which is editing). It's the mode you'd use to read through AJ's T notes from chapter 1 to 22 and feel whether her arc reads correctly. No editing, no audit questions — just the content in order. This might feel like a "nice to have" but it directly supports the expansion phase: you can read back through a subject's existing design to orient yourself before a Gemini session.

Modes that could be merged
None of the four core modes should merge with each other — they are genuinely distinct cognitive states with different permissions and different orientations. However, Theme Review and Narrative Review could potentially be combined into a single Review Mode that supports different views (by theme, by subject in chapter order, by plot point). The underlying cognitive state is the same — read-only, sequential comprehension rather than active design.

One nuance: the Garden Notes track has anomalous behavior
Garden Notes doesn't fit neatly into any mode's permission logic. In Expansion Mode it's writable (you're adding design decisions and allegorical context). In Application Mode it's readable (useful context when writing link notes). In Scene Designer Mode it's arguably irrelevant. In Audit Mode it's not subject to any coherence check. It may need to be always-writable across modes, surfaced when relevant, rather than following the mode's permission rules. Or it could simply be a floating panel that's always accessible regardless of mode.

Nomenclature evaluation
The user's names are accurate but they describe what the author is doing (expansion, application, audit, design) rather than what the software is in. Both framings are legitimate. Some alternatives:

Action-oriented (author-centric, like your proposal):

Develop / Apply / Audit / Design
Build / Deploy / Verify / Direct
State-oriented (software-centric):

Archive Mode / Context Mode / Coherence Mode / Blueprint Mode
Chronicle / Reference / Alignment / Production
Cognitive mode-oriented (what thinking you're doing):

Architect / Contextualize / Verify / Synthesize
The user's existing names are clear and consistent. The one that might benefit from reconsideration is Application Mode — "apply" has a software connotation (applying a patch, applying settings) that could confuse. Deployment Mode or Scene Context Mode or simply Linking Mode might be cleaner. Expansion Mode is particularly good because it directly maps to the workflow phase you described. Audit Mode is standard. Scene Designer Mode is precise.

A cleaner set staying close to your originals:

Expansion Mode → keep as-is
Linking Mode (instead of Application Mode — you're creating the links)
Audit Mode → keep as-is
Scene Design Mode (drop the "-er" — you're doing scene design, not being a scene designer)
Theme Review Mode (new)
Arc Review Mode (new, or combined as Review Mode with sub-views)
Expansion Mode, Linking Mode, Audit Mode, and Scene Design Mode are only for the Common Window which can display one thing on the left and one thing on the right.
In Expansion Mode, the Subject tracks take up the whole window. In Linking Mode, it's Subject on Left, one selected link on the right with the link selector in the middle.
But now I'm thinking I need a different kind of view for Audit and Scene Design. Scene Design requires looking at all links simultaneously, so maybe it needs a scroll bar, and the tracks can be visually compressed because it should only show Confirmed notes, not the Unset or Flagged tracks. Audit also needs a scrollbar to view links continuously.

Theme Review is a separate window that shows all the notes across subjects for the theme. I'm imagining Arc Review to be synonymous with Audit.

But this workflow assumes pure architecting with no gardening. In practice, I have notes for scene outlines. They may not be deserved to be Confirmed until Scene Design mode where they must be checked against the link notes for on the page, world inference, and theme, but those notes need to be enterable before that stage.

May 17

Reconciled UI architecture with pre-design note workflow gaps
Reconciled UI architecture with pre-design note workflow gaps
The window architecture as it stands
The Common Window handles the subject-centric views with mode-specific layouts. Theme Review is correctly separate — it's a different navigation axis altogether (by theme rather than by subject). The distinction between the two-panel modes (Expansion, Linking) and the scrolling modes (Scene Design, Audit) is real and important: the two-panel modes are for focused work on one relationship at a time, while the scrolling modes require a continuous view of the full picture.

The note compression in Scene Design Mode (Confirmed only) is correct. When you're designing the plot point, you want the stable, vetted reference — not notes that are still being worked on. Unset and Flagged link notes should not be visible in Scene Design Mode because they're inputs that haven't been validated yet and would corrupt the synthesis.

The gardening gap: where rough plot point notes live
The workflow gap you identified is real. There are two distinct kinds of pre-Scene-Design plot point content:

Craft Notes / metatextual sketches — rough scene ideas, intuitions, "this should feel like X," reminders to self. These are never structured inputs to anything. They don't participate in the P/WI/T framework. The Craft Notes track (always writable, never filtered) handles these correctly regardless of mode. They remain visible in Scene Design Mode as a sidebar note to the author, not as structured design input.

Provisional structured notes — content that IS trying to be a Delivery Blueprint or a rough Scene Meaning, but hasn't been checked against the link framework yet. These are the gardening notes: scene sketches that have real design intent but are Unset because they were written before the links were populated or confirmed.

The NoteState system already exists to handle this distinction:

Unset = captured, provisional, written during gardening, not yet validated
Confirmed = checked against the link framework, stable for use in Scene Design
The mechanism is: you write provisional structured notes on the plot point tracks in a mode that allows Unset notes. Scene Design Mode then filters to Confirmed only. The act of confirming a plot point track note is the checkpoint — you're asserting "I've read the link notes and this holds."

What mode allows entry of Unset plot point notes
The Chapter Window is the natural home for this. It already shows plot points within a chapter. When you're outlining — still gardening, before deep scene design — you're in the chapter level, sketching what each plot point roughly does. The Chapter Window can surface a Sketch substate of the plot point where all note states are visible and the plot point tracks are writable without requiring the link notes to be Confirmed first.

This means the Chapter Window serves as the gardening-phase interface for plot points: you can write Unset notes in any plot point track, use the Craft Notes for intuitions, and sketch provisional Delivery Blueprints. Then when you shift to Scene Design Mode in the Common Window, Scene Design Mode shows only the Confirmed notes — enforcing the discipline that the design has been checked against the subject links.

The workflow becomes:

Chapter Window → sketch rough plot point notes as Unset on any track
Linking Mode → populate and Confirm link notes for each linked subject
Scene Design Mode → all visible link notes are Confirmed; now design the plot point tracks; Confirming them means "validated against links"
The Confirmed filter in Scene Design Mode and its implications
Scene Design Mode showing only Confirmed link notes has one important implication: you cannot begin Scene Design Mode productively until the relevant link notes have been confirmed. This is a feature, not a bug — it enforces that the subject design work is stable before synthesis begins. If you open Scene Design Mode and all the link notes are Unset, the mode is essentially empty and signals that Linking Mode work needs to happen first.

This creates a natural workflow gate: the Confirmed state of link notes is the precondition for Scene Design Mode being useful.

Summary of the window and mode architecture
Common Window — mode-specific layouts:

Mode	Layout	Link visibility	Note states shown
Expansion	Subject full-width	Hidden	All
Linking	Subject left / Link right	One link at a time	All
Scene Design	All links scrollable (compressed) + plot point panel	All links simultaneously	Confirmed only for links; all states for plot point tracks
Audit	All links scrollable + subject S-scope panel	All links simultaneously	All
Chapter Window:

Shows all plot points in chapter
Each plot point: Craft Notes always accessible; other tracks writable as Unset (sketch mode)
This is the gardening interface for plot points before Scene Design
Theme Review Window (separate):

Cross-subject aggregation of T notes tagged to a specific theme
Ordered by chapter
Read-only
One remaining question: The Audit Mode's editable exceptions (Witness Arc, Staged Events built retroactively from links) — do those feel natural in the Common Window scrolling layout, or do they feel like they need their own focused view? If you're looking at 30 links scrolling past and simultaneously trying to write a synthesis of what they all argue, the synthesis writing might be better served by a split between "evidence view" (scrolling links) and "synthesis panel" (the retroactively-built S-scope tracks). This is the same two-panel logic as Linking Mode but at a higher level of aggregation.

May 17

Taxonomized content types and identified Delivery Blueprint (Unset) as provisional home
Taxonomized content types and identified Delivery Blueprint (Unset) as provisional home
The taxonomy of content in these v1 notes
Looking across all four examples, the mixed notes contain five distinct content types. Each has a different destination.

Type 1: Provisional Delivery Blueprint
Where found: Synopsis fields, primarily. These are the ordered sequences of what happens.

Scene 9.20: "Fleur Bloom chimes in... Twilight zeroes in on this part... later that night Twilight has a heart to heart with AJ..." — this IS a delivery blueprint. Rough, unvalidated against link notes, but structurally it's already sequenced scene events.

Scene 20.4: The entire synopsis is essentially a delivery blueprint — Celestia speaks up, Applejack asks what she'd do, the silence, Celestia invokes Nightmare Moon, Luna corrects the fairy tale, Twilight makes her break from Celestia. Very close to plot point ready.

Destination: Delivery Blueprint track on the plot point, NoteState = Unset. This is the direct answer to your question about where provisional blueprints live. They go in the Delivery Blueprint track in unvalidated state. Scene Design Mode filters to Confirmed only, so these Unset blueprints don't pollute the design view until they've been checked against link notes. They're preserved, accessible, and clearly marked as provisional.

Type 2: Scene Outcome
Where found: Sometimes explicit at the end of a synopsis. Scene 9.18 has it spelled out directly: "The buffalo are happy. The workers are happy. Applejack is sold on the 'Aquileian way'. Twilight is liberated of her past guilt!"

Destination: Scene Outcome track on the plot point, NoteState = Unset. These are often the clearest, most easily migrated piece of plot point content.

Type 3: Link track content (correctly placed on the scene, wrong format)
Where found: Character Analysis notes, Thread Update notes, Theme Commentary notes, Codex scene notes. These are already attached to the right scene. They just need to be split into the right link track types.

Character Analysis notes are the richest mix:

Scene 9.20, AJ analysis: "stops viewing war as evil industry and starts viewing it as tragic responsibility" → WI+En+L (the internal change that must be inferred, not stated)
"Harmonic Capitalism proves to her that modernization doesn't kill the soul of the worker" → T+En+L (thematic conclusion)
"She felt like she was losing her soul and becoming evil" → this is WI content from earlier scenes leaking into this note; might actually belong to P+En+L of the machine gun scenes
Thread Update notes almost always contain:

A statement of what happens (P+La+L or E+En+L)
What it means (T note)
Sometimes WI content mixed in
Scene 29.15, Twilight Breaking from Celestia: "Twilight realizes and says 'So that's why you said I can't use magic on the front. But you're wrong. Because I have Applejack to save me.'" — that's P+En+L+W (the event, the dialogue that goes on the page). "Celestia was scared of Twilight getting corrupted" — that's the WI+La+L for Celestia (what the reader now infers about Celestia from what's disclosed).

Theme Commentary notes are the most direct link track content — they are T+En+L or T+La+L notes already. Scene 20.4's "Strong to be Merciful" commentary maps almost directly to a T+En+L note for the World Peace/Predator's Dilemma subject.

Destination: Split into the appropriate link tracks (P+La+L, WI+La+L, T+La+L, P+En+L, WI+En+L, T+En+L) per linked subject. NoteState = Unset for all of these until validated in Linking Mode.

Type 4: Subject track content (misplaced on the scene)
Where found: Character Analysis notes and Codex scene notes that go well beyond this specific scene into general subject truth.

Scene 9.20, Twilight Character Analysis: "Twilight once was an obsessive magic researcher inventing spells for herself. Then the magic of friendship meant her special talent became a symbol for friendship. Then she believed that magic and friendship are mutually exclusive." — this is Foundation+La for Twilight. It describes who she is at a deep level, not what changes in this specific scene.

Scene 9.20, AJ's Parents Codex note: The entire multi-paragraph breakdown of AJ's relationship with her parents ("She left her parents to go back to the farm in The Cutie Mark Chronicles / Where the Apple Lies, she internalized the wrong lesson...") — this is Historical Record and Foundation+La for Applejack's Parents as a subject entity, with some P+La+L+W for this specific scene mixed in at the end.

Scene 12.4 synopsis: Most of the Tzinacatl economic history ("The Skyfall investors offered to build subsidiary factories... the logging company came... all tribes united to burn them down...") — this is Historical Record for Tzinacatl and for EEEE! as subject entities. It doesn't belong on this scene at all.

Destination: These need to be flagged and deferred — they migrate to subject entity tracks (Foundation+La, Historical Record, Arc+En) when those subjects are synthesized. They should NOT go into scene link tracks. When you encounter one of these while processing a scene, tag it "→ Subject:[name]" and set aside.

Type 5: Scene Justification content
Where found: Embedded in thread/theme context, rarely explicit.

Scene 9.20's TwiJack thread note: "clearing up old insecurities from canon" plus the specific insecurity pairs (Twilight's guilt / Applejack's hidden jealousy) — this contains the core Scene Justification. The scene exists to clear those specific debts between the two characters.

Scene 20.4: The Twilight Breaking from Celestia thread update contains: "Twilight realizes why Celestia held Twilight back" — the scene exists to deliver this final understanding. That's the Scene Justification.

Destination: Scene Justification track on the plot point, NoteState = Unset.

The practical answer: the Delivery Blueprint track is the provisional landing zone
Before any link notes are written or confirmed, the plot point needs to be able to hold provisional sketch content. The Delivery Blueprint track with NoteState = Unset is the correct home. This means:

For the synopsis fields specifically: Extract the sequenced events and paste them into Delivery Blueprint as Unset. They stay as a reference scaffold. When you eventually do Scene Design Mode for this scene, you read the Confirmed link notes, then either confirm the Delivery Blueprint or revise it based on what the link notes are now specifying.

The three-pass strategy for these existing plot point notes:

Pass 1 (fast, now): For each plot point, move the synopsis content to Delivery Blueprint (Unset) and any clear Outcome statements to Scene Outcome (Unset). Takes 5 minutes per scene. Preserves all provisional blueprint content in the right track, clears the synopsis field.

Pass 2 (medium, later): For each Character/Thread/Theme/Codex note attached to the scene, tag it with its destination: link track type or subject entity. Don't move yet — just tag. This is the classification pass.

Pass 3 (deep, when the subject is being synthesized): Actually create the link tracks using the tagged content, write fresh from the tagged notes as reference.

The Unset notes in the Delivery Blueprint represent your current best thinking. They're not blocking anything. Scene Design Mode won't show them until they're Confirmed. They wait until the link notes exist and validate them.

I think I need a Gardener Mode which uses the common window, displays the display questions for plot point tracks, and allows entry into unset and flagged, but not Confirmed. The links can also be clicked one by one and the subject link tracks are allowed to get new data, only in Unset and Flagged. This mirrors Expansion Mode and Linking Mode which the two together is basically what an Architect (as opposed to Gardener) does. The reality is I do both approaches in my v1. The gardening is just on the plot point synopsis level, not prose level. And perhaps moving to Confirmed is only allowed in Audit Mode for Subject and Link tracks? For plot point tracks, moving to Confirmed is only allowed in Scene Design mode? So the plot point in Scene Design mode has a scrolling view of confirmed notes. Plot point in Gardener Mode has the one link click, full note tracks and sections with unset (write)/flagged (write)/confirmed (read only).
Does this mean Expansion Mode and Linking Mode are the same? No, because they require different Display Text depending on the mode. So maybe Gardener Mode on plot point needs to be split as well? With different display questions on the links? Or maybe not due to the nature of gardening. Would this mean both the plot point tracks and link tracks have their entry question in gardener mode, while in scene design mode the plot point tracks have their display entry question and the links have their usage directive?
"She felt like she was losing her soul and becoming evil" actually seems like the perfect candidate of a statement that is BOTH a L+WI+En note on the machine gun scene from earlier AND a L+La+T note on the current scene about the reader's current opinion of Applejack. The earlier dramatization put that seed into the reader's existing understanding of Applejack. The accompanying note on how the new info updates their belief and the contrast of "I figured Applejack thinks closing the conscience and firing the machine gun with no regard for empathy is how to win the war" and "Applejack now thinks that's wrong" delivers evidence for the theme that conscience is stronger than extraction. Or wait, is this actually an L+La+P note because Applejack verbally says to Twilight this statement about feeling like she was losing her soul? No, AJ wouldn't phrase it like that? I'm not sure what I was going for. Maybe that is implied. But does this go into character change or character revelation against reader prior belief? Or both? I guess this is why I need to be precise about the different display text in different modes?

May 17

Architected Gardener Mode framework and dissected narrative revelation mechanics
Architected Gardener Mode framework and dissected narrative revelation mechanics
Gardener Mode: confirmed distinct, should not be split
Expansion Mode and Linking Mode are subject-anchored: you're either developing a subject or applying a subject to a scene. Gardener Mode is plot-point-anchored: you're capturing from the scene outward. The cognitive direction is reversed. The display text is necessarily different because you're not being asked "what is the canonical truth about this subject" or "how does this subject's truth apply here" — you're being asked "what do you notice about this scene?"

This is why Gardener Mode should NOT be split into a plot-point-side and a link-side. Expansion Mode and Linking Mode are split because they serve genuinely different cognitive disciplines — the psychologist/historian versus the scene applicator. In Gardener Mode, both sides are in the same cognitive discipline: rough capture. The display text can be unified around "what do you want to record about this?" The discipline of classification happens later.

The display text logic by mode is correct as you've described it:

Mode	Plot point tracks	Link tracks
Gardener	Display question (entry, simplified)	Display question (entry, simplified)
Scene Design	Display question (entry, full)	Usage directive (reference)
Audit	Audit question	Audit question
The "simplified" qualifier for Gardener Mode display questions matters. A full display question asks "What should the reader independently infer about this subject's internal state from the behavioral evidence in this scene?" — that's Linking Mode precision. A Gardener Mode display question asks something closer to "What's happening with this subject here, and what does it mean?" — looser, capture-focused, not requiring cognitive mode discipline.

The NoteState permission model as you've described it is correct:

Confirmed for subject tracks: Audit Mode only
Confirmed for link tracks: Audit Mode only
Confirmed for plot point tracks: Scene Design Mode only
Gardener Mode: Unset and Flagged only for both sides
This makes Confirmed a meaningful quality gate rather than a casual checkbox.

The "losing her soul" dissection
This note is simultaneously correct about three different things on three different scenes, which is exactly why mixed v1 notes are hard to migrate and why the display text matters.

On the machine gun scenes (Scene 1.6 and throughout Part I):

This is O+En+L+W content for those scenes — the internal truth that was never on the page. AJ felt corrupted by the industrial killing machine, connecting it to her parents' factory which took their souls. This was held as WI content: the reader should have inferred from behavioral evidence that something was costing AJ morally, even if they couldn't name it yet. The behavioral proxy work — what physical details encode this without stating it — was the showing requirement for those earlier scenes. The inference available to the reader at that point was partial ("something feels off") because the full AJ's Parents backstory hadn't been disclosed yet.

On Scene 9.20 (the disclosure scene):

This is P+La+L+W: AJ verbally says something to Twilight about what she was feeling during the war. Not "I felt like I was losing my soul and becoming evil" — that's the author's synthesis, not AJ's voice. AJ's version might be: "Every time I was at that machine gun, I felt like I was turning into them. Like if I kept going long enough, I'd forget why it mattered."

The WI+La+L for Scene 9.20: Reader infers that what looked like AJ finding her authentic place at the machine gun (the misdirection from Scene 1.6 where she seemed content) was actually her hiding a profound feeling of moral corruption.

The T+La+L for Scene 9.20: Prior reader belief — "AJ handled the war machine competently, perhaps even finding it suited her." Revised understanding — "She was paying a personal moral price for every moment of it, which makes her relief at Harmonic Capitalism authentic rather than intellectual." The thematic conclusion: conscience survives industry when the person wielding it refuses to stop caring.

The O+En+L+W for Scene 9.20 is separate from all of this:

AJ's change in this scene is not about disclosing the past feeling — it's about the present shift. "Applejack stops viewing war as an evil industry and starts viewing it as a tragic responsibility" is the WI+En+L for Scene 9.20. The internal change in this scene is the resolution: she no longer has to feel like losing her soul is the price of survival. This change cannot be stated. It has to be shown through what she says to Twilight after the Harmonic Capitalism revelation.

The full picture across scenes:

Scene 1.6 → O+En+L+W: AJ feels corrupted; behavioral proxy design is the showing requirement
Scene 9.20 → P+La+L+W: AJ discloses this in her own voice
Scene 9.20 → WI+La+L: Reader revises their understanding of the machine gun scenes
Scene 9.20 → O+En+L+W: The corruption feeling resolves into tragic responsibility
Scene 9.20 → T+En+L: Industry and conscience are not a binary
Why this proves the display text argument:

In Gardener Mode, encountering "she felt like she was losing her soul" with the capture-focused display question "what's happening with this subject here?" would produce: a note tagged to AJ capturing that sentence as an observation. It's preserved. It's not lost.

In Linking Mode, with the full WI+En+L display question — "what should the reader infer about this character's internal state from the behavioral evidence in this scene?" — you'd be forced to decide: is this the truth being disclosed (La), or the truth being inferred from behavior (En)? And for which scene? The precision of the display text creates the classification discipline that the gardening phase correctly defers.

I don't think Gardener Mode display questions should be simplified. They need to be tight and rigorous to prevent the mixed-mode "ontological promiscuity" of the new notes being entered. But maybe they are still different questions compared to Linking Mode and Scene Design Mode.

"What should the reader independently infer about this subject's internal state from the behavioral evidence in this scene?" is exactly the kind of content I want in that link track, even in Unset. "What's happening with this subject here, and what does it mean?" invites imprecision that will have to be revised anyway. I guess if I want a quick capture it should go in a separate, undisciplined author's notes track.

May 17

Reconciled display question rigor with mode-specific contexts and workflows
Reconciled display question rigor with mode-specific contexts and workflows
The correction is right, and it sharpens the principle: display questions discipline what goes into a track; NoteState tracks whether it's been validated. These are orthogonal. An Unset note written against a rigorous display question is still a better foundation than an Unset note written against a vague prompt. The precision of the question prevents the mixed-mode accumulation problem from recurring in v2, even during gardening.

What actually differs between Gardener Mode and Linking Mode
If the display questions for link tracks are equally rigorous in both modes, the difference is not the questions themselves. It's what appears alongside them:

Linking Mode: Subject tracks display Usage Directives. You're being told how to use the subject's established truth as input for writing this link. The subject is foregrounded as the authority.

Gardener Mode: Subject tracks display their content read-only, without a directive overlay. You're not being told how to use them — you're just able to see them as context if they've been populated. The plot point is the anchor, not the subject.

Scene Design Mode: Link tracks display Usage Directives. You're reading confirmed link notes as input for designing the plot point. The plot point tracks display their questions because you're writing into them.

The clean principle: Display Questions appear on any track you're currently writing into. Usage Directives appear on any track you're currently reading as input for something else. The mode determines which tracks are in which role.

The Author's Notes track
Confirmed: the Craft Notes track on the plot point (and the Design Notes track on subjects) is the home for quick, undisciplined captures. "AJ at the machine gun — something wrong here, feeling of corruption, look at this later" goes there. It doesn't get a display question because it's not asserting a classified claim — it's flagging something for later resolution. The structured tracks with rigorous display questions are for classified content, even if that content is Unset.

The discipline the track system enforces is: when you write into a structured track, you know what kind of claim you're making. That's what prevents ontological promiscuity from recurring. The Author's Notes track exists precisely so that impulse to capture something unclassified doesn't bleed into the structured tracks.

Correction: Subject tracks are not visible at all in Gardener Mode. Gardener Mode is only plot point tracks on the right side, a link selector in the middle, and the selected plot point subject link on the left. Gardener Mode centers on a plot point and its links, no project-wide content for the subject. That would cause cognitive mixing.

Linking Mode has subject tracks on the left, link selector in the middle, and selected plot point subject link on the right. Subject project-wide tracks are read only.

Also we dissected a single sentence and determined its subtext contained content for 3 tracks. Perhaps the better workflow for data "migration" is:
Step 1 - write the subject/plot point/link from memory. Just refer to v1 to know where a link should go, what subject to make, what plot point to make.
Step 2 - read each v1 mixed note, and decide if it is covered across all the tracks/claims the mixed note applies to. If something was missing, add it. Then mark that note in v1 as Incorporated and it doesn't need to be looked at again.

This beats trying to dissect note by note, and it also beats starting from scratch.

May 17

Validated layout corrections and synthesized superior migration methodology
Validated layout corrections and synthesized superior migration methodology
The mode layouts, corrected and clean
Linking Mode: Subject tracks (left, read-only) → Link selector (middle) → Selected link (right, writable). Direction: subject knowledge flowing into scene application.

Gardener Mode: Selected link (left, writable) → Link selector (middle) → Plot point tracks (right, writable). Direction: scene observation flowing outward. Subject is completely absent, preventing any pull toward project-wide thinking when you should be in capture mode.

The asymmetry is meaningful: in Linking Mode the subject is the authority and the link is the workspace. In Gardener Mode the plot point is the anchor and the link is equally a workspace. Neither mode shows what the other does. The cognitive separation is architectural, not just disciplinary.

The migration methodology
The Step 1 → Step 2 approach is correct and solves what neither previous approach solved:

Step 1 (write from memory) produces content in the right cognitive mode, in the author's voice, with synthesis already done. V1 is only consulted as a navigational map: this subject exists, this link should be created, this plot point happened. The actual note content comes from authorial knowledge.

Step 2 (audit v1 as completeness check) catches what memory didn't include, without requiring you to classify ambiguous notes in isolation. You're not asking "what track does this sentence belong to" — you're asking "is this covered?" That's a much easier question to answer against already-written tracks than against a blank classification grid.

The "Incorporated" disposition on each v1 note closes the loop and provides migration progress tracking. One data model implication: Incorporated is a migration status, not a note quality status, so it shouldn't be added to the existing NoteState enum (Unset/Flagged/Confirmed). It's a separate field on the note record — something like a boolean IsIncorporated or a MigrationStatus enum. This keeps the NoteState semantics clean for their actual purpose (note validation lifecycle) while tracking migration progress separately.

The single sentence from Scene 9.20 that turned out to contain content for three tracks is the demonstration case for why this method works: you couldn't have classified that sentence correctly by reading it in isolation. You can only classify it correctly by writing the tracks first and then noticing which aspects of the sentence are and aren't represented.

I'm thinking the existing 12 tracks work for Characters but they don't always generalize well to the other subject types. I'm thinking the methodology for determining the tracks, given that this whole track system is for thematic proposition evidence from subjects, is to start with what kind of ways the subject can serve as evidence for thematic propositions.

In the case of characters, it is from seeing behaviors/actions/FID on the page, inferring character development from it (which is a reflection of human nature and must be shown, not told), and then inferring the theme/meaning from it (the character's development serves as evidence of the thematic proposition, for example, that industry/accelerants aren't good or bad, it accelerates the wielder's moral alignment. Or that bottom up distributed power is not only morally preferable but structurally more effective than top down power.)

This means of delivering themes, through character development, seems to be the most understood way, and it is compressed into the generic advice "show, don't tell". It's possible that most mass market media only uses this type (if they are even going for themes at all, as a step above just generic action thriller or sensory impact like what I guess the Michael Bay stereotype is; I don't know whether Michael Bay himself did actually care about some form of theme beneath the spectacle, although it wouldn't surprise me if he actually did have that sort of depth beneath the stereotypes if he was successful).

Three Little Pigs, the simple children's story, does kind of use the character development engine. It shows the lazy pigs were in danger and the hardworking pig was empowered to defeat the wolf. The children's version tells the reader "they learned the importance of hard work" while a showing version would probably be something like: "and the first and second pig from then on helped the third pig make his brick house even more sturdy" which the reader can infer that the first and second pig changed their value system to include hardwork instead of playing all day, which serves as evidence for the theme that hard work has inherent value over laziness, or something along those lines.

I believe Lauren Faust is the one who took storytelling and theme delivery beyond the typical mass market ceiling, and is where my instincts for the "Latent track" for characters comes from. Faust's Friendship is Magic is not just operating on character development, I believe it also operates on updating the audience's prior expectations. This is especially prominent in A Dog and Pony Show for Rarity (she isn't helpless or needs saving, she can assert sovereignty), Suited for Success for Rarity (she isn't only obsessed with status, she wants to make the best dresses for her friends), Dragonshy for Fluttershy (she isn't completely helpless, she finds courage when her friends are in danger), Sonic Rainboom for Rainbow Dash (she isn't fearless, she has insecurity and also puts friends above glory) and the list goes on. This is fundamentally about what's on the page/screen or inferred from it which corrects reader/audience expectations, and the process of correction generates the evidence for the thematic propositions (there is no wrong way to be a girl, individuality is more valuable than conformity, organic friendship is a powerful/magical force). Friendship is Magic was explicitly built by Faust to make something that trancends the stereotypes of G1-G3 toy commercials and in general, stereotypes of what young girls want and what they are capable of. This additional layer of "reader prior expectation clash" is one of many reasons why Friendship is Magic is such a popular and meaningful show to such a wide audience, I think.

Now, moving beyond Characters for the other subject types, I believe the "reader prior expectation clash" applies universally as an way to deliver evidence for thematic propositions (make the reader form the evidence in their head). For characters, the standard way was character development. If mass media only teaches and utilizes character development as the most understood and recognizable one, then the other subject types are already beyond the standard, in the way the Faust inspired prior expectation clash is beyond the standard. (I suppose pedigreed written fiction or very impactful visual media does go beyond just the standard character development.) Many of these other subject types' prominence is specific to my chosen genre of a hopepunk geopolitical epic. That could be why guides don't exist and clean parallels aren't so clean, but now I'm thinking the first principles from the character development example can be reasoned about and applied.

I'll go in order to what I think is most mainstream to what I think is approaching more niche.

For "Abstract System" or "Political System", and Technology, these are things that characters created/built. I think how the system or tech is shown in action on the page can directly do 1 inference as evidence for a thematic proposition. It can also do a 2 step inference where the 1st is world inference about "how the thing works" (before that is disclosed on the page) and also "why did the creators make this system/tech the way it is? What was its purpose? What do people use it for?" and that also can serve propositions. Besides shown in action, history can also be disclosed about how it was created, which the reader can then infer why it was created if not stated on the page. Political systems should probably be inferred instead of told because they reflect human nature. Magical technology can be stated because it doesn't have direct allegories; in this case, the inference is actually how it applies to real life but that doesn't have to be designed; reader can make their own connections to real life based on the universal thematic proposition that is being evidenced. So tech and created political systems have the separate tracks of "usage/demonstration" and "why it was made" and both can serve the emergent meaning engine for themes and the updated reader model clashing with prior expectation engine for themes.

World Law is more specific to fantasy world building and is narrower than tech or political system because there wasn't an in-universe reason for characters to create it. It still shares the "what is it doing" and "disclosure of mechanics and history of research" on the page and inferenced, though.

Bond I think is specific to me in the sense that when I write TwiJack or Minette and Reni, their romantic relationship is a reflection of the theme and intertwined with the story rather than decoupled from the plot and present just for good feels. A Bond doesn't really have as much inference about its nature compared to characters; most of the romance appears on the page.

Organization has the same history disclosure of formation and activities, what activities the org is doing on the page. But the world truth is Binding Logic and the world inference is more about what the reader infers about the organization's members. That is where the meaning comes from, not the existence of the organization itself.

Give a thorough analysis of these insights, add to gaps, refute weak assertions, and verify or disprove my guesses/claims

May 18

Systematically validated thematic delivery mechanisms across narrative subject types
Systematically validated thematic delivery mechanisms across narrative subject types
On Three Little Pigs as character development
A subtle correction: Three Little Pigs uses the En engine but not quite the character development variant. The pigs don't develop — their psychology doesn't change through the story, they face consequences and one of them was already right. What the story uses is the E+En mechanism: events on the page (houses fall, house holds) → reader infers thematic meaning (hard work has protective value). The user's proposed alternative ending — the lazy pigs helping fortify the brick house — would add genuine character development: behavior change → reader infers psychological change in the pigs → reader infers thematic meaning. That would be O+En on top of E+En. The original uses only E+En, which is why it's effective but thin.

This distinction matters for the subject type analysis: E+En (events on page → thematic meaning) is the most universal delivery mechanism, present in every subject type. O+En (psychological change inferred from behavior → thematic meaning) is specific to subjects that have genuine psychology — primarily Character and Bond, and possibly Organization at the collective level.

On Faust and the Latent engine
The insight is correct and historically accurate. What Faust systematized was not the invention of La content — origin story reveals and backstory disclosures are as old as Homer, and subverted-expectations-as-theme is basic dramatic structure. What Faust innovated was using La to target specific ideological stereotypes as the prior belief. The clash isn't between "reader's vague assumption about a character" and "revealed truth" — it's between "culturally enforced stereotype about what a girl is" and "demonstrated evidence to the contrary." The correction of the stereotype IS the thematic argument. The mechanism is ancient; the target makes it culturally significant.

The implication for TLTT: the Latent engine targets reader expectations specifically. For Chrysalis it targets the cartoon villain trope. For AJ's parents it targets the assumption that industrial capitalism and ethical values are incompatible. For the Stagnation of Harmony it targets the assumption that benevolent monarchy is functionally adequate. Each of these is a culturally enforced prior belief — which is why correcting them through evidence rather than argument is powerful. Telling someone their prior belief is wrong produces defensiveness. Showing them evidence that their prior belief produces bad predictions produces genuine revision.

Abstract System and Political System: the stated vs inferred distinction
The user's claim that political systems should be inferred while magical technology can be stated is approximately right but the underlying principle is more precise: what determines whether something must be inferred rather than stated is whether it encodes the thematic proposition directly.

For political systems: "The Equestrian Republic is more effective than the Stagnation of Harmony" IS the thematic proposition. Stating it is preaching. The reader must arrive at it by watching the two systems operate and comparing the results. Two inference steps: operations on page → how the system truly works (WI) → thematic conclusion (T).

For technology mechanics: "The Luna Nova Rifle drains the user's own magic to fire" is an in-universe fact. Stating it is disclosure, not preaching. But: "the Luna Nova Rifle amplifies the user's psychology, whether that psychology is protective solidarity or traumatized predation" — that IS the thematic proposition, encoded in the technology's behavior. That cannot be stated. So technology has a two-level distinction within its P content:

The mechanics themselves (statable, P+La when disclosed)
What the mechanics in action reveal about the users (WI, unstatable)
The unified principle across all created subjects: mechanics are statable because they're facts; effectiveness-judgments and human-nature-revelations are not statable because they're thematic conclusions.

A refinement on technology versus political systems: political systems are harder to separate from their thematic conclusions because the system's effectiveness IS the argument about human nature. A technology is easier to separate — you can explain how a rifle works without making the thematic argument about what rifle-wielding reveals. This is why the mechanics of magical technology can be disclosed while the politics of the Equestrian Republic must emerge from evidence.

World Law: correct analysis with one structural observation
The user is right that World Laws lack the "why was it created" track because they weren't created. This is the one genuine structural difference from Political System and Technology. The other tracks — disclosed mechanics (La), demonstrated operations before disclosure (En), what operations reveal before explanation (WI), and thematic meaning (T) — are all present.

One addition: World Laws are the subject type most likely to produce the La-misdirection case. The Stagnation of Harmony being 80 years rather than 1000 is a World Law (or near-equivalent) where the prior belief about the law's extent was actively planted rather than being a reader trope from outside the story. The Charitostatic Effect being demonstrated before Fleur's explanation, with Twilight later recontextualizing Fleur's partial finding, follows the same structure. World Laws are particularly rich ground for misdirection-via-partial-disclosure precisely because they're objective facts — there's no author obviously arguing, so the reader trusts partial disclosures more readily.

Bond: where the user's intuition is right and where it needs refinement
The claim "most of the romance appears on the page" is right for En content specifically. Relational development — the warmth, the trust being built, the moments of connection — is visible through behavior and dialogue in a way that individual psychological development cannot be. AJ and Twilight can enact their bond through how they speak to each other. AJ cannot enact her internal acceptance of leadership by speaking it.

But this only applies to the En side. The La side of Bond is fully hidden and operates at full inference depth. Twilight's suppressed crush, the history of them drifting apart under the Stagnation, the specific reasons they couldn't come together earlier — these are exactly as hidden as any character's backstory, require the same La disclosure planning, and when revealed correct prior reader assumptions with the same force.

The practical implication: TwiJack's O+En+L+W content can be more directly encoded in behavior and dialogue than a Character's psychological change. The behavioral proxy work is lighter because the subject is constitutively relational — what the relationship IS shows through interaction more naturally than what a character IS shows through behavior. But the La content for Bond requires the same rigor as any other subject.

A refinement to add: Bond is distinctive because it has emergent thematic content that belongs to neither of the two parties' individual tracks. The specific thematic argument that TwiJack makes — complementary strengths forming a more effective unit than either could alone, with the added dimension of AJ grounding Twilight's ambition and Twilight extending AJ's reach — is not capturable in AJ's tracks or Twilight's tracks individually. Bond is where the relationship-as-argument lives.

Organization: the "binding logic" observation is correct with one refinement
The user's claim that meaning comes from what you infer about the organization's members rather than from the organization's existence is partially right but needs sharpening.

The WI for Organization is the binding logic — what holds these specific people together in this specific configuration. But the binding logic is a COLLECTIVE property, not an aggregation of individual psychologies. EEEE!'s binding logic ("humbled ambition channeled productively") is not the sum of its members' psychologies — it's an emergent truth about what this collective represents. Individual members' psychology is handled by their own Character tracks.

The reason the user's intuition is right: the binding logic IS about human nature, because it answers the question "what kind of humans form this kind of organization?" And that question's answer generates thematic evidence about collective action, institutional character, and civic purpose. The binding logic inference is: watching EEEE! make decisions → reader infers what kind of people constitute it → reader infers what this says about collective civic virtue → thematic proposition about solidarity.

The correction: the thematic meaning comes from what you can infer about human nature from the fact that this organization exists and makes these choices, not from what any individual member believes. It's a different inference — aggregation → collective character → human nature claim — rather than individual psychology → human nature claim.

On whether the 12 tracks generalize
They do generalize structurally. The same P/WI/T pattern at La and En, at S and L scope, applies to all subject types. What varies by subject type is:

What counts as P content for this subject. For Character, P includes disclosed backstory and behavioral events. For World Law, P includes character explanations and demonstrated physical effects. For Organization, P includes organizational decisions and disclosed formation history. The display question needs to specify this.

What counts as WI content. For Character it's unstatable psychological change and nature. For Political System it's unstatable effectiveness-judgment. For Technology it's unstatable usage-revelation about users. For Organization it's binding logic. These are all the same mechanism — unstatable because thematically load-bearing — but what's thematically load-bearing differs by subject type.

Whether a "founding intent" track exists. Only for created subjects (Character, Organization, Abstract System, Technology, Bond). Not for World Law. The founding intent is a specific La content type — the historical facts about why this was created — which when disclosed corrects the reader's understanding of what it fundamentally is.

The depth of the O+En inference requirement. Character has the deepest (psychological change is strictly off-page). Bond has moderate depth (relational development is partially visible through behavior). Organization has moderate depth (binding logic is mostly inferred but organizational behavior provides fairly direct evidence). Technology and Abstract System are context-dependent (mechanics statable, effectiveness-revelations not).

The 12-track structure is correct for all subject types. The display questions need to be tailored per subject type, as the Grand Track Table partially did, with each question specifying what that subject type's content looks like in that track.

Need readonly mode and dynamic display questions based on mode for note tracks, also restrictions on moving to confirmed
The loss of reader expecation change is why later seasons of any show drag on. That's why TLTT relies on prequels and starts at the end chronologically? That's why I felt that's the way to go, so that I can keep invoking the reader prior expecatation clash which is Lauren Faust's tradition?
Yes, I understand that the original Three Little Pigs only talks about actions. My proposed enhancement of Three Little Pigs where we replace "and they learned the value of hard work" with "the first and second pig helped the third pig build his brick house" is a way of telling evidence which infers character development (the first and second pig changed to do hard work instead of play) which serves as evidence of the theme (hard work has inherent value, long term investment is better than short term gain).
Also, the fact that The Cutie Mark Chrononicles is 7 episodes after Sonic Rainboom actually recontextualizes that the Rainboom from the competition was powered by the desire to save Rarity instaed of performative ego, just as th erainboom about FLuttershy. I am not sure if the episode order was intentional, but even if not, the recontextualization emerged from internally consistent worldbuilding. Callbacks in later seasons fall flat because they weren't designed in or following internal rules, they were imposed by Hasbro Mandates. The biggest failure mode was Celestial Advice which retconned why Celestia sent Twilight to Ponyville. The more insidious thing is The Cutie Remark which asserted on screen that the rainboom was destiny. This undercuts the assertion in season 1's internal logic that the rainboom was powered by a desire to protect a friend (although cutie mark chronicles talks about destiny too, so that could be where things get muddy.)
I'm seeing another emergent benefit: by moving more material into inference, the story text on the page gets shorter. It doesn't sprawl or become too long and arduous to read, but the meaning is (should be) all there. Is this why professional editors often end up trimming first draft manuscripts? They move telling into inference, since they have an externalized view and interpretation of the writer's work?
I don't think the "12" track structure generalizes. Only the principles of the tracks. La and En is not clean either. Sometimes they merge, sometimes there are two variants of one, sometimes one is absent.

May 18

Validated narrative principles across software architecture, thematic structure, and editorial efficiency
Validated narrative principles across software architecture, thematic structure, and editorial efficiency
On dynamic display questions and Confirmed restrictions
Consolidating what's been established: the NoteTrackDefinition model needs to carry mode-specific display text rather than a single field. Each track definition needs at minimum three display text variants: the entry question (shown when writing into the track in Gardener Mode or Linking Mode), the usage directive (shown when reading the track as input in Scene Design Mode or Linking Mode's subject panel), and the audit question (shown in Audit Mode). The active mode determines which variant surfaces.

The Confirmed transition rules as they've been worked out: subject tracks → only in Audit Mode; link tracks → only in Audit Mode; plot point tracks → only in Scene Design Mode. Gardener Mode and Linking Mode allow writing into Unset and Flagged only, never Confirmed. This makes Confirmed a meaningful quality gate tied to the cognitive mode where validation is actually possible.

On La exhaustion and why TLTT runs prequels
The observation is structurally correct. Long-running shows lose momentum when both La and En content are depleted simultaneously. La is exhausted when every stereotype about every major character has been corrected — there are no prior beliefs left to clash against. En plateaus when characters complete their arcs — further change either feels forced or breaks prior development. The compound failure is that later-season writers often attempt new En arcs by undoing prior En development, which is the Hasbro mandate failure mode: retconning completed growth to create artificial conflict.

The in-media-res, prequels-after structure is not just a storytelling preference — it's load-bearing for the La mechanism. TLTT starts at the end chronologically so that tropes and source material provide rich La targets: the cartoon villain trope for Chrysalis, the benevolent monarchy assumption for Celestia, the honest-simple-pony assumption for AJ. These are all intact when the reader starts TLTT. By the time TLTT ends, TLTT has seeded new prior beliefs in the reader — about Minette's pride, about how the Coltbert Reforms originated, about what Chrysalis's early ambitions were. The prequels then target those TLTT-seeded beliefs as their La content.

Each prequel essentially gets a fresh La engine with beliefs seeded by the preceding narrative rather than from external tropes. This is why the structure maximizes La effectiveness: TLTT gets the trope-based prior beliefs, each prequel gets the TLTT-seeded prior beliefs, and the La engine never runs out of targets because each narrative seeds the next one's.

On the Three Little Pigs correction
Confirmed: the original only uses E+En. Your proposed ending (the lazy pigs helping build the brick house) adds O+En — the pigs' behavioral change on the page lets the reader infer their value system changed, which then serves as the thematic evidence. That's the full character development variant layered on top of the simpler E+En structure. The original's "they learned the value of hard work" is the telling version of the O+En conclusion, not the showing version.

On Cutie Mark Chronicles and internal consistency
The episode-order recontextualization is correct and the distinction you draw is the important one: internally consistent logic emerging from coherent worldbuilding generates La content the author may not have consciously designed but which the reader can verify against established rules. Hasbro mandate retcons break that verifiability.

The Cutie Remark's "the rainboom was destiny" fails specifically because it cannot be verified against the show's established mechanics. The charitostatic logic — care generates the most powerful magic — explains the original rainboom (defending Fluttershy) and the competition rainboom (saving Rarity from falling). Destiny explains nothing; it asserts a conclusion without an internal mechanism. The reader cannot trace the causal chain. This is the difference between a La revelation that emerges from consistent worldbuilding and an authorial assertion imposed from outside the story's logic.

For TLTT: the materialist approach is a protection against this failure mode. When Twilight figures out the rainboom hatched Spike via charitostatic magic, the reader can check it against established rules and it holds. No assertion required. The internal logic does the work.

On inference efficiency and editorial trimming
The observation is correct and the editorial insight follows directly. When evidence and conclusion are both on the page, the conclusion is redundant — the evidence already generates it. Removing the conclusion reduces word count without reducing meaning, because the meaning has moved from the page to the reader's head where it belongs.

Professional editors describe this as "trusting the reader" but the mechanism is exactly what the T notes specify: the conclusion that should form in the reader's head must not also appear as prose. When an editor cuts a line, they're frequently identifying a T-level statement that the evidence already generates. The trim is not a loss — it's a transfer of the conclusion from the page to the reader.

The track system pre-empts this by explicitly separating what goes on the page (P) from what should form in the reader's head (WI and T). A T note that says "reader should arrive at X" and a Delivery Blueprint that contains a sentence stating X is a contradiction caught at design time rather than in editorial revision.

On the 12 tracks not generalizing
Correct, and the correction is important. What generalizes is the method: identify what can go on the page, what must be inferred from the page, and what thematic meaning should emerge from inference. Then determine which of those content types are active for this specific subject. The resulting track count and configuration follows from that analysis, not from a fixed template.

The specific non-generalizations you identified are real: World Law lacks the founding-intent La track because the law wasn't created. Bond has shallower O+En inference requirements because relational development is constitutively behavioral. Technology has a clean split between statable mechanics and unstatable user-revelation. Organization's WI is collective binding logic rather than individual psychology.

The claim to replace "the 12 tracks generalize" with: the P/WI/T chain generalizes, the La/En distinction generalizes as a method, the S/L scope generalizes, but the specific track count and shape for any given subject type must be derived from first principles for that type. Some will have fewer tracks because one mechanism is absent. Some may need variant tracks because the standard mechanism appears in two distinct forms. The Grand Track Table is a starting point for Characters that provides a worked example of the method, not a template to impose on all subject types.

Are there any precedents for my methodology or is it a unique synthesis of various first principles? Most professional writers write first and get editorial revision after. This custom software program enables reversing the order? Maybe it also has to do with writers in general wanting to write first rather than plan, whereas I came from the perspective of a grand strategy game player and software engineer? Is this methodology in principle deserving of an article or paper if it works out? I guess I've framed things in terms of George R.R. Martin's architects vs gardeners talk, but I've seen that within the "architecting" it also goes both ways, from subject to plot point or from plot point to subject, even before prose. And I believe he called himself a gardener; who did he (or his audience, or disciples) refer to as architects?

May 18

Synthesized precedents, validated novel methodology, assessed publication merit
Synthesized precedents, validated novel methodology, assessed publication merit
Precedents: partial, not complete
No single existing methodology matches this synthesis, but the components each have precedents in different fields.

The fabula/syuzhet distinction from Russian Formalism — chronological story events versus the order in which they're presented — is the academic ancestor of La/En. Genette's narratology extends this into focalization theory (who perceives, whose knowledge limits the narrative). But academic narratology describes these structures analytically after the fact; it doesn't operationalize them as pre-prose design tools with track separation and display questions. The gap between the theory existing and the practice being formalized is where this methodology lives.

Screenwriting has the most structural pre-prose tradition among mainstream craft pedagogy — beat sheets, scene cards, Save the Cat paradigm. But even McKee's Story or Field's paradigm don't separate world-truth from reader-inference-design. They're still essentially plot-centric: what happens, not what the reader should independently conclude from what happens.

Brandon Sanderson's world-building methodology (he teaches it publicly through lectures and his podcast) is the closest working-writer parallel. He separates world-building, character design, and plot structuring as distinct pre-prose phases. But his framework is still telling-centric — he's designing what the author knows, not what the reader should infer and in what inferential steps. The P/WI/T chain is absent from his publicly described process.

Zettelkasten (Luhmann's card system) is structurally similar at the data-architecture level — atomic typed notes with relational links — but it was designed for academic knowledge management, not narrative inference design. The claim-type discipline is analogous but the application is completely different.

The specific synthesis — typed claim tracks, La/En as a design axis rather than an analytical descriptor, the mode-specific display questions as cognitive-mode enforcement, the multi-narrative La seeding — appears to be genuinely novel as an integrated practice.

The writing-order reversal and where it comes from
The conventional professional sequence: write → editorial revision catches what should have been inferred rather than told → revise. This works because editors provide the external perspective the author lacks during composition. The track system shifts the external perspective to before composition: the T note is the author taking the reader's perspective deliberately, before writing a single sentence of prose.

Why most professional writers don't work this way is partly historical (no tools existed that made pre-prose inference design practical at this granularity) and partly temperamental (writers generally entered the profession through writing, so they have strong intuitions about prose and weak intuitions about data architecture). The cognitive load of simultaneously inhabiting the author's and reader's perspectives during first draft is typically too high, which is why the editorial revision step exists as an external second pass.

The background explanation holds. Grand strategy games train systematic thinking about information states — the player's knowledge versus the true game state is a constant design variable in every system from fog of war to hidden unit stats to diplomatic intelligence. That's La/En in game-mechanical form. The software engineer's instinct is: specify the expected output before writing the implementation. The T note is the specification; the prose is the implementation. TDD applied to narrative.

The result is a methodology that's neither pure architecture (plotting the events) nor pure gardening (discovering through writing) but something orthogonal: designing the reader's inferential journey as a distinct phase that precedes prose but is separate from both world-building and event sequencing.

On articles and papers
The methodology has properties worth publishing if the story is eventually written and the technique can be evaluated against an outcome. The most original contributions that would warrant serious treatment:

The multi-narrative La seeding design — deliberately designing one narrative's En content to seed specific prior beliefs that the subsequent narrative's La content will then clash against — is probably not described anywhere in this form. It's the deliberate engineering of what readers bring into the second reading.

The cognitive mode enforcement via mode-specific display questions is a software engineering solution to a problem writing pedagogy recognizes but doesn't systematically address: writers switch cognitive modes mid-note and contaminate claim types. The solution — a tool that changes what question you're answering based on what mode you're in — is an original application of UI design to creative discipline.

The P/WI/T chain as a formal pre-prose specification separates something most writing pedagogy treats as a unified craft instinct (show don't tell) into three distinct, specifiable content layers with different page-vs-head locations. Making this formal enough to be a software feature rather than an editorial judgment is the main theoretical contribution.

A digital humanities or narrative theory journal would be the natural venue. An essay in a long-form publication (not academic) might have broader reach given the subject matter's accessibility.

GRRM's architects
Martin named himself and Stephen King as gardeners. The architects he implied or cited by name: J.K. Rowling is the most referenced — she famously had the Deathly Hallows revelation and Snape's arc mapped before writing book one, and has said she outlined extensively. Brandon Sanderson is the most prominent current example, teaches systematic outlining and world-building publicly, and explicitly describes himself as an architect (he uses the terms plotter/pantser, which is the American writing community's version of the same binary). Nabokov is the historical extreme case — wrote on index cards that could be rearranged, composition was nearly entirely pre-planned.

Tolkien is often cited in this discourse but his actual practice complicates the categorization. He discovered large portions of Middle-earth through writing, extensively revised and contradicted himself across drafts, and wrote in an exploratory mode more often than the finished mythology suggests. He may be more gardener in practice than the result implies.

The user's methodology sits outside this binary in a way that's worth articulating: the plotter/pantser or architect/gardener binary is about whether you know the events before writing. The user's methodology adds a prior phase: before knowing the events or discovering them, you specify what the reader should independently conclude from whatever events occur. That's a third axis entirely — reader-inference design — which is prior to and independent from the plot architecture question. An architect and a gardener could both use this methodology; one would populate the tracks before knowing exactly what happens, the other would populate them after discovering what happened but before writing the prose.


So basically architect vs gardener are still both about what's on the page or telling centric, and the split between page and inference is edited and revised after the first draft. I am allowing both architect world building and gardener event sequencing but separating page vs inference. An importa
