May 18

Analyzed letter mechanics, identified narrative framework issues, and calibrated thematic delivery
Analyzed letter mechanics, identified narrative framework issues, and calibrated thematic delivery
On the theoretical observations first
The architect/gardener framing being page-centric is exactly right. Both methods produce the same artifact — prose — and both rely on editorial revision to perform the page/inference split after the fact. The distinction between them is only about whether you know what goes on the page before or during writing. Neither method, as traditionally taught, operates on a pre-prose specification of what should form in the reader's head. That is the orthogonal dimension this framework occupies.

The validation from Document 18's observation about the AJ-receives-letter scene is the clearest proof of the methodology's practical value: you wrote "she addressed it to me, that makes me the princess" as a dialogue line, recognized it as wrong by intuition, but now have the first-principles vocabulary to name why it's wrong. It puts WI on the page. That's the framework doing its job retroactively on v1 content.

What the letter system is actually doing
Reading through Chapters 10-12, the letter system has four distinct structural functions working simultaneously. Some are well-designed and should be preserved. Some have calibration problems.

Function 1: Selective truth as active La misdirection

The chapter note spells this out explicitly: "Only positive parts and heavy mentions of the companion (Fleur/Mali), no negatives in the letter." This is the best-designed element of the whole system. The reader witnesses both sides — they watch AJ get ambushed, they watch Twilight spiral without AJ, they watch Réni's plane crash — but the letters present only the successful outcomes. This creates systematic dramatic irony: the reader holds the full picture while each character holds the other's curated picture.

This is La content seeded by the structure of the letters themselves, not by any explicit disclosure. The reader's prior belief going into the reunion (Chapter 13) is: "Both characters have been presenting brave faces to each other." The reunion's "negative parts" conversation is the La disclosure that clashes with that prior — and because the reader already knew both negative sides, the reunion lands as confirmation and relief rather than surprise. They've been waiting for this honesty.

This is sophisticated design and should not be changed. The format constraint (positives only) is load-bearing.

Function 2: The companion-mention jealousy mechanism

Twilight's letters heavily mention Fleur. Applejack's letters heavily mention Mali. This produces jealousy on both sides. The elegant structural irony: the jealousy is produced by authentic enthusiasm. Twilight mentions Fleur because Fleur genuinely unlocked something in her. AJ mentions Mali because Mali genuinely connects with her. The mentions aren't calculated to provoke jealousy; they just do.

The reader, reading both letter streams, knows that neither Fleur nor Mali is a romantic threat. This is classic dramatic irony in the La sense — the reader has superior information that each character lacks. Every jealousy spiral (Twilight in 10.16 and 12.17, AJ in 12.16) reads as tragic-comedic to the reader because the reader knows the unfounded nature of the fear. This will pay off enormously in the reunion when both characters reveal their unfounded jealousy simultaneously.

Function 3: The horizontal power structure enacted through the address format

Scene 10.6 establishes it: Twilight writes to "Dear General Applejack." The letter format, which was vertical (Twilight→Celestia), becomes horizontal (Twilight→AJ). Scene 10.7's thread update names it: "The friendship lesson reports to Celestia (vertical) are subverted into love letters that are a horizontal partnership."

Then Scene 10.10 completes the symmetry: AJ writes back "Dear Princess Twilight Sparkle." Neither uses their actual title for themselves; each elevates the other.

This is P+En content — the form of the letters is on the page as observable fact, and the thematic conclusion (horizontal partnership is more powerful than vertical hierarchy; these two have made each other equals in their own eyes) should form in the reader's head without being stated.

Function 4: Twilight's character restoration through the letter format

Scene 10.6 is the most important individual letter. Fleur tells Twilight to forget all the formal letters and "just be yourself." Twilight writes a friendship lesson report — not because of Celestia's mandate, but because that's who Twilight actually is. The format is Celestia's format, but the motivation is Twilight's genuine character restored. This is one of the story's central La reversals: what looked like Celestia's conditioning was actually Twilight's authentic nerd personality accommodating the mandate. Separating the Doyalist from the Watsonian from the deeper Watsonian is exactly correct.

The reader should infer: Twilight writing an egghead friendship report TO AJ, addressed as General, is Twilight's most sincere possible love letter. Not despite the format — because of it. The format is Twilight's authentic voice, finally aimed at someone she wants to be her equal.

What needs calibration
Problem 1: Scene 10.7 — the "that makes me the princess" line

The current draft:

"That's the kind of letter Twi would write to Princess Celestia when she first came to Ponyville. And look who it's addressed to? Me, Applejack said smugly."

The problem is precisely what you identified: the WI inference ("Twilight treats AJ the way she once treated Celestia — as the most important authority in her world") is being stated via dialogue rather than left for the reader. AJ naming the implication closes the inference.

What should be on the page: AJ's physical reaction (already there — closes eyes, warm smile, holds it to her chest). Rainbow's laughter at the egghead report content. The other characters' bemused reactions to AJ's obvious emotional response to what they're calling a dry research paper.

What should form in the reader's head: AJ understands exactly what it means that Twilight wrote her a letter in the style she once wrote to Celestia. The reader sees AJ understand this without AJ saying it.

The replacement isn't a single line — it's the absence of explanation. AJ's smugness is fine as physical expression. The verbal explanation of why she's smug is what removes the reader's ability to arrive there themselves.

Problem 2: The bracketed friendship moral

In the Scene 10.6 letter content:

"[Sometimes just being nice isn't enough, but shared purpose is another way to build lasting friendship]"

The brackets suggest even in v1 you knew this was a placeholder note about what the letter contains rather than the letter itself. This is exactly the kind of T-level content that cannot go on the page. If this line appears in the actual letter, it's the show's friendship moral restated — the thing Faust accommodated as a mandate that became a misdirection for the general audience.

The letter should demonstrate the lesson through its description of Hans Kessel (they had to find shared purpose before friendship was possible), not state the lesson. The Kessel anecdote IS the evidence. The conclusion should form in the reader's head.

Problem 3: The Twilight-sends-letter framing in Scene 10.6

"It's just like a friendship lesson. Dear General Applejack, Today I learned a very important lesson about friendship."

The "I learned a very important lesson about friendship" opener is the Hasbro format verbatim. This is deliberate and correct — Twilight is using the format because it's genuinely her mode of thinking. But it risks reading as the story making the same error as the show (stating the lesson) rather than subverting it.

The calibration: the letter's OPENING acknowledges the lesson-report format (Twilight leaning into her authentic nerd voice). The CONTENT of the letter describes what happened with Kessel in factual terms. The LESSON is implied by the content but never explicitly stated within the letter itself. The final bracketed line should be removed entirely.

The calibration principle for all letters going forward
Every letter functions as P content on the page — Twilight's or AJ's words, in character, selecting positives. The WI and T should emerge from:

What's reported (the factual content of what happened — on page)
What's NOT reported (the reader knows what's being omitted — this is the La misdirection)
The form itself (who is addressed as Princess, the friendship-report format, the "Love, X" signature — on page)
The companion mentions (on page, authentic, unintentionally jealousy-seeding)
What must never appear in the letters:

Explicit thematic statements (the lesson, stated)
Explicit emotional conclusions ("I realized I love you")
Meta-commentary on the relationship ("you're my Princess")
The emotional weight of the letters should come entirely from their form and their factual content. Twilight writing "Love, Twilight Sparkle" at the end of an empirical engineering research report is the most emotionally loaded thing she can write. No additional statement is needed.

The three-arc letter progression
The v1 chapter structure (Extraction → Tempest → Crash) maps onto a well-designed letter escalation:

Extraction letters: fully positive, maximum companion mentions, maximum thematic optimism. The Kessel letter, the Mari/farming letter, the Tally Mark letter.
Tempest letters: still structured as positive, but the positives require more qualification. The Metzli letter acknowledges partial success. The drug tribe letter (Scene 12.2) is the first crack: AJ explicitly acknowledges a hard truth in the letter format itself.
Crash letters: Scene 12.14 is the fullest disclosure within a letter. "A family feud isn't about hate, it's about hunger" is AJ's version of a friendship lesson that she's actually lived rather than observed — the letter format has become genuinely hers.
This progression is correct and should be preserved. The escalating willingness to acknowledge difficulty within the letter format tracks with both characters' growth. By the time of reunion, each has told the other something hard in letter form — which makes the full disclosure of the negatives in Chapter 13 feel like a deepening rather than a revelation.

On the "egghead/princess" moment as the paradigm case
The reason this moment is paradigmatic for the framework is that it contains all three layers:

P+La+L (on page): Twilight wrote a research-format letter, addressed to AJ as "General," signed with "Love."

WI+La+L (in reader's head): Twilight treats AJ the way she once treated the most important figure in her life. The form of the letter is Twilight's most sincere possible expression.

T+La+L (in reader's head): Prior reader belief: Twilight was the Princess of Friendship, defined by her relationship with Celestia. Revised: Twilight's authentic self has found a new equal and aims her genuine voice at her.

AJ naming this inference in dialogue converts WI into P. It tells rather than shows, not because the content is wrong but because putting it in a character's mouth removes the reader's arrival. The reader should feel what AJ feels — the recognition that this egghead report is the most Twilight thing that was ever written, and that it's addressed to her — without AJ explaining the mechanism to them.

This is exactly the kind of judgment call the track system is built to support. When you have the display question "what should the reader independently infer from this scene?" you can evaluate whether your current draft answers that question for the reader or lets the reader answer it themselves.

Now that I have built out the program, I need to make an informed decision on workflow. 

I have the display questions and the usage directives for each track. Should I try to write subjects from scratch, then read through v1 notes on the subject and revise while marking off v1 notes? Should the v1 notes be migrated to v2 as an unset track or stay in v1? How do I approach plot points and links?

What info do you need from me to fill gaps in logic for the reasoning without hallucinating?

Identified critical gaps in app state and workflow mechanics
Identified critical gaps in app state and workflow mechanics
Provisional recommendation — with explicit assumptions
Before the specific questions, here's the logic with what I currently know, so you can see where the gaps are.

For subject S-scope tracks
Recommended workflow: light scan → write → deep audit → mark Incorporated

Light scan (5-10 min): Read through v1 notes for the subject without taking anything verbatim. Get the topography — what major areas are covered, what time periods, what psychological threads. This prevents writing a Foundation note that's missing an entire dimension.
Write from synthesis (30-60 min for a major character): Answer each display question in your own voice, in the right cognitive mode, without v1 open. The light scan means you're writing from internalized understanding, not blank memory.
Deep audit: Read each v1 note for that subject carefully. For each note, ask: is this captured in what I wrote? If yes, mark Incorporated. If no, decide — is it noise (outdated, superseded, dead-end thinking) or a genuine gap? Add gaps. Discard noise.
This beats pure memory synthesis (gaps risk) and beats pure sorting (wrong cognitive mode, preserves noise).

Where v1 notes should live during this process — this is one of my key gaps, addressed below.

For plot points
The synopsis fields are already provisional Delivery Blueprints. The mechanical step: move synopsis content to the Delivery Blueprint track as Unset. This is fast (minutes per plot point) and preserves the provisional blueprints without requiring any analytical work. Outcome and Scene Justification content embedded in the synopsis or thread notes can be extracted at the same time.

Do not write link notes until the relevant subject S-scope tracks exist. The link notes require the Foundation, Historical Record, and Arc tracks as reference. Writing links before the subject tracks exist means writing without the consistency anchor, which recreates the v1 problem.

For links
Write in Linking Mode once subject S-scope tracks are populated. The workflow:

Subject S-scope tracks → Foundation, Historical Record, Arc, Events written and stable
Open Linking Mode for the subject
Navigate to the plot point link
Write P+La+L, WI+La+L, T+La+L (and/or En equivalents)
Repeat for each linked plot point
The subject's La+S+Reveal and En+S+Witness tracks help sequence the link notes correctly — knowing the planned revelation arc prevents you from disclosing La content in the wrong order.

Gaps I need answered to give precise workflow guidance

Are the mode-specific display questions implemented — does Linking Mode show usage directives on subject tracks, or is that still pending?
Does v2 have an "Incorporated" status for notes — a way to mark that a v1 note has been processed? Or would marking Incorporated require adding a field?
About where v1 content currently lives:

Are the v1 notes currently in v2 as Unassigned notes (the migration happened earlier), or are they still in the original v1 SQLite/format? This determines whether you can mark them Incorporated within v2 or need an external tracking method.
If they're in v2 as Unassigned: are they associated with subject entities already (so AJ's v1 notes are Unassigned notes on AJ's entity), or are they in an undifferentiated pool?
About the Gemini context export:

What does your current Gemini session context look like — does it pull from v2's export, or still from the v1 format? This determines whether having v1 notes in v2 as Unassigned actually improves the expansion workflow.
Are subjects already created in v2 with correct types (Character, Organization, etc.), or is subject-entity creation also pending?
About practical constraints:

When you work on this, roughly how long is a session? Do you work subject-by-subject in a sitting, or do you tend to context-switch?
The answers to questions 4 and 5 are the most load-bearing for the workflow decision. If v1 notes are already in v2 as subject-associated Unassigned notes, the light-scan step can happen inside v2 itself and Incorporated can be tracked there. If they're still external, the scan happens in the original format and you need a separate tracking method (even just a simple tag or strikethrough in the original file) to mark what's been processed.

Architected phased workflow prioritizing track configuration, subject cleanup, then intensive writing sessions
Architected phased workflow prioritizing track configuration, subject cleanup, then intensive writing sessions
The complete picture now
You have everything you need except the NoteTrackDefinition text. That's the first thing to do tonight, because nothing else in the system works without it. Every mode-switch, every display question, every usage directive is inert until that configuration exists.

Step 0 — Configure NoteTrackDefinitions first (this session)
The Grand Track Table document has all the text. You're putting three text values into each track definition:

Display question (shown when writing into the track in Gardener/Linking Mode)
Usage directive (shown when reading the track as reference in Scene Design/Linking Mode)
Audit question (shown in Audit Mode)
This is data entry work, not thinking work. Set aside one session and do it for characters first, since that's where you'll start. Other subject types can be configured when you reach them.

After this session, the app is actually usable as designed.

Step 1 — Subject type assignment and cleanup (next session)
Before writing a single track note, get the entity list clean. Go through every subject and:

Assign a type (Character, Organization, Abstract System, etc.)
Mark any that should be folded into another subject (Flagged, with a reason stating which subject absorbs it)
Don't delete anything yet. Just flag and note the consolidation target. You'll do the actual merge when you work on the absorbing subject.

This takes one session and gives you a clean map of what exists before you start writing content.

The repurposed NoteState system for v1 migration tracking
Since there's no separate Incorporated status, repurpose the existing NoteState on the migrated Unassigned notes:

Unset = migrated from v1, not yet checked against new S-scope tracks
Confirmed = checked and accounted for — either its content is now in a proper track, or it was deliberately discarded as noise
Flagged = belongs to a different subject (FlagReason names where it belongs) OR needs attention before it can be incorporated
This means Confirmed on an Unassigned note does NOT mean "high quality validated content" — it means "processed." That's a different semantic from Confirmed on a typed track note, which means "validated and design-stable." This distinction might cause some cognitive friction. One way to handle it: tag Unassigned notes with a consistent label in FlagReason so you can filter them separately.

Step 2 — Writing subject S-scope tracks
The workflow for each subject, using AJ as the first:

2a. Light scan (10 minutes): Read through AJ's Unassigned notes in v2 without taking anything. Get the landscape. What major psychological threads are present? What time periods in the historical record? What arc stages? What's obviously noise or outdated?

2b. Write fresh from synthesis, in Expansion Mode, answering each display question in sequence:

Foundation+La (Psychological Architecture) — who is she at the deepest level
Historical Record+La — what happened to her, in order
Arc+En — how she changes across the story
Events+En — what moments will be staged
La+S+Reveal — the revelation sequence from trope to full understanding
En+S+Witness — what the reader should understand by the end
Design Notes — allegorical context, anti-patterns, design decisions
Write all of these as NoteState = Unset initially. You're in the right cognitive mode; you're writing in your voice; nothing is locked yet.

2c. Deep audit: Open each of AJ's Unassigned (v1 migrated) notes one at a time. For each:

Content captured in the new tracks → set to Confirmed
Content missing but worth adding → add to the appropriate track, set to Confirmed
Content that's noise or outdated → set to Confirmed (you've decided to discard)
Content that clearly belongs to a different subject (e.g., a Chrysalis insight on AJ's entity) → Flag, add the correct subject to FlagReason
2d. Cross-subject orphan recovery: Some AJ-relevant content lives in other subjects' v1 notes. You can't fully catch this now. Set a calendar reminder or a Garden Note on AJ's entity: "Check Twilight's Unassigned notes for AJ content when working Twilight." The problem surfaces naturally as you process each subject.

Step 3 — Plot point provisional extraction (can interleave with Step 2)
For chapters you've already planned in detail (Chapters 10-13 from the v1 content you pasted), do this mechanical pass:

For each plot point:

Synopsis content → Delivery Blueprint track, NoteState = Unset
Outcome statements → Scene Outcome track, NoteState = Unset
Scene purpose embedded in thread notes → Scene Justification, NoteState = Unset
This takes minutes per plot point and preserves all your provisional blueprints in the right place. You're not writing anything new — just routing content to the right track. The original synopses become Delivery Blueprint stubs that wait for validation in Scene Design Mode once the links exist.

Do not try to write Link notes during this step. You don't have the subject S-scope tracks to check against yet.

Step 4 — Link notes (after subject tracks are stable for that subject)
The trigger: once AJ's S-scope tracks are written and the light audit is complete, you can start writing link notes for plot points where AJ is doing thematic work.

In Linking Mode: AJ's Foundation on the left (read-only, showing usage directive). The link note on the right (display question active).

Write in sequence for each link:

P+La+L (what is disclosed about AJ in this scene)
WI+La+L (what should be inferred from that disclosure — NOT stated)
T+La+L (prior reader belief + revised conclusion after the clash)
P+En+L (what AJ does in this scene)
WI+En+L (what internal change is occurring — NOT stated, behavioral proxy needed)
T+En+L (thematic conclusion from witnessing)
Set all link notes to Unset initially. They become Confirmed in Audit Mode once you've checked that they're internally consistent with the subject S-scope tracks and with each other across the arc.

The priority sequence for subjects
Given intensity of thematic function and how much the rest of the project depends on them:

Applejack — most developed, most referenced, every plot point touches her
Chrysalis — richest Latent content, revelation plan affects every chapter
Twilight — second POV character, heavily referenced in links
TwiJack (Bond) — once AJ and Twilight's individual tracks are stable
Stagnation of Harmony (Abstract System) — most-referenced world system
Celestia — Latent content affects late chapters significantly
Everyone and everything else in rough order of first-appearance
The one open question worth naming
You said v2 doesn't have an Incorporated status and Confirmed is repurposed. This is workable but creates a semantic collision: Confirmed on an Unassigned note means "processed from v1" while Confirmed on a typed-track note means "design-stable." If you find this confusing in practice, the lightest fix is a Design Notes field on each subject that acts as a processing log: "Unassigned notes audited [date], outstanding flags: [list]." You don't need this before starting — just flag it as a potential friction point to resolve if it creates confusion in the first session of actual use.

Weighed technical feasibility against architectural complexity and synchronization risks
Weighed technical feasibility against architectural complexity and synchronization risks
The instinct is right — you want a reference layer and a working layer without them contaminating each other. But two instances of v2 creates a synchronization problem: the Confirmed-tracking happens in the v1 instance, the actual work happens in the v2 instance, and they're separate databases. Subjects exist in both. If you add a new subject to the clean copy, it doesn't exist in the reference copy. Over time the two diverge.

The separation you want already exists within one database — Unassigned notes are a different track type from typed track notes. The question is whether the UI makes that distinction visible enough that you don't feel like you're drowning in mixed content when working on a subject.

The cleaner version of the same idea: use the v1 app alongside v2, not a second v2 instance.

V1 app (already built, already navigates the v1 content): your reference and audit panel. You already know how to navigate it. Use it to scan and check during the audit step.
V2 app (clean): your working environment for writing typed tracks.
The audit step becomes: write fresh tracks in v2 → open v1 app → read each relevant note → check it against what you wrote → mark it in v1 (however v1 supports this, even just a strikethrough or a tag) → done.

You don't need the migrated Unassigned notes in v2 at all for this workflow. The migration script was useful for preserving IDs and subject associations, but if the UI clarity suffers from having both types in the same view, those Unassigned notes can be hidden, deleted after incorporation, or simply ignored in favor of the original v1 app as the reference tool.

The only reason to keep the migrated Unassigned notes in v2 is if the v2 context export should eventually include them for Gemini sessions — the untyped raw content as background signal alongside the clean typed tracks. If you want that capability later, keep them. If you don't, deleting them after you've processed a subject keeps the v2 database clean.

Two v2 instances only makes sense if the app's subject view has no good way to separate Unassigned notes from typed track notes visually. Is that currently a problem — does the subject view show everything at once with no filtering?

Validated dual-instance strategy while identifying critical tracking and structural gaps
Validated dual-instance strategy while identifying critical tracking and structural gaps
The analysis
The two-instance approach is sound for the specific reason you named: the clean instance is not a migration of the reference instance, it's a rebuild with a corrected entity structure. The reference instance is a frozen artifact you consult; the clean instance is the living document you maintain. These have different purposes and different lifecycles. Running them as separate databases is correct.

What makes this work:

The reference instance has subjects that reflect v1's organizational logic — which was topic-based, not claim-type-based, and which has consolidation errors. Fixing those errors in-place would require database surgery on every note's OwnerId and OwnerType when subjects get merged or renamed. That's exactly the software engineering work you don't want to do on v1 data. Starting clean in the second instance means subject creation is authorial work (deciding what entities should exist and what types they are), not data migration work.

The reference instance becomes a read-only audit panel. You never write to it in the normal workflow. You open it when auditing a subject to scan what v1 contained, then close it.

The tracking question:

If you're not engineering the reference instance, you can't mark notes as Incorporated there in any systematic way. Options that require zero software work:

Keep a simple external log — a text file or spreadsheet with one row per subject: "AJ — Foundation done, Historical Record done, audit complete [date]." Crude but zero-friction.
Use the Garden Notes track in the clean instance as the incorporation log: "AJ Foundation written [date], reference audit complete, outstanding orphan suspects: Twilight's Unassigned notes."
Rely on the fact that once you've written and audited a subject in the clean instance, you simply don't need the reference instance for that subject anymore. The reference instance doesn't need to know it's been superseded.
The second option integrates neatly with the clean instance's Design Notes purpose.

The plot point and chapter structure question:

This is the main gap I need you to clarify. The clean instance presumably needs the chapter and plot point structure to exist so that link notes have somewhere to attach. Options:

Rebuild from scratch: create chapters and plot points in the clean instance as you design scenes. Slow but gives you the opportunity to rename, split, or merge plot points that were wrong in v1.
Migrate structure only: write a lightweight script that copies just the Chapter, PlotPoint, and their ordering — no notes, no links — into the clean database. Gives you the scaffold without any of the content. This is small engineering work but might be worth it given the scale.
Hybrid: don't migrate structure, but don't rebuild all of it either. Create plot points in the clean instance only as you reach them in the workflow. Subjects can exist without any plot point links until you get to that chapter.
The hybrid option means early sessions focus entirely on S-scope subject tracks, which don't require plot points to exist. You only need the plot point structure when you start writing link notes, which is Step 4 in the workflow — later.

Gaps to clarify
Gap 1: What does the clean instance start with right now? Is it a fresh empty database with NoteTrackDefinitions configured, or has anything been seeded into it?

Gap 2: For the plot point structure — are you planning to rebuild it as you go, migrate it minimally via script (which you'd be willing to write), or start from the reference instance and gradually clean it up? This determines whether Step 3 (provisional blueprint extraction) is possible early or only when you reach each chapter.

Gap 3: What's your plan for the reference instance long-term — does it get deleted after the clean instance is complete, or does it remain permanently as an archive? This affects whether you care about marking anything in it at all.

Gap 4: When you say subjects need consolidation, is there a list of the consolidation decisions you've already made, or is discovering the right consolidation part of the work? If the consolidation decisions are already known, that list could drive the clean instance's initial subject creation.

Gap 5: The reference instance has link notes on its plot points (the migrated v1 Character Analysis, Thread Update, and Theme Commentary notes from v1). Are those links being migrated to the clean instance in any form, or are link notes being rebuilt entirely from scratch in the clean instance using the fresh typed tracks as the starting point? If they're not migrated, the reference instance link notes serve as an additional audit layer when writing the clean link notes — a different purpose from the Unassigned subject notes.

May 19

Weighed unified database architecture against separation needs and identified critical gaps
Weighed unified database architecture against separation needs and identified critical gaps
Same database, separate windows — analysis
What this actually means architecturally

The app doesn't currently have a native concept of "two layers in one database." The SubjectLibrary shows all subjects together. CommonWindows open per subject regardless of origin. So "separate windows" in practice means either: two separate subject views filtered differently (requires code), or simply opening different CommonWindows for legacy vs clean subjects (already possible, just behavioral). The cleaner implementation without new code is addressed below.

Pros

Single source of truth for the context export. When you build the Gemini export, one database query captures everything — clean tracks, legacy unassigned notes, plot points, all links. You don't have to merge two exports or decide which database is authoritative.

Plot point continuity. The existing plot points and their IDs remain valid across both layers. When you create new clean link notes for the same plot point, they coexist with the old unset notes. You can compare them in the same view rather than switching applications.

Subject consolidation is directly visible. When merging "Bright Mac" and "Pear Butter" into "AJ's Parents," both legacy subjects and the new consolidated subject are in the same database. The OwnerId reassignments are straightforward SQL rather than cross-database references.

No synchronization problem. One file to back up. One file to export.

Cons

No native separation without code. The SubjectLibrary currently shows everything together. Without a IsLegacy flag or equivalent, there's no enforced visual distinction between legacy subjects and clean subjects. You can accidentally edit legacy content when you meant to work on clean content.

The "separate windows" constraint requires discipline, not enforcement. Two instances on different databases creates a hard boundary. Same database with separate windows is a soft convention. Whether that's acceptable depends on how much you trust yourself not to cross-contaminate.

The minimal-code approach that provides usable separation

The SubjectDefinition system already creates grouping in the SubjectLibrary — subjects are grouped by their definition type. You can exploit this without any code changes:

Legacy subjects have no SubjectDefinitionId yet — they're currently ungrouped. Create a SubjectDefinition called "Legacy" or "Archive" as a catch-all. Assign all migrated subjects to it. They appear as their own group in the library, visually separated from the clean subjects that have real types. When you consolidate a legacy subject into a clean one, you reassign or merge it and the Legacy group shrinks over time.

This requires only data configuration work, no software engineering.

The plot point problem is the harder question

Your old plot points were expansion captures, not designed units. A synopsis that covers what should be three plot points doesn't have stakes or outcomes because those weren't part of your methodology then. You can't just inherit these into clean.

The options:

Keep old plot points as reference stubs: Don't delete them. Don't try to correct them in place. Treat them the way you treat the Unassigned subject notes — reference material during the audit step. When you design a section of the story properly (defining stakes, outcome, single shift), you create new plot points in the clean structure. The old synopsis content feeds the new Delivery Blueprint as raw material.

This means old links on old plot points are reference only, not migrated. New clean links are written fresh, attached to new properly-scoped plot points.

The one-to-one link problem disappears if you don't try to migrate links at all. Links are written from scratch in Linking Mode once clean subjects and clean plot points both exist. The old links in the legacy layer serve as notes on what subjects were connected to which scenes — you consult them during link writing but don't move them.

Prose fragments: these are a special case. If a v1 synopsis contains a sentence or two of actual prose that's good enough to keep, it belongs in the Delivery Blueprint track of the new plot point as a Craft Notes or Unset Delivery Blueprint note — not in a link track. The plot point's own tracks are where prose-adjacent material lives.

Suggested starting point for clean

Configured NoteTrackDefinitions with display questions and usage directives for all modes. SubjectDefinitions for all types. Nothing else — no subjects, no chapters, no plot points.

The chapter and plot point structural hierarchy is the one thing worth migrating via a small script: just Chapter records and PlotPoint records with their titles and ordering, no notes, no links. This saves you from manually recreating the scene numbering. Everything attached to those records gets built fresh. Without this, you create plot points as you design scenes, which means Link Mode work can only happen for the few scenes you've gotten to.

Gaps to clarify
Gap 1: When you say "separate windows," do you mean two separate instances of the SubjectLibrary or Definitions panel side by side, or just opening individual CommonWindows for each subject you're working on? The first requires UI code. The second is already how the app works.

Gap 2: Is the Legacy SubjectDefinition approach sufficient for visual separation, or do you need a harder boundary? Your answer determines whether this is data configuration work or software work.

Gap 3: For plot points — are you willing to leave the old plot points completely untouched in the legacy layer and build an entirely new plot point structure in clean as you design scenes? Or do you want to reuse the existing plot point IDs/records and just correct them? Reusing them means in-place editing; leaving them means a clean rebuild.

Gap 4: The chapter and plot point structural migration script — small SQL INSERT...SELECT that copies chapters and plot points with their titles and ordering but nothing else. Are you willing to write that, or would you rather create chapters and plot points manually as you reach them?

Gap 5: Prose fragments in v1 — are these in synopsis fields, in character analysis notes, or elsewhere? Knowing their location determines how they get routed into the clean Delivery Blueprint tracks versus discarded.

Gap 6: How do you plan to discover subject consolidations? If they emerge as you work (you notice "Bright Mac" and "Pear Butter" should be one subject while writing AJ's Historical Record), the consolidation decisions can be made lazily as you encounter them. If you want to do a full consolidation audit first, that changes the sequencing.

May 20

Excavated v1 design patterns and formulated clarifying questions
Excavated v1 design patterns and formulated clarifying questions
The "Tentative Migration Workflow" document in Drive is directly relevant — it shows an earlier v2 design with explicit stages: Stage 1 (canonical truth on links), Stage 2 (reader experience design: CharacterBelief, ReaderPerception, GapType, FIDAnchors, ReaderState), Stage 3 (Synopsis as clean ordered delivery sequence). The current framework has evolved Stage 2 into the P/WI/T link tracks and Stage 3 into the Delivery Blueprint. That history tells you something important about what plot points were being asked to do.

First principles: what a plot point is, from the framework up
The link tracks handle what each subject contributes to a scene: P (what goes on the page), WI (what the reader infers), T (what thematic conclusion forms). The plot point is the entity that exists prior to subjects — it is the scene unit that subjects attach to. The plot point's own tracks synthesize all those subject contributions into the actual page design.

This means a plot point is not where world truth lives. It is not where project-wide arc plans live. It is specifically the unit that:

Has a narrative reason to exist — Stakes: what question does this scene answer, raise, or complicate?
Has a single bounded outcome — what objectively changed by the end?
Has a unified reader experience — the Reader Understanding Target, which is the synthesis of all the link T and WI notes from every subject attached to it
Specifies the page design — Delivery Blueprint (the ordered sequence) + WI Translation Notes (behavioral proxies for the unstatable content)
The plot point is only about what happens at one location in the story's time and space, serving one primary function.

What v1 examples reveal
Looking at Chapter 10's scene list, three distinct types of "plot points" appear in v1:

Type A — Genuine scene units with their own time/space, multiple subjects doing thematic work, discernible Stakes and Outcome. Scene 10.5 (Hans Kessel), Scene 10.8 (meeting Rarity and the CMCs), Scene 10.16 (visiting Camp Fluttershy). These map cleanly to v2 plot points.

Type B — Transitional moments that are beats within a larger scene rather than scenes themselves. Scene 10.6 (Twilight sends the first letter), Scene 10.7 (Applejack receives the first letter). These might be Delivery Blueprint beats inside a larger plot point rather than standalone plot points.

Type C — Conceptual notes in scene clothing. Scene 10.4 ("Why didn't Chrysalis invent a magic rifle?") has no time/space continuity, no characters doing anything, no observable outcome. It is a world-building explanation note that was given a scene heading. Scene 10.3 (Griffon magic) is similar. These are not plot points at all — they are subject content (world mechanics of griffon magic, Chrysalis's strategic logic) that migrated into the plot point structure.

The v1 structure couldn't cleanly separate these because everything was "data capture" — whatever the synopses contained, that was the scene. The framework now separates them: Type C content belongs in subject tracks, not plot points.

What "originally a single note" means
The Tentative Migration Workflow document clarifies the lineage. The original conception was closer to a story beat card — a brief assertion that something happens at a particular moment. It became scene-level as you started attaching characters, codex entries, and threads to it, which demanded more structure to hold those relationships. The Synopsis grew to accommodate everything that didn't have a better home yet.

The v2 plot point is the synthesis unit, not the capture unit. The capture happens in Gardener Mode via the Delivery Blueprint (Unset). The synthesis happens in Scene Design Mode once the subject links exist.

Clarifying questions before giving v2 guidance
Question 1: In the v1 app, what determined where one plot point ended and another began? Was it primarily time/space continuity (a scene break or time skip), narrative function (a new question being raised), or just "I ran out of synopsis"?

Question 2: Looking specifically at v1 Type B moments — scenes like 10.6 (Twilight sends the letter) and 10.7 (AJ receives it) — do you see those as discrete scenes with their own Stakes and Outcome, or as beats you'd fold into the surrounding scene? Your answer determines whether plot points in v2 map to "scene" or to something smaller or larger.

Question 3: For the chapters you've planned most thoroughly (Chapters 9-13 are clearly detailed), do the "chapters" in v1 feel like containers for multiple plot points, or do some chapters correspond roughly to a single extended scene? The answer affects whether chapter boundaries and plot point boundaries are the same problem or different problems.

Question 4: When plot points were "originally a single note," what kind of assertion was that note making — was it "X and Y talk and something important happens," or was it closer to "AJ realizes she doesn't have to become the changelings to defeat them"? The first is a scene descriptor; the second is an outcome/thematic conclusion. The distinction determines whether plot points started as event containers or as functional story beat markers.

Question 5: For Type C content — the conceptual notes in scene clothing like 10.4 — do you want those to simply be removed from the plot point structure entirely (their content moves to subject tracks) and replaced with genuine plot points where those concepts are demonstrated rather than explained? Or do some of them represent scenes that need to be designed from scratch?

"griffon magic" was a placeholder for a plan to uncover griffon magic through a new character but that character wasn't invented yet. "Why didn't Chrysalis invent a magic rifle?" I can see being a flagged chapter note to make sure to include the question somewhere. 

Chapters I imagine are units of publishing with an arc that the chapter name alludes to. Sometimes they grew big enough to be split into chapters. But they aren't arbitrary plot point groups or single scenes

Where one plot point ended and another began in v1 can come down to: scene break, time skip, character change. But there were also extended sequences in time but plot into different plot points when the vibe changed. There was no method to this. That's why I need to derive a helpful method that informs the page prose from first principles moving forward, as was done rigorously for subjects and plotpointsubjectlink

What I meant by "originally a single note" I was refering to a time when the whole planner was just chapters and threads meant to intersect at one sentence summaries. The planner grew beyond that era

May 20

Derived plot point boundaries from framework: POV, Stakes, Understanding Target, Outcome
Derived plot point boundaries from framework: POV, Stakes, Understanding Target, Outcome
The first-principles definition from the framework
The framework established that link tracks hold what a subject contributes to a scene: P (what goes on the page), WI (what the reader infers from it), T (what thematic conclusion forms). The plot point's own tracks synthesize all those link inputs into the actual page design.

This means the plot point is defined by what it synthesizes. The Reader Understanding Target — the single unified statement of what the reader should conclude from all linked subjects combined — is the most fundamental constraint on a plot point's scope. If the Reader Understanding Target requires two statements that don't reinforce each other, the scene is two plot points.

The four first-principles tests, derived from the framework:

One POV character. FID discipline requires one perspective. Every element of the Delivery Blueprint, every WI Translation Note, every behavioral proxy is filtered through one character's access to the world. When the perspective shifts, you're in a new plot point regardless of whether continuous time has passed.

One Stakes question. The Scene Justification answers: what narrative question does this scene exist to answer, raise, or complicate? One question. If you find yourself writing "and also it raises whether..." that's a second plot point's Stakes question emerging.

One Reader Understanding Target. The synthesis of all subject T notes into a coherent single statement. Not "the reader should conclude X and then separately conclude Y" — one unified conclusion that the accumulated evidence builds toward.

One Outcome. What objectively changed. One thing. If the Outcome requires independent sentences that don't causally connect, it's two outcomes from two plot points.

What "vibe change" maps to
Your intuition about vibe change was tracking something real. From the framework, a vibe shift within a continuous POV scene corresponds to one of:

A new Stakes question emerging mid-scene — the narrative function shifts from answering one question to raising another. If both questions can be served by one Reader Understanding Target, they belong in one plot point's Delivery Blueprint, sequenced appropriately. If they point toward different Reader Understanding Targets, they split.

A La disclosure that changes the epistemic state so substantially that what follows is responding to a different reader prior belief. The disclosure closes one inference arc and opens another. That transition can be a plot point boundary.

The practical test: can you write one Stakes question and one Outcome that honestly describes the whole extended sequence? If yes, it's one plot point — the Delivery Blueprint handles the internal sequencing. If the answer requires "first... and then separately..." it's two.

What the v1 examples reveal about Type B moments
The letter-sending scenes (10.6, 10.7, 10.10, etc.) are the clearest test case. Each one is short — a beat rather than a scene — but they carry genuine Stakes and Outcome:

Scene 10.6 Stakes: Can Twilight find her authentic voice in a letter to AJ? Outcome: She writes the first real friendship-format letter, addressed to AJ as an equal. Reader Understanding Target: Twilight's authentic self has found its natural expression again.

Scene 10.7 Stakes: How does AJ receive Twilight's letter? Outcome: AJ interprets the egghead report as the most sincere possible declaration from Twilight. Reader Understanding Target: these two regard each other as equals in a way that subverts the vertical Celestia relationship they both grew up in.

These are genuine plot points — small, but with real Stakes and Outcomes. The Delivery Blueprints are brief. That's fine. A plot point doesn't have to be long; it has to be functionally bounded.

The chapter notes embedded in v1 as scene headings — the griffon magic placeholder, the Chrysalis rifle question — are not plot points. The griffon magic placeholder is a Design Note on a future subject entity. The Chrysalis rifle question is a Craft Note on whichever plot point will demonstrate the answer through Twilight or Fleur's thinking, not a standalone scene.

The chapter relationship
Chapters are publishing units with local arcs, as you described. The chapter name alludes to that arc. Chapter 10 (Extraction) has a local question — something like "can they extract what they need (the rifle technology, the tribal alliances) before the situation worsens?" — and the chapter's plot points collectively answer it.

Plot points are the scene-level atomic units within that arc. The chapter's local question is not the same thing as any individual plot point's Stakes question — it's the arc that emerges from the sequence of plot points.

Gaps still needing clarification
Gap 1: For the extended sequences that you split by vibe change — can you identify one concrete example from Chapters 10-12 where you'd now want to draw the boundary differently? Seeing a specific case helps establish whether the vibe-change splits were correctly tracking Stakes question shifts or were splitting Delivery Blueprint content that should stay in one plot point.

Gap 2: In the chapters where AJ and Twilight are separated, you have two simultaneous timelines. In v1, were the Twilight scenes and the AJ scenes in the same chapter interleaved (Twilight scene, then AJ scene, then Twilight scene), or were they sequenced as blocks (all Twilight scenes for this period, then all AJ scenes)? The answer affects whether a scene in one timeline and a scene in the other can belong to adjacent plot points in the same chapter, or whether they're in parallel tracks.

Gap 3: For plot points that you sense are "too big" in v1 — can you articulate what the wrong thing is? Is it that a single synopsis covers multiple locations, or multiple POV shifts, or multiple Stakes questions, or something else? Naming the pattern will let us turn it into a reliable splitting criterion going forward.

Gap 4: When you imagine the prose — the actual text a reader would encounter — how long is a "scene" in your mental model? A few pages? A chapter? The prose duration gives a reality check on whether a plot point that feels right at the framework level is actually producible as a coherent reading unit.

Maybe I need a parallel data track for a chapter, of raw capture beats and plans and prose fragments, separate from refined plot points that need stakes and outcome and reader understanding target? which allows me to not have to edit gardened text in place when it is time to determine these things. Or would this just create more mess to have different data structures?
The key thing is that I am not in a position to pencil in stakes or outcome because I haven't even finished expanding the world. Also note that in my old v1 workflow I would sometimes make plot points with no chapter; they were floating. They captured something I felt should reach the reader, often with links to subjects. Perhaps they are also sometimes acting as containers for cross subject things that reach the reader. But these floating plot points are a minority.
If two links that talk about different outcomes shouldn't share a plot point, does that mean if any links converge on a plot point, they should each be serving as evidence for the same thematic proposition? May 20

Synthesized raw capture workflows, thematic coherence rules, and reader understanding target nomenclature
Synthesized raw capture workflows, thematic coherence rules, and reader understanding target nomenclature
On the raw capture problem: the two-stage model
You've identified something real. There are genuinely two different things that have been called "plot point":

Stage 1 — Scene Area / Capture Bucket: "Something happens around here, involving these subjects, and I have a sense of what it should accomplish." Stakes unknown or unformulated. Outcome unclear. Subject links exist but their T notes are Unset. Dialogue fragments and clever moments captured. This is gardening mode.

Stage 2 — Refined Scene: Stakes question answered. One Outcome. Reader Understanding Target written. Delivery Blueprint ordered. All link T notes Confirmed or at least coherent. This is architecting mode.

The question is whether these need separate data structures. The argument against a separate structure: you already have NoteState (Unset/Flagged/Confirmed) to mark refinement level, and Craft Notes for free-form capture. The argument for: the display questions for Delivery Blueprint and Stakes assume Stage 2 thinking, so showing them in Stage 1 context is misleading friction.

The minimal solution: Stage 1 uses only the Craft Notes / Garden Notes track, which has no cognitive mode discipline. Everything else is locked. Stage 2 activates the other tracks. The transition is deliberate.

Whether this is implemented as one entity with locked tracks or two different entity types is a data model decision with tradeoffs. A single entity with a "refinement level" field avoids structural complexity. Two entity types (BeatCapture → PlotPoint) gives stronger type safety but requires a promotion mechanism.

Gap 1: Which do you prefer? One entity with staged track availability, or two entity types with explicit promotion?

On the same thematic proposition question: soft rule, derived from first principles
The hard rule is: one coherent Reader Understanding Target per plot point. One statement the reader walks away with.

The soft rule follows from that: links serving completely incompatible themes make a coherent Reader Understanding Target impossible to write. But links serving related or complementary themes can coexist in one plot point if they reinforce each other.

Example: a scene where the Love Harvester is compared to donation-based magic could simultaneously evidence "technology is an accelerant for user psychology" AND "solidarity outperforms extraction" — these are different theme tags but they're facets of the same scene's argument. The Reader Understanding Target synthesizes both: "the difference between coercive and consensual use of the same technology demonstrates that the user's moral orientation, not the tool itself, determines whether the result is solidarity or suffering."

That's one statement. The scene works.

Where it breaks: if one link is arguing for theme A and a completely unrelated link is arguing for theme B and neither informs the other, you can't write one coherent statement. That's the actual split criterion — not "same theme tag" but "one synthesizable statement."

So: soft rule on theme alignment, hard rule on Reader Understanding Target coherence.

On links defining plot points: the insight is correct
Your bucket model is the right mental model. The hierarchy in practice is:

Links are discovered first (subject doing thematic work in a region of the story). Buckets accumulate links. Buckets get refined into plot points when you have enough links to see what coherent Reader Understanding Target they collectively support.

The data model (plot points have links) doesn't need to change. The workflow changes: plot points start as loose buckets, accumulate links, and the Reader Understanding Target emerges from examining the accumulated T notes. The moment you can write one coherent Reader Understanding Target, the bucket has earned its refinement.

This also explains the floating plot points in v1. They were buckets that hadn't been located in a chapter yet because the chapter they belong to hadn't been settled. They're valid interim containers. In v2 they'd be Stage 1 plot points with no chapter assignment.

Gap 2: For the large v1 plot points with 6+ subjects — do those feel like "all these subjects are genuinely contributing to one scene" or "this is a bucket that hasn't been examined yet"? Can you give me a specific example from Chapters 10-12? That distinction determines whether large multi-subject plot points are correctly-scoped rich scenes or under-refined buckets.

Reader Understanding Target: from first principles
Working backwards from the framework's inference chain: P (page) → WI (world inference, reader's head) → T (thematic conclusion, reader's head). The T notes are the final step — the thematic proposition evidence a subject deposits in a given scene.

A plot point connects multiple subjects. Each subject's T note deposits some evidence. The plot point's synthesis track asks: if you collected all those T notes and a reader read this scene, what is the single thing they would independently conclude?

This is not the same as any individual subject's T note. It's the gestalt. What does AJ's T note + Twilight's T note + the Love Harvester's T note add up to, as a unified reader experience?

It's also not the Stakes question (why the scene exists) and not the Outcome (what objectively changed in the world). Those are authorial and observational. The Reader Understanding Target is epistemic: what did the reader come to understand, without being told?

Concretely: it's the answer to "if a reader closed the book after this scene and tried to articulate what they now believe or understand that they didn't before, what would they say?"

The failure mode of getting it wrong: writing the Reader Understanding Target as an Outcome ("AJ agrees to the drug deal") instead of as a thematic conclusion ("the predator's dilemma sometimes requires becoming a version of the thing you're fighting against, and the difference between corruption and strategy is knowing which line you haven't crossed"). The Outcome is on the page. The Reader Understanding Target is what forms in the reader's head from the Outcome and everything else.

Nomenclature options:

Name	Strength	Weakness
Reader Understanding Target	Precise, specifies it's a target not a given	Clinical, long
Scene Meaning	Intuitive, conversational	"Meaning" is vague
Scene Proposition	Directly connects to the thematic proposition framework	Sounds like the scene is arguing, which it is, but feels exposing
Witnessed Conclusion	Emphasizes the reader's role in arriving at it	Slightly awkward
Scene Takeaway	Most immediately intuitive	Too casual, loses precision
Convergent Meaning	Captures the synthesis from multiple subjects	Uncommon term
Reader Inference	Connects to the P/WI/T chain	Sounds like one inference step, not the final one
My suggestion: Scene Argument — because the scene IS making an argument (the thematic proposition framework established themes as propositions, scenes as evidence). It's what argument this scene is making to the reader that the reader should independently arrive at. It's different from "argument" in the confrontational sense.

Or if you want to preserve the "reader" framing: Reader Arrival — what the reader should arrive at by scene's end, as opposed to what they're handed.

Gap 3: Which of these feels most natural when you imagine filling it in? The right name is the one that, when you see the field, immediately tells you what cognitive mode to enter.

On Chapters 10-12 interweaving
The interweaved structure (AJ chapter → letter → Twilight chapter → letter → AJ chapter) creates a genuinely unusual reading experience: the reader holds two simultaneous Reader Understanding Targets, one for each thread, and the letters are the scenes where those threads comment on each other.

Gap 4: When you imagine what a reader should feel after reading an AJ scene immediately followed by a Twilight scene in that arc — is the intended experience "these two parallel arcs are converging on the same thematic conclusion from different angles" or "these are genuinely separate arcs that will eventually reunite"? The answer determines whether the two threads share a chapter-level Reader Understanding Target or whether they're designed to be experienced as distinct until the reunion.

Gap 5: The letters themselves sit at the intersection of both threads. When you envision Scene 10.7 (AJ receives the letter) — is the primary Reader Understanding Target about AJ's experience of receiving the letter, or about what the reader understands about the AJ/Twilight relationship from seeing AJ's reaction? Those could be the same thing, but they could also point to different framings of the scene's purpose.

May 20

Synthesized narrative tension with thematic coherence, identified framework gaps
Synthesized narrative tension with thematic coherence, identified framework gaps
On narrative questions vs. thematic evidence: not orthogonal, different registers
The reader operates in two simultaneous registers:

Narrative register: "What happens? Will they succeed? What's the cost?" — suspense, anticipation, relief. The reader forms a question and wants it answered.

Thematic register: "What does this mean? What am I now convinced of?" — understanding, recognition, the feeling that something true was just demonstrated.

These aren't orthogonal because they're deeply entangled in practice — you care whether AJ succeeds at convincing the last tribe precisely because the story has made you care about what her failure would mean — but they're distinct enough that a scene can fail in one register while succeeding in the other. A scene where interesting things happen but nothing means anything satisfies the narrative register but not the thematic. A scene that makes a powerful argument but where nothing was actually at risk satisfies the thematic register but not the narrative.

The existing framework captures both, but in different tracks:

Stakes question is the narrative register: what question is the reader asking as they read this scene? What is genuinely at risk?

Outcome is the answer to that question at the plot level: what objectively changed?

Reader Understanding Target is the thematic register: what did the reader conclude from witnessing the Outcome and the process of arriving there?

All three together produce the full reader experience. Stakes without Reader Understanding Target = plot without meaning. Reader Understanding Target without Stakes = argument without tension. The Outcome is what makes both registers cash out — it's where the narrative question gets answered and the thematic evidence gets deposited simultaneously.

This means Stakes isn't just the authorial "why does this scene exist." It's the reader-facing question that makes them want to keep reading. Designing the Stakes is designing the reader's anticipation.

On complementary themes: the implication is significant
You're right that if all themes are complementary — facets of one core proposition about what kind of society enables flourishing — then a scene weaving multiple themes together doesn't violate coherence. The Reader Understanding Target synthesizes them into one insight precisely because the themes are facets of the same underlying argument.

For TLTT specifically, the meta-proposition looks something like: horizontal relationships built on genuine contribution outperform vertical relationships built on coercion, in every domain simultaneously. The individual themes (harmonic capitalism vs. rugged individualism, technology as accelerant, organic friendship vs. manufactured loyalty, bottom-up vs. top-down) are all demonstrations of this same proposition in different domains.

This has a practical implication: a scene can legitimately have AJ's T note evidencing harmonic capitalism AND Twilight's T note evidencing technology as accelerant AND the Love Harvester's T note evidencing technology as moral neutral — and the Reader Understanding Target synthesizes them into one statement about how all three domains converge on the same conclusion in this scene. That's not incoherence. That's the story's argument landing from multiple angles at once.

On chapters 10-12 as parallel demonstrations
If both AJ's and Twilight's narratives are demonstrations of the same ideals from different domains, the interweaved structure is doing something specific: it's forcing the reader to perceive the parallel in real time. They're not told "these two things are related" — they experience both threads close together and arrive at the parallel themselves.

This means the interweaved structure is itself a narrative architecture decision that creates its own Reader Understanding Target at the arc level, above any individual scene: something like "the principles that work in a laboratory also work in a jungle negotiation, which means these principles are genuinely universal rather than context-specific."

That arc-level Reader Understanding Target would be the seed planted by chapter 9's town hall, watered across 10-12, and harvested at the reunion.

On separate entity types
Confirmed that separate entity types (BeatCapture → PlotPoint with explicit promotion) is the right call. Avoids in-place mutation of gardened content and makes the distinction between "captured" and "designed" explicit rather than implied by NoteState.

The actual gap: I don't have scenes 9.15-9.22
You referenced "pasted in scenes 9.15 to 9.19" and described 9.20 as a mega bucket and 9.21/9.22 as probably well-scoped — but the content didn't come through in the message. I need the actual v1 notes for those scenes to analyze them concretely.

For 9.20 specifically, the conceptual question is: in the deep conversation between AJ and Twilight, does the conversation have one central Stakes question that runs through the whole scene, or does it go through distinct phases where the Stakes question itself shifts? A conversation where both characters are working toward the same Stakes resolution the whole time is one plot point regardless of length. A conversation where an early resolution creates new Stakes (they resolve whether to trust each other, and then that resolution creates the new Stakes question of what to do with that trust) is two.

But I can't apply that test without seeing what the conversation actually covers. Can you paste the content of 9.15 through 9.22?
