Validated revised workflow and flagged scale implications for sustainable execution
Validated revised workflow and flagged scale implications for sustainable execution
The revised workflow is cleaner
Moving link creation into the plot point audit (rather than as a separate provisional step) resolves the earlier problem neatly. The sequence now is effectively:

Write display text for subject types
Create subjects and S-scope tracks from memory after quick v1 scan
Scan v1 chapter structure, create correctly-scoped empty plot point buckets
Audit subjects from v1 — fill gaps, mark v1 notes Confirmed as incorporated
Audit plot points from v1 — route subject-level content to subject tracks, create links with at least provisional P/WI/T notes, write Stakes and Outcome, keep compatible delivery blueprint content as Unset
Steps 3 and 4 can now run concurrently since neither depends on the other being complete. The links don't exist until Step 5, so there's no provisional-link cleanup problem.

The 100/400/300k scale changes the operational question
At this scale, "complete all subjects before touching plot points" produces months of subject work before any usable linked chapter exists. That's a long time before expansion can resume on anything.

The more practical approach given what's already detailed: work chapter-by-chapter, doing both subject and plot point audit together for each chapter's cluster. When you audit Chapter 9, you complete the 10-15 subjects active in that chapter and audit its 20-odd plot points simultaneously, creating links as you go. This produces a fully linked, audited Chapter 9 before Chapter 10 is touched. Expansion can resume on Chapter 9 while Chapters 10+ are still pending.

The practical priority order becomes: subjects active in Chapter 9 first (AJ, Chrysalis, Twilight, Harmonic Capitalism, Comet Shine, etc.), then Chapter 9 plot point audit, then move to Chapters 10-12's subjects (Fleur, Hans, Mali, the TwiJack Bond, the Tzinacatl entities) and their plot points, and so on.

This also handles the uneven development problem across 400 plot points. The well-developed chapters (9-13) get full audit treatment. Later stub plot points get correctly-scoped empty buckets created from the v1 chapter list with minimal audit — they're deferred until expansion reaches them.

The audit's practical output per plot point
When you read a v1 plot point synopsis during Step 5, the audit produces:

Links to the subjects doing thematic work, each with at minimum a flagged T note ("this subject is active here, full P/WI/T TBD") and ideally at least one of the track types written out
Delivery Blueprint content that survived from the synopsis (Unset — these are plans, not commitments)
A Stakes question (Unset until you're confident in it)
A Scene Outcome (Unset)
None of this needs to be Confirmed during the audit. The audit makes things exist in the right structure; the Scene Design work later makes them Confirmed. The Unset state is the honest state for planning-not-yet-validated content.

The v1 subject notes being mostly backstory and arc plans rather than plot point specific data is actually an advantage here — there's less risk of confusing which layer content belongs to when doing the routing.

The selective export UI is a dependency for expansion
You mentioned it's planned but not yet implemented. Until it exists, resuming expansion with Claude means either pasting full chapter content manually or working from the v1 full-context Gemini sessions — neither of which benefits from the v2 structure you're building.

The export UI design follows from the workflow: you need selection by chapter (all subjects/links/plot points active in Chapter X), by subject (AJ's full arc across all chapters), and by theme (all T notes tagged to a given proposition). The chapter-based selection is probably the most immediately useful — it lets you send a clean Chapter 9 context to Claude for expansion work once that chapter's audit is complete.

Remaining gaps
Gap 1: When creating empty plot point buckets from the v1 chapter list (Step 3), how will you determine scope when v1 hasn't already decided it? For well-developed plot points, the scope judgment comes from reading the synopsis. For stubs that are just titles, the bucket is created and deferred. But some v1 plot points are the exact mega-bucket problem — one entry that covers three distinct reader experiences. Are you planning to split those during Step 3 (creating two or three buckets from one v1 entry), or flag them for splitting during Step 5?

Gap 2: The chapter-by-chapter approach requires knowing which subjects are active in which chapter before the links exist. Are you planning to establish this from the v1 plot point content (reading which subjects appear in which chapters) or from subject-level memory? This affects whether Step 3 and Step 4 can truly run concurrently or whether Step 4 needs to precede Step 3.

Gap 3: The selective export UI — what's the timeline for building it? If it's weeks away, the chapter-by-chapter audit can proceed for the early well-developed chapters (9-13) before it's ready, since those are the ones you'd want to expand first anyway. But if expansion needs to resume sooner than the UI will be ready, there may be a gap period where you're doing audit work without the expansion capability that motivates it.

Gap 4: For subjects that span many chapters (AJ, Chrysalis, Twilight), the S-scope tracks cover the whole story. During a chapter-by-chapter audit, do you complete these subjects' S-scope tracks fully upfront (before Chapter 9's audit begins), or do you write the S-scope tracks progressively as you move through chapters? Completing them upfront is correct for AJ/Chrysalis/Twilight since their Foundation tracks need to exist as a reference before you can write their link notes accurately.

I don't think I should go chapter by chapter because everything in all chapters relate to and are designed on each other. If I spend a ton of effort on refinement of chapters 9-12, them discover big story planning shifts later, 9-12 need to be reworked. This is why expansion must finish before refinement starts anywhere. In order to finish expansion, i need to sort existing data into something workable and comprehensible first, but not refine it because everything could potentially be replaced.

May 21

Recognized expansion-before-refinement constraint, restructured workflow phases accordingly
Recognized expansion-before-refinement constraint, restructured workflow phases accordingly
The constraint you've identified, stated precisely
The story is a unified argument where every chapter constrains every other chapter. Refining any chapter before the full argument is settled risks rework when expansion shifts the foundation. Therefore the workflow has a mandatory sequencing:

Expansion must complete before any refinement begins anywhere.

This changes what the v2 work before expansion is for. It isn't refinement — it's organization. The goal is getting existing content into comprehensible structure so expansion sessions can use it as coherent context, not so refined content can be built on top of it.

What this means for the v2 work phases
Phase 0 — Configuration (prerequisite, no content work) Write display text and configure NoteTrackDefinitions. This is structural scaffolding and doesn't get invalidated by expansion shifts.

Phase 1 — Organization, not synthesis (enables expansion)

Everything in this phase is Unset. Nothing gets synthesized, validated, or Confirmed. The work is purely routing: getting v1 content into the right structural containers so it becomes navigable.

This means the "write subjects from memory first" approach doesn't belong here. Memory-synthesis is refinement — it produces integrated claims about the world that could be contradicted by later expansion. What Phase 1 does instead is create the container (the subject entity, with its correct type and tracks) and route the v1 content into it verbatim or minimally reformatted. The content is now in the right place without anyone having committed to what it means.

Same for plot points: create the correctly-scoped bucket, route the v1 synopsis to the Delivery Blueprint track as Unset, route subject-level content that wandered into the synopsis back to the relevant subject tracks as Unset. No Stakes written. No Outcomes written. No T notes. The bucket exists; the content is organized; nothing is finalized.

Links in this phase are minimal: a connection marker indicating this subject is active in this scene, without P/WI/T content. The connection is structural information, not thematic claim.

Phase 2 — Expansion (using organized v2 as context)

The organized-but-Unset v2 becomes the expansion context. Gemini or Claude sessions use selective exports rather than the full 300k. New insights, world-building resolutions, flagged questions answered — all enter as Unset notes appended to the appropriate tracks. World-building shifts that invalidate existing Unset notes get marked Flagged.

Expansion is complete when the story's foundational claims are stable enough that you can commit to them without expecting them to shift. Not every plot point resolved — but the thesis proposition, the four supporting propositions, the character psychologies, and the major world mechanics stable enough to build on.

Phase 3 — Refinement (after expansion)

This is where the synthesis work lives: writing Foundation tracks from memory after scanning v1, defining Stakes and Outcomes, writing P/WI/T link notes, Confirming stable content. The "write from memory, then audit" approach belongs here, not in Phase 1.

The critical dependency is still the export UI
Phase 2 can't function without selective export. Sending the full 300k words to Gemini is already hitting needle-in-haystack problems at the scale you've described. The organized v2 structure is only useful for expansion if you can extract chapter-specific or subject-specific slices from it.

Until the export UI exists, Phase 2 expansion is constrained to the same full-context Gemini approach you already have — which means Phase 1 organization work is primarily clearing the path for Phase 2, and the path opens fully when the export UI is ready.

Gaps to clarify
Gap 1: What does "expansion complete enough to start refinement" look like? Is there a specific set of outstanding questions you'd consider the expansion's closure criteria — world-building uncertainties that need resolution, underdeveloped chapters that need sketching, thematic arguments that need stress-testing? Or is expansion effectively ongoing and refinement starts chapter-by-chapter when a chapter feels settled?

Gap 2: For Phase 1 routing, when v1 synopsis content belongs partly to the Delivery Blueprint and partly to subject tracks — is a rough split acceptable (paste the whole synopsis into Delivery Blueprint, flag the parts that should be routed elsewhere), or does each piece need to be correctly placed before Phase 1 is considered done for that plot point?

Gap 3: The 32 chapters at similar detail to 9-12 — are any of them in a state where the v1 content is so uncertain or contradictory that even organizational routing would produce noise? Or is the v1 content generally stable enough to organize, even knowing individual claims will be revised during expansion?

Gap 4: The export UI timeline relative to Phase 1 completion — can Phase 1 organization complete before the export UI is built (running in parallel), or is there a dependency between them that makes their sequencing matter?

Currently the v1 mixed text gives some context for AI but uses way too many tokens and has ambiguity they leads to hallucination. And it is not helpful for a prose writer in this unorganized mode.

In order to answer a flagged question, I need to ask AI for analysis about it, which requires context. I am hitting a wall where the analysis is degrading due to too much text context and tokens. That's why I need to organize, so I can be more selective about context. Doing analysis WILL create more flagged questions. I'm not in a rush to write. I don't care about timeline. I care about correctness and not doing things out of order that will make me redo things. I don't want to repeat myself.
I want all the backstory and foundation concrete before I work on really making plot point deliveries and reader inference design. I want to map out where everything connects before hand. There is provisional blueprints of ideas I think might work which need a home too. That's what the plot point skeleton would be for. But these can be invalidated, which is why they must not be refined.
Don't just use phase numbers, attach a descriptor to it for disambiguation.
I want to make sure all text in v1 synopses that should be in v2 subject or link tracks makes it to their track. I want the working copy of v2 to have no track question violations. The archive version of v2 (imported v1 so that every text field can gave independent unset or confirmed for tracking, and v1 synopses were split into separate notes using double return carriage new line) is what ever mix of v1 was there and won't contaminate the future looking v2 database instance.

Synthesized workflow requirements and identified critical sequencing gaps
Synthesized workflow requirements and identified critical sequencing gaps
Stating what I now understand precisely
The two instances: Archive is built and functional. Working copy is empty. The archive's purpose is reference during subject building and tracking incorporation; it's retired when all notes reach Confirmed. The working copy becomes the permanent master database.

What subject building produces: Track-separated synthesis written from memory after briefly consulting v1. Provisional anchor, not permanent commitment, but built rigorously to minimize later revision. Foundation and Historical Record are the priority tracks; Arc and Reveal Plan can remain empty. The output has no track question violations — every note answers the question of the track it lives in. The goal is to get scattered v1 material into specific track homes so it can serve as clean, selectable LLM context.

What plot point setup produces: Correctly scoped buckets from the v1 chapter list. Clean v1 text (plain dialogue, plain action statements) copies verbatim into Unset Delivery Blueprint notes. Tangled mixed-mode paragraphs stay in archive and are not directly migrated. Links are created only when meaningful text exists in v1 link fields or when plot point synopsis content demonstrably belongs in a subject link rather than a Delivery Blueprint. No Stakes, Outcomes, or Reader Understanding Targets written — those are refinement work.

What the flagged questions represent: Everything uncertain, unresolved, or contradictory across 110+ notes. None can be Confirmed in the archive until resolved. Resolving them requires targeted LLM analysis with selective context from v2. Analysis will generate more flagged questions. Prose writing doesn't begin until zero remain.

The sequence at the highest level: Subject building + plot point setup (data transmutation) → selective export UI → targeted expansion on flagged questions → zero flags → archive Confirmed pass → delivery blueprint refinement and reader inference design → prose.

Remaining gaps
Gap 1 — Subject consolidation timing: 226 v1 subjects include cases like Chrysalis split across three entries. Does consolidation happen before subject building begins (create the right consolidated entity list first, then build each), or during (encounter the three Chrysalis entries, decide to consolidate while building, fold them)? The answer affects whether the first task is a consolidation audit of all 226 entries or whether that judgment is made on the fly.

Gap 2 — All-subjects-first vs. partial start for expansion: You said all subjects should be built to a rigorous standard, and also that with selective export you don't technically need all subjects before expansion can start. Is the intent to build all subjects to the rigorous standard first, then start expansion? Or build the subjects most relevant to the highest-priority flagged questions, use those for targeted expansion, then continue building the rest? The first is cleaner but delays expansion longer. The second allows expansion to begin earlier but requires judgment about which subjects to prioritize.

Gap 3 — Google Keep and AI chat notes: These contain material not yet in v1. Do they need to be evaluated before subject building begins (in case they contain foundation-altering content), used as additional reference alongside v1 during subject building, or addressed as a separate pass after the main v1 transmutation?

Gap 4 — Hidden flagged notes in plot point text: When encountered during plot point scanning, these are currently buried in v1 synopses. Where do they go in v2? Into the plot point's Craft Notes track as Flagged notes in the working copy, or extracted to the archive as individual flagged notes there? The answer matters because it determines whether the working copy accumulates its own set of flagged items or whether all flags live in the archive during the transmutation phase.

Gap 5 — Rigorous standard definition: When a subject is built to the rigorous standard you mean, what does done look like? Is it: every piece of v1 content for this subject has been evaluated and its applicable material is in a track — or is it: the subject's Foundation track makes coherent, internally consistent claims regardless of whether all v1 material has been evaluated? Or both?

Gap 6 — Selective export UI timing: Subject building can proceed without the export UI because it doesn't require LLM analysis. But expansion can't produce good results without it. Is the plan to build subjects while the export UI is developed in parallel, making them ready to use together when both are complete? Or is there a reason the UI needs to exist before subject building starts?

Clarification: every flagged note in v2 working copy has to be resolved before anything in v2 working copy can move to confirmed. For the archive moving to confirmed, the note's text just needs to evaluated that all of its statements and nuances/subtext made it into v2. Flagged notes in the archive become flagged notes in v2.

Tangled v1 paragraphs have to evaluated: I need to dissect the nuance of the text and put the insights into the appropriate tracks. This is too hard working from a blank slate, which is why the tracks should be prepopulated from memory as a starting point before trying to evaluate v1 tangled paragraphs.

Consolidation doesn't happen before subject building begins because the text data is not disciplined. The judgement should be made on the fly.

Many Google Keep and AI chat notes DO contradict data in v1. But I have to start somewhere. If I remember that v1 data is outdated from undocumented data, then I'll skip or put flagged notes. This seems to suggest that the google keep and AI chat note entry into v2 should come before the v1 evaluation pass that moves notes to Confirmed in the archive to mean included. The biggest example is a chat where I decided EEEE! is a machinist's guild, not a combative union. So when I encounter notes in v1 about EEEE! being a union, I would not put it in v2.

Hidden flagged notes in plot point text can become flagged in the v2 plot point with a flag question. Flagged notes and reasons absolutely accumulate in v2. Flags should not go to the archive. But crucially, the flag question is a property of a note in v2's design, not a separate note. Thus the owning note can belong to the correct track, which maintains rigorous sanitation. Flagged notes also appear in a different section of the UI as unset notes.

Rigorous standard means no cognitive mixing. What's on the page stays separate from what the reader should infer. It doesn't mean content completeness. It can have flagged notes.

I want to get some subjects into v2 so that I can test the selective export with some real data. Also I've done a lot of software engineering work on the v2 application and want to defer the export so I can do author-related tasks instead of software-related tasks for the short term.

Synthesized workflow phases and identified remaining clarification gaps
Synthesized workflow phases and identified remaining clarification gaps
Updated understanding, restated precisely
The two Confirmed meanings are distinct: Archive Confirmed = evaluated for inclusion in v2 (can happen during data transmutation; doesn't require v2 flags resolved). Working copy Confirmed = content is validated and stable (requires 0 flags in working copy first). These are sequential to each other but on different tracks.

The memory-first rationale is now clear: Prepopulated subject tracks are the context that makes tangled v1 paragraph dissection tractable. Without that context, extracting layered insights from mixed-mode v1 text is working from a blank slate. The sequence is: write subject tracks from memory → then evaluate tangled v1 content against those tracks. Even clean v1 content doesn't go directly into subject tracks — it gets evaluated against what memory wrote first.

Google Keep/AI chat notes supersede v1 in places: Undocumented decisions (EEEE! as machinist's guild, not union) mean some v1 content would be skipped during archive evaluation. Google Keep/AI chat content needs to be incorporated before the archive evaluation pass begins, or at minimum surfaced as reference during it, so that when you encounter superseded v1 content you know to skip it rather than migrate it.

Flagged notes in working copy are notes in the correct track, with a flag property. Not separate notes. Cognitive discipline is maintained. Archive flags become v2 flags. All v2 flags must reach zero before working copy Confirmed pass begins.

Rigorous standard = no cognitive mixing, not content completeness. Flagged notes are acceptable.

The workflow as I now understand it
Configuration Write display text for all NoteTrackDefinitions. Prerequisite for everything.

Subject Foundation Building Review Google Keep and AI chat notes first — surface undocumented decisions that supersede v1 content. Then create subject entities with correct types (consolidating on the fly). Write S-scope tracks from memory using display questions: no cognitive mixing, can have flagged notes, content completeness not required. Flagged notes accumulate in the working copy as properties of correctly-placed track notes.

Plot Point Skeleton Building (runs concurrently with Subject Foundation Building) Scan v1 chapter list, create correctly scoped plot point buckets. Route clean v1 text — plain dialogue, plain action statements — verbatim into Unset Delivery Blueprint or Craft Notes tracks. Create links when meaningful v1 text exists in v1 link fields or when synopsis content demonstrably belongs in a subject link. Flag overscoped buckets. Tangled plot point paragraphs don't get migrated here; they're addressed during archive evaluation.

Archive Evaluation Pass For each archive note: evaluate whether all statements and nuances made it into v2. Route what's missing from subject tracks (dissecting tangled paragraphs against the now-prepopulated subject tracks). Skip what's superseded by Google Keep/AI chat knowledge. Tangled plot point content that can't yet be cleanly dissected becomes a flagged note in the v2 plot point. Mark archive note Confirmed when fully evaluated. When all archive notes Confirmed, archive is retired — v2 becomes master database.

Selective Export UI Build (short engineering task; can overlap with late archive evaluation work) Required before expansion produces useful results.

Expansion Use selective export to send targeted context to Claude or Gemini for specific flagged questions. Each resolved flag may generate new flags. Continue until working copy flags reach zero.

Delivery Blueprint Refinement Define Stakes, Outcomes, Reader Understanding Targets for plot points. Write P/WI/T link track content. Move working copy notes to Confirmed as they stabilize.

Prose Write from the confirmed, stable foundation.

Remaining gaps
Gap 1 — Google Keep and AI chat notes entering v2: You said their entry should come before the archive evaluation pass. When they're evaluated, where do they go in v2? Do superseding decisions (EEEE! is a machinist's guild) go into the relevant subject's Foundation or Historical Record track as an Unset note? Or are they handled purely as reference during subject building from memory — informing what you write without themselves becoming notes?

Gap 2 — Tangled plot point content during archive evaluation: When a v1 plot point synopsis contains a tangled paragraph (event mixed with reader experience mixed with theme), and the plot point's tracks haven't been prepopulated from memory the way subject tracks were — does the tangled plot point content become a flagged Craft Notes entry in the v2 plot point (pending dissection during refinement), or does it go through a different handling?

Gap 3 — Starting point before export UI: You want to get some subjects into v2 to test selective export with real data once the UI is built. Which subjects do you plan to build first — the highest-priority ones (AJ, Chrysalis, Twilight) as established earlier, or specific subjects that would exercise the most interesting edge cases for testing the export?

All 5 facts in the Updated Understanding are correct and aligned.

My usual workflow back in v1 times would involve starting a conversation by pasting in the entire context, asking a question that was a flagged question, getting an answer, and then iterating until I like the result. Then I would read the conversation in reverse and add what I like into v1. I pick what insights I like and leave behind what I didn't like. There are probably 20+ full conversations that I have to read through and add the insights that I like into v2's appropriate tracks. But I think I need to start with subjects from memory first so that there's a starting point.

The tangled plot point should have all things that belong on subject tracks or subject plot point link tracks go to those. The rest can go to appropriate tracks on the plot point; I will have appropriate display questions. No tangled paragraphs whatsoever should enter v2; v2 must have notes in appropriate tracks. I think this makes sense; I previously included "Unset" as a track in v2 to import old notes, but now I think those old notes stay in archive as mixed and never enter v2.

Synthesized workflow phases and clarified data integration sequencing
Synthesized workflow phases and clarified data integration sequencing
The workflow
Configuration Phase Write display text for all NoteTrackDefinitions. No content work starts until this is done.

Foundation Building Phase (highest-priority subjects first: AJ, Chrysalis, Twilight, then others)

Build subject entities with correct types — consolidating on the fly. Write S-scope tracks from memory using display questions: no cognitive mixing, flags allowed as properties of correctly-placed notes, content completeness not required. Concurrently, create correctly-scoped plot point buckets from the v1 chapter list. Route clean (non-tangled) v1 text — plain dialogue, plain action statements — directly into appropriate plot point tracks (Unset). Flag overscoped buckets.

Subjects must exist before links can be created and before tangled content can be dissected. Clean plot point buckets can be created in parallel with subject building. Links and tangled content dissection wait until the relevant subjects exist.

Reference Source Integration Phase (after subjects have a starting point; before v1 archive evaluation)

Read through 20+ AI conversation threads and Google Keep notes. Extract insights into appropriate v2 tracks — subject tracks, plot point tracks, or link tracks. These sources supersede v1 in places. Knowing what's been decided here determines what v1 content gets skipped rather than migrated during the archive evaluation pass.

Archive Evaluation Phase

For each archive note: evaluate whether all statements and nuances have a home in v2. Dissect tangled content against the now-populated subject tracks — subject-level content goes to subject tracks, link-level content goes to link tracks, plot point delivery content goes to plot point tracks. Skip content superseded by the reference source integration. Unresolvable questions become flagged notes in the appropriate v2 track. Mark each archive note Confirmed when fully evaluated. When all archive notes are Confirmed, archive is retired and v2 becomes master database.

Export UI Build Phase (short engineering task; can overlap with late archive evaluation work)

Required before expansion produces useful targeted results. Build enough to select by subject and by chapter cluster.

Expansion Phase

Use selective export to send targeted context for specific flagged questions. Each resolved flag may generate new flags. Continue until working copy flags reach zero.

Delivery Blueprint Refinement Phase

Define Stakes, Outcomes, Reader Understanding Targets for plot points. Write P/WI/T link track content. Move working copy notes to Confirmed as they stabilize.

Prose Phase

One remaining gap
The Reference Source Integration Phase — 20+ AI conversation threads plus Google Keep — is a large body of material. Is the intent to process all of it in a single dedicated phase between Foundation Building and Archive Evaluation, or to work subject-by-subject (build AJ from memory → review all AI conversations and Google Keep entries relevant to AJ → then move to Chrysalis)? The subject-by-subject approach keeps context tighter and might surface contradictions earlier. The dedicated phase approach is cleaner as a workflow stage but delays archive evaluation longer. Which fits how you work?

May 22

Mapped CognitiveMode values against framework layers and proposed consolidation
Mapped CognitiveMode values against framework layers and proposed consolidation
Tracing the origin
The original five values came from the cognitive role the writer occupies when writing different kinds of notes — not what the note contains, but what thinking mode produces it:

ZeroFocalization → InUniverseContent: writer as in-universe historian/physicist, asserting world truth as if writing an encyclopedia about a real world. Source: the five-layer framework's Layers 1-3 (mechanics, history, psychology).

SceneArchitecture → ReaderExperienceDesign: writer as director, designing how readers experience the other layers. Source: Layer 4 (narrative architecture/syuzhet).

Metatextual → NotesToSelf: writer talking to future self, treating the project as a project. Source: Layer 5 and meta-level design work.

Analogical: writer connecting the project world to real-world parallels. Source: the authorial commentary layer (author's lived experience informing themes).

LinguisticExecution: writer as prose craftsman, sentence-level decisions. Source: Layer of actual prose drafting.

Page and WorldInference were appended when the P/WI/T inference chain was formalized, splitting what "goes on page" from "what reader infers."

Assessment against the current framework
InUniverseContent → WorldTruth: KEEP, rename Still precisely valid. W tracks (Foundation, Historical Record, Arc, Events) require exactly this mode: asserting canonical world truth independent of any reader experience. The renamed version "WorldTruth" is more precise — it's not just "in-universe" content, it's the canonical ontological layer. Maps cleanly to W tracks.

ReaderExperienceDesign: OBSOLETE as a standalone value When this was written, it was the only reader-experience category. The P/WI/T framework has since decomposed it into three distinct layers — Page, WorldInference, and thematic conclusion — each requiring a different cognitive mode. Keeping ReaderExperienceDesign alongside Page and WorldInference creates ambiguity about which tracks it governs. It should be retired and replaced by the more specific values.

NotesToSelf → Metatextual: KEEP Still precisely valid and clearly distinct. Design Notes and Craft Notes require this mode: the writer is not asserting world truth or designing reader experience, but thinking about the project as a creative artifact. The rename to Metatextual more precisely describes the mode.

Analogical: FOLD INTO Metatextual The framework now explicitly requires that real-world allegory live only in Design Notes, never in T tracks. Analogical content is therefore a subset of metatextual content. As a separate top-level CognitiveMode it creates confusion about where real-world parallels belong; as a recognized sub-type of Metatextual it's handled by the "no allegory in T notes" discipline. The background color doesn't need to distinguish Analogical from other metatextual notes — the track's purpose does that.

LinguisticExecution: RETIRE Prose drafting is out of v2's scope. The framework stops at the Delivery Blueprint and WI Translation Notes levels. If craft-voice observations appear, they go into Craft Notes under Metatextual. No track in v2 is specifically the "prose writer mode" track, so this value has no track to govern.

Page: KEEP Valid and correctly placed. P tracks (P+La+L, P+En+L, Delivery Blueprint) require a specific mode: "what goes literally on the page, staged for the reader to observe." This is distinct from WorldTruth (canonical assertion) and WorldInference (reader construction).

WorldInference: KEEP Valid and correctly placed for WI tracks and WI Translation Notes. Also the correct mode for La+S+Reveal and En+S+Witness — these are project-wide reader inference design, not canonical world assertion.

ThematicArgument: ADD Currently missing. T tracks (T+La+L, T+En+L) require a distinct cognitive mode from WorldInference: not "what does the reader infer about the world" but "what thematic proposition does the reader arrive at." These are the second inference step, philosophically distinct from the first. Without a CognitiveMode for T tracks, they'd need to share with WorldInference, which is imprecise and loses the visual signal that you're now doing philosophical argumentation rather than world-inference design.

Proposed updated values
Value	Tracks	Cognitive role
WorldTruth	Foundation, Historical Record, Arc, Events	In-universe historian/psychologist/physicist — canonical assertion
PageDesign	P+La+L, P+En+L, Delivery Blueprint	Director — staging what reader observes
WorldInference	WI+La+L, WI+En+L, WI Translation Notes, La+S+Reveal, En+S+Witness	Reader psychologist — designing what reader constructs from evidence
ThematicArgument	T+La+L, T+En+L	Philosopher — designing the proposition reader independently arrives at
Metatextual	Design Notes, Craft Notes, Stakes, Scene Outcome	Author thinking about project as project — design decisions, allegory, craft notes
On EditorMode and CognitiveMode being orthogonal
These are correctly separate. CognitiveMode is a fixed property of a track definition — it describes what cognitive mode is required to write correctly into that track. EditorMode is a dynamic window state — it determines which display text appears and which tracks are active. The same track definition carries its CognitiveMode regardless of which EditorMode the window is in. The background color from CognitiveMode and the toggled display text from EditorMode are independent UX signals serving independent purposes: "what thinking mode does this track require" versus "what am I doing right now in this window."

I'm thinking each of these cognitive modes should be accompanied by a description, both on the UI for me to read as a reminder and to be part of the ai llm context export to signify what the notes are for.

So PageDesign would say "These notes are written by a Director staging what readers should observe." WorldInference would say "These notes are written by a Reader Psychologist designing what the reader constructs from evidence."

Therefore, I think WorldTruth needs to be broken up into the layer 1/2/3 splits to accomodate the different Physicist/Historian/Psychologist stances required to author and analyze that track. This is where differentiation between subject types like Character and WorldLaw and PoliticalSystem and Bond come in.

I believe Metatextual (which I renamed to NotesToSelf to be more intuitive) is limited in scope for specifically talking about the project. Analogical is different, it's connecting the project to the real world. Neither of them reach the page.

Show more
May 22

Architected cognitive mode framework with layered stances and descriptions
Architected cognitive mode framework with layered stances and descriptions
Validating the WorldTruth split
The split is correct and the reason is precise: the Foundation track for a Character requires a different stance than the Foundation track for a WorldLaw, even though both are "canonical world truth" tracks. The three stances are:

Physicist — asserting ground rules that exist independent of any agent's knowledge or story event. WorldLaw Foundation, Technology Foundation, AbstractSystem Foundation. The world operates this way whether or not anyone in it knows it.

Historian — recording what objectively happened in chronological order, independent of any character's perception. Historical Record tracks across all subject types. Also Arc tracks when they record developmental sequences rather than psychological states.

Psychologist — establishing who this creature or collective is and why, including epistemic limits and belief structures. Character Foundation, Bond Foundation, Organization Foundation (whose binding logic is collective motivation). These are claims about inner architecture that can't be observed from outside.

The subject type determines which WorldTruth variant a Foundation track gets. Character Foundation = Psychologist. WorldLaw Foundation = Physicist. Organization Foundation = Psychologist (binding logic is motivational). AbstractSystem Foundation = Physicist (how the system operates, like an institutional analyst). Technology Foundation = Physicist.

One potential gap: PoliticalSystem as a subject type might need both stances on its Foundation — the institutional mechanics are Physicist, but the actors' beliefs and motivations that sustain the system are Psychologist. Whether this requires a fourth variant or is handled by having two separate tracks (one per stance) is a design decision.

On Analogical staying separate from NotesToSelf
Correct to keep them separate. The descriptions reveal why:

NotesToSelf — These notes treat the project as a project. Design decisions, research obligations, craft observations, things to do, plans for future work. The author is speaking to their future self about how to build the artifact. Never reaches the page.

Analogical — These notes ground the fiction in real-world parallels: biographical inspiration, historical models, social commentary context. The author is connecting the project world to lived reality. Never reaches the page.

These are cognitively distinct. NotesToSelf is about the construction process. Analogical is about the source material and real-world resonance. An LLM context export needs to distinguish them: NotesToSelf notes should be treated as operational meta-commentary about the project's development; Analogical notes should be treated as the author's thematic grounding material that must not contaminate T notes or subject canon.

Both explicitly flagged as "never reaches the page" — that constraint is load-bearing for both the writer's discipline and the LLM's interpretation.

Full proposed set with descriptions
Physicist — These notes assert ground rules about how the world operates: physics, magic mechanics, biological systems, institutional functions. Written by a Systems Physicist establishing what is true independent of any agent's knowledge or the story's events.

Historian — These notes record what objectively happened in chronological order, independent of any character's perception or the narrative's sequencing. Written by a Historian documenting the causal record as an omniscient observer.

Psychologist — These notes establish who this creature or collective is and why — psychological architecture, wounds, beliefs, motivations, and epistemic limits. Written by a Psychologist mapping interior architecture that cannot be directly observed from outside.

PageDesign — These notes specify what the reader literally observes on the page: disclosed facts, staged events, dialogue, behavior, sensory detail. Written by a Director staging the observable surface.

WorldInference — These notes design what the reader constructs from observable evidence — world truths that cannot be stated because they are thematically loaded. Written by a Reader Psychologist designing what forms in the reader's head, not on the page.

ThematicArgument — These notes specify the thematic proposition the reader independently arrives at — the final step of the inference chain that must never be stated. Written by a Philosopher designing the argument the reader will believe they discovered themselves.

NotesToSelf — These notes treat the project as a project: design decisions, research obligations, craft observations, plans, questions. The author speaks to their future self about how to build the artifact. Never reaches the page.

Analogical — These notes ground the fiction in real-world parallels: biographical inspiration, historical models, social commentary context. The author connects the project world to lived reality. Never reaches the page.

One design decision to flag
The Historical Record track on a Character is Historian, but a Character's Arc track records how the character changes through the story — this is psychological development, not just historical sequence. Should Arc tracks be Psychologist, or Historian, or does it depend on what's being asserted? The distinction: if Arc notes describe what psychological shift occurs they're Psychologist; if they describe what events produce the shift they might blend Historian and Psychologist. One option is that Arc tracks are assigned Psychologist since they ultimately assert psychological state changes, with Historian notes about the triggering events living in the Historical Record. But this may need verification against how you're actually writing Arc content.

I don't think Foundation and Historical Record are a one size fit all presciption for subject types. For Technology and Political System I was considering tracks like History, How it works, and then Why it was created which is psychologist or economist or sociologist.

I would like a deconstruction of the "Physicist" cognitive mode into first principles based on what could appear, and then from the total, maixmal first principles list of cognitive modes I can decide which one apply to subject types.

May 22

Decomposed Physicist mode into eight distinct analytical stances
Decomposed Physicist mode into eight distinct analytical stances
Deconstructing "Physicist" from first principles
The Physicist stance was originally doing one job: asserting how something works independent of any agent's knowledge or the story's events. But "how something works" is not one question — it depends entirely on what kind of thing is being analyzed. The domain determines the discipline.

The root question that generates Physicist: "What are the immutable rules governing this phenomenon, which exist whether or not anyone in the world knows them?"

This is the correct question for natural laws, biological mechanics, and magic systems. But the moment you ask it about a technology, a political system, or an economic model, the correct answering discipline changes — because those things are products of design, choice, and social forces, not natural law.

The maximal first-principles list for WorldTruth cognitive modes
Physicist Root question: What immutable rules govern how this phenomenon operates? Domain: Natural laws, physical constraints, biological mechanics, magic systems as metaphysical substrate. These exist independent of any agent's design or choice — the world simply operates this way. Description: These notes assert ground rules that exist independent of any agent's knowledge or action. Written by a Physicist observing a universe whose rules were not chosen.

Engineer Root question: How was this artifact constructed, and how does it function given its design? Domain: Technology, built systems, designed mechanisms. Unlike Physicist, an Engineer's subject was made — it has a construction, components, operational requirements, failure modes, intended purpose. The Love Harvester, the Luna Nova Rifle, the Star Spade. Description: These notes specify how an artifact works from the inside out — components, mechanisms, operating requirements, design constraints. Written by an Engineer documenting what was built and how.

Historian Root question: What happened in what order, and what caused what? Domain: All subjects' developmental records — events in sequence, causal chains, chronology of change. Independent of any character's perception of those events. Description: These notes record what objectively happened in chronological sequence, independent of how characters perceived it or the narrative presents it. Written by a Historian documenting the causal record.

Psychologist Root question: What is the interior architecture of this mind — its beliefs, wounds, motivations, and epistemic limits? Domain: Individual characters, romantic and friendship bonds, the psychological foundations of collectives (what shared belief or wound holds an organization together). These are claims about inner structure that cannot be directly observed from outside. Description: These notes establish interior architecture — beliefs, wounds, motivations, epistemic limits, psychological patterns. Written by a Psychologist mapping what cannot be observed from the outside.

Economist Root question: What incentive structures, exchange mechanisms, and material constraints shape behavior at the systemic level? Domain: Economic systems, trade structures, resource flows, financial instruments, market forces. Distinct from Psychologist because the analysis is structural rather than interior — not why an individual wants something, but why the system rewards certain behaviors and punishes others. Harmonic Capitalism's mechanics, Chrysalis's economic scheme, Skyfall's insurance racket. Description: These notes analyze the incentive structures, exchange mechanisms, and material pressures that shape behavior systemically — not individual motivation but structural forces. Written by an Economist analyzing why rational actors in this system do what they do.

Sociologist Root question: What forces bind or fragment collectives — and how do shared systems produce collective outcomes beyond individual choice? Domain: Cultural forces, institutional binding logic, social contracts, group identity, class dynamics, collective behavior patterns. How the changeling hive coheres despite individual variation. Why the Tall Tale farming community rejected industry and then accepted it. Description: These notes explain how collectives cohere, fragment, or change — cultural forces, institutional logic, social contracts, group identity. Written by a Sociologist observing how shared systems produce outcomes no individual chose.

Political Scientist Root question: How is power acquired, maintained, and legitimated — and how do those in power remain there? Domain: Governance structures, authority relationships, ideological justification, resistance mechanisms, the organization of coercion and consent. The Equestrian Republic's constitutional structure, Chrysalis's control apparatus, the Stagnation as political project. Description: These notes document how power flows, how authority is structured, and how governance is legitimated or contested. Written by a Political Scientist analyzing who controls what and how they maintain it.

Anthropologist Root question: What cultural practices, rituals, traditions, and meaning-making systems define how this group understands the world and itself? Domain: Cultural practices, ceremonies, naming conventions, taboos, oral traditions, symbolic systems, how a group constructs identity through shared practice. The Tzinacatl raiding culture, changeling naming conventions, Equestrian harvest festivals, griffon artisan traditions. Description: These notes record the cultural practices, rituals, traditions, and symbolic systems through which a group makes meaning and constructs identity. Written by an Anthropologist observing a culture from the outside trying to understand it from the inside.

How this maps to subject types
The subject type determines which of these modes its tracks can have. A Character entity will never have a Political Scientist track. A PoliticalSystem entity will have tracks spanning Historian, Political Scientist, Economist, and Sociologist. A Technology entity spans Engineer, Historian, Economist (why it was created, what demand it fills), and Psychologist (what drove the inventor).

This is also why "Why it was created" doesn't fit Physicist — creation requires an agent, an intent, and a context. Depending on what kind of answer you want:

What technical problem does it solve → Engineer
What economic demand drove its creation → Economist
What did the inventor want to achieve → Psychologist
What political purpose did it serve → Political Scientist
What cultural moment made it possible → Anthropologist
These are genuinely different questions that produce different kinds of notes — and different LLM instructions about how to interpret what the notes are saying.

What are the pros and cons of declaring these 8 cognitive modes to be mutually exclusive, requiring different tracks, or if some can be combined in a track, but the track still clearly labels what two things are operating and that it's not to be a mix of all 8? Are there natural overlaps, are overlaps productive or shooting myself in the foot? Why or why not? Because it seems like political scientist and economist and sociologist have overlap regarding political systems and potentially others. Should these lenses be mixed in a track or does that create problems? From the perspective specifically of a world builder/project manager/writer; that is to say, my specific project's purposes. Please ask clarifying questions for gaps instead of assuming.

May 22

Weighed mutual exclusivity versus compound modes, identified critical gaps
Weighed mutual exclusivity versus compound modes, identified critical gaps
The core tension
Mutual exclusivity enforces the discipline that makes v2 different from v1. Combination acknowledges that some subjects — particularly PoliticalSystems and economic-political hybrids — exist precisely at the intersection of multiple modes. The question is whether allowing combination solves a real problem or recreates the original problem with a label on it.

Pros of strict mutual exclusivity
Cognitive discipline enforced structurally. When a track has one mode, any sentence you're writing that drifts into a different mode is automatically a signal: this belongs elsewhere. The structure prevents drift rather than relying on your discipline in the moment.

Clean LLM interpretation. If a track is labeled Economist, the LLM treats every note in it as incentive-structure analysis. It doesn't need to distinguish between types of claims within the track. The cognitive mode description becomes a reliable framing instruction for everything in that track.

Easier auditing. When you audit a track for cognitive mixing — the "no mixed notes" standard you established — a single-mode track has a clear pass/fail criterion. A compound track requires you to evaluate whether the note is addressing both modes correctly, which is a harder judgment.

Forces insight separation. Writing the Economist insight separately from the Political Scientist insight often produces sharper versions of each. When you have to fill the Economist track without reaching for political claims as a crutch, you discover what the economic mechanism actually is on its own terms.

Cons of strict mutual exclusivity
Genuine conceptual entanglement. Political Scientist and Economist aren't just nearby disciplines — they frequently address the same phenomena from different angles. Chrysalis's debt trap IS her political control mechanism. Describing the economic mechanism without the political consequence feels artificially incomplete, and vice versa.

Fragmentation of insight across tracks. If you have a sharp insight about how economic dependency becomes political leverage, writing it in the Economist track produces "economic dependency is created," then writing it again in the Political Scientist track produces "this dependency is then exploited for control." The insight is actually about the connection between them — splitting it loses the connection.

Some subjects ARE fusions by definition. Harmonic Capitalism is a PoliticalEconomy — it was explicitly designed to make political power and economic participation inseparable. Writing an Economist track for it without political claims would be falsifying what it is.

The natural overlaps and whether they're productive
Political Scientist + Economist. The most significant overlap in your project. These are genuinely asking different questions: Economist asks what the incentive flows are, Political Scientist asks who controls whom and how. But for subjects like Chrysalis's Economic Scheme, Harmonic Capitalism, Skyfall's insurance racket, the economic mechanism IS the political control mechanism — they're the same device viewed from two angles. Separating them produces complementary analyses that strengthen each other when read together.

Sociologist + Anthropologist. Sociologist focuses on structural forces; Anthropologist focuses on cultural meaning-making. For the Tzinacatl tribes, these are distinct questions: why do the tribes fragment (Sociologist — zero-sum drug economy) vs. what practices constitute their identity (Anthropologist — raiding culture, echolocation traditions). These separate cleanly for most subjects.

Political Scientist + Sociologist. Political structures are embedded in social ones. The Stagnation of Harmony is simultaneously a political project (Celestia maintaining power) and a sociological phenomenon (a whole society organized around pacification). These ask somewhat different questions but the boundary is blurrier than Sociologist/Anthropologist.

Psychologist + anything else. The clearest case for mutual exclusivity. Individual psychology is categorically different from systemic analysis. When psychology mixes with Economist content, you get "Chrysalis does this because she wants power AND the incentives structure makes it rational" — which confuses motivation with mechanism.

The key risk of allowing combination
The reason v1's notes were problematic wasn't that multiple modes were present in the same document — it's that they weren't held consistently within a note. "Compound track" can easily become permission to write undisciplined mixed content with a label on top. The writer sits down to write a "Political Scientist + Economist" track note and ends up writing a paragraph that's mostly Psychologist with some Sociologist and a sentence about power at the end.

The label doesn't enforce discipline inside the note. Only the display question does. And a compound display question — "How do the incentive structures create and sustain political control?" — is still a single question that keeps both modes in focus simultaneously. That's different from a track that says "write about all the economic and political things."

A possible middle position
Single mode per track, but compound subjects get more tracks. Rather than a "Political Scientist + Economist" compound track, a PoliticalSystem subject gets a Political Scientist track AND an Economist track. Each note is clean. The synthesis happens when you read both tracks together — which is the same synthesis that used to be tangled in one paragraph.

The cost: more tracks per subject, more to populate. The benefit: each track is auditable, each note has a clear criterion, and the LLM gets unambiguous framing for each section.

Compound tracks as explicit bridge questions. A third track labeled "How does the economic mechanism become political control?" isn't Economist OR Political Scientist — it's specifically about the connection. The display question is narrow enough that it can't drift. This is different from a general "compound" label.

Gaps before I can give a sharper recommendation
Gap 1: When you previously analyzed something like Chrysalis's economic scheme in v1, did the economic and political analysis naturally separate into distinct insights, or did they appear as inseparable claims where the economic fact was also a political fact in the same sentence? A specific example would help calibrate whether single-mode tracks would lose real content or just reorganize it.

Gap 2: For your LLM export use case specifically — when you ask a question about a PoliticalSystem subject, do you want the AI to receive the economic analysis and political analysis as separate sections (which lets it compare them), or as integrated analysis (which gives it the synthesis but not the decomposed parts)?

Gap 3: Are there subjects in your project where the subject type IS explicitly a fusion by design — not just a system that has economic and political dimensions, but a system whose core argument is that those dimensions are inseparable? Harmonic Capitalism seems to be a candidate. If so, is that subject better served by a compound track or by two tracks that both address the same material from different angles?

Gap 4: When you're writing a scene that involves a PoliticalSystem, which reference do you actually reach for — the incentive analysis, the power analysis, or an integrated account of both? The answer shapes whether fragmentation into two tracks serves the writer or frustrates them.

I think when Claude initially diagnosed "notes need to become typed claims and separate tracks" this was a correct diagnosis of history, psychology, reader inference and what's on page and stuff like that needing separation, but upon looking deeply at the nuances, I think political and economic separation is too far. These do belong in one track for things like Harmonic Capitalism because they are designed together for worldbuilding and story telling. So I think things need to be framed in terms of authorship/storytelling/story planning. Psychology is very special, specifically individual character psychology, because that is load bearing. History/backstory is separate. Physicist (the magical systems that are made up for the story) is separate. Then stuff like economy, political, sociology, anthropology needs a good umbrella term distinct from the other three pillars. They belong in the same pillar because they serve the same worldbuilding/storytelling purpose, I think. I also need a precise term for the "Physicist" considering I am making subject entities for world laws that I am creating for the story/world, such as "Earth Pony Magic". I'm not making subject entities for world laws that exist in real life like "haber-bosch process" or "nitrocellulose gunpowder" or "how oil is formed". I guess Ontology is what I'm creating for the world, so maybe ontologist? Or fantasy system builder? Don't know if these are too specific.

With this insight in mind, I don't think the LLM should receive economic and political analysis separately. They seem to be part of the same worldbuilding pillar - separate from backstory and character psychology and immutable world physics that were created. These are things created by characters, kind of like technology too. And technology doesn't have entries for things that already exist in WW2-era tech; I am making entries for magical/original stuff for TLTT (and since I made them, they serve a storytelling and probably a thematic purpose, hence get a subject entry).

When imagining a scene with a political system, an integrated account of both power analysis and incentive analysis is probably what is most helpful and meaningful for the author and reader. Similar answer for whatever happens with technology.

May 22

Restructured worldbuilding taxonomy into four pillars with naming options
Restructured worldbuilding taxonomy into four pillars with naming options
The emerging four-pillar structure
Your insight resolves cleanly into four WorldTruth cognitive modes, not eight:

Pillar 1 — Individual Psychology Load-bearing, stays fully separate. The interior architecture of individual minds and bonds. Nothing from any other pillar should contaminate this because the moment you start explaining character psychology through economic incentives or institutional forces instead of interior wounds and beliefs, you've lost the character.

Pillar 2 — Chronological Record Historian stance. What objectively happened in what order, independent of perception. Stays fully separate. History is the substrate; psychology, institutions, and world physics all leave traces in it, but the record itself is just sequence and causality.

Pillar 3 — Invented World Physics The created metaphysical and biological ground rules of the fictional world. Earth Pony Magic, the Charitostatic Effect, changeling biology as a species trait, love-energy as a physical phenomenon. These exist as natural law in the world — not designed by any character, just discovered or encountered. The author's stance is creator-god asserting rules, not an analyst of agent behavior.

Pillar 4 — [The umbrella] Everything agents built: economic systems, political orders, social structures, cultural practices, organizations, and invented technology. Integrated, because these are co-designed for worldbuilding and storytelling purposes and produce more useful reference as a whole account than as separated disciplines.

On naming Pillar 3
You're right that "Physicist" is wrong — it implies real-world physics the author didn't invent. The stance is specifically "author establishing the ground rules of their created world." Candidates:

Ontologist — your own word, and philosophically precise. Ontology is the study of what exists and how. In your context: asserting what fundamentally exists in this world and how it operates at the substrate level. Slightly academic but accurate. UI description could make it accessible: "These notes assert the fundamental rules of the invented world — how its unique physics, biology, and metaphysics operate. Written as the author establishing ground truth that exists independent of any character's knowledge."

World-Mechanic — more accessible, immediately descriptive. Risks sounding like a maintenance worker rather than a creator-god.

Lore Architect — common in gaming/fanfiction. Too informal for a disciplined planning tool?

System Founder — the author founding the rules of the system. Slightly odd.

My lean is Ontologist if the description makes it intuitive, because it's the most precise word for "I am asserting what fundamentally exists in this world."

On naming Pillar 4
The umbrella for economic + political + sociological + anthropological + organizational + technological analysis of agent-created systems. What unifies them:

All created by agents (characters, civilizations, institutions) rather than by natural law
All integrated because they were co-designed for worldbuilding and thematic purposes
All serve as material context that drives the story (materialist historicism: material conditions drive history drive culture drive character drive plot)
Candidates:

Civilizational — broad, encompasses all agent-built systems. "Civilizational Analysis" as a mode. UI: "These notes analyze the economic, political, social, cultural, and technological systems that agents built — integrated because they were co-designed for worldbuilding and serve the story's material conditions."

Material Conditions — deeply resonant with your own framework's vocabulary (materialist historicism), but possibly too abstract for a UI label someone needs to intuit quickly.

Social Systems — clean but might read as excluding technology and economic structures.

Constructed Order — what civilization constructed, as opposed to natural law. Precise but slightly formal.

World Architecture — what agents architected within the world. But "architecture" already carries meaning in narrative architecture.

Human Systems — except your world has ponies and changelings, not humans.

One complication to flag before you decide
Technology sits across a boundary. The Love Harvester has two kinds of content:

How it works mechanically given the world's magical physics → Pillar 3 (Ontologist), because it operates by the invented world's rules
What it was designed for, what it does to society, what it reveals about Chrysalis's civilization → Pillar 4 (the umbrella)
If Technology subjects only get Pillar 4 tracks, the mechanical "how it actually works" content either gets flattened into the civilizational analysis or loses its home. If Technology subjects can have both Pillar 3 tracks (for mechanics) and Pillar 4 tracks (for social/political/economic function), the distinction between pillars does real work for Technology entities specifically.

Does that match your intuition — that the Love Harvester's mechanical operation and its civilizational role are worth distinguishing even within the same subject entity? Or is the integrated account what you want for Technology too?

I made the tracks for Character and started going through but realized some character notes really should be links. So now I think I need to make the plot points first but leave them empty so that links can be made. Currently I'm flagging them in the v1 archive with a flag note of where I think it should land.

I want to minimize double work. I am okay with the process of migration and sanitation being long, but I want the process to be optimized.

Analyzed workflow to eliminate double-touching flagged content through skeleton creation
Analyzed workflow to eliminate double-touching flagged content through skeleton creation
What's happening
You're encountering link-level content while building subject tracks, but the destination (plot point entity) doesn't exist yet. The flag is a pointer — "this content belongs at Scene X." A link IS a pointer. You're doing the same routing decision twice: once to write the flag, once to create the link when plot points exist. The flag step is the double work.

The optimization
Create plot point skeleton entities — titles only, correctly scoped, chapter-assigned — before continuing character track building. Then when you encounter link-level content, create the link directly instead of flagging. One touch instead of two.

This is just the plot point skeleton step from the established workflow, moved earlier than planned because you've discovered it unblocks character work.

What a link needs to exist
Only a SubjectId and a PlotPointId. The plot point just has to exist as an entity with a title. No Stakes, no Outcome, no Delivery Blueprint. The skeleton is sufficient for a link to be created.

What to put in the link during data transmutation
This is the judgment call: the no-tangled-paragraphs rule still applies to link tracks. Options:

Route the content to the correct link track now — requires making the P/WI/T and La/En judgment at time of encounter. Correct but slower.

Create the link as a structural skeleton with no content — the connection exists (subject → plot point) but the content routing is deferred to refinement. The v1 archive note gets Confirmed with a note that the link exists but content routing is pending.

The second option is probably right for data transmutation phase. The structural map — which subjects connect to which plot points — is the valuable output of this phase. The specific track routing within links is refinement work.

Gaps before confirming
Gap 1: When you encounter a v1 note that should be a link, can you usually identify which specific track it belongs in (P vs WI vs T, La vs En) from the v1 content alone? Or is it typically undifferentiated enough that track routing requires deliberate analysis you don't want to do now?

Gap 2: For the current characters you're building — are the link-level notes you're encountering mostly connected to specific, identifiable plot points (so you could create the link directly), or are they vague scene references ("something happens with Chrysalis during the battle") that don't map cleanly to a specific plot point yet?

Gap 3: How disruptive would the plot point skeleton pass feel right now — is it a natural stopping point to do that scan and come back to character building, or would you prefer to continue character building while someone else structured a batch approach?

The scene specific content comes out of a tangled paragraph. For example: 974 - Coltbert stopped Gisele's abuse by saying cruelty is uncouth. He used manners as a weapon. He offers a proper affair but she has to ask nicely; she throws him out. The other griffonesses brag to Gisele, showing off their trophy shelves (tableau de chasse). Gisele asks Minette to make a nice suit for Coltbert. Coltbert asks Minette if the Gisele made it, Minette lies for Gisele under pressure but Coltbert asks for the truth, saying it would be a shame if Gisele is lying, and Gisele admits that Minette made it. Then Coltbert says have your servant teach you how to make the suit, then he'll accept it. So Minette spends days teaching Gisele how to make the suit, and the other servants are totally left alone. Minette is fascinated at how Coltbert forced her to negotiate by calling her a brute. She helps Lady Gisele fill out her tableau de chasse (trophy shelf) to compete with the other noble griffonesses.

This old note on Minette is partly history for her, but the specific line "He used manners as a weapon" isn't a Minette note at all, it's a Coltbert note for whatever plot point is the dramatization of this summary in Minette's prequel, and that line implies a WI and T (I can feel it in the subtext) that I haven't written out but eventually should. I never made plot points for Minette's prequel, but it seems like I should in order for the Coltbert insight to be properly categorized in a way that is useful going forward.

May 22

Disentangled subject-level insights from dramatization-specific content requirements
Disentangled subject-level insights from dramatization-specific content requirements
What's actually in this tangled note
The paragraph contains four distinct things:

Routeable now — Minette's Historical Record: The 974 event sequence (Coltbert stops the abuse, the suit challenge, Minette teaches Gisele, the tableau de chasse). This is chronological record of what happened. Goes in Minette's Historical Record track regardless of whether any plot point exists.

Routeable now — Minette's Foundation or Arc: "Minette is fascinated at how Coltbert forced her to negotiate by calling her a brute." This is a character development observation about what Minette understood from witnessing that dynamic. Goes in Minette's Foundation or Arc track — it's a claim about her psychological architecture, not a scene-specific dramatization note.

Routeable now — Coltbert's Foundation: "He used manners as a weapon." This is a claim about who Coltbert is — his method, his operating principle. It's not scene-specific. It belongs in Coltbert's Foundation track as a Psychologist-mode note. This can be written now.

Deferred — the WI and T you can feel but haven't written: The dramatization of this scene in Minette's prequel would produce a link note: when the reader watches Coltbert use courtesy to disarm and dominate, what should they infer (WI) and what thematic conclusion should they arrive at (T)? This is link content — it requires both a plot point and analytical work to articulate. Neither exists yet.

The key distinction
Subject-level insights don't need plot points to have a home. A plot point is only required for the WI and T that emerge from witnessing the dramatization. The character truth — "Coltbert uses manners as a weapon" — can live in his Foundation track as a permanent claim about who he is, whether or not that scene ever gets formally dramatized.

The link track version would be: in this specific scene, the reader watches Coltbert do X, infers Y about him, arrives at Z thematically. That's different from — and downstream of — the Foundation claim.

For prequels with no arc plan
Don't create prequel plot points yet. Instead:

Route what's routeable to subject tracks now. For the dramatization implication — the part that requires a plot point — put a Flagged note in the relevant subject's Foundation or Arc track: "This implies a prequel scene involving Coltbert. Link content (WI, T) TBD when prequel arc is planned."

This is the right home because:

It lives in v2 (not in the v1 archive)
It's in the correct subject's track, not floating
The Flagged state signals it needs future work
It doesn't block the subject track from being considered built
It doesn't create speculative plot points that will shift when expansion reaches the prequel
For main story plot points: create the skeleton now so links can be made immediately. For prequels: capture what's capturable in subject tracks, flag the dramatization implication, defer the plot point.

The scope question this raises
You said the Coltbert line "implies a WI and T that I haven't written out but eventually should." That content doesn't exist in v1 — you'd be generating it from analysis.

Generating WI and T content is expansion work, not data transmutation. Transmutation captures what v1 contains and routes it correctly. Expansion produces new analytical content through deliberate design work.

Gap 1: When you encounter implied-but-unwritten WI and T during transmutation, is your intention to flag it and defer to expansion phase, or to write it out in the moment if it's clear enough? The answer affects how long transmutation takes and whether transmutation and expansion are staying cleanly separated.

Gap 2: Of the tangled paragraphs you've encountered so far while building character tracks, what proportion contain prequel-specific content (like Minette's 974 backstory) versus main story content? If prequel content is a significant proportion, knowing that changes how much of the "flag for prequel dramatization" pattern you'll be using.

The thing is, I used Minette's character entry in v1 as essentially a dumping ground for plans for her prequel. Yes, "he uses manners as a weapon" is part of Coltbert's foundation, but it's ALSO present in this specific event. If I don't create a home for that fact in v2 by making a plot point in Minette's prequel, that insight, to invoke Coltbert using manners as a weapon in that specific scene is Lost. Right now it is implied because his psychological foundation is mentioned alongside the tangled scene. This seems to mean the text can't just be moved somewhere, it really needs a proper home in order to preserve the insights.

I don't intend on writing out the WI and T during transmutation. The empty fields on the link would signify future work required.

Prequel content is probably not that common. I looked at Minette because she would be complex. Now I'm thinking to defer her. But maybe it's better in the long term to consider her now. I don't want to do double work (some is inevitable, but optimizing work order is preferred), and I don't want to lose insights.

Vague scene references I probably would just flag. Some "link content" my head goes straight to where I think it should belong, hence why I think those links need to be made on the spot to avoid double work. I think I can do the plot point skeleton for the main story now, not sure about prequels. They do have some sort of outline.
