May 22

Reconciled insight preservation with workflow efficiency through minimal prequel scaffolding
Reconciled insight preservation with workflow efficiency through minimal prequel scaffolding
You're right — the insight has two dimensions that need separate homes
"He uses manners as a weapon" in Coltbert's Foundation preserves the general character truth. But the specific scene in 974 where the reader gets to witness that truth is a different claim — it's architectural information about where and how that truth gets demonstrated. Without the plot point, you preserve the what but lose the where, and the where is what enables prose design later.

The resolution: on-demand prequel stubs
You don't need to design the Minette prequel arc to create a minimal plot point bucket. The bucket just needs a title and prequel assignment — "974 — Coltbert manners scene" — so the link can be made. No Stakes, no Outcome, no scope commitment beyond "this scene happened and Coltbert does this thing here."

When expansion eventually designs the prequel arc, that stub either:

Survives as correctly scoped
Gets absorbed into a larger scene (the link moves)
Gets split (the link moves to the relevant sub-scene)
In all three cases, the P+En+L note you put on the link — "Coltbert stops the abuse through elaborate courtesy, conditions acceptance on Gisele learning the craft from Minette" — remains valid regardless of rescoping. The fundamental observation doesn't become wrong just because the plot point boundaries shift.

The two-track approach going forward
Main story plot points: Do the full skeleton pass now. 32 chapters, clear arcs — create all buckets from the v1 chapter list. This enables direct link creation for all main story content.

Prequel plot points: Create minimal stubs on demand, only when you encounter specific scene-level insights during transmutation that clearly need a home. Not a prequel arc design pass — just enough to make the link on the spot. One stub per identifiable scene, title only.

The test for "create a stub now vs. flag and defer": if your head goes straight to which scene this belongs in, create the stub and make the link. If it's a vague reference without a clear scene identity, flag in the subject track.

On Minette specifically
Minette appears in the main story (Chapters 12-13, Stalliongrad crash). Her subject tracks should be built now — Foundation, Arc, Historical Record for her main story arc. That's not deferrable.

The prequel material you encountered is incidental — you happened to open her entry and found it. The prequel stubs get created on demand when that material demands it. Her being complex is an argument for building her main story tracks carefully, not for deferring her entirely.

The v1 entry being a "dumping ground for prequel plans" just means the transmutation pass for her entry will produce some prequel stubs alongside the subject track content. That's the expected output, not a reason to skip her.

Should there be some overlap between character's psychology track and history track? Because their psychology is caused by the history. How do I keep the tracks clean such that they are useful, but still have the necessary insights? It's starting to seem like these are not mutually exclusive tracks but rather the psychology/characterization builds on the history.

May 25

Reconciled history-psychology causality through cross-referencing framework
Reconciled history-psychology causality through cross-referencing framework
The tracks are distinct but not independent
Psychology is caused by history — that's correct and is the materialist premise the whole framework is built on. The tracks don't need to be independent to be clean. They need to be written from different stances, and the causal link is preserved through cross-reference, not duplication.

The distinction is:

Historical Record answers: what happened?
Foundation/Psychology answers: who is this person because of what happened?
The second track presupposes the first. That's fine. A psychologist writing a case assessment references formative events without reproducing them — the history section already did that work.

How to write at the boundary
When an insight sits at the intersection — a historical event AND its psychological consequence — split it into two notes with a pointer between them.

Historical Record note: "In 974, Minette observed Coltbert deflect Gisele's cruelty through elaborate courtesy rather than direct confrontation."

Foundation note: "Minette's belief that gentleness can be weaponized — that cultivated manner is more durable armor than strength — derives from watching Coltbert's method with Gisele. [→ 974, Historical Record]"

The historical note doesn't contain the psychological interpretation. The Foundation note doesn't reproduce the historical account. The reference pointer preserves the causal chain without duplication.

What the reference pointer does
For you as writer: you can read either track and know where the derivation comes from. For LLM context: the Foundation note delivers a clean psychological claim; the LLM knows it's derived from history because of the pointer, without the historical content cluttering the psychological track.

The Foundation note saying "because of X" — where X is a brief reference rather than a full account — is still in Psychologist mode. It's asserting a psychological consequence. The Historian's job is to document X in full. The Psychologist's job is to note that X produced Y, with enough pointer that X can be found.

One case where this gets harder
When a note fuses observation and interpretation so tightly that splitting feels artificial: "Minette saw Coltbert use manners as a weapon, and that became the template for her survival strategy."

The fusion is actually two claims:

An event occurred and Minette perceived it a certain way (Historical Record)
That perception became a lasting psychological structure (Foundation)
The split forces you to be more precise about each claim — which is the actual purpose of the separation. You discover that the Historical Record claim is about the event plus Minette's immediate perception of it, and the Foundation claim is about what that perception crystallized into over time. These aren't the same statement. Forcing the split produces sharper notes than the fusion did.

The practical rule
Write Foundation notes as psychological conclusions with brief causal pointers. Write Historical Record notes as events with no psychological interpretation. When you feel the urge to put psychological interpretation into a history note, that's the signal to split — the interpretation becomes a Foundation note with a reference back.

Here is an example of a v1 note:

Crystal ponies have emotion sense just like changelings. When they crystalize, it's a visual indicator of caloric surplus and charitostatic magic. Crystals are stored magic, so the transparent crystalization effect is stored friendship.  Adjacent to how thymodynamic magic from windigos are stored in crystals
Example from the canon episode: the crystal ponies literally get color back to their dull coats after hearing about the crystal faire
The big blast at the crystal faire makes even non crystal ponies get the crystalization effect

What track should the "example from the canon episode" go into? World truth ontology? Analogical? Should a new kind of track be defined?

May 25

Architected new cognitive mode for source material evidence tracking
Architected new cognitive mode for source material evidence tracking
What this note is doing
The canon episode reference is doing something none of the existing modes cover: the author observing behavior in source material and interpreting it as evidence for an invented world mechanic. It's not an assertion of world truth (Ontologist), not a historical record of what happened in the story world (Historian), not real-world social commentary (Analogical), and not author meta-commentary (NotesToSelf).

It's a distinct cognitive act: treating the source material as empirical data that your invented world mechanics explain.

A new track type: Source Evidence
Description for UI and LLM export: "These notes record observations from the source material — MLP:FIM canon, EaW lore — that the invented world mechanics explain or are consistent with. Written as an analyst interpreting source observations through the TLTT world-building lens. These are evidence for mechanics, not assertions of them. They never reach the page."

This mode recurs across all world-law subjects, character entries, and system descriptions in a fanfiction project. For every Ontologist claim you make, there's often a corresponding "and here's where you can see this in the source" — that observation needs a typed home that tells the LLM: this is interpretive evidence, not ground truth assertion.

Routing this specific note
Ontologist track: "Crystal ponies have emotion sense just like changelings. When they crystalize, it's a visual indicator of caloric surplus and charitostatic magic. Crystals are stored magic, so the transparent crystalization effect is stored friendship. The same principle by which thymodynamic magic from windigos is stored in crystals."

Source Evidence track: "S3E01: crystal ponies literally regain color to their dull coats after hearing about the crystal faire — consistent with charitostatic magic accumulating in response to collective emotional warmth." "S3E01: the blast at the crystal faire produces the crystalization effect in non-crystal ponies — consistent with stored charitostatic magic discharging into the environment."

Why this isn't Analogical
Analogical connects the project to the real world — lived experience, historical parallels, social commentary. Source Evidence connects the invented mechanics to the source material being adapted. These are genuinely different cognitive acts: one is grounding in reality, the other is grounding in the fiction you're building on top of. Keeping them separate means the LLM knows which external reference system a note is invoking.

Is there overlap with the reader opinion plan where I'm outlining how the reader's understanding updates, starting with tropes or preexisting material? Or this is something a bit different, where if they watch the canon show again after reading my story, they might view the show differently? It's similar to the reader prior belief clash but applied to the show itself which already existed and how my worldbuilding affects the interpretation of the show? Since a lot of my world building works within the constraints of the hasbro mandates to recontextualize them.

May 25

Distinguished canon recontextualization from reader belief updates within narrative
Distinguished canon recontextualization from reader belief updates within narrative
Three distinct things, not two
You're describing something genuinely different from both La+S+Reveal and Source Evidence. Here's the precise distinction between all three:

Source Evidence — author-facing, construction direction. Canon observation → validates TLTT mechanic. "Crystal ponies regain color at the crystal faire" supports the charitostatic storage mechanic. Used during world-building to check consistency. The LLM uses it to know the mechanic is grounded.

La+S+Reveal — reader-facing, inward. Tracks how the reader's beliefs about TLTT itself update as they read. The reader comes in with priors about the TLTT world and characters, and La disclosures progressively revise those priors toward the intended understanding.

What you're describing — reader-facing, outward. Tracks how TLTT changes the reader's experience of the source material when they return to it. The reader comes in with priors about the show, and TLTT's world-building retroactively reframes those priors.

Source Evidence and Canon Recontextualization are inverse operations on the same canon moment. During construction: canon observation → inspires or validates the mechanic. After reading: the mechanic → reframes the canon observation permanently. They're the two ends of the same relationship, pointing in opposite directions.

This warrants its own track type
Call it Canon Recontextualization. Description for UI and LLM export: "These notes design how this world-building claim changes the reader's experience of the source material once they return to it. Written by the author designing the retroactive reframing — how TLTT makes familiar source moments look different or carry new meaning."

For crystal pony charitostatics: after reading TLTT, the color restoration in S3E01 reads as literal physics — crystallized emotional surplus visibly discharging — rather than as metaphor or animation shorthand. The show's visual language becomes a measurement instrument rather than a symbol.

The Hasbro mandate application is the most interesting case
You said "a lot of my world-building works within the constraints of the Hasbro mandates to recontextualize them." This is Canon Recontextualization operating at a meta-narrative level — not just "this scene reads differently" but "the show's own evolution is predicted and explained by the story's world-building."

TLTT's Stagnation of Harmony, once understood, doesn't just reframe individual episodes. It reframes the arc of the show's production history. The reason Celestia became increasingly passive and institutional in later seasons, the reason friendship lessons became formulaic, the reason magic became less creative — TLTT is claiming these are the in-universe expression of the Stagnation as a historical force. The Hasbro mandate becomes evidence for the Stagnation, not an authorial failure to be apologized for.

This is a stronger and more specific version of Canon Recontextualization: the world-building doesn't just reframe a scene, it provides a causal theory for why the source material became what it became. For subjects like Celestia and the Stagnation of Harmony, this distinction might be worth capturing as a sub-type — or at minimum, as a note flag within the Canon Recontextualization track.

The three-track system for World Law subjects
For subjects like Crystal Pony Magic or Earth Pony Magic, you'd now have:

Ontologist — what the mechanic IS (ground truth assertion)

Source Evidence — canon observations that the mechanic explains (construction validation, author-facing)

Canon Recontextualization — how knowing the mechanic changes what the reader sees when watching the show (retroactive reader effect, outward-facing)

These aren't redundant. Source Evidence points from canon to mechanic. Canon Recontextualization points from mechanic back to canon. They're reciprocal, and both are meaningfully different from the Ontologist claim itself.

I'm thinking canon recontextualization doesn't need to be designed. It happens to a reader based on my ontology and reveals. This track doesn't seem to impact the design of my story.

And what is the purpose of source evidence for design of my story, if any?

May 25

Examined canon recontextualization as emergent effect; interrogated source evidence's design utility
Examined canon recontextualization as emergent effect; interrogated source evidence's design utility
On Canon Recontextualization
You're right. It's an emergent effect of your Ontologist mechanics and La reveals combined — not something you design separately. The reader recontextualizes canon automatically when the mechanics land. Designing it explicitly would mean duplicating work you've already done in those other tracks. No track needed.

On Source Evidence: what design value it actually has
The honest answer is that Source Evidence has one legitimate design function and one non-design function.

The design function: It specifies the reader's prior belief about a canon moment — the exact prior that your world mechanic will update through the La mechanism.

The reader watched S3E01. Their prior understanding of crystal ponies regaining color is probably "symbolic restoration of morale." Your charitostatic mechanic makes that prior specifically wrong in a productive way — the color restoration is literal accumulated magic discharging, not metaphor. That prior belief is the La setup. Without knowing what the reader's canon understanding was, you might design the La reveal without realizing you have a strong prior belief to leverage.

So Source Evidence → reader's prior belief → La+S+Reveal design. But that means the design-relevant content of Source Evidence belongs as context inside the La+S+Reveal track, not in its own separate track. The canon moment is part of specifying what prior the reader brings.

The non-design function: Validation — confirming your invented mechanic doesn't contradict observable canon behavior. This is authorial verification, not story design. It would live in NotesToSelf if it needs to live anywhere.

The conclusion
Source Evidence as a standalone track doesn't serve story design directly. Its useful portion belongs as prior-belief context inside La+S+Reveal notes. Its remainder is NotesToSelf-level validation.

The one exception: if you want the AI analysis to have grounding in canon observations when you're doing expansion on a World Law subject, having those observations somewhere in the context export improves analysis quality. That's a practical workflow value, not a design value.

Gap: Is AI expansion quality sufficient reason to maintain a track in your system, or should it be folded into NotesToSelf or dropped entirely in favor of pasting canon observations directly into expansion prompts when needed?

It really feels like inspiration from real world (analogical) and inspiration from source material warrant a separation. Maybe their usage for design overlaps, but still they feel distinct. The thing is, source material is also fantasy like TLTT, not real world analogies.

Are there any other important points that could warrant the distinction? Why do they feel distinct?

May 25

Excavated seven functional distinctions between real-world and source-material inspiration
Excavated seven functional distinctions between real-world and source-material inspiration
The distinctions that feel real are real
The intuition is tracking something precise. Here are the meaningful separations:

The type of reference is categorically different. Analogical crosses the fantasy-to-reality boundary — it grounds your fictional construction in observable, verifiable, shared reality. That's a specific kind of meaning-making: "this invented thing connects to something that actually exists." Source Evidence stays entirely within the fantasy domain — one fictional construction grounded in another. These are different kinds of grounding even when they serve similar creative purposes.

The author's relationship to each is different. Analogical material is the author as a person in the world, drawing on what they know and have experienced. It's your knowledge of how real economics or history or sociology works, imported into the story. Source Evidence is the author as adapter and steward, in dialogue with someone else's prior creative work. One is the author's expertise; the other is the author's obligation.

The design stakes are different. This is the most practically important distinction for your purposes. Analogical material is optional enrichment — you can use the MEFO bills parallel for Chrysalis's economic scheme or design it differently. Real-world parallels are a toolbox you pick from freely.

Source material is constraint. TLTT is built on top of MLP:FIM. The canon events happened. The audience you're writing for has watched the show and will hold TLTT accountable to it. Your mechanics must be consistent with observable canon, or the fanfiction community will notice. Analogical notes are invitations you can accept or decline; Source Evidence notes represent obligations you can't ignore.

The reader's relationship is different. Your audience may or may not know the real-world historical parallel you're drawing. The MEFO bills connection enriches TLTT for readers who know it but doesn't break the story for those who don't. But your audience definitively knows the source material — it's why they're reading TLTT. The source relationship is mandatory knowledge, not optional enrichment.

The design function that separates them most cleanly
Analogical notes are generative — they help you extrapolate and enrich. An AI analyzing your economic scheme alongside the MEFO bills parallel generates richer analysis.

Source Evidence notes are constraining — they validate and bound. An AI analyzing your mechanic alongside canon observations confirms whether your construction can actually hold, and flags where you must address something the canon shows.

One expands design space; the other bounds it. Both are useful, but they do different work, which is why they feel distinct. Keeping them separate lets you and the AI treat each correctly: Analogical as inspiration to explore, Source Evidence as constraint to respect.

The "Stagnation of Harmony" concept recontextualizes the canon show not as a utopia, but as a Nursery.
    The Nursery: A nursery is safe, soft, and innocent. But you cannot stay in a nursery forever. The Changeling invasion kicked down the door.
    The Trauma of Puberty: The war acts as a metaphor for the nation hitting puberty. They discover sex (Aquileian influence), violence (The Front), and independence (The Republic).
    Celestia as the Overprotective Mother: Her attempt to "White Peace" the conflict is a parent trying to shove the grown child back into the crib. Applejack’s refusal ("I am the Lioness") is the moment the child moves out. It is painful, but necessary.

I'm really not sure how to decouple and categorize the above notes. What are they saying? What story planning/design purpose do they serve? Are they too broad? Already implied? Is there a home for them?

May 26

Decomposed frame notes into constituent design tracks and implications
Decomposed frame notes into constituent design tracks and implications
What these notes actually are
They're a single conceptual frame operating at an architectural level — the Nursery metaphor is the root from which several different track-level claims derive. The reason they feel hard to categorize is that they're doing MULTIPLE things simultaneously, all downstream from one analogical anchor.

Let me trace what each part actually is:

"Recontextualizes the canon show not as a utopia, but as a Nursery" Two things combined: a design intention ("I'm building TLTT to make readers read the show differently" → NotesToSelf) and an Ontologist claim ("Equestrian society under the Stagnation IS structured like a nursery — safe, infantilizing, preventing adult capability development"). The design intention doesn't need a track; the Ontologist claim does.

"Safe, soft, innocent. But you cannot stay in a nursery forever. The Changeling invasion kicked down the door." Three things: the Nursery as an Analogical frame defining the Stagnation's properties, an Ontologist claim about what those properties produce in the world, and a Historian fact (the invasion as structural rupture). The "you cannot stay forever" is already a ThematicArgument seed — the reader should arrive at this independently.

"The war acts as a metaphor for the nation hitting puberty. They discover sex, violence, and independence." This is an Analogical frame that generates design decisions — it specifies WHAT must be included in the war's effects and in what register. The puberty analogy tells you: Aquileian influence must involve genuine adult sexuality (not sanitized), the Front must produce genuine confrontation with death and killing, the Republic must involve genuine self-determination at real cost. Without this frame, those three arcs could drift into something softer. The analogy constrains the thematic register of everything downstream.

"Celestia as Overprotective Mother / White Peace / AJ's refusal / the child moves out" Four separate things: an Analogical frame for Celestia's character archetype (which belongs in her Foundation track as the operative metaphor for who she is), a ThematicArgument claim about what the White Peace moment means for the reader, an En scene specification (the Lioness declaration as the moment of rupture), and a characterization of that scene's emotional register (painful but necessary).

Are they already implied?
Partially but critically not fully. If your Ontologist track on Stagnation of Harmony is well-written, the nursery structure should be implicit in the mechanics. If Celestia's Foundation is well-written, the overprotective parent psychology should be implicit. But without the explicit analogical frame, two things fail:

First, the coherence between them is invisible. The Nursery, the Puberty, and the Overprotective Mother are all the SAME metaphor applied at three different scales — societal, historical, and personal. That unification is lost if the three tracks hold the implications without the root.

Second, the design constraints they impose become weaker. "The war produces adult discoveries" is a design constraint that keeps each of the three arcs (sex, violence, independence) in the right register. Without the puberty frame making that constraint explicit, individual scene decisions can quietly drift.

Where they belong
The frame itself — preserved as a unit — belongs in the Analogical track on the Stagnation of Harmony subject. This is exactly what Analogical is for: grounding the fictional construction in a real-world conceptual structure that generates coherent design decisions. The Nursery/Puberty/Mother frame is the single most useful Analogical note this subject has.

The Celestia piece also belongs in Celestia's Analogical track, and its operative implication ("overprotective mother archetype") belongs in her Foundation track as a Psychologist claim.

Each frame implies claims in other tracks:

Stagnation Ontologist: what specific properties the Nursery structure produces in the world
Stagnation ThematicArgument: the puberty proposition the reader should arrive at
Celestia Foundation: the overprotective parent as her operative psychological archetype
A specific plot point link for the Lioness declaration: the En moment where AJ refuses and the reader witnesses the child-moves-out rupture — this needs a plot point to exist
The AJ piece is the one that can't live in a subject track alone. "The moment the child moves out" is a reader experience design claim about a specific scene. It needs to be a link T note on that scene's plot point, with the Analogical note on Stagnation as its conceptual root.

The practical upshot
Write the full frame as a single Analogical note on Stagnation of Harmony, intact. Then write the Ontologist, Foundation (Celestia), and ThematicArgument implications as separate notes in their respective tracks, each one a claim derived from the frame. The frame note is the anchor that keeps all of them coherent with each other. When the AI receives the Stagnation export, it gets the frame AND the specific claims — the frame tells it why the claims are structured as they are.

What order should v1 plot points be evaluated to populate v2? I want the possible logic for making this decision. I am hesitant to go in chapter order because early chapters actually feel less developed because I wanted the big picture before seeing the early chapters with things with payoff later. I'm wondering if plot points that are immovably load bearing would go first as anchors? Like the Town Hall which lays out the thesis. But the Stagnation chapter which is a big turning point is a disorganized mess and could get stuff added to it or cut still.

May 27

Synthesized stability, dependency, and thematic chains into ordering framework
Synthesized stability, dependency, and thematic chains into ordering framework
The axes that determine the logic
Three independent axes are in tension, and the ordering should balance all three:

Stability — how certain is this plot point's content? Will it survive expansion without rework?

Load-bearing weight — how many other plot points depend on this one being correctly documented first?

Subject readiness — are the subjects this plot point primarily involves already built in v2?

The problem with "most load-bearing first" is that the Town Hall is both extremely load-bearing AND well-developed — but the Stagnation turning point is extremely load-bearing AND unstable. Load-bearing alone doesn't resolve the order. You need all three axes.

The resulting priority logic
Do first: High stability + high load-bearing These are the fixed points. Plot points that are definitionally required by the story's architecture AND whose content is settled. The Town Hall is the clearest example — stable, fully developed, foundational to everything in Chapters 10-12. These become the reference anchors that calibrate everything adjacent to them.

Do second: High stability + subject-ready, regardless of chapter position Plot points where the relevant subjects are already built and the content is solid. If AJ's subjects are built before Twilight's, evaluate AJ-heavy stable plot points before Twilight-heavy ones. This is why subject-building order directly determines plot-point evaluation order — the subjects being built are the prerequisite for writing clean links.

Do third: Well-developed middle chapters in narrative order within themselves Chapters 9-13 seem to be in this category. Once the anchor plot points are documented and the relevant subjects are built, going through these chapters in sequence makes sense because thematic chains flow in order within them — the prior being planted in scene 10.6 only makes sense as a prior if you already know what scene 12.14 is building toward.

Do fourth: Culminating moments before their setups This addresses your observation that early chapters were written after knowing the big picture. The culmination of AJ's arc — whatever plot point that is — is more stable than the setup in Chapter 1, because the setup's meaning is entirely determined by what it's setting up. Documenting the culmination first lets you correctly identify what prior beliefs the early scenes are planting. Working backwards from culmination to setup prevents you from documenting early scenes without knowing what they're for.

Defer: Unstable high-stakes chapters The Stagnation turning point chapter — load-bearing for thesis, content still uncertain. The right move for these is skeleton-only: create the plot point title and chapter assignment so links can be made, but don't evaluate the synopsis content until expansion has developed it. This preserves the structural anchor without locking in content that will change.

Last: Prequel material and underdeveloped chapters Least stable, smallest benefit from being early.

The exception worth naming
A plot point can be structurally necessary for links (so you need its skeleton to exist) without its content being ready to evaluate. The skeleton creation and the content evaluation are separable operations. When a plot point is both load-bearing and unstable, create the skeleton immediately — but mark the content evaluation as deferred. This lets you make links to it without committing to synopsis content that might be replaced.

Gaps before the order can be precise
Gap 1: What is AJ's culminating arc moment — the plot point that everything in her arc is building toward? Same for Twilight. Knowing these anchors determines where "work backwards from culmination" starts.

Gap 2: The Stagnation turning point chapter — is this a chapter where the Stagnation system is explicitly confronted or dismantled, or something else? Understanding what makes it load-bearing would clarify whether it should get a full skeleton with deferred evaluation, or whether even the skeleton is too speculative to create now.

Gap 3: Beyond Chapters 9-13, which chapters do you consider developed enough to evaluate without expecting significant expansion to change them? The "well-developed middle chapters in narrative order" tier depends on knowing which chapters qualify.


TLTT v2-definitions.md
md

Here are the tracks I came up with. Give an analysis, comparing to the principles established throughout this conversation. What is working well? What could be meaningfully improved, if any? Are there any significant gaps?

May 27

Evaluated track system against established principles, identifying strengths and refinement opportunities
Evaluated track system against established principles, identifying strengths and refinement opportunities
What's working well
The La mechanism is correctly implemented across all subject types. The three-layer chain — PageDesign (Revelation/Stated Principles/Demonstration) → WorldInference (Characterization Inference/World Inference/Reader Understanding Plan) → ThematicEvidence (Reader Prior Belief Clash + Themes) — appears consistently in every subject type's link tracks. The structure is sound.

Reader Prior Belief Clash is correctly placed at link level, not project-wide level. This is precise. A prior belief clash is specific to how a subject is deployed in a scene, not a property of the subject itself. Keeping it out of project-wide tracks and into link tracks is correct.

World Law's Historical Study track is precisely formulated. "The history of how the world truth was studied in-universe by characters, and how the characters' understanding evolved through in-universe history" — this is not just history of the law, but history of in-universe epistemic access to the law. The distinction between the law existing and characters knowing the law is exactly right, and it prevents the track from being contaminated with the World Truth itself.

World Law's World Truth Usage Directive is load-bearing. "If the page text contradicts the truth, it should be intentional due to character epistemological limits" — this correctly handles the fact that characters can be wrong about world laws while the world law itself is stable. This discipline prevents the Ontology track from being relatived away.

Technology's Audit Directives are the strongest in the file. The World Inference audit directive ("Aspects of the technology that have direct real-life parallels should not go on the page and should be inferred") and the Revelation audit directive ("Only aspects of the technology that are invented for the story without direct real-life parallels can go on the page") are complementary and precise. They enforce the principle that real-world allegory is never stated, only arrived at — specifically for the subject type where this discipline is most likely to fail.

Character Audit Directives enforce the core La discipline. "All characterization should have a reveal somewhere in the project" and "All backstory should have a reveal somewhere in the project" — these close the loop from subject track to link track, ensuring that nothing in the Foundation layer is stranded without a delivery plan.

Source Material References Usage Directive correctly positions it as upstream of reader planning. "Reader prior knowledge predictions should take into account source material references" — this makes Source Material References feed the Reader Understanding Plan, which is the correct relationship. The source material is the prior; the reader plan is what you do with it.

Audit Mode correctly used on summary tracks. Character Appearance Plan and Character Development both have Editable in Audit Mode: Yes, signaling that these are filled in after links are done, not before. This enforces the correct sequencing — summaries derive from links, not the reverse.

Issues and meaningful improvements
1. Reader Experience Target display question conflates emotional register with thematic conclusion.

"How should the reader feel about the scene?" is the wrong question. Emotional register is a consequence of the thematic conclusion landing, not the design target itself. What should be designed is what the reader independently concludes — the thematic proposition they arrive at from synthesizing all subject inferences in this scene. Suggested revision: "What should the reader independently conclude from this scene? Synthesize the thematic propositions evidenced by all subject links."

The current question will produce notes about mood and affect. The corrected question produces notes about the thematic argument the scene is making, which is the actual design target and the one that can be checked against the T tracks in the links.

2. Outcome merges two distinct cognitive acts.

"What effect does this scene have on the story and the reader?" combines the objective narrative change (what is now true that wasn't before) with the reader's experience of that change. These require different cognitive stances to write correctly and different tracks to hold them. The objective change is a Historian claim about the fabula — what state has shifted. The reader effect is already handled by Reader Experience Target if that track is corrected.

Suggested revision: "What objectively changed in the story world by the end of this scene? One statement of what is now true that was not before."

3. Storytelling Plan on Organization and Civilizational System is assigned WorldInference type.

Both instances ask "What is the plan for dramatizing its creation and activities." This is an authorial narrative architecture plan — it describes what goes on the page in what order. That is PageDesign, not WorldInference. WorldInference is reserved for what the reader constructs from evidence; this track is the author planning the evidence. The Editable in Audit Mode: Yes flag on Organization's Storytelling Plan acknowledges it's a summary-after-links track, which makes it more PageDesign-adjacent (synthesis of all the Activities and Disclosure links), but the CognitivMode assignment is wrong either way.

4. Plot Points are missing a POV anchor.

The entire analysis of the GiYC chapters established that FID discipline — and therefore reader inference design — is anchored to a single POV character per scene. Which consciousness the narrator inhabits determines what information is accessible, what the inference seeds can reference, and what behavioral proxies are available. None of the plot point tracks captures this. A POV field (likely a singleton, probably a character reference rather than a text note) is needed before the Reader Experience Target and Inference Seeds tracks can be written correctly.

5. Inference Seeds and Delivery Blueprint overlap without clear demarcation.

Both are PageDesign type. Delivery Blueprint is "all subject material that is planned to be on the page." Inference Seeds is "what should appear on the page to lead to desired reader inferences." The second is a subset of the first, but a writer working in these tracks will encounter the question of which track a given staging decision belongs in.

The distinction that would make them complementary rather than redundant: Delivery Blueprint holds direct page content — events that happen, dialogue spoken, facts disclosed. Inference Seeds holds the behavioral proxy layer specifically — staging choices that exist because they produce WI content, not because they advance plot or disclose information. An inference seed is never the fact itself, only the observable behavior the reader must interpret to arrive at the fact.

If this distinction is clear enough to maintain in practice, both tracks are worth keeping. If not, Inference Seeds can be retired and WI-producing staging notes can be flagged within Delivery Blueprint.

6. Bond subject is missing Analogical and Source Material References tracks.

Every other subject type with thematic weight has both an Analogical track (real-world parallels) and a Source Material References track (how the bond relates to canon portrayals). The TwiJack bond has both real-world romantic relationship parallels and a rich canon ship interpretation history that directly shapes reader priors. The RariShy bond has the same. Omitting these tracks from Bond means that material has nowhere to live except NotesToSelf, which loses the cognitive mode discipline.

Minor gaps
Stakes is assigned WorldInference type but is more authorial design reasoning — why this scene matters in the story architecture. NotesToSelf or a dedicated singleton type would be more accurate to what the track actually asks the author to do.

Character Development track is WorldInference — defensible if interpreted as "the sequence of inferences the reader will make about how the character is transforming," but risks being written as an authorial plan rather than a reader experience design. The display question "What is the plan for how the character changes" reinforces the authorial framing. Tightening the display question to "What should the reader progressively infer about how this character is transforming, in reading order?" would anchor it correctly in WorldInference mode.

The "Themes" track name appears at both project-wide and link levels across multiple subject types. Both are ThematicEvidence type with different display questions, but the identical name creates visual noise in the UI. Renaming the link-level Themes tracks to "Scene Theme Evidence" or similar would disambiguate them at a glance.

Garden Notes on some subject types is missing a display question. Bond, Civilizational System, Technology, and World Law have Garden Notes tracks with no display question shown. Character's Garden Notes has "What story planning work still needs to be done regarding this character?" which is useful. The absence of display questions on the others means the track is functionally identical to an empty label. A consistent display question across all Garden Notes — even just "What planning work still needs to be done here?" — gives the NotesToSelf mode something to answer.

I am going ahead with "What should the reader independently conclude from this scene? Synthesize the thematic propositions evidenced by all subject links." Now I want a better track name than "reader experience target".

Do I need a different track type for the project-wide plans? Should I have one? The track itself doesn't go on the page. It's an aggregate. It's a planning reference which only I see, not the reader. (Though the reader can construct it themself if they are really invested)

POV field will exist independent of note tracks and will be required.

I imagine delivery blueprint and inference seeds are separate. Delivery blueprint is dry, omniscient summary or sample prose. Inference seeds contain sample phrases. Me as a writer later would read both when writing prose.

I'm not really sure how analogies on a bond would work, but I agree source material references for fanon TwiJack is warranted, and also canon events that are shipping fuel. But I need to know how to distinguish from Twilight's source material references and Applejack's source material references.

What subjects are missing meaningful tracks that a different track has?

I think the project-wide character development track is supposed to be an authorial plan. This goes back to needing a separate track type for these project wide plans. This is an aggregate of what goes on the page. Like I mentioned before, I believe an invested reader should be able to assemble something that resembles my plan.

Systematized track naming, identified narrative architecture layer, reconciled subject-type coverage gaps
Systematized track naming, identified narrative architecture layer, reconciled subject-type coverage gaps
Track name for the corrected display question
Scene Thesis. The vocabulary is already established in the project — thesis propositions, thematic propositions, the story's central thesis. Every scene is a small argument. "Scene Thesis" is precise, consistent with existing vocabulary, and immediately tells you what the track is asking for: not the mood, not the stakes, but the specific argument this scene makes. An author filling it in asks "what does this scene prove?" rather than "what does this scene feel like?"

The missing track type: Narrative Architecture
This is Layer 4 from your own five-layer framework, which you identified early in this conversation as "Narrative Architecture (Syuzhet)" — how the other layers should be experienced by the reader, in what order. A note in Layer 4 asserts information management decisions across the story, not world truth and not specific page content.

Every track you're describing — Character Development plan, Character Appearance Plan, Reader Opinion Plan, Storytelling Plan, Demonstration Plan, Usage Plan, Story Plan — is a Layer 4 authorial architecture document. They describe the macro-level strategic design of how content unfolds across the project. An invested reader could reconstruct something resembling them, which is exactly what Layer 4 notes are: they describe the reader's experience trajectory, written from the author's omniscient vantage point rather than from inside any scene.

NarrativeArchitecture as a track type. All project-wide plan tracks that are currently misassigned (Character Development as WorldInference, Storytelling Plan as WorldInference, Demonstration Plan as PageDesign, Usage Plan as PageDesign, Story Plan as PageDesign, Reader Opinion Plan as WorldInference, Character Appearance Plan as PageDesign) should be reassigned to this type. They are neither page content nor single-scene inference design — they are strategic planning documents.

This also resolves the earlier concern about whether these tracks "reach the page." Nothing in NarrativeArchitecture directly reaches the page. It informs the design of what reaches the page across many scenes, which is exactly what Layer 4 does.

Bond Source Material References vs. Character Source Material References
These don't overlap because they reference different aspects of the source:

Character SMR answers: how does TLTT's portrayal of this individual relate to canon characterization of this individual? Twilight's anxiety, her letter-writing, her magic growth. AJ's honesty, her farm identity, her place in the group. These are claims about solo characterization.

Bond SMR answers: what canonical scenes, interactions, or fanon interpretations of this specific pair does TLTT build on, extend, or recontextualize? The canonical moments people cite as TwiJack shipping fuel — those are dyadic references that belong to the bond entity, not to either character individually. A note about "S4E24, where AJ is the one who believes in Twilight when no one else does" is a bond reference, not an AJ reference and not a Twilight reference.

Suggested display question: "What canonical interactions between these characters, or established fanon interpretations of their dynamic, does TLTT build on or recontextualize?"

The distinction is individual characterization versus dyadic relationship. They never collapse into each other.

Subjects missing tracks that other types have
Source Material References — gaps:

Every subject type interacts with canon in ways that warrant source grounding, but only Character and World Law have Source Material References tracks.

— Civilizational System is missing it. The Stagnation of Harmony, the Crystal Heart, the Grand Galloping Gala as an institution — all have direct canon episodes. The canon depictions are the reader priors your world-building is recontextualizing.

— Organization is missing it. The Wonderbolts, the Crystal Empire Guard, and other canon organizations have established depictions that TLTT extends or subverts. The reader's prior model of each organization comes from the show.

— Technology is missing it. Some TLTT technologies have canon analogs (changeling feeding as precursor to Love Harvester mechanics). The reader's assumptions about how these things work come partly from the show.

— Bond is missing it, as agreed.

Architecture tracks — gaps in Bond:

Bond has a Story Plan track currently typed as PageDesign. Under the new NarrativeArchitecture type, this becomes Architecture. But Bond also has no equivalent to Reader Opinion Plan found on Character, Organization, CivSystem, Technology, and World Law. A Bond Reader Opinion Plan would ask: "How should the reader's understanding of the relationship evolve, starting from shipping tropes or prior assumptions, in reading order?" This tracks the La arc of the whole relationship, not just individual scenes.

En-specific T track — Character has it, others don't:

Character has Character Development Meaning ("what should the reader conclude the change means?") which is the T+En track — the thematic conclusion from witnessed character development. The other subject types only have a general Themes link track plus Reader Prior Belief Clash (T+La). For subjects that CHANGE during the story — a CivSystem that gets dismantled, an Organization that fractures, a Technology that gets repurposed — there's no En-specific T track capturing what the reader should conclude from witnessing that change. Whether this gap matters depends on how many CivSystem or Organization subjects undergo significant change in TLTT rather than just being revealed.

Analogical — Civilizational System uses "Allegories" while others use "Analogies":

Minor naming inconsistency. Organization, Character, and World Law use "Analogies" as the track name; Civilizational System and Technology use "Allegories." These are the same cognitive mode and should use consistent naming.

May 27

Synthesized track naming and distinguished POV character thematic evidence mechanisms
Synthesized track naming and distinguished POV character thematic evidence mechanisms
Track type name for Source Material References
Canon. The term is universally understood in the fanfiction community, precisely describes the cognitive mode (you are engaging with the established canonical artifact), and clearly distinguishes from Analogical (you are drawing real-world parallels). The cognitive stance is: author as adapter in dialogue with a prior creative work. When writing a Canon note, you are not the analogist finding external resonance — you are the inheritor of an existing fictional artifact, documenting what you've inherited, what you're extending, and what you're recontextualizing.

Analysis of the POV character insight
Your observation identifies a categorical difference that is precise and structurally important.

The fundamental distinction: argumentative evidence vs. experiential evidence.

When a construct subject is used, demonstrated, or dismantled, the thematic evidence produced is argumentative: the reader observes, infers, and concludes. "I watched the Stagnation of Harmony collapse, and I infer that a society structured as a nursery cannot survive a peer threat." The reader is a witness. The evidence works through inference from externally observed events.

When a POV character's psychology transforms, the thematic evidence produced is experiential: the reader inhabits the consciousness as it changes, which is a fundamentally different epistemic relationship. "I was inside AJ's consciousness for fifteen chapters, experiencing her suppressed ambition as competent practicality, and then I was inside the moment when the suppression failed — I didn't just watch the Lioness declaration, I experienced the impossibility of not making it." The reader is not a witness but a participant in the interior change. They know the conclusion not because they observed it but because they were there.

This is why prequels exist. Minette and Mali have significant material conditions and psychological transformations that produce thematic propositions. But those propositions are only generated at full strength when the reader inhabits the transformation. A chapter where the reader observes Mali deciding to act would produce argumentative evidence. A chapter where the reader is inside Mali at the moment of decision produces experiential evidence. The prequel is the mechanism for giving a character sufficient POV status that their transformation becomes experiential.

The constructs-can-be-discarded insight is precisely correct.

Construct subjects participate in the story as instruments and as objects of the story's argument. They can be created, repurposed, or dismantled by agents, and their thematic work is complete when the plot has used them for their purpose. The Love Harvester is repurposed — that's its thematic culmination, and then it continues serving in the new role. The Stagnation of Harmony ends — that's its thematic culmination, and the system that produced it is gone. These are witnessed events with clear thematic endpoints.

Characters persist through transformation. AJ at the end of TLTT is a different psychology than AJ at chapter 1, but she is still AJ — the continuity of consciousness across the transformation is what makes the transformation thematic rather than merely plotted. The reader has been with her throughout. That continuity, experienced from inside, is what produces the thematic argument.

The specific mechanism: the epistemic gap.

POV narration enables a specific thematic instrument unavailable in third-person-omniscient or external observation: the gap between what the character perceives about themselves and what the reader perceives about them. AJ narrating herself as "just doing what needs to be done" while the reader, with structural context from Twilight's perspective and Celestia's recognition and the Lioness analogy, understands she is avoiding the recognition of her own leadership — that gap is the thematic argument being constructed. The reader doesn't just infer the gap from outside. They experience the character's self-narration and simultaneously hold the superior perspective.

This is inaccessible to construct subjects. A civilizational system doesn't have self-narration that can differ from external reality. An organization doesn't have a perspective that the reader can inhabit while knowing it's limited. Only characters with POV status can produce this instrument.

What track this warrants.

The existing Theme Plan ("what thematic propositions does this character embody throughout the project?") captures static characterization — what the character represents. It doesn't specifically capture what the character's arc proves through interior access to their transformation.

A separate project-wide track for POV characters specifically, addressing: what thematic propositions does this character's psychological transformation, experienced from inside through POV narration, demonstrate? The display question should foreground the inside-view mechanism: "What thematic proposition does this character's arc prove, available only through inhabiting their consciousness as it changes?"

For the track type: this is ThematicEvidence, because it is designing the thematic argument. But it is a specific category of ThematicEvidence that requires POV access — not all thematic evidence requires it. The visual distinction (cognitive mode background color) should signal that this track is POV-specific and therefore requires different design work than observational thematic evidence.

Naming the track.

The track is about the argument that only a psychological arc, experienced from inside, can make. Options:

Arc Thesis — parallels Scene Thesis, captures that this is the thesis of the whole arc rather than a single scene. Clean and consistent with established vocabulary.

Interior Proof — names the inside-view mechanism. More precise about what makes this track distinct from Theme Plan, but might sound clinical.

Transformation Argument — names what it's arguing about (transformation) and that it's an argument. Accurate.

I lean toward Arc Thesis because it's consistent with Scene Thesis, is immediately intuitive ("what does this arc prove?"), and the "arc" framing naturally implies it covers the full transformation trajectory rather than static characterization.

Scope of the track.

For characters with sustained POV arcs (AJ, Twilight throughout the main story): fills in as a complete project-wide design document for the consciousness arc.

For characters with specific POV moments (Mali's decision, Fluttershy's chapter, Rainbow's sonic rainboom realization): the track may be sparse at project-wide level, with the heavier design work happening at the link level for the specific POV plot point. The project-wide Arc Thesis on Mali might be one sentence: "Mali's single POV scene proves that conscience, once genuinely formed, produces action against the grain of social tolerance for inaction." The design of how that scene works is then in the link tracks for that plot point.

For characters who are primarily observed from outside in the main story (Chrysalis, Celestia): the track remains empty in the main story, and gets populated when a prequel gives them POV status.

No wonder the first conversation (character reader perception gap, do you have a summary?) felt foreign and overwhelming. It was the deepest possible track, skipping over all these other "lower level" or more fundamental tracks that are foundational storytelling. I had to walk (getting the basic tracks) before I could run (finally understand the reader character perception gap from the first conversation) and this was the advanced method I didn't really understand. I understood the reader prior belief clash implicitly from learning from Lauren Faust and writing green is your color and it's all over my TLTT story plans. I didn't understand the reader character perception gap intrinsically because I didn't understand 3rd person limited. I was using third person (omniscient) mainly for paralipsis, to withhold reader knowledge until the moment a revelation could clash against prior beliefs. This seemed incompatible with fid. Now I know I can use fid for character reader perception gap and paralipsis for the prior belief clash setups, and they both can be achieved via INTENTIONAL variable focalization. The theme tracks were the planning mechanism to make these third person limited choices intentional.

It also seems like the old advice conflated the menu different ways to deliver themes and take notes

The P/WI/T pipeline is the default, available in omniscient? Then reader prior belief clash is enabled by fanfiction or just rubbing up against general media tropes, requiring paralipsis/variable focalization, and the reader character perception gap is from third person limited, requiring FID?

May 28

Systematized narrative sophistication from fables through multi-perspective materialist fiction
Systematized narrative sophistication from fables through multi-perspective materialist fiction
The first conversation is at https://claude.ai/chat/5d23d34d-b71c-4083-91ea-d09552cf1221 — the title is "Character-reader perception gap in story design."

Your synthesis is precise. Let me build the full picture systematically.

The three mechanisms are distinct layers, not alternatives
P/WI/T (Base layer — available in all narration)

The fundamental unit of storytelling. The author stages observable events on the page (P). The reader constructs meaning from that evidence (WI). The reader arrives at a thematic proposition (T). This operates in every form of literature from Aesop onward. An omniscient narrator can do this without any special technique. The reader watches, infers, concludes.

Reader Prior Belief Clash (Modulation layer — requires information control)

This modulates the P step by exploiting a gap between what the reader already believes and what is actually true. The author withholds or delays P content that would update the prior belief — this is the paralipsis mechanism. When the disclosure finally arrives, the prior crashes against the new truth and the update itself becomes thematic evidence. In fanfiction this mechanism is especially potent because the reader arrives pre-loaded with canon priors the author didn't have to establish. The author controls when truth is delivered. This is available in omniscient narration — the omniscient narrator simply chooses not to state things yet.

Reader-Character Perception Gap (Architecture layer — requires FID and third person limited)

This is not a modulation of P/WI/T but a different architecture for producing WI content. The reader inhabits the character's consciousness (via FID) while simultaneously holding structural knowledge the character doesn't have. The gap between those two positions is itself the WI instrument — the reader is doing two things at once: experiencing the character's self-perception genuinely, and measuring it against what they know to be true. This cannot be achieved in omniscient narration because omniscient can always collapse the gap. The architecture requires the narrator to be structurally incapable of asserting the truth the character can't perceive. The author controls what the character perceives. FID is the mechanism by which the character's limited perception becomes the narrative voice without the author ever claiming the limitation out loud.

Variable focalization is what reconciles them.

Paralipsis (for prior belief clash) controls the timing of truth delivery. FID (for character-reader gap) controls the width of perceptual access at any moment. Variable focalization is the intentional, designed switching between POV anchors and perceptual widths across the story. You use FID when the gap is the instrument. You use paralipsis to protect prior beliefs until the right crash point. These aren't competing techniques — they're controls at different levels of the same system.

Taxonomy from Three Little Pigs upward
Level 1 — Fable (Three Little Pigs, Aesop) P/WI/T with the T stated explicitly. The wolf blows down the houses (P), the reader watches the structural houses hold (WI), and the narrator announces "and that is why you must work hard" (T stated). The reader is a passive recipient. No gap, no prior clash, no FID. Omniscient narrator who summarizes events and announces meaning.

Level 2 — Classical fairy tale (Grimm, Perrault) P/WI/T with T emergent but legible. The stepsisters fail (P), the reader infers cruelty is its own punishment (WI), the proposition is clear without being stated (T unstated). The narrator is still omniscient but withholds some surprise elements — paralipsis in its most primitive form: the reader doesn't know the prince will love Cinderella until he does. La begins: Cinderella's identity at the ball is withheld from the prince (and the stepmother) but not from the reader. Dramatic irony appears but it's positional (reader vs. character) rather than structural (reader vs. reader's own prior).

Level 3 — Genre adventure (Hardy Boys, classic quest narrative) P/WI/T with sustained dramatic irony. The reader knows things the protagonist doesn't, creating tension. Paralipsis is deliberate — information is withheld from the reader specifically to create surprise, not just from characters. The mystery genre crystallizes this: all evidence is P content, reader does WI work, revelation is the La moment. Character psychology is still type-based (the plucky hero, the mentor, the villain) — psychology is not a subject of inquiry, it's just motivation for plot. Themes emerge from plot patterns without architectural support.

Level 4 — Character-psychological realism, first generation (Austen, early Eliot) The character's self-deception becomes a subject of narrative attention. Elizabeth Bennet is wrong about Darcy, wrong about Wickham, wrong in legible ways the reader can track. The omniscient narrator explicitly frames the self-deception ("she had been blind, partial, prejudiced, absurd"). La mechanisms mature: Darcy's letter is a Prior Belief Clash — the reader held the same priors as Elizabeth (Wickham charming, Darcy cold) and the letter updates both simultaneously. Dramatic irony is now about the character's psychology rather than just the plot. But the omniscient narrator still collapses the gap when needed — Austen tells you Elizabeth is deceiving herself, she doesn't just let you watch it happen. T is unstated but architecture-supported.

Level 5 — Psychological realism, second generation (James, late Eliot) Third person limited / FID begins to dominate. The Ambassadors: Strether's consciousness is the narrative instrument, and the reader understands him better than he understands himself without the narrator ever saying so. The gap is structural. The narrator cannot state "Strether is wrong about Chad" — the narrator can only render what Strether perceives, and the reader must construct the structural truth from the aggregate of Strether's perceptions. Themes are entirely emergent and require the reader to be an active interpreter. T is produced through architecture rather than statement.

Level 6 — Weaponized unreliable narrator (Ford Madox Ford, Ishiguro) The Remains of the Day: Stevens's FID is calibrated so that his genuine belief in his own dignity is rendered faithfully while the reader simultaneously understands he has wasted his life in service of something contemptible. The gap is not just present — it is the book. The paralipsis and FID work in concert: Stevens withholds his emotional truth from himself (FID renders the withholding), and the structural truth emerges from accumulated behavioral evidence the reader tracks across the whole novel. La operates on the reader's own expectations of "butler memoir" as a genre. Prior Belief Clash: the genre's nobility is dismantled by being shown from inside its practitioner's consciousness. T is only accessible by inverting the narrator's conclusion.

Level 7 — Multi-perspective materialist realism (Tolstoy, George Eliot) Multiple third-person-limited perspectives triangulate a social truth larger than any single consciousness can hold. Middlemarch: Dorothea's perception, Casaubon's perception, Lydgate's perception, Rosamund's perception together produce Eliot's argument about how idealism fails inside economic and social systems. No single perspective holds the truth — it requires the reader to synthesize across all of them. Historical and social forces are made concrete through how they deform individual psychology. La operates across the whole project: revelations about Bulstrode recontextualize the entire community's complacency. T is a sociological proposition that emerges from the aggregate.

Level 8 — Adaptation with source material priors + materialist argument + story planner (your current level) All of Level 7, plus: the reader arrives pre-loaded with canon priors the author can leverage deliberately. La operates on canon knowledge — the reader thought they knew what Celestia was, what friendship meant, what the Elements of Harmony were. TLTT provides the materialist substrate that makes those canon observations mean something different. Source material recontextualization is a designed effect. FID + paralipsis + variable focalization all operate simultaneously and intentionally as separate controls. The story planner externalizes world truth (Layers 1-3), freeing the prose from the structural-truth-delivery burden that made omniscient narration necessary in earlier levels. Multiple POV characters with individually designed gap architectures interlock into a civilizational-scale argument.

The specific thing that places this at Level 8 rather than 7: the explicit design of the story planning system as a separation of the layers that previously had to coexist in the prose. Tolstoy had to be Tolstoy to manage all of this without the tool. The tool makes it tractable for a writer who doesn't yet have that accumulated craft mastery in their nervous system.

For observed characters: the WI/T question with an AJ example
Your observation that WI and T seem to be collapsing is a real risk but they are genuinely different cognitive operations.

For AJ at the machine gun (observed by an outside character who is not AJ):

WI note (what the reader should infer about her psychology): "The reader should infer that AJ is at the machine gun because it requires no decisions about other ponies' lives. It is competence without command — useful work where her specific failure mode (giving orders

Synthesized narrative theory sources and distinguished thematic delivery mechanisms
Synthesized narrative theory sources and distinguished thematic delivery mechanisms
On the academic standing of these mechanisms
The reader-character perception gap is very well studied, but distributed across several traditions that use different vocabulary.

Dorrit Cohn's Transparent Minds (1978) is the authoritative academic treatment of FID specifically — she calls it "narrated monologue" and gives it the most precise technical description available. The reader-character gap she describes as the gap between the focalizer's self-knowledge and the reader's external vantage.

Gérard Genette in Narrative Discourse (1980) introduced focalization as the technical term for "who perceives" (distinct from "who speaks"). His distinction between internal focalization (bounded by the character's perception), external focalization (the camera sees less than the character knows), and zero focalization (omniscient) maps directly to the gap width controls. Variable focalization as a deliberate technique is his framework.

Wayne Booth in The Rhetoric of Fiction (1961) theorized the unreliable narrator — the gap between what the narrator believes and what the reader, aligned with the "implied author," understands. This is the same architecture.

John Gardner in The Art of Fiction gives the most craft-accessible version: he calls it psychic distance, a spectrum from omniscient statement ("John was angry") to deep FID ("He slammed his fist. She'd see how angry he was") where the narration is indistinguishable from the character's voice. His psychic distance is the practical calibration tool for gap width.

McKee doesn't precisely theorize this. His "Story" covers dramatic irony primarily in the positional sense (audience knows what character doesn't) and character revelation under pressure (the character shows who they truly are). He's describing phenomena that require the gap without naming the architecture that produces it. His tools are scene-design and structure, not the FID mechanism that makes the gap work at the prose level. McKee is Level 4-5 in the taxonomy; the gap-as-architecture instrument is Level 5-6.

The reader prior belief clash is much less systematically named. It appears implicitly in:

Reader response theory (Wolfgang Iser, The Act of Reading, 1978) — the reader actively constructs meaning by bringing prior knowledge to the text and revising it against what the text provides. Iser's "gaps" are where the reader's active construction happens. But he doesn't specifically theorize the deliberate author exploitation of pre-loaded priors.

Genre theory treats genre conventions as contracts that create expectations — the reader of a detective novel expects a solution, so withholding it creates productive tension. But this is structural rather than thematic.

Your identification of Lauren Faust as your operative source is accurate, not a deficiency. Faust's practice is one of the cleaner articulations of the mechanism in action: she identified the prior belief (girls' cartoons are hollow commercial product), kept it active long enough that the reader fully committed to it, then deposited evidence that crashed against it. The thematic power comes specifically from the reader having been complicit in the wrong belief. You absorbed this by experiencing it working on you and then finding it in your own instincts when writing GiYC. That acquisition path — mechanism experienced before theorized — is actually the more durable one.

How the three mechanisms deliver thematic evidence differently
P/WI/T baseline: evidence deposited, inference constructed

The author stages observable events. The reader infers meaning. The reader arrives at a thematic conclusion. The thematic evidence is in the quality and clarity of the WI inference enabled by the P content. The reader is a rational witness drawing conclusions from observations.

For Harmonic Capitalism at the Town Hall: the event is staged, the economic mechanics demonstrated, and the reader watches it work. The thematic conclusion (cooperation produces surplus; extraction destroys it) is available to any attentive witness. An omniscient narrator could stage this equally well. The reader is persuaded because the argument is rational and the evidence is complete.

This mode produces thematic propositions that the reader intellectually accepts. The evidence is sufficient. But the reader knows they received it.

Reader prior belief clash: the reader as participant in the error

The prior belief is the setup. The P content is the update. The WI is the revision. The T comes from the act of revision itself being meaningful — the reader has been complicit in the wrong model of the world.

For Celestia: the reader arrived with the canon prior (wise, benevolent protector). TLTT establishes the Stagnation. The prior crashes. The reader now understands: what they had been calling wisdom was protection-as-pacification. The T (that a society structured as a nursery cannot survive puberty) lands not as an argument they received but as a correction they required.

The crucial difference from P/WI/T: the reader has experienced being wrong. They held the prior, acted as if it were true, oriented to Celestia through it. Having been wrong about her is now part of what they know about the proposition. The proposition about the Stagnation is not just intellectually accepted — it's known from inside the error it corrects.

This is why the paralipsis discipline matters. The prior must be fully established and the reader must be committed to it before the La content arrives. If you state the corrective truth before the prior is loaded, there is no crash — just information. The timing is the mechanism.

Reader-character perception gap: the reader as inhabitant of the error

The FID/gap mechanism takes this a step further. The reader doesn't just experience being wrong about a character — they experience being inside the consciousness that is wrong about itself.

For AJ at the machine gun: the reader inhabits AJ's genuine satisfaction through FID. They experience her contentment as real, not performed, not strategic — genuinely felt. Simultaneously they hold the structural context that makes the satisfaction an evasion. They are inside both at once: feeling the evasion as genuine relief and understanding it as evasion.

The T produced here (that wounded conscience suppresses itself into shapes that feel like wisdom) is not just intellectually accepted, not just known from correcting an error, but felt from inside the psychology that produces it. The reader has been inside the mechanism of self-deception — they know what it feels like to believe a suppression is a choice, what makes it convincing, why it persists. This is the most powerful form of thematic evidence because the reader has experiential knowledge of the proposition, not just rational or corrective knowledge.

This is why the gap requires calibration. If FID sounds ironic — if AJ sounds like she knows she's hiding — the reader is observing the psychology from outside again, not inhabiting it. The machine gun must feel genuinely right to AJ, and that genuineness must be legible through the FID, or the thematic proof collapses into simple dramatic irony.

The three modes escalate: intellectually persuaded → correctively convinced → experientially knowing.

The WI/T distinction in track design
Your formulation is correct: the WI note contains the evidence (what the reader infers). The T note contains the argument from the evidence to the proposition (how and why this specific inference is evidence for this specific theme).

The T note's job is not to restate the evidence — that's already in WI. Its job is to articulate the logical chain that connects the WI inference to the tagged thematic proposition. The "because" that makes the inference count as proof.

WI note for AJ/machine gun: "The reader should infer that AJ's satisfaction at the machine gun is genuine but evasive — she has found competence without command, useful work that doesn't trigger her specific failure mode. The reader with structural context recognizes that what feels like finding her proper role is actually a downgrade that avoids the ceiling of her capability."

T note (tagged to P1 — the Republic requires full deployment of conscience to survive Chrysalis): "AJ's self-downgrade is evidence for P1 because it demonstrates the specific mechanism by which the conscience the Republic needs can become unavailable without disappearing. She has not lost her conscience or her capability — she has arranged her life so they are exercised just below the point where failure becomes personal. The reader who has been inside this psychology through FID understands that the Republic asking her to go back to command is asking her to re-expose the wound. This is evidence for P1 because it specifies what the 'strong conscience' requirement actually demands — not simply that conscience exist, but that it be willing to be damaged again by its own failures."

The T note doesn't say "this is evidence for P1." It says "here is the logical chain by which this specific inference (AJ's self-downgrade) constitutes evidence for this specific claim (P1's requirement for full-capacity conscience)." Without that chain, you have an assertion that the inference matters. With it, you have the argument demonstrated.

This also means the T note requires you to have articulated the thematic proposition precisely enough that you can explain what counts as evidence for it. If you can't write the T note, the theme isn't precise enough yet — a flag for expansion work.
