May 28

Synthesized creative revision as distinct category from satire through authorial sincerity
Synthesized creative revision as distinct category from satire through authorial sincerity
Your memory is accurate with one small correction: her username is Fyre_flye (y in both words). Firefly was Lauren Faust's favorite G1 character and the direct basis for Rainbow Dash — the name couldn't be used due to trademark issues. The gallery confirms she kept actual G1 toys including Firefly, Applejack, Posey, Surprise, Twilight, and Glory — Rainbow Dash was specifically designed as "Firefly's best bud" in Faust's original materials. 
X + 2

The category you're identifying
This is not simply fanfiction and not satire. The precise mechanism is what I'd call sincere revision: the author believes the prior tradition was reaching toward something real and valuable that it couldn't articulate properly, and the new work is the completion of that reaching, not an exposure of it as hollow.

The operative distinction from satire is the author's relationship to the prior:

Satire: The prior is the enemy. The satirist maintains ironic distance, wants to expose the prior as absurd or wrong, and the reader's conclusion is "I was foolish to believe in this genre." Monty Python doesn't love the Arthurian legend. They love comedy, and Arthurian is ammunition.

Sincere revision: The prior is an incomplete version of the truth. The reviser believes the tradition was pointing at something real but couldn't reach it — due to commercial constraint, craft limitation, or the conventions of its era. The reader's conclusion is not "this genre was bad" but "this genre was always trying to say THIS, and it finally can."

Faust is the cleanest case. She loved the G1 toys specifically because the toys seemed to promise more than the show delivered — she made up her own adventures to complete what the toys implied. G4 is not a rejection of G1. It's the fulfillment of what G1 was gesturing toward. The childhood play-sessions filling the gap between what the show was and what it could be were the original composition. FiM was the publication.

The spectrum of your examples
Mulan is a sincere revision. The Hua Mulan ballad is genuine — it's patriotic and moving and the legend is genuinely beloved. Disney adds psychological depth (the matchmaker opening establishes and then dismantles the prior about what kind of story this will be) and then delivers the ballad's actual proposition: heroism has no gender. The prior wasn't wrong to revere the legend; it just couldn't fully articulate why.

Frozen is the most mechanically explicit La mechanism of all of them. The entire first act is a setup of the romantic-prince prior. The audience who has watched decades of Disney princess films has a fully loaded prior about Hans. The film lets that prior load completely — Hans is charming, the romance is convincing, "Love Is An Open Door" is genuinely good — before crashing it against Anna's actual arc. The T is not "romantic love is stupid" but "the love story you were watching was actually between the sisters, and the romantic template blinded you to it." The revision reveals what the fairy tale tradition was actually about.

The Princess Bride and Shrek are the hybrid cases. Goldman loves what he's parodying — the irony and the genuine stakes coexist. The comedy comes from the deflation of swashbuckling conventions; the emotional investment in Westley and Buttercup is completely sincere. Goldman is simultaneously the satirist (the "good parts" framing, the chapter-length pauses to mock adventure novel conventions) and the true believer (the actual love story works). Shrek does the same: fairy tale conventions are targets of parody AND the vehicle for genuine emotional stakes about being seen as you are. In both cases, the author's love for the genre is what makes the parody land — they're laughing with the conventions from a position of deep familiarity, not attacking them from outside.

Monty Python is primarily satiric. The Arthurian legend isn't being completed — it's being used as absurdist material. Python doesn't have a childhood relationship with Arthurian the way Faust had with G1. The conclusion Holy Grail is building toward is not "here is what Arthurian was reaching for." It's "institutions are absurd and authority is arbitrary," and Arthurian happened to be the available vehicle.

What separates sincere revision from satire precisely
The question is: does the author believe the prior tradition was reaching toward something real?

If yes → the revision completes what the prior was reaching for. The T is generous: "your belief was correct but imprecisely aimed." The reader is not shamed for having held the prior.

If no → the satirist exposes the prior as hollow or absurd. The T is adversarial: "you were wrong to believe in this." The reader is invited to feel superior to their former credulity.

The hybrid (Princess Bride, Shrek) holds both simultaneously: yes, this convention is absurd, AND yes, it was reaching toward something real that we're going to deliver anyway.

The TLTT-specific sophistication
TLTT does something none of the examples in your list do: it incorporates the production history of the source material into the thematic architecture.

Faust's FiM was commissioned by Hasbro, achieved extraordinary creative ambition within commercial constraints, and then had that creative control withdrawn when the vision exceeded the institution's risk tolerance. This is the exact structural dynamic TLTT encodes: Celestia's Stagnation is what happens when an institution tries to maintain control over a society that has outgrown the nursery. The White Peace attempt is Hasbro trying to put Faust's G4 back in the G3 bottle. AJ's "I am the Lioness" is the story refusing to go back to being a toy commercial.

The reader who knows this production history arrives with a meta-prior: MLP as an IP is a creative vision that was partially captured and partially released by institutional control. TLTT's thematic argument about harmonic republicanism, the predator's dilemma, and the costs of the Stagnation lands against that prior simultaneously with the in-universe prior about Equestria.

This is what makes TLTT structurally more ambitious than even Frozen or FiM: those works use genre convention as the La substrate. TLTT uses the production history of its source material as additional La substrate, so the story is simultaneously arguing about Equestrian politics AND about what happens to creative visions under institutional control AND about whether harmony can survive in the real world. Three La mechanisms operating on three different reader priors at once.

Faust completed what G1 was reaching for. TLTT completes what both G1 and G4 were reaching for, and incorporates the reason G4 couldn't complete it as evidence for its central thesis.

May 28

Synthesized narrative mechanics across fanfiction, mass media, and creative biography
Synthesized narrative mechanics across fanfiction, mass media, and creative biography
On separating WI tracks for La vs standard
The tracks are already separated (WI+La vs WI+En). What needs to be different is the display question, because these are genuinely different cognitive operations.

WI+En display question: "What should the reader construct about this subject from witnessing this event?" — one component, forward-looking inference.

WI+La display question: "What prior belief does the reader hold entering this moment? What does the La disclosure correct that prior to? What revised understanding should the reader arrive at after the clash?" — three components: prior, disclosure, revised model.

The La note's three-part structure is load-bearing. Without specifying the prior, you don't know what you need the reader to have committed to before the La content arrives. Without specifying the revised model, you don't know what the disclosure needs to accomplish. The standard WI doesn't need this because it's not crashing anything — it's building forward. The La WI is fundamentally a before/after structure. Same track type, different display question, different cognitive operation.

On Sincere Revision and mass media
The mechanism is ancient. Virgil's Aeneid is a sincere revision of Homer — completing the Trojan War's argument for a Roman audience that needed to see itself in the foundation myth. Dante sincerely revises Virgil. Shakespeare revises Holinshed and Plutarch and Boccaccio repeatedly, not parodying his sources but completing what they were reaching for. Milton revises Genesis. The prior belief crash and sincere completion has been operating for as long as there have been shared narrative traditions.

What mass media changed is not the mechanism but the precision and shared load of the prior.

A folk tale prior varies. Every village tells Cinderella differently. The audience's model of "how this goes" is distributed and fuzzy. Disney's 1950 Sleeping Beauty is not fuzzy — it's synchronized. Two billion people have seen exactly the same frames in exactly the same order. The prior belief is not an approximate genre expectation; it's a specific memory. When Frozen dismantles the prince-saves-princess structure, it's crashing a more precise, more uniformly loaded prior than any pre-industrial folk tradition could produce. The La mechanism becomes more powerful when the prior is more exactly shared.

The second thing mass media changed is commercial degradation as the specific prior to be reversed. This is genuinely new. Before IP ownership and studio mandates, a narrative tradition could be shallow or rich depending on who told it, but it wasn't degraded by institutional mandate in a way that left a legible scar. The Hasbro toy commercial mandate is visible in the text of G3. The degradation is documentable. The reader's prior isn't just "girls' cartoons are shallow" — it's "THIS specific property was institutionally prevented from being what it was trying to be." Faust's G4 doesn't just fulfill what the genre was reaching for. It fulfills what the specific compromised IP was reaching for, with the compromise itself as the La substrate. That's only possible when a single institution owns and has visibly degraded a prior.

The third change is fan culture as infrastructure for the meta-prior. Before the internet, you couldn't develop the collective awareness "this could have been more." You might personally feel it. The fan community that formed around G1 toys, making up adventures for them because the show was insufficient, is now a visible, documented, constitutive part of the prior. Faust didn't just revise MLP for herself — she revived a community's decades-long intuition that there was more there. That community's accumulated meta-prior made her revision land with a specific weight that sincere revision of folk tales couldn't achieve.

So: the mechanism is ancient, the substrate is modern. Virgil didn't have IP lawyers or a synchronized billion-viewer prior. He had a shared mythological tradition with local variation. The precision, the commercial degradation, and the fan infrastructure are specifically products of the 20th century media industry.

On Silver and your creative biography
Reading the chapter, the La mechanism is working through pure behavioral setup across 13 chapters. Silver has been the one to fight, to forage, to make decisions. Eve has been protected. The reader's model of their roles is deeply loaded through accumulated behavior, not through statement.

Eve's Giga Impact reveal is a single moment that crashes the entire accumulated prior. The author doesn't editorialize. She doesn't announce "by the way, this challenges gendered protective norms." She just has Eve dispatch the Houndour efficiently and then Silver process his inadequacy through his father's voice.

Notice: the author is using omniscient narration throughout, not FID. "His father had always bred into him, both by lessons and example, that the male should be able to protect the female and keep her safe" is reported thought, not free indirect discourse. "He had been unable to do either. Did that mean he shouldn't try to be Eve's mate?" is rendered as internal reflection but without the voice becoming genuinely Silver's — the narrator is summarizing his psychology.

This is a clean demonstration of the taxonomy we developed. The La mechanism does not require FID. The prior belief crash works entirely through behavioral setup and behavioral revelation. The reason it hit you with such force as a teenage boy is that the prior was loaded through 13 chapters of accumulated behavioral evidence, and the crash was delivered through action, not through a narrator telling you what to think. You were an attentive witness doing rational inference from staged behavior. The prior loaded. The prior crashed. The T landed in your actual life.

What you learned from Silver was La — not the reader-character perception gap. The gap isn't in this chapter. Silver's self-doubt is reported omnisciently; you're watching it from outside, not inhabiting it. The thing that worked on you was the 13-chapter prior and the single moment of behavioral disclosure. And that is the mechanism you have been deploying ever since, with increasing sophistication.

The line of inheritance is precise: Silver (La via 13-chapter behavioral setup) → your 9 years of Pokemon fanfic → GiYC (La via source material episode structure) → your encounter with Faust (La via commercial-degradation prior + sincere revision) → TLTT (La via canon recontextualization + within-story behavioral setup + Greek tragedy prequel structure).

On why fanfiction specifically: Fanfiction gives you La substrate you didn't have to build. In original fiction, you must establish the prior within your own work. The Silver author spent 13 chapters building the prior from scratch. In fanfiction set within Pokemon, the reader already comes with 13 years of "Eevee doesn't normally learn that move" and "boys are the battlers in adventure fiction" loaded before page one. You start with compounded prior capital. That's the specific draw — the La mechanism is turbocharged by pre-existing audience commitment that the author didn't have to earn.

The freedom from commercial constraint is the second piece. Silver can have Eve defeat the Houndour. No publisher is going to reject this because it threatens toy sales. The EeveeInHeat of 2014 had complete freedom to crash the prior precisely because no institution owned the outcome. Faust didn't have that freedom once Hasbro got involved. The fanfiction author always has it.

On the Chrysalis prequel as maximum deployment of the reader-character gap
Your observation is structurally precise. The Greek tragedy reader-character gap is the inversion of the standard gap:

Standard FID gap: reader doesn't know what character knows (world truth is withheld from reader, character has it)
Greek tragedy gap: reader knows what character doesn't know (the outcome is withheld from the character, reader has it)

In the Chrysalis prequel, the reader arrives from TLTT knowing her machine is flawed, her empire will fail, and the specific moments where her justifications collapsed under pressure. Chrysalis inside the prequel is a competent rational CEO who believes she is building something durable. She has grievances against the old nobility that are genuinely legitimate. Her analysis is sound by her own premises. Her narcissism is functional — it produces results.

The reader inhabiting that consciousness, experiencing her competence as genuine and her analysis as rational, while simultaneously holding the structural knowledge of her collapse, is the maximum deployment of the gap because:

The prior (from TLTT) is not a genre convention but a personally witnessed outcome. The reader was there when it failed.
The FID renders her confidence genuinely — not ironically, not as obvious hubris. Her justifications must feel valid from inside her psychology.
The tragic structure means there is no gap-collapse moment where she realizes her error within the prequel. She goes to her defeat still believing her premises. The reader carries the knowledge she never acquires. The gap doesn't close — it becomes permanent at the moment of failure.
This produces a different T than the standard gap. The standard FID gap produces "I understand how this kind of self-deception feels from inside." The Greek tragedy gap produces something closer to: "I have experienced a competent, rational, genuinely wronged person build the architecture of their own destruction with complete sincerity, and I understand that the flaw was not in her rationality but in the premise her rationality was optimizing for."

The TLTT thesis about conscience and capacity without harmony — Chrysalis is its demonstration. Not its villain but its proof.

The WI+En and WI+La tracks are separated for characters, but not for the other subjects. I'm wondering if they should be separated. And also, characters have been revealed to have three tracks: witness, prior belief clash, and narrator perception gap. Do these all need to be in separate tracks on Character Plot Point Link? Or, does narrator perception gap go only on plot point? Maybe it does have a home on character link too? Character link enables auditing with subject. Maybe it's intertwined with character arc?

I'm still confused about what should actually go into WI+La versus T+La for prior belief clash. Give an example for the following: the characters in TLTT believe the Stagnation of Harmony was a system Celestia put in place for 1000 years to keep the ponies in a nursery and maintain pacified harmony. Then at the white peace chapter, Celestia tells Applejack it was only 80 years ago that the current policies were put in place as a response to ponies adopting griffon industry (like the Gilded Age) and the ambition turned inward to harm others, which is why ponies at the time petitioned Celestia to ban griffon-style industry. Before 80 years ago, ambitious ponies would be directed to the edge of the Everfree Forest to tame it and settle more land (this is the canonical recount that Granny Smith gives of the founding of Ponyville in the canon episode Family Appreciation Day).

May 28

Systematized track separation logic and distinguished WI+La from T+La mechanisms
Systematized track separation logic and distinguished WI+La from T+La mechanisms
WI+En vs WI+La separation for all subject types
Yes, the same logic applies to every subject. If the Stagnation of Harmony's WI track mixes "reader infers from watching this system operate" with "reader's prior about what this system is gets crashed and revised," you have the same cognitive mixing problem v1 had — just disguised as a single track instead of a tangled paragraph. The three-part structure (prior, disclosure, revised model) is incompatible with the one-part structure (inference from observation) as a display question. The separation should be universal.

Where the three mechanisms live on Character links
WI+En (standard observational inference): On the character link. The reader watches AJ do X and infers Y about her psychology. Works for both POV and non-POV scenes from the reader's external observer position.

WI+La (prior belief clash): On the character link. The prior attached to this character gets crashed by a disclosure about them in this scene. Three-part structure: prior → disclosure → revised model. This lives on the character's link because the prior is about the character as a subject.

Narrator/reader perception gap: On the character link AND intertwined with arc design. The gap is scene-specific (this is where AJ's self-deception about the machine gun manifests) but arc-progressive (the gap width changes as her arc develops). The link-level note specifies: "the FID voice should render X as genuine; the reader's structural context from Y allows them to simultaneously hold Z." The Arc Thesis / NarrativeArchitecture track on the character subject holds the project-wide trajectory of the gap — how it widens, where it starts narrowing, when it closes. Individual link notes calibrate the gap at specific scenes.

The perception gap on the character link is not a separate track from WI+En. It's a FID-calibrated version of WI+En — the display question needs to ask about both what the character perceives (FID voice to render) AND what the reader simultaneously holds from structural context. The POV field on the plot point flags that this is a FID scene, which triggers the gap-calibration framing in the WI+En note.

Summary: Three separate tracks for La, En, and the gap is overkill. The right structure is WI+La (separate, three-part) and WI+En (covers both standard observation and FID gap observation, with the gap calibration being the note's additional content when POV applies). The Arc Thesis holds the project-wide gap trajectory.

WI+La vs T+La: the Stagnation/White Peace example
The prior loaded by this point in the story: both AJ and the reader hold the model that Celestia deliberately imposed the Stagnation for 1000 years as her own project of paternalistic control. This is what the characters believe and what the reader has adopted.

P+La+L note: Celestia tells AJ directly: the current policies were established 80 years ago in response to ponies adopting Gilded Age industry and ambition turning inward to harm others; ponies themselves petitioned for the restrictions; before those 80 years, ambitious ponies were directed outward to settle the Everfree — which is what Granny Smith's account of Ponyville's founding describes.

WI+La note — what the reader now knows:

Prior: The Stagnation is Celestia's 1000-year project, top-down, a tyrant's imposition of pacification on ponies who did not choose it.

Disclosure: The policy is 80 years old. Ponies experiencing Gilded Age-style harm petitioned Celestia to restrict griffon-style industry. Before the restriction, Equestrian ambition was channeled outward into expansion. Celestia responded to democratic demand; she did not originate the system.

Revised model: The nursery was built by the children, not imposed by the parent. The Stagnation is a democracy's trauma response to what ungoverned industrial ambition produced within living memory. Celestia's White Peace is not a tyrant trying to reimpose her system — it is a frightened administrator watching ponies re-enter the conditions that caused the original petition.

T+La note — why this revision is evidence for the thematic propositions:

Tagged to P4 (Stagnation was necessary but insufficient): The 80-year revision proves P4 rather than merely asserting it. The reader now knows the alternative to the Stagnation was tried and failed within living memory — Gilded Age ambition-turned-inward produced enough harm that ponies collectively chose safety. P4's claim that the Stagnation built the Moral Surplus that lets Equestrians rehabilitate changelings rather than exterminate them is now supported by showing what the pre-Stagnation conditions produced. The insufficiency is simultaneously confirmed: the policy was a contingent historical response to specific conditions, not an eternal principle, which means it can and must end as conditions change.

Tagged to P1 (the Predator's Dilemma): Ponies faced the Predator's Dilemma with unchecked industrial ambition and chose the mercy-without-capacity solution because they lacked sufficient Red Love to defend the conditions of Pink Love generation. The petition is evidence that P1's dilemma is real and that societies facing it without the full Harmonic Republican solution will reach for suboptimal partial solutions — not because they lack conscience, but because they lack the strength to deploy conscience against the conditions that generate the dilemma.

The distinction is: the WI+La note records the epistemic update — what the reader now understands about the story world. The T+La note records the argumentative claim — why that specific update constitutes proof of a specific thematic proposition. The WI note answers "what did the reader just learn?" The T note answers "why does what the reader just learned count as evidence for X?" The T note never repeats the content of the WI note — it explains the logical chain from the revised model to the thematic claim.

Without the T note, you have a world-truth update floating unconnected to the story's argument. Without the WI note, the T note is claiming evidence it hasn't specified. Both are necessary and neither contains the other.

uninhabited note tracks should stack on the side instead of be on full display

Because there is something special about the POV character versus the other characters who the POV character is observing. Observed characters can do La revelation and En actions. The POV character additionally has special power. What is it precisely? What are the pros and cons of this conditional approach? Is it overkill or helpful?
Does the WI+La note need to restate the thing that is clashing when the P+La note has that fact already? So the WI+La note can just say the prior belief and how the reader's belief is being updated in response to the content in P+La? May 28

Architected narrative tracking system with POV mechanics and thematic evidence layers
Architected narrative tracking system with POV mechanics and thematic evidence layers
The POV character's special power, stated precisely
An observed character can produce La evidence (prior beliefs about them get crashed by disclosures about them) and En evidence (the reader watches them act and infers meaning). What they cannot produce is the gap — because the gap requires the narrator to simultaneously render a consciousness from inside and withhold structural truth that consciousness lacks. The narrator can only do this when bounded to a specific perspective.

The POV character's additional power is: the reader inhabits the limited perception rather than observing it. For observed characters, the reader is outside watching. For the POV character, the reader is inside experiencing. This distinction produces three consequences:

Thematic evidence becomes experiential rather than argumentative — the reader doesn't just watch AJ deceive herself, they experience the deception feeling like truth
The narrator's epistemic boundary is structural, not chosen — the narrator genuinely cannot assert what AJ doesn't know, making the irony impossible to fake
The reader's superior understanding becomes active rather than passive — they're measuring their structural knowledge against the perception they're inhabiting in real time
On the conditional UI display
Pros:

The mechanism genuinely only applies when the link's subject IS the plot point's POV character. Showing the gap track for an observed character's link would be a category error — the gap for observed characters is held in the POV character's link (how AJ perceives Twilight) not in Twilight's own link. The conditional enforces a structural truth about where the gap lives.

It maintains cognitive mode discipline at the software level — consistent with the whole design philosophy of making it structurally hard to put content in the wrong place.

It keeps the interface clean for the common case. Most links in most scenes are observed characters producing La and En content.

Cons:

Variable focalization edge case: if a plot point has brief POV shifts within it, a secondary POV character's gap track would be invisible under this logic. Probably rare enough to handle manually.

Secondary gap design — how AJ misperceives Chrysalis, for instance — lives in Chrysalis's observed-character link, not as a "gap track" but as an ordinary WI+En note about what AJ's perception produces. The conditional correctly excludes the gap track here, but the writer must remember to capture AJ's misperception in the WI+En note instead. This is manageable but requires awareness.

Verdict: Not overkill. The mechanism is precise enough that a conditional display adds structural clarity. The writer has already committed to cognitive mode discipline as a core principle. Having the software enforce it at the display layer is consistent and appropriate.

WI+La note structure
Correct — the WI+La note does not need to restate the P+La content. P+La holds the disclosure itself. WI+La holds the epistemic delta:

Prior belief: what the reader held before this scene Updated belief: what the reader holds after receiving the P+La content

For Silver/Eve: P+La holds "Eve uses Giga Impact and dispatches the Houndour easily." WI+La holds: "Prior: Eve is the one being protected; Silver's masculine provider role is necessary and correct. Updated: protection flows both ways; the prior gender role model was an assumption, not a fact about this pair."

The WI+La note doesn't say what Eve did — that's already in P+La. It says what the reader believed before and what they believe after. The update is the delta, not the source of the delta.

T+La then says why that specific delta constitutes thematic evidence — not what changed, but why the change having happened proves something. In the Silver case: "The reader EXPERIENCED being inside the wrong model for 13 chapters and had to revise it, which proves the masculine-protector model is a trained assumption rather than a natural fact — precisely because the reader themselves held it without question and had to give it up."

The T note is always about why the reader's having been wrong and having been corrected is evidence for the proposition. The update being experienced by the reader (not just observed) is what makes it thematic proof rather than mere information delivery.

Granny Smith / Family Appreciation Day: source material reference vs. prior belief clash
These are two sides of the same move, operating in opposite directions on the same episode.

Source Material Reference (Canon track on the Stagnation subject): Direction: canon → validates TLTT mechanic. "Family Appreciation Day establishes the Zap Apple pioneer settlement as a canonical record of outward-expansion Equestria. This is the historical evidence that the 80-year revelation refers to." The author used the episode as world-building grounding. It points from canon toward TLTT.

Prior Belief Clash (WI+La on the White Peace plot point's Stagnation link): Direction: TLTT mechanic → crashes reader's understanding of canon. Prior: "Family Appreciation Day is a heartwarming founding story about Apple family heritage, filed under 'backstory episode, no political significance.'" Updated: "Family Appreciation Day is a historical document of the pre-Stagnation world — the specific era of outward expansion that ended when the nursery was chosen 80 years ago."

How they're intertwined: The source material reference is what generates the La crash. You can't update the reader's understanding of Family Appreciation Day without first using it as world-building evidence. The crash happens BECAUSE TLTT treats the episode as a historical document. One produces the other.

How they're not intertwined: They operate at different layers with different subjects. Source Material Reference is the author's design tool — I used this canon episode to ground my mechanic. Prior Belief Clash is the reader's experience — my understanding of this episode has been revised. The same episode is being processed by different actors doing different things: the author using it as evidence, the reader having their prior about it updated.

You could have the source reference without the crash: if the reader doesn't remember Family Appreciation Day, using it still grounds the TLTT mechanic, but there's no loaded prior to crash. You could have canon recontextualization without explicit source citation: if the Stagnation framework made the pioneer era vivid enough that viewers retroactively reread their old understanding of Granny Smith's story without TLTT explicitly naming the episode.

The sincere revision layer:

This is what makes the Granny Smith case specifically sincere revision rather than just source citation. Family Appreciation Day was reaching for historical-materialist depth that the show's constraints prevented from being articulated: a founding moment, a specific historical culture, a transition from one world to another. Within the show it was a heartwarming backstory episode. It was doing exactly what Faust's design always did — pointing at something real that the format couldn't fully reach.

TLTT completes that reach. It says: yes, the pioneer era Granny Smith describes is politically significant — it's the world before the nursery was built, and the nursery's construction was a specific historical event with specific causes. The episode was reaching for that depth. TLTT delivers it.

For the story planner, the Canon track on the Stagnation subject holds the world-building layer (here is what the episode establishes that supports the mechanic). The WI+La on the White Peace link holds the reader experience layer (here is how the reader's understanding of the episode is being updated). The Analogical track holds the sincere revision frame if you want to capture it explicitly: "Family Appreciation Day was reaching for pre-Stagnation historical depth that TLTT completes."

In prior belief clash (La): reader observes disclosure on page, then experiences a clash and update of their OWN understanding.
In narrator perception gap: reader experiences the character's earnest belief. This clashes with their structural understanding.

Validated three narrative mechanisms and excavated diverse textual examples from source material
Validated three narrative mechanisms and excavated diverse textual examples from source material
Confirming your three descriptions
All three are correct. One precision worth adding on the gap: La is sequential (prior held → disclosure arrives → update occurs). The gap is simultaneous — the reader holds both positions at the same time rather than moving through them. They experience AJ's earnest belief as genuine AND measure it against structural knowledge in the same moment. The mechanism doesn't produce an update; it produces a duality the reader carries continuously.

The machine gun in v1
Yes, it is explicitly there. Note 5: "subsequent need to be at a machine gun to feel like she's contributing in chapter 1." The Command scene analysis: "Applejack chooses the machine gun over the general's star because a machine gunner does not have to make hard moral choices." It's a real, deliberate design choice — not something I invented.

Its relative importance: it's the establishing instance of the gap, introduced early precisely because it's legible. The mechanism is at its most readable here. But the most thematically load-bearing examples are later, where the gap does more complex work. I've been over-using it because it's the easiest to explain, not because it's the most important.

En examples: reader observes and rationally constructs
1. Comet Shine's factory observation

The scene: Comet Shine notes that a normal soldier wouldn't spend their rest day investigating his factory's operations.

P: AJ is there, asking questions, on her own time. Comet Shine marks the behavior explicitly.
WI: The reader infers — her claim to be "just a soldier" is contradicted by her own behavior. She cannot stop being a general even while insisting she isn't one. Nobody made her investigate the factory.
T (tagged to Honesty vs. Poseurs): Genuine character reasserts itself against its own denial. Refusing one's actual role while continuing to perform it is itself a form of self-deception. You cannot dismiss yourself into being a simple soldier; what you are shows in what you cannot stop doing.

2. The return to the machine gun at the end of Chapter 3

The scene: AJ returns to the machine gun, this time described as with "resolve instead of guilt."

P: The exact same observable action as Chapter 1, callback established.
WI: The behavioral callback signals interior transformation without stating it. The reader infers the shift by recognizing the same action now carries different weight.
T: The same action can constitute either evasion or commitment depending on the interior orientation. What changes is not visible from outside — but the story has given the reader the structural context to perceive the difference.

3. Trimmel: "You played Synovial like a fiddle"

The scene: The most competent enemy commander AJ has faced, after surrendering, acknowledges her tactical genius.

P: Trimmel — who the reader has seen defeat the Equestrian army, who represents everything AJ believed herself incapable of — grants her respect unprompted and specifically on military terms.
WI: The reader infers that the most credible possible external validator has now contradicted the impostor syndrome narrative. The analysis notes: this "relieves Applejack of some of the imposter syndrome of being a farmer in a general's suit." The reader arrives at this before AJ fully does.
T: Legitimacy certified by the worthy adversary carries more argumentative weight than any volume of self-assessment. An enemy with nothing to gain by flattery is a reliable witness.

La examples: reader holds a prior, disclosure crashes it
1. The Flim and Flam recontextualization (the most load-bearing canon La in AJ's arc)

Prior loaded across the whole story (and pre-loaded from canon): "The Super Speedy Cider Squeezy 6000 proved that honest quality work defeats soulless extraction. The Apple family won because their craft mattered."

La content: Twilight reveals she had the spell to beat the machine calculated in seconds and chose not to use it. The first batch — when Flim and Flam ran their machine properly — was actually good. Even Granny tried to hide that she thought it tasted good. They lost only because Flim and Flam were too greedy to keep the quality up. "A smarter, less full-of-himself version of Flim and Flam would have won."

WI+La: Prior was "honest craft beats industrial extraction." Update: the machine itself was not the enemy — the extractive business model was. Industry is capable of producing good cider; Flim and Flam's character flaws just prevented it.

T (tagged to P2 — accelerants are morally neutral): This is the most direct proof of P2 in AJ's arc. The reader's prior — which AJ also held, which the canon episode itself seemed to endorse — turns out to be a misread. The machine was not the villain. The business relationship was.

This is also significant because it is a sincere revision La mechanism operating on a specific canon episode: the reader's prior was built by watching that episode, and TLTT corrects what the episode seemed to prove.

2. Kemerskai's posture as genuine tool

Prior established through AJ's POV and adopted by the reader: "Kemerskai demands theatrical speeches and performative ceremonies out of vanity — or worse, manipulation. He is the kind of strongman AJ despises."

La content (delivered through his history over the Cloudbury chapters): The Universal Translator was purchased at extortionary prices to bypass cynical senators and speak directly to ordinary griffons. He executed his own father. He absorbed political attacks, knowingly wearing the "tyrant" accusation, because the alternative was losing the republic. "The truth does not speak for itself, you have to roar." He instituted elections and plans to step down when conditions allow.

WI+La: Prior was "posture is the enemy of honesty." Update: posture deployed in service of truth is itself a form of honesty — it is the mechanism by which truth reaches those who need it. Kemerskai's theatrics were democratic legitimacy, not vanity.

T (tagged to Honesty vs. Poseurs and P4): This is the most direct argument against AJ's foundational belief that honest work speaks for itself. The v1 note puts it explicitly: "The truth does not speak for itself, you have to roar. This is the opposite of the cider episode lesson." TLTT is arguing: the cider episode's lesson was incomplete.

3. AJ's parents' departure

Prior: "AJ's parents left the farm for industry and abandoned the family — she went back to the farm because the farm was right."

La content (accumulated across the arc): "Her parents left because they realized the world had more truth. But Applejack wanted to go back to the farm, so they respected it." The parents' departure was epistemically honest — they followed where understanding led. AJ's return was also honest, and they honored both.

WI+La: Prior was "industry takes souls away — that's what it did to my parents." Update: they left the farm to find truth, not to abandon her. Her rejection of industry was a misread of their departure.

T: The people you thought betrayed their roots may have been the ones honest enough to grow. AJ's foundational wound about industry was built on a category error about her parents' motives.

Gap examples: reader inhabits earnest belief, simultaneously holds structural knowledge
1. The love donation as "sick day" — the gap that inverts

The scene: AJ confesses to Twilight that she only did the love donation to get a sick day from being the General, not out of heroic compassion. She presents this as exposing herself as a fraud.

FID voice to render: AJ's genuine self-condemnation. She isn't performing humility — she truly believes that confessing the impure motive proves she was never the hero people thought. The reader inhabits this.

Structural knowledge the reader holds: A person who is honest enough to admit they did a good act for impure reasons IS the Element of Honesty. The analysis notes: "I'm not the hero you think I am. I only donated that love because I wanted to stop being a General for five minutes. You defied Celestia for a fraud." Twilight's response: "I didn't defy Celestia for 'General Applejack.' I defied her for the mare who is honest enough to admit she's tired."

The gap: Her self-description as fraud IS the evidence that she isn't. The FID renders her self-condemnation as genuine while the reader simultaneously understands that the capacity for honest self-condemnation is the very quality she's claiming to lack. This is the gap at its most complex — the character's earnest belief and the structural truth are not just different assessments of the same fact; they are the same act producing opposite evaluations simultaneously.

2. The Kemerskai mission as distraction

The scene: AJ's FID perspective on being sent to Cloudbury for Kemerskai's theatrical speeches is that this is a distraction from the real war.

FID voice to render: Her frustration is genuine. She has fought for the Republic. She wants to finish the job. The theatrical ceremony feels like vanity while changelings are still fighting.

Structural knowledge the reader holds: The v1 note states it directly — "her mission was the distraction. The republic she was reluctantly being shown in Cloudbury was the thing she was urgently fighting to build. She couldn't see this because she was looking forward at the enemy when the thing she needed was already existing behind her."

The gap: AJ is looking at the wrong thing and the reader can see it. The republic exists. The thing she was fighting to build is in the room. Her frustration at being sent to Cloudbury is the exact same psychology as Twilight's frustration at being sent to make friends in Ponyville when she wanted to research the Elements — the directive felt like a distraction from the mission; the mission was the distraction.

The T earned by the gap: You cannot see the thing you're building while you're facing the obstacle. The civic reality that makes a republic is not visible at the front line; it requires standing still in the society the war made possible.

3. The hard truth speech — AJ sincerely says one thing, Comet Shine hears another

The scene: AJ gives what she genuinely experiences as a speech about defending ancestral farmland and community values. Comet Shine, already briefed by Fleur, hears coded authorization for phosphorus extraction and weapons manufacture. "She is speaking literally about community values" while "he thinks Applejack is speaking in coded metaphors about the chemical reality."

FID voice to render: AJ's sincerity is complete. She is not being strategic or coded. She genuinely means the farming language she's using. The reader inhabits this sincerity.

Structural knowledge the reader holds: Comet Shine's interpretation is also correct within his context. AJ's words are simultaneously a genuine farming speech and, accidentally, an industrial authorization. She has said something true in a specific domain that maps perfectly onto something true in another domain, without knowing it.

The gap here is unusual: it isn't about AJ being wrong about herself, but about the reader witnessing a bifurcated reception of her sincere utterance. The gap is between what she means and what is being understood — both accurate, neither communicating. This is thematically central to the posture problem: honest speech that isn't calibrated for its audience produces unintended effects.

Now give examples of the mechanisms from this passage from Pax Chrysalia written by Brazen Gauge. This is a fanfiction of EaW that takes the opposite direction of TLTT: in Pax Chrysalia, Chrysalis appears as a narcissist but is actually a harmonist where the arbitrary cruelty is a mask. The changelings defeated Equestria and now Chrysalis has to reign in the predators of her own race because she wants a meritocracy of both changelings and ponies. This passage is specifically of Vaspier, the leader of VOPS, who is a changeling supremacist. The chapter depicts him through FID as genuinely feeling right, while the reader holds the structural context that he's both a monster and also totally outgunned by Chrysalis's supporters, due to the earlier Chrysalis POV chapters. May 28

Analyzed narrative gaps between Vaspier's self-perception and structural reality
Analyzed narrative gaps between Vaspier's self-perception and structural reality
Correcting the descriptions first
Your three descriptions are correct with one precision worth adding on the gap mechanism: La is sequential (prior held → disclosure → update). The gap is simultaneous — the reader holds both the character's earnest belief and their structural knowledge at the same time, continuously. There is no update moment; the duality is the mechanism.

Now the Pax Chrysalia passage.

En mechanisms: reader observes, rationally constructs
1. The nose

P: "He winced, a shot of pain from his nose reminding him of his recent… setback." Later, when Windy mentions Chrysalis: "gently rub his aching snout." The wound is physically activated by the name.

WI: The reader connects the wound to Chrysalis without the text stating it. His most recent defeat has Chrysalis's name written on it — literally in his body.

T: The person who claims everyone including Chrysalis is "afraid of his ability" carries Chrysalis's refutation in his face. His body is arguing against his self-narrative before a word of the self-narrative is spoken.

2. Windy Gale's ideology as mirror

P: "I think we all need to be shown our place. Changelings should rule over us and break us whiny cunts down into something useful." A serial killer who specifically targeted mares declares herself a changeling supremacist. Her agreement with his worldview is complete.

WI: The most enthusiastic endorsement of Vaspier's worldview in the passage comes from a predator who murdered members of her own species. Her agreement doesn't validate the ideology; it characterizes it by who it attracts.

T (En version, from what the reader watches): The people who most eagerly agree with a supremacist worldview are not its best argument for it.

3. The carpet and the coat

P: "Vasiper wasn't entirely sure how they'd managed to get to this side of the reading room... He was sore in places one should never be sore in... Where had his coat gone?" The chapter opens with Vaspier as the composed, commanding figure in control. It ends with him disoriented on the floor in a room he can't explain entering.

WI: The reader observes the behavioral evidence of a man who believes he is the apex predator having been substantially unmade by the interaction he just had with his "toy."

La mechanism: prior held → disclosure → update
1. Windy Gale's identity

Prior loaded through the scene's opening: a cheerful, hypersexualized pony who is obsessed with Vaspier, makes advances, and serves as his housekeeper. She is filed under "unhinged loyal companion."

La disclosure: "The Bray Street Butcher. In truth, Windy Gale — and Manehattan's most prolific serial killer, caught by VOPS, slated for death row, saved by me, because I believed a pony that good at killing mares would be better served as an asset than a corpse."

WI+La: Prior = manic, sexually aggressive housewife. Update = the "predator would a prime rib" look, the serpent tongue, the way she categorizes herself as "prey," the comfort with discussing her own potential murder — these were always the tells of a killer. The reader reprocesses every flirtatious gesture through the new lens.

T+La: The disclosure is evidence for the chapter's central argument about Vaspier's worldview — he finds the Bray Street Butcher a more useful asset than a monster. His capacity to instrumentalize a serial killer without moral complexity tells the reader what his rationality actually is.

2. "She's just misguided, she's not—"

Prior loaded from his character presentation: Vaspier is a cold, clear-headed supremacist. His relationship to Chrysalis is presumably one of pure strategic opposition — she is in his way.

La: An incomplete sentence. He starts defending Chrysalis, stops himself, and holds a sore nose when her name comes up. Windy slaps him specifically for sounding like a loyalist.

WI+La: Prior = clean antagonist relationship. Update = something warped and complicated. He calls her "misguided," not evil. He rubs his nose as a reflex when she's named. His incomplete sentence is itself La content — what he was about to say is withheld, and the gap it leaves is load-bearing.

T+La: The supremacist's ideology is not cleanly separated from his personal relationships. Chrysalis means something to him he cannot articulate, which means his opposition to her is not purely ideological. That complicates everything about his "decisive action" at the end.

Gap mechanism: reader inhabits earnest belief, simultaneously holds structural knowledge
This is where the passage is most interesting, and it maps directly onto the Greek tragedy gap structure you identified for the Chrysalis prequel.

The structural context the reader carries from earlier chapters: Chrysalis is actually a harmonist. Vaspier is both a monster and politically outgunned. His move to "act" is overreach the reader already knows will fail.

1. "Even Chrysalis is afraid of my ability"

FID voice rendered as genuine: "Ponies cower in their homes from me, changelings dread my arrival, even Chrysalis is afraid of my ability. Everyone on the planet is afraid of me, in some manner."

This must be rendered through FID as completely sincere — not ironic, not performed, not self-consoling. He genuinely believes it.

Structural knowledge the reader holds from the chapter itself, let alone earlier chapters: His nose is broken. The person he identifies as "afraid of his ability" broke it. He defends her when her name is mentioned. He rubs the wound.

The gap: His apex predator self-image is not a lie he tells himself — it is his earnest experience of his own identity. The gap is between that experience (genuine and consistent with much of the external evidence: he IS feared, he IS dangerous) and the one specific piece of evidence that contradicts its completeness. Chrysalis broke his nose and outmaneuvers him politically, which means "afraid of his ability" is his interpretation of a dynamic that also includes her defeating him.

T from the gap: The most dangerous self-deception is the one that is almost entirely true. He is feared. He is dangerous. The gap between his self-assessment and reality is not large — but it is precisely at the point where it matters most.

2. His "ownership" of Windy

FID voice: "Obsession of this kind was useful; it made her malleable. It made her willing to do whatever the Great Imperial Noling commanded." She is "prey," an "asset," his "one exception" who will be "by his side" — he has organized the entire relationship from the position of the party who holds all power.

Structural knowledge: She slapped him. He found himself on the carpet disoriented, missing his coat, "sore in places one should never be sore in." He catches himself "reaching out to run a hoof through her luscious bubblegum pink mane" as an involuntary possessive gesture. He characterizes his future wife (a proper changeling Queen he'll "make a mare of") but then says Windy "won't be negotiable" and will be his one exception. "Even though he would never be hers, she was his."

The gap: He believes he holds all power in this relationship. The behavioral evidence rendered in the same scene — the disorientation, the involuntary touch, the "she will be by his side, his one exception" — shows she has substantial power over him that his self-narrative doesn't account for. The FID renders his dominance framing as completely sincere. The reader occupies that framing while watching it not quite add up.

T from the gap: The feudal lord framing ("I provide, she serves, I own, this is natural order") is exactly what a person would construct when their actual relationship produces an experience of being owned. The more elaborate the framework for ownership, the more it may be compensating.

3. "I'm going to call a meeting"

FID voice: Decisive. He is energized, has decided, the waiting is over, he'll invite "the Party." He cracks his back, stretches his neck. The chapter ends on his momentum.

Structural knowledge: He arrived home nursing an injury from Chrysalis. He has just been physically and emotionally unmade on his own carpet by the asset he describes as a "toy." He is now in a post-feeding altered state making decisions energized by a serial killer's enthusiasm. The reader knows from earlier chapters that his position is weaker than he understands.

The gap: He experiences this as a moment of clarity and decision. The reader experiences it as a man in a vulnerable altered state making the decision that will overextend him, urged on by the only person who agrees with him enthusiastically — who is Manehattan's most prolific serial killer.

The contrast with AJ's gap worth naming
AJ's gap: the gap is between her self-underestimation and the reader's fuller appreciation of her worth. The reader is rooting for her to close the gap. The T is hopeful — this conscience is real despite her inability to see it.

Vaspier's gap: the gap is between his self-overestimation (or more precisely, his worldview's internal coherence) and the reader's knowledge of his actual position. The reader watches a man build his own trap with complete sincerity. The T is tragic in the Greek sense — not because he lacks ability, but because his framework for understanding his own ability has a precise blind spot that will determine his outcome.

This is the same gap structure you identified for the Chrysalis prequel: reader holds foreknowledge of the collapse; character experiences present confidence as genuine. In both Vaspier's chapter and your planned Chrysalis prequel, the gap doesn't close within the narrative — it becomes permanent at the moment of failure. That's what distinguishes the Greek tragedy deployment from the standard arc-progression gap.

Can the prior belief clash come from both revelation and enacted events -> inference?
Similarly, for tech or civ system, reader can infer ontology/how it works from revelation about history on page, not just seeing it in action on page. Same for characters, readers can infer their characterization from actions on page or disclosed backstory on page
So the the "La inference and theme" are not strictly from "La reveal" aka backstory. Same for the standard "witnessed action, rational inference, argument evidence" pipeline. Rational inference and argument evidence can come from backstory or history reveal too.

May 29

Validated theoretical framework and identified multiple track definition inconsistencies
Validated theoretical framework and identified multiple track definition inconsistencies
On the prior belief clash mechanism
Yes — your observation is correct and precise. Prior belief clash is a mechanism for how reader understanding updates, not a constraint on whether that update comes from disclosure or witnessed action. The three paths:

La path (direct): history or truth is disclosed on page → prior crashes against the disclosed fact → reader updates

En path (direct): behavior is staged on page → reader infers meaning from watching → inference crashes the prior → reader updates

Hybrid (the case you're raising): history is disclosed on page → reader infers ontology, function, or psychology FROM that history → the inference crashes the prior → reader updates

The hybrid is the important one. The Love Harvester's function could be disclosed through someone explaining its history of invention rather than the reader watching it operate. That historical disclosure enables an inference about how it works, which can crash a prior about "this is just a weapon." The prior belief crash is produced by the inference, not by directly witnessing the technology in action. Same mechanism, different path to the inference.

This means the Reader Prior Belief Update track's display question should not restrict itself to one source. Looking at the current file: most already handle this correctly with "or" language — Bond says "disclosure, demonstration, or their inference," Character says "revelations, events, or inferences." The principle is already embedded in the better-written questions.

Analysis of the definitions file
1. CivSystem Reader Prior Belief Update — missing display question

The only track in the file that has no display question despite needing one. Suggested: "What would be the reader's prior assumptions or understanding of the civilizational system? How does the disclosure, demonstration, or their inference update their belief?"

2. Technology "Usage Plan" — wrong track type

Currently PageDesign. This is a project-wide summary track that asks "How is the technology shown and used throughout the story?" — a macro-level plan across all appearances, not what happens in a specific scene. Its Usage Directive confirms it: "These notes should cover the sum of the links." An aggregate summary of appearances is NarrativeArchitecture. PageDesign is reserved for what goes on the page in a specific scene. This should be NarrativeArchitecture.

3. World Law Themes — Supports Theme: No (inconsistent)

Every other ThematicEvidence track that asks about thematic propositions has Supports Theme: Yes. Technology Themes: Yes. Organization Themes: Yes. CivSystem Theme Plan: Yes. World Law Themes is typed ThematicEvidence but has Supports Theme: No. This looks like a field entry error rather than an intentional design choice.

4. Character Reader Prior Belief Clash — cognitive mode inconsistency with other subject types

For Bond, CivSystem, Organization, Technology, and World Law, the prior belief mechanism is split across two tracks:

Reader Prior Belief Update (WorldInference type): holds the prior and the update
Reader Prior Belief Clash (ThematicEvidence type): holds how the update serves as thematic evidence
For Character, these are collapsed into one Reader Prior Belief Clash track (ThematicEvidence type) with a three-part display question covering all of it. This means the prior + update content is filed under ThematicEvidence, which is the wrong cognitive mode for that content — the prior and the update are WorldInference material; the thematic evidence is ThematicEvidence.

This is worth resolving in one of two directions: split Character's Reader Prior Belief Clash to match the other subjects, or accept the three-part combined format for Character and update the display question framing to acknowledge it's intentionally combined. Either is defensible; the inconsistency is the problem.

5. Garden Notes — missing display questions for four subject types

Character has "What story planning work still needs to be done regarding this character?" and CivSystem has "What should I take into account when writing about this civilizational system?" But Organization, Technology, World Law, and Bond have no display question. Either a consistent question across all types or type-specific ones are both fine; blank is not.

6. "Allegories" vs "Analogies" — naming inconsistency

CivSystem, Technology, and World Law use "Allegories" as the track name. Character and Organization use "Analogies." Both use the Analogies track type. The naming of the track type is consistent; the display name is not. Pick one and standardize.

7. Scene Thesis — track type

Currently WorldInference. The display question asks "What should the reader independently conclude from this scene? Synthesize the thematic propositions evidenced by all subject links." — this is the designed thematic output of the scene, synthesizing T-level content from all the link ThematicEvidence tracks. This is more ThematicEvidence than WorldInference. WorldInference is for what the reader constructs about how the world works; ThematicEvidence is for the thematic argument the reader arrives at. Scene Thesis is the latter.

8. Stakes — track type

Currently WorldInference. "Why does this scene matter?" is the author articulating narrative architecture reasoning about importance — why this scene belongs in the story and what it serves. This is NotesToSelf or NarrativeArchitecture reasoning, not a reader inference design note. A reader doesn't need to "infer why this scene matters" — they experience it. The Stakes track is the author answering that question for themselves before designing the scene.

I'm not sure what is the scope of things that go into garden notes considering I have flag reasons for things that need more planning across any track. Please review this claude chat conversation history to find what should end up in garden notes and where the track came from.

What is the difference between allegories and analogies, from first principles? Which should I use, or if there is a genuine difference, should both exist? Why or why not?

I believe all the non character tracks for links now have the full separation of tracks covering Page, World Inference, Theme evidence, and across both standard witnessing/rational inference and the reader prior belief clash where the reader themself is involved. I haven't revised character yet because of the extra nuance that was discussed in this conversation. Please review it. Character tracks need those tracks that the others have, and in addition needs the two extra tracks for the next separate mechanism about character reader perception gap? Inference and Theme, and note that it's a continuous mechanism. But not any other need for more Page tracks? Also I am wondering if the character link and subject tracks currently conflate things that are needed for character reader perception gap with things for the standard rational witness inference and the reader prior belief update/clash?

Please analyze, from first principles (ignoring what is currently in the character tracks) whether the baseline is established across the other subjects, and then whether that baseline should be deployed properly for character, and then additional character stuff added, potentially only visible if the plot point that is being linked to has that specific character as the POV character. Once the first principles of what should be there are established logically, then compare those first principles with what is currently in the character tracks, and identify current standing differences, how to rectify, and why.

May 29

Synthesized track architecture across subjects and identified character track conflations
Synthesized track architecture across subjects and identified character track conflations
Garden Notes: scope and origin
The earlier conversation established this track under the name "Project Notes" — one notes-to-self track per entity holding "open questions, work directives, cross-reference reminders, revision history summaries, and meta-observations about the entity's current state of development." It explicitly never exports to LLM sessions.

The key distinction from flagged notes: a flagged note is content in the correct track with an unresolved question about that specific content. A Garden Note is a subject-level operational note that doesn't belong in any specific cognitive mode track — it's the author talking to themselves about the subject as a planning artifact, not about any individual assertion.

Examples of things that belong in Garden Notes:

"Before doing archive evaluation, review the 2025-11-15 Gemini session — may contradict what's in Characterization"
"This subject needs to be checked against the Aquileian Cartel subject before expansion"
"I haven't decided whether this should be its own subject or folded into Star Energy"
"Downstream plot points in Chapters 12-15 depend on this being resolved before I can write those links"
These are not content claims. They're not flaggable notes (no wrong track to correct). They're the project management layer for the subject itself.

The CivSystem display question "What should I take into account when writing about this civilizational system?" has drifted from this scope into craft guidance territory. It should match the Character version's framing: "What story planning work still needs to be done for this subject?"

Allegories vs Analogies: from first principles
Analogy is a comparison drawn for the author's own reasoning. It says "X is like Y." It helps the author understand implications, engineer internal consistency, and think through consequences. The comparison is a design tool. It's the author importing real-world knowledge to make better decisions about the fictional construct.

Allegory is an intentional parallel designed to be read by the reader. It says "this fictional X is meant to invoke Y in the reader's mind." The fictional element is constructed to be decodable. The parallel is reader-facing, not just author-facing.

For a story planner, these serve genuinely different design purposes:

Analogy notes inform construction — "the Love Harvester is designed like addiction economics, so I should engineer dependency mechanics that mirror that pattern." This helps the author get the mechanics right.

Allegory notes inform reader experience design — "the Love Harvester should be readable as the AI dependency economy, so the inference seeds must include behavioral proxies that invoke those parallels." This informs what needs to be in the WI tracks for the reader to make the connection.

The practical test: if you deleted the note, would you lose design knowledge about how to build the subject (analogy) or about what the reader should derive from it (allegory)?

Current inconsistency: Character uses Analogies ("What real-world figures serve as inspiration?") — clearly author-side design inspiration. Organization uses Analogies but asks "What does this represent in the real world?" — clearly reader-facing allegory. CivSystem/Technology/World Law use Allegories and ask about real-world equivalents — reader-facing.

Recommendation: Keep both, with distinct display questions. The Analogies track is design-side: "What real-world equivalents help the author understand and engineer this subject correctly?" The Allegories track is reader-facing: "What real-world equivalents should an attentive reader be able to derive from inference seeds?" Note that allegory content informs what goes into WI tracks; analogy content informs how to write world truth, history, and characterization correctly.

Character link tracks: first principles, then comparison
The baseline from other subjects
All linked subjects require the same six fundamental track types:

Two PageDesign tracks (La and En paths):

La: what disclosed history/truth/function goes on page
En: what staged action/effect/behavior goes on page
One WorldInference track:

Standard rational inference the reader makes from both paths combined
One ThematicEvidence track:

How the inference serves as evidence for a thematic proposition
Two Reader Prior Belief mechanism tracks:

Reader Prior Belief Update (WorldInference): prior the reader holds + how La/En/inference updates it + revised understanding
Reader Prior Belief Clash (ThematicEvidence): how the process of updating itself constitutes thematic argument
This baseline appears consistently across World Law, Technology, CivSystem, and Organization. Six track types, the last two specifically for the prior crash mechanism.

Why Character diverges from the baseline
Character needs to split two of the six baseline tracks because characters have two distinct dimensions that other subjects don't:

Static psychology (who they already are): revealed by both La (disclosed history) and En (behavioral evidence), but the inference target is existing psychological architecture.

Dynamic development (how they're changing): primarily En-driven, with the inference target being transformation-in-progress.

These require separate WI targets and separate T tracks — not because the mechanisms differ, but because the inference products are different claims. What the reader infers about AJ's existing wound is a different note from what the reader infers about how that wound is healing. Both are WI, but they answer different questions.

This produces Character's baseline (replacing the single WI and single T with pairs):

Two PageDesign tracks: Revelation (La) + Character Actions (En) Two WorldInference tracks: Inferred Psychology (existing psychology from both paths) + Inferred Character Development (change, primarily En) Two ThematicEvidence tracks: Character Scene Evidence (what psychology/behavior argues) + Character Development Meaning (what the change argues) Two Reader Prior Belief tracks: Reader Prior Belief Update (WI) + Reader Prior Belief Clash (T)

Eight total for the character baseline.

The additional POV-conditional tracks
For scenes where this character IS the POV anchor, the gap mechanism adds two more tracks that don't apply to observed characters:

Gap Inference (WorldInference, POV-conditional): What does the reader simultaneously experience from inside the character's FID-rendered consciousness AND understand from structural context that the character cannot? This is a continuous duality the reader holds, not a discrete inference from external observation.

Gap Evidence (ThematicEvidence, POV-conditional): What thematic proposition does the reader's continuous experience of inhabiting this limited consciousness prove? This is different from Character Development Meaning (which argues what a discrete change proves) because the gap produces experiential proof rather than argumentative proof.

Ten total for a POV-scene character link.

Comparison with current tracks
First-principles track	Current status
Revelation (PageDesign)	✓ Exists
Character Actions (PageDesign)	✓ Exists
Inferred Psychology (WorldInference)	✓ Exists — but see conflation below
Inferred Character Development (WorldInference)	✓ Exists
Character Scene Evidence (ThematicEvidence)	✗ Missing
Character Development Meaning (ThematicEvidence)	✓ Exists
Reader Prior Belief Update (WorldInference)	✗ Missing — conflated into Reader Prior Belief Clash
Reader Prior Belief Clash (ThematicEvidence)	⚠️ Exists but conflated — contains WI content (prior + update) AND T content (how update is evidence)
Gap Inference (WorldInference, POV-conditional)	✗ Missing
Gap Evidence (ThematicEvidence, POV-conditional)	✗ Missing
The conflation in current Inferred Psychology
For an observed character in a standard scene, Inferred Psychology correctly handles external inference about their psychology. But for a POV character, the reader's relationship to that psychology is different — they're inhabiting the consciousness, not observing it. Currently, if a writer is designing a POV scene, they'd put the gap content into Inferred Psychology alongside the standard WI content. These are distinct cognitive operations that produce different design notes.

The POV-conditional Gap Inference track cleans this up: when the character IS the POV, Gap Inference handles the duality content and Inferred Psychology handles only the standard external-observation-style inference that the reader also performs alongside the inhabitation. When the character is NOT the POV, Gap Inference is hidden, and Inferred Psychology is unambiguous.

Rectification
Split current Reader Prior Belief Clash into two tracks:

Reader Prior Belief Update (WorldInference): "What would be the reader's prior opinion of this character entering this scene? How do revealed history, staged behavior, or their inferences update that prior? What is the reader's revised understanding?"
Reader Prior Belief Clash (ThematicEvidence): "How does the process of the reader updating their prior opinion about this character serve as evidence for a thematic proposition?"
Add Character Scene Evidence (ThematicEvidence): "How does this character's behavior or revealed psychology in this scene serve as evidence for a thematic proposition?" This covers the T for static psychology/behavior — the equivalent of World Law's Thematic Proposition Evidence — which is currently absent.

Add Gap Inference (WorldInference, POV-conditional): "What does the reader experience from inside this character's FID-rendered consciousness in this scene, and what does the reader simultaneously understand about this character that the character cannot?"

Add Gap Evidence (ThematicEvidence, POV-conditional): "What thematic proposition does the reader's continuous experience of inhabiting this character's limited consciousness prove?"

For a long time, in the beginning part of this Note Categorization Bootstrapping conversation, I was compressing characters to 6 and it was missing stuff. This total, complete expansion and disambiguation makes a lot more sense.

Why Character has two extra WI/T tracks, and whether other subjects need equivalents
The root cause: two distinct inference targets

Character is the only subject type that has both a state and an arc that require continuous reader inference simultaneously:

State (who they are): psychological architecture, wounds, epistemic limits. Static truth about an existing system. The reader infers this from both disclosed history (La) and staged behavior (En).
Arc (how they're changing): psychological transformation as an organic, designed journey across the story. The reader tracks this progressively across scenes, inferring incremental development each time.
These produce different inference questions. "What does AJ's behavior at the machine gun tell me about who she is?" (state) and "What does this scene tell me about how AJ is changing?" (development) are not the same question. They can produce different WI notes, and they produce different T notes: what her existing psychology argues differs from what the fact of her transformation argues.

The extra two tracks (Inferred Character Development + Character Development Meaning) exist because this is a second, distinct inference pipeline running in parallel with the state pipeline, using the same PageDesign content but targeting different conclusions.

Why the other subjects don't need this:

The test: does this subject have a continuous developmental arc that the reader tracks as an organic interior transformation across the story, producing thematically distinct conclusions from its static nature?

World Law: World laws don't transform. Earth Pony Magic is the same law throughout; it only gets discovered progressively. Discovery is a La sequence, not an arc. No equivalent needed.

Technology: A technology's function is fixed; it can be repurposed, but repurposing is an event in its History track, not a continuous developmental arc. No equivalent needed.

CivSystem: The Stagnation can end and the Republic can be established. But these are historical events — episodic and externally driven, captured in the History track. There's no "how is the Stagnation psychologically developing?" question requiring ongoing inference. No equivalent needed.

Organization: Organizational changes are event-driven (external decisions, losses, gains of membership, shifts in power). The reader infers consequences of events, not a continuous interior arc. The binding logic can change, but as a sequence of plotted events, not as an organic psychological journey. No equivalent needed.

Bond is the exception that parallels Character

A bond has the same two-plane structure Character has:

State (what the bond currently is): what makes these two characters have something special, what the relationship means to each. Currently captured in Characterization Inference (WI) and Scene Theme Evidence (T).
Arc (how the bond is growing or deepening): the TwiJack bond doesn't stay static — it transforms from professional respect to romantic partnership. The reader tracks this development across scenes, inferring how the bond is changing each time they interact.
Currently Bond's link tracks have no equivalent to Inferred Character Development or Character Development Meaning. The reader asking "what does this scene tell me about how the TwiJack bond is developing?" has no designated track. That inference and its thematic argument are currently either squeezed into Characterization Inference (conflating state and arc) or fall through entirely.

Bond should have 8 link tracks, same structure as Character:

Existing 6:

Revelation (PageDesign, La)
Demonstration (PageDesign, En)
Characterization Inference (WorldInference, state)
Scene Theme Evidence (ThematicEvidence, state)
Reader Prior Belief Update (WorldInference, La mechanism)
Reader Prior Belief Clash (ThematicEvidence, La mechanism)
Missing 2:

Bond Development Inference (WorldInference): "What should the reader infer about how the bond is growing or changing in this scene?"
Bond Development Meaning (ThematicEvidence): "What should the reader conclude this stage of the bond's development means, as evidence for a thematic proposition?"
The same PageDesign tracks (Revelation and Demonstration) serve both state and arc — the page content is the same. The split is at the inference layer: the same observable scene produces two different inference questions, and the author needs to design both answers separately.

Now that we have established that character and bond have 8 tracks (plus two conditional on POV for character), and the rest have 6, please re-evaluate the Subject Tracks/Project-Wide Tracks. Take in all insights and history of deliberation from the transcripts and journal file when analyzing. What should be present for the typical subject types, and then for bond, and then for character?
