Synthesized universal track structure across subject types and identified missing elements
Synthesized universal track structure across subject types and identified missing elements
The transcripts establish the framework history cleanly. The five-layer model (world mechanics → history → psychology → narrative architecture → thematic propositions) maps directly to the cognitive mode taxonomy. The key insight from the earlier sessions: every subject needs tracks serving each layer that applies to it, with subject-specific variation in which world-truth layers are relevant.

The universal structure
Every subject type needs the same seven categories of project-wide track, with subject-specific cognitive mode assignments:

World truth layer(s) — one or more tracks, cognitive mode varies by what the subject is
Historian — chronological record of what happened
NarrativeArchitecture (prospective) — author's plan for how reader understanding develops
NarrativeArchitecture (retrospective) — aggregate summary after links are complete, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes
ThematicEvidence — what this subject argues project-wide
External grounding — Analogies (design-side) and Allegories (reader-facing), distinct purposes
Canon — source material connections
NotesToSelf — Garden Notes operational track, consistent display question
The world-truth layer varies by subject type:

World Law: Ontologist only. Natural laws aren't agent-created and have no Civilization layer.
Technology: Ontologist (how it works) + Civilization (why it was created, what it does to the world)
CivSystem: Ontologist (system rules) + Civilization (why it was created, causality)
Organization: Civilization (what it does) + Characterization (binding logic — the organization's collective psychology)
Typical subject analysis with current discrepancies
World Law — closest to complete, three issues:

Themes track has Supports Theme: No — this is a field entry error. It is ThematicEvidence; it must be Yes.
No split between prospective plan (Reader Understanding Plan exists ✓) and retrospective aggregate. Demonstration Plan is currently NarrativeArchitecture with Editable in Audit Mode: Yes, which serves this function — but its display question asks about examples rather than the aggregate result. Rename or add a display question that distinguishes planning from summary.
Garden Notes has no display question.
Technology — two issues:

Usage Plan is typed PageDesign but its display question ("How is the technology shown and used throughout the story?") describes a project-wide macro plan — an aggregate across all appearances. This is NarrativeArchitecture. PageDesign is reserved for what happens in a specific scene.
Garden Notes has no display question.
CivSystem — one issue:

Reader Prior Belief Update link track has no display question (previously identified). Project-wide tracks are otherwise complete.
Organization — one issue:

Garden Notes has no display question.
Naming inconsistency across all types: CivSystem, Technology, and World Law use "Allegories"; Character and Organization use "Analogies." These are currently being used inconsistently — some display questions are asking about design-side inspiration ("what helps the author understand"), others are asking about reader-facing allegory ("what the reader should derive"). These are different cognitive acts. The resolution:

Analogies track: design-side — "What real-world equivalents help the author understand and engineer this subject correctly?" Never reaches the page. Informs how to build world truth.
Allegories track: reader-facing — "What real-world equivalents should an attentive reader be able to derive from the inference seeds?" Never reaches the page. Informs what belongs in WI tracks.
Both tracks are Analogies cognitive mode. Both exist for all subject types where applicable. Standardize naming across all subject types.

Bond: the baseline plus two additions
Bond has the same world-truth structure as Character (Characterization layer for The Spark) and the same History layer, but is currently missing two tracks that the Character development analysis proves are needed.

Current project-wide tracks: History ✓ · Story Plan (NarrativeArchitecture) ✓ · The Spark (Characterization) ✓ · Reader Opinion (NarrativeArchitecture) ✓ · Themes (ThematicEvidence) ✓ · Source Material References (Canon) ✓ · Garden Notes (no display question) ⚠️

Issue: Story Plan conflates prospective design with retrospective aggregate. Character splits this into Reader Opinion Plan (prospective, not Editable) and Character Development (retrospective aggregate, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes). Bond should follow the same pattern:

Story Plan becomes the prospective design plan (not Editable) — plan for how the bond forms and evolves
Add Bond Development Summary (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes) — retrospective aggregate of how the bond actually developed across links
Missing: Arc Thesis (ThematicEvidence, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes). Themes asks "what themes does this bond represent?" — that is the static thematic claim about the bond's nature. But what does the arc of the bond's development prove? The TwiJack bond moving from professional respect to something more proves a specific proposition about how genuine shared purpose generates genuine intimacy. That's a different ThematicEvidence claim from what the bond statically represents. Arc Thesis is Editable in Audit Mode: Yes because it synthesizes the scene-level Bond Development Meaning link notes.

Missing: Analogies track (Analogies, optional). Source Material References covers canon. But there may be real-world relationship dynamics that inform the bond's design or that the reader should be able to derive. Optional, lower priority than the others.

Garden Notes needs a display question.

Complete Bond project-wide structure: History · The Spark (Characterization) · Story Plan (NarrativeArchitecture) · Bond Development Summary (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable) · Reader Opinion (NarrativeArchitecture) · Themes (ThematicEvidence) · Arc Thesis (ThematicEvidence, Editable) · Analogies (Analogies, optional) · Source Material References (Canon) · Garden Notes (NotesToSelf)

Character: the baseline plus Arc Thesis
Character's project-wide tracks are the most developed and are largely correct. One addition needed, one cleanup.

Current: Characterization ✓ · Backstory ✓ · Character Appearance Plan (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable) ✓ · Reader Opinion Plan (NarrativeArchitecture) ✓ · Character Development (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable) ✓ · Theme Plan (ThematicEvidence) ✓ · Source Material References (Canon) ✓ · Analogies (Analogies) ✓ · Garden Notes (with display question) ✓

Missing: Arc Thesis (ThematicEvidence, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes). Theme Plan asks "what thematic propositions does this character embody throughout their appearances?" — this is the static characterization's thematic contribution. But what does this character's psychological transformation arc prove? These are different claims.

Theme Plan: what AJ's honesty and conscience represent as thematic evidence across the whole story. Arc Thesis: what AJ's transformation from impostor-general to Washington-figure to stepping-down president proves — specifically that reluctant leadership produces legitimate authority, that wounded conscience suppresses itself rather than disappearing, that being strong enough to be merciful requires precisely the kind of testing she undergoes.

Arc Thesis is Editable in Audit Mode: Yes because it synthesizes all the Character Development Meaning link-level notes into a project-wide arc argument. You cannot write it until you've designed the link-level arc. It's the capstone ThematicEvidence claim that only a character with a sustained arc can produce.

Cleanup: Arc Thesis also resolves POV-conditional character arcs. For characters with brief POV moments (Mali's one decision scene) the Arc Thesis may be a single sentence. For sustained POV characters (AJ, Twilight), it's the central project-wide argument their arcs produce. The same track, different depth.

Complete Character project-wide structure: Characterization (Characterization) · Backstory (History) · Character Appearance Plan (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable) · Reader Opinion Plan (NarrativeArchitecture) · Character Development (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable) · Theme Plan (ThematicEvidence) · Arc Thesis (ThematicEvidence, Editable) · Source Material References (Canon) · Analogies (Analogies) · Garden Notes (NotesToSelf)

It should be warranted when it is not introducing complexity, but merely managing complexity that already inherently exists in the project, rather than making that complexity appear blended in other tracks. But creating tracks that aren't warranted is also a failure mode leading to fragmentation. So the track design choices need to be precise.

May 29

Architected track hierarchy mapping analogies, world truth, narrative design, and character arc systems
Architected track hierarchy mapping analogies, world truth, narrative design, and character arc systems
On Analogies vs Allegories: one track, mode-dependent framing
The content converges substantially. A Pétain reference for AJ serves both as design inspiration (helps engineer the collaborator-vs-lioness arc correctly) and as a reader-facing allegory target (the reader who recognizes it gets depth, but the thematic payload should be derivable without it). Having two tracks produces discipline without adding new content.

The more useful distinction isn't separate tracks — it's the editor mode framing of the same material:

Expansion mode display question: "What real-world figures, systems, or events inspired this subject? What parallels help the author understand and engineer it correctly?"

Linking mode usage directive: "These parallels inform what should be designed into WI inference seeds. The reader should be able to derive the analogous real-world significance without the specific reference being named on the page."

On whether every design-side analogy becomes an allegory target: no, with an important nuance. Analogies vary in specificity. MEFO bills is a precise historical parallel the author uses to engineer Chrysalis's debt mechanics. Most readers won't recognize MEFO bills; what they should derive is the more general pattern — predatory debt instruments create political dependency. The specific reference is design-side; the general pattern is what needs to be in WI tracks. The track holds both levels; the WI tracks carry only what's reader-accessible.

The test: can the thematic payload be delivered without the reader recognizing the specific reference? If yes, the analogy is design-side inspiration that informs WI engineering. If the specific recognition IS the intended reader experience (a knowing audience reading TLTT would recognize the Pétain parallel and get meta-textual depth from it), then the WI tracks should be designed to invite that recognition. Most of the time for TLTT, recognition is optional depth-enrichment, not a prerequisite for the T payload.

One track, no naming inconsistency. Call it "Analogies" uniformly. Retire "Allegories" as a separate track name. The usage directive in Linking mode carries the allegorical function.

The mapping you're theorizing: evaluation and refinements
Your theorized structure is essentially correct. Let me verify and sharpen each connection.

World Truth tracks → PageDesign link tracks: reference, not mapping

Correct directionally. World Truth tracks (Ontology, History, Civilization, Characterization) are the source pool from which PageDesign link notes draw. Revelation link notes draw from History and Characterization. Demonstration/Activities notes draw from Ontology and Civilization. The Audit Directives on Character already encode this: "Revelations should draw from here." The relationship is: World Truth is the canonical reference; PageDesign is the selected deployment of specific items from that reference into specific scenes.

Retrospective NarrativeArchitecture ← aggregates from ← link PageDesign + WI

Correct. Character Appearance Plan and Character Development (both Editable in Audit Mode: Yes) are aggregates that fill in after the link-level PageDesign and WI tracks exist. The direction is upward: link tracks produce content that the retrospective NarrativeArchitecture summarizes. This is why they're Editable in Audit Mode — you can't write them until the link work exists.

Prospective NarrativeArchitecture → informs → Reader Prior Belief Update WI

Correct in direction but with a refinement. Reader Opinion Plan (prospective NarrativeArchitecture) is the architectural blueprint for the whole La arc: starting assumptions, update sequence across story order. Reader Prior Belief Update (WI link track) is the execution of one specific update in that blueprint. The prospective track is the design authority; the link track is the scene-specific execution. They're in the same conceptual space but at different scales — the prospective track CONSTRAINS and INFORMS the link-level work rather than mapping one-to-one.

Theme Plan (prospective) → claims that link-level evidence builds toward

Yes, but with a crucial distinction you're intuiting about the arc. The full picture:

Theme Plan (ThematicEvidence, not Editable): prospective — "these are the propositions this character's EXISTENCE serves as evidence for." Written ahead of time, claims the story is designed to prove.
Scene Theme Evidence / Character Development Meaning (link-level T tracks): deposits evidence for those propositions scene by scene.
Arc Thesis (ThematicEvidence, Editable): retrospective aggregate — "now that I've designed all the scene-level Character Development Meaning notes, what does the arc collectively prove?" This is the synthesis after the evidence is deposited.
Theme Plan → CLAIMS → link-level T tracks deposit evidence toward → Arc Thesis SYNTHESIZES what the arc proved.

The Character Development link WI track ↔ Character Development project-wide track

Yes: scene-level Inferred Character Development (WI link tracks) aggregate into Character Development (NarrativeArchitecture, Editable). These map cleanly — the aggregate is the summary of all the scene-level inferences. Same direction as Appearance Plan aggregating from Character Actions.

Does Character Development Meaning (link-level arc T) go into Theme Plan or Arc Thesis?

Separate track warranted. These are genuinely different claims:

Theme Plan: what AJ's honesty and willingness to be honest about hard things represents project-wide (static characterization as thematic argument)
Arc Thesis: what AJ's TRANSFORMATION — from impostor-general through the Lioness phase to Washington stepping down — proves as a thematic proposition (dynamic arc as thematic argument)
Character Development Meaning link notes → aggregate into → Arc Thesis.
The T claim that AJ's static existence-and-nature makes → Theme Plan.

The POV gap: warranted project-wide track for prominent protagonists
The gap is not captured by Character Development (what changes) or Reader Opinion Plan (how reader opinion evolves). The gap is specifically the designed relationship between those two trajectories — at each arc stage, what does the character believe about themselves versus what does the reader simultaneously understand?

Reader Opinion Plan tracks: "reader starts thinking X, updates to Y at chapter 9, updates to Z after chapter 27"
Character Development tracks: "character starts not understanding their capability, recognizes it by chapter 20, accepts it by chapter 28"
Gap Architecture would track: "at chapter 1: reader holds structural knowledge from Luna's recognition + Twilight's external view; character holds 'I am just a motivated volunteer'; gap width = maximum. At chapter 9: reader sees AJ starting to lead; character still insists she's following the experts; gap narrowing slightly. At chapter 27: gap closure begins when AJ recognizes her own authority as legitimate..."

This is genuinely different design work from either of the others. The gap calibration produces the FID calibration notes for each scene. Without a project-wide gap map, Gap Inference link notes would be designed in isolation per scene rather than as a continuous designed trajectory.

POV Gap Architecture (NarrativeArchitecture, conditional on sustained POV character)

Display question: "Map the gap trajectory across the arc: at each major stage, what does this character believe about themselves, and what does the reader simultaneously hold as structural counter-knowledge? When is the gap widest? When does it begin closing? When does it close or permanently invert?"

This only warrants population for characters with sustained POV arcs — AJ, Twilight, and potentially Chrysalis in her prequel. For one-off POV moments (Mali, Rainbow), the gap is designed at the link level only, and this project-wide track stays empty or unpopulated.

On track count precision
Your principle is correct: tracks are warranted when they manage inherent complexity that would otherwise be blended, not when they introduce new complexity. The POV Gap Architecture track passes this test — the trajectory information exists in the story plan already; without a track, it lives partly in Reader Opinion Plan and partly in Character Development and nowhere distinctly. The Arc Thesis passes the test — without it, the arc's thematic argument is either duplicated in Theme Plan or lost as an aggregate claim. The split between Theme Plan and Arc Thesis passes the test — one is about static characterization thematic argument, the other about dynamic transformation argument; conflating them produces notes that answer different questions in the same track.

So Narrative Architecture tracks on project wide are:
For all subjects:
Appearance Plan - aggregate of disclosure/enactment/standard inference. Editable in audit mode
Reader Opinion Plan - seems like it should not be editable in audit mode. It is declared from subject level and then constrains the links and plot? Although I can discover new twists I want too. So maybe not so simple. It's a two way iterative process?
For character and bond only:
Character Development Plan - also an iterative process?
Reader Character Perception Gap Plan - mostly top down planning in response to character development plan, and only applicable to prominent narrators?
Then does Appearance Plan end up being a two way iterative process too?

Also do Theme Plan and ArcThesis really need to be split or can they be one track? It can have multiple entries. The theme evidence from the gap plan also has to go somewhere. In a consolidate subject-wide themes track or in separate theme tracks?

May 29

Reconciled iterative track directionality and consolidated thematic evidence architecture
Reconciled iterative track directionality and consolidated thematic evidence architecture
On the directionality of NarrativeArchitecture tracks
You're right that all of them are iterative. The distinction between prospective and retrospective describes their dominant workflow direction and expected fill-time, not a one-way lock:

Prospective tracks (Reader Opinion Plan, Character Development Plan, POV Gap Architecture) are primarily written before or during early link design, because the link work needs an architectural target to aim at. But discovering through link work that a better arc exists is normal — you update the prospective track and continue. The "Editable in Audit Mode: No" flag means specifically this: during the archive evaluation phase, the audit doesn't generate primary content for these tracks. They were already written. This doesn't mean they never change; it means the audit pass treats them as stable anchors, not as fills-in-from-links.

Retrospective aggregate tracks (Appearance Plan, Character Development summary) are primarily filled from link work — you can't write a complete aggregate before you've designed any links. "Editable in Audit Mode: Yes" means: the audit pass will actively populate these, because evaluating v1 content creates link appearances that generate their content. But a rough initial sketch can exist from early in the project.

So yes — Appearance Plan is also iterative. The author plans rough appearances top-down ("AJ should appear in chapters 9, 12, 15, 27, 32"), discovers through link work that a chapter 15 appearance has no thematic warrant and chapter 17 would be richer, updates the Appearance Plan, and continues. The "Editable in Audit Mode: Yes" flag just says the audit specifically generates content here. The track can also receive top-down entries.

The POV Gap Architecture is the most constrained prospective track because gap width at each scene is load-bearing for FID calibration — the whole arc depends on exactly where the gap is at each stage. It's more resistant to mid-link revision than Reader Opinion Plan. But discovery can still reveal that the gap needs to stay wider longer than planned, or that a specific scene creates an unintended gap-narrowing.

On Theme Plan vs Arc Thesis: consolidate them
One consolidated Themes track with multiple entries is the right answer. Here's why the split was proposed and why the consolidated version is better:

The split was motivated by the Editable/Not Editable distinction — Theme Plan (prospective claims) vs Arc Thesis (retrospective synthesis aggregate). But you correctly point out that the NoteState flags already handle this distinction: an entry is Unset (provisional, prospective claim) or Confirmed (stable synthesis, written after enough link work exists). You don't need the track to distinguish workflow timing — the individual notes do it.

The cognitive discipline concern was: "the author might write prospective claims and retrospective synthesis in the same place, confusing them." But this is handled by display question guidance and NoteState. An author writing an early "I think this arc will prove X" keeps that Unset. After designing thirty relevant links, they write a synthesis entry and can Confirm it. Both coexist in the same track, distinguishable by state.

What belongs in a single consolidated Themes track:

Thematic propositions this subject's existence and nature serve as evidence for (what was Theme Plan)
Thematic propositions this subject's arc transformation proves (what was Arc Thesis)
Thematic propositions produced by the continuous reader/character perception gap (what I was calling Gap Theme Evidence — only for POV-applicable characters)
All are ThematicEvidence cognitive mode. All answer "what does this subject argue?" at the project level. Multiple entries, clearly labeled by type, with NoteState managing their stability.

Display question: "What thematic propositions does this subject serve as evidence for, project-wide? Consider: what their nature/characterization argues (observable throughout), what their arc transformation proves (requires reading the arc to landing), and for POV characters, what the reader's experience of inhabiting their limited consciousness proves (experiential rather than argumentative)."

The gap-specific evidence goes here — same track, labeled entry, NoteState handles whether it's been fully designed or is still provisional.

The full NarrativeArchitecture track picture with directionality corrected
Track	Direction	Editable in Audit Mode	Notes
Appearance Plan	Iterative (primarily bottom-up)	Yes	Audit creates link appearances; rough top-down sketch also valid
Reader Opinion Plan	Iterative (primarily top-down)	No	Written as architectural anchor; refined when discovery suggests better arc
Character Development Plan	Iterative (primarily top-down)	No	Same pattern as Reader Opinion Plan
Character Development (aggregate)	Iterative (primarily bottom-up)	Yes	Aggregates Inferred Character Development WI link notes; early sketch valid
POV Gap Architecture	Iterative (primarily top-down, most constrained)	No	Most resistant to mid-arc revision; only for prominent sustained-POV characters
Bond Story Plan	Iterative (primarily top-down)	No	Same as Character Development Plan for bonds
Bond Development Summary (aggregate)	Iterative (primarily bottom-up)	Yes	Same as Character Development aggregate for bonds

Analogies — design-side inspiration + reader-facing allegory target, one track with mode-specific framing

Garden Notes — NotesToSelf, operational
Character adds:

Character Development Plan — NarrativeArchitecture, iterative, primarily top-down, Not Editable
Character Development (aggregate) — NarrativeArchitecture, iterative, primarily bottom-up, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes
POV Gap Architecture — NarrativeArchitecture, primarily top-down, conditional on sustained POV, Not Editable
Bond adds:

Bond Story Plan — NarrativeArchitecture, iterative, primarily top-down, Not Editable
Bond Development Summary (aggregate) — NarrativeArchitecture, iterative, primarily bottom-up, Editable in Audit Mode: Yes
No Arc Thesis as a separate track — consolidated into Themes.

I don't think "Character Development Plan" and "Character Development (aggregate)" are two different things. Shouldn't they be the same track? By the track being an iterative process between the project wide data and the link data for scene specific, both top down and bottom up are allowed. Same for "Bond Story Plan" and "Bond Development Summary (aggregate)".

For the themes, currently most subjects have two possible thematic proposition evidence tracks: standard/witnessed/rational inference, and evidence the reader arrives at from processing and thinking about the clash with their prior beliefs. Then character/bond has a third: evidence witnessed from character/bond development. And character has a fourth: theme evidence the reader arrives at from processing and thinking about the character-reader perception gap.
That's what we're working with on the link level. Should the subject level Theme Plan track aggregate all 4 across all links? Keep in mind the themes themselves are the same, but I'm not sure if the data is radically different to warrant different tracks or if they should be the same subject wide tracks because it's the same themes. But maybe different tracks are good because the same theme can be supported through different mechanisms? If they are all one track, then multiple notes in the track reference the same theme but hold different data talking about different mechanisms? Or, is the data going to be similar or actually the same, despite the discovery mechanisms (rational inference, prior belief clash, inference on character development, gap perception)?

May 29

Consolidated redundant tracks and reconciled thematic evidence mechanisms
Consolidated redundant tracks and reconciled thematic evidence mechanisms
On Character Development and Bond Story Plan: you're right
One iterative track each. The top-down conception (early entries, Unset) and the bottom-up synthesis (later entries, Confirmed after link work) are just the same track at different stages of development. NoteState handles the temporal distinction. Calling them two separate tracks was over-engineering a single iterative process.

Character project-wide NarrativeArchitecture: Appearance Plan · Character Development (one track) · Reader Opinion Plan · POV Gap Architecture

Bond project-wide NarrativeArchitecture: Appearance Plan · Bond Development (one track) · Reader Opinion Plan

On the four theme mechanisms at subject level
The mechanisms are genuinely different arguments. Let me show this concretely for AJ and P1 (the Predator's Dilemma):

Standard rational inference: The reader watches AJ choose the machine gun over command and infers — conscience that has been wounded strategically downgrades itself, which means capacity-with-self-suppressed-conscience is functionally incapacitated even when materially present. P1 evidence: the solution requires conscience willing to deploy fully, not just to exist.

Prior belief clash: Reader held "AJ is a farmer in the wrong war." La corrections about her parents' ambition, Celestia's recognition, her material preparation — the act of correcting that prior argues P1: the preparation mechanism for the solution precedes the agent recognizing themselves as the solution.

Arc transformation: AJ's arc from impostor-general to Washington-who-steps-down is lived proof of P1 as a viable proposition. Not "this is what P1 requires theoretically" but "this is what a person who actually embodies P1 looks like from birth to completion."

Gap: The reader inhabiting AJ's "just doing what needs to be done" while simultaneously understanding she's the only one holding the Republic together gives the reader the interior experience of what it feels like to be the P1 solution without being able to see yourself as one. This proves something the other three mechanisms can't: that P1 requires external recognition or crisis to become visible to the one living it.

These are four different arguments for the same proposition. The data is genuinely different. But — and this is the important question — should different mechanisms require different tracks?

The argument for one consolidated Themes track: They're all ThematicEvidence, same cognitive mode, same propositions. The author asking "what does AJ argue?" benefits from seeing all four mechanism-types together rather than hunting across four tracks. The display question can prompt the author to cover all relevant mechanisms. Fewer tracks.

The argument against consolidation: Different mechanisms require different cognitive stances when writing. An author might write thorough standard-inference notes and never realize the gap mechanism adds a distinct argument. Separate tracks enforce the prompting.

The resolution: One Themes track, but the display question explicitly names all four mechanisms as distinct contribution types to address. An entry might cover one mechanism applied to one proposition, or multiple. The author writing the track sees the prompt and knows they need to consider each mechanism that applies to this subject type.

Display question: "What thematic propositions does this subject serve as evidence for? Note the mechanism: (1) rational inference from witnessed events, (2) reader's prior belief correction process, (3) arc transformation [character/bond], (4) inhabited gap experience [POV character]. The same proposition evidenced through different mechanisms produces different arguments — each is worth a separate entry."

Multiple entries per track, each entry noting which proposition and which mechanism it addresses. NoteState distinguishes prospective claims (Unset) from confirmed post-link syntheses.

Why this structure is correct, not just convenient
The alternative — four separate Themes tracks corresponding to the four mechanisms — would produce fragmentation without payoff. When asking "what does this subject argue?", the answer draws from all four simultaneously. The proposition is the organizing unit, not the mechanism. Notes in a single track can be read together and understood as a multi-part argument for each proposition. The mechanism distinction belongs inside the note as a labeled attribute, not as a track boundary.

The only case where separate tracks would add value is if the display questions needed to be radically different from each other. But they don't — they all ask "what does this subject argue?" The mechanism is a sub-question within that: "and through what mechanism does this argument arrive?" That's display question territory, not track separation territory.

Character-Reader Perception Gap (link): Unset → WorldInference

Gap Meaning (link): Unset → ThematicEvidence

2. Character Theme Plan display question is too narrow

Current: "What thematic propositions does this character embody throughout their appearances across the project?"

This only asks about static characterization as thematic evidence. We established the project-wide Themes track should be consolidated and prompt all mechanism types. The Arc Thesis was retired as a separate track precisely because it belongs here. Suggested revision: "What thematic propositions does this character serve as evidence for? Note: evidence arrives through different mechanisms — what their nature and characterization argues, what their arc transformation proves, and for POV characters, what the reader's experience of inhabiting their limited consciousness proves. Each mechanism may support the same or different propositions and warrants a separate entry."

Bond's Themes display question has the same issue — "What themes do this bond represent?" doesn't prompt consideration of the bond development arc as a distinct mechanism. Every other subject type uses "Analogies" as the track name. World Law uses "Allegories." The World Law display question is also currently only reader-facing ("What does this made-up world law serve as an allegory for?") and misses the design-side half. Suggested revision: "What real-world parallels inspire this world law's design, and what allegorical meaning should attentive readers be able to derive through inference seeds without it being named on the page?"

5. Analogies display question coverage inconsistency

CivSystem and Technology both cover both dimensions correctly — design-side inspiration and the reader-facing note that allegory must not appear on page. Character and Organization only ask about the design-side ("What real-world figures serve as inspiration?", "What real world inspiration informs this organization's design?"). 6. Character Development Usage Directive is misleading

Current: "Fill this in after links are done." This frames the track as purely retrospective. We established it as an iterative track — initial conception can and should be written early as an architectural anchor, then updated as link work reveals what actually emerged. Suggested: "Initial conception can be written as an architectural anchor before link design. Update and refine from link work as the arc emerges."

7. Technology Usage Plan Editable in Audit Mode should be Yes

May 29

Validated distinction between design-inspired analogies and reader-inferred allegories
Validated distinction between design-inspired analogies and reader-inferred allegories
The Analogies/Allegories distinction is real, and the current file was tracking it correctly
The naming inconsistency is actually a principled distinction, not an error. Two genuinely different relationships exist:

Design-side (Analogies): The author started from a real-world referent and built the fictional thing outward. Pétain and Washington for AJ. Nordic cooperative economics for Harmonic Capitalism. The author asks "what real-world X am I modeling this after?" The track records design reference material.

Reader-facing (Allegories): The author invented the thing from story needs, but designed it to invoke real-world parallels in readers through inference. Earth Pony Magic wasn't designed by thinking "I want it to work like quantum mechanics" — it was invented for the story world. But it's designed so readers derive something about collective agricultural labor. The track records intended reader inference targets, not design inputs.

The file is correct that World Law and Technology use "Allegories" as the display name. Both are reader-facing only. The track type "Analogies" remains consistent across all (same cognitive mode — the author thinking about the story-to-real-world relationship). Only the display name and display question differ.

One fix needed: Technology's current display question asks "What real world technology inspires this in-universe fantasy technology?" — this implies design-side analogy, which the user says doesn't apply. Technology is allegory-only. Remove the first half. Revised: "What real-world parallels should attentive readers be able to derive from this technology's design and usage, through inference seeds without being stated?"

CivSystem correctly has "Analogies" because Harmonic Capitalism and Fascist Aquileia genuinely are designed FROM real-world political-economic systems. The design inspiration and the reader-facing allegory are the same notes — the author wrote "this is like Nordic social democracy" and that simultaneously records what inspired it AND what readers should derive.

Bond: no Analogies/Allegories track confirmed correct
Bond's Source Material References track covers what external material it actually draws on — canon moments and fanon shipping. The TwiJack bond wasn't designed by thinking "I'll model this after [real-world couple]," and it's not specifically engineered to invoke a particular real-world relationship type in readers' minds. Neither design-side analog nor reader-facing allegory applies. The track correctly doesn't exist for Bond.
